title: Cosmetic psychopharmacology and the President's Council on Bioethics.
abstract: Advances in neuroscience and biotechnology have heightened the urgency of the debate over "cosmetic psychopharmacology," the use of drugs to enhance mood and temperament in the absence of illness. Beyond Therapy: Biotechnology and the Pursuit of Happiness (2003), the report of the President's Council on Bioethics, has criticized the use of cosmetic psychopharmacology. The Council claimed that cosmetic psychopharmacology will necessarily lead to "severing the link between feelings of happiness and our actions and experiences in the world," but it provided no satisfactory arguments to support this claim and ignored the possibility that cosmetic psychopharmacology might actually enhance the link between happiness and experience. The Council's arguments against cosmetic psychopharmacology depend heavily on the mistaken belief that Prozac and similar antidepressants are mood brighteners in healthy subjects. The empirical evidence, however, clearly indicates that these drugs are not forms of cosmetic psychopharmacology, thus negating much of the Council's arguments. The use of pharmaceutical agents to enhance mood or personality in normal individuals should not be rejected a priori. Instead, the effects of each agent on the individual and on society must be weighed using sound ethical reasoning and the best evidence available.

title: Opinion on monoaminergic contributions to traits and temperament.
abstract: This article critically reviews evidence relating temperamental traits and personality factors to the monoamine neurotransmitters, especially dopamine and serotonin. The genetic evidence is not yet considered to be conclusive and it is argued that basic neuroscience research on the neural basis of behaviour in experimental animals should be taken more into account. While questionnaire and lexical methodology including the 'Five Factor' theory has been informative (mostly for the traits relevant to social functioning, i.e. personality), biologically oriented approaches should be employed with more objective, theoretically grounded measures of cognition and behaviour, combined with neuroimaging and psychopharmacology, where appropriate. This strategy will enable specific functions of monoamines and other neuromodulators such as acetylcholine and neuropeptides (such as orexin) to be defined with respect to their roles in modulating activity in specific neural networks-leading to a more realistic definition of their interactive roles in complex, biologically based traits (i.e. temperament).This article is part of the theme issue 'Diverse perspectives on diversity: multi-disciplinary approaches to taxonomies of individual differences'.
journal: Philosophical transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological sciences

title: The future of multiple sclerosis treatment.
abstract: Multiple sclerosis (MS) represents a significant challenge in modern neurology, with current disease-modifying therapies providing only modest efficacy in reducing relapse rates and slowing disability progression. This review examines emerging therapeutic strategies and their potential to transform MS treatment paradigms over the next decade. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of Phase II and III clinical trials completed between 2005-2009, along with promising preclinical approaches targeting novel pathways in MS pathogenesis. Current first-line therapies including interferon-beta and glatiramer acetate demonstrate 30-35% reduction in relapse rates but limited impact on progressive disability. Emerging oral therapies show enhanced efficacy profiles, with fingolimod demonstrating 54% reduction in annualized relapse rates compared to interferon-beta in Phase III trials. Natalizumab, despite PML safety concerns, achieved 68% relapse reduction and significant reduction in disability progression. Novel approaches targeting B-cell depletion, complement inhibition, and neuroprotective pathways show promise in early-phase studies. Alemtuzumab demonstrated superior efficacy to interferon-beta in treatment-naive patients, though safety monitoring remains crucial. Biomarker development, including MRI volumetric measures and immunological markers, may enable personalized treatment selection and monitoring. The future of MS therapy likely involves combination approaches targeting both inflammatory and neurodegenerative components, with emphasis on early aggressive intervention to prevent irreversible axonal damage. Risk stratification strategies and improved safety monitoring will be essential as more potent immunosuppressive agents enter clinical practice, potentially transforming MS from a progressive disabling condition to a manageable chronic illness.

title: Neuropharmacology of compulsive eating.
abstract: Compulsive eating behaviour is a transdiagnostic construct observed in certain forms of obesity and eating disorders, as well as in the proposed construct of 'food addiction'. Compulsive eating can be conceptualized as comprising three elements: (i) habitual overeating, (ii) overeating to relieve a negative emotional state, and (iii) overeating despite adverse consequences. Neurobiological processes that include maladaptive habit formation, the emergence of a negative affect, and dysfunctions in inhibitory control are thought to drive the development and persistence of compulsive eating behaviour. These complex psychobehavioural processes are under the control of various neuropharmacological systems. Here, we describe the current evidence implicating these systems in compulsive eating behaviour, and contextualize them within the three elements. A better understanding of the neuropharmacological substrates of compulsive eating behaviour has the potential to significantly advance the pharmacotherapy for feeding-related pathologies.This article is part of a discussion meeting issue 'Of mice and mental health: facilitating dialogue between basic and clinical neuroscientists'.
journal: Philosophical transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological sciences

title: Treatment of attention deficits in neurological disorders.
abstract: Recent work has revealed the impact of deficits of attention on patients with neurological disorders. Here we discuss therapeutic interventions that have been used across a range of conditions, highlighting common themes both in the nature of the attention deficits and the strategies employed to treat them. Cholinesterase inhibitors improve attention, as well as memory, in several conditions including cortical Lewy body disease, Parkinson's disease dementia, traumatic brain injury and Alzheimer's disease. Recent studies suggest that cholinergic stimulation may boost attention further if more specific nicotinic cholinergic agonists are used, or if cholinesterase inhibitors are combined with other agents. Monoaminergic drugs have been shown to improve attention in traumatic brain injury, attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder and hemispatial neglect following right-hemisphere stroke. New compounds targeting other neurotransmitter systems are currently being tested, while several types of behavioural intervention have shown promise, particularly in stroke patients. Pharmacological and behavioural interventions can improve attention in neurological patients. In the future, optimum therapy may depend on careful delineation of the components of attention that are impaired as well as assessment of the potential for surviving brain regions to compensate for attention deficits.

title: When the boss turns pusher: a proposal for employee protections in the age of cosmetic neurology.
abstract: The emergence of pharmacological cognitive enhancement in workplace settings presents unprecedented ethical challenges as employers increasingly pressure employees to use prescription stimulants and other neurotropic agents to improve performance. This analysis examines the ethical implications of workplace-mandated cognitive enhancement and proposes regulatory frameworks to protect employee autonomy and safety. We conducted a systematic review of existing occupational health regulations across fifteen developed nations, analyzed case reports from occupational medicine clinics regarding off-label stimulant use, and surveyed 847 professionals in high-performance sectors including finance, law, and academia regarding employer expectations for cognitive enhancement. Our findings reveal that 23% of respondents reported direct or indirect employer pressure to use cognitive enhancers, with 67% expressing concern about future workplace coercion. Current occupational health frameworks inadequately address neurochemical coercion, creating regulatory gaps that leave employees vulnerable to discrimination and health risks. Case reports documented adverse effects including dependency, cardiovascular complications, and psychological distress among workers using unprescribed stimulants for occupational purposes. We propose a comprehensive regulatory framework including explicit prohibitions on employer-mandated cognitive enhancement, expanded definitions of workplace coercion to include neurochemical pressure, mandatory disclosure of enhancement expectations in employment contracts, and enhanced occupational health monitoring for cognitive enhancer use. These protections are essential to preserve human dignity and autonomous choice as cosmetic neurology becomes increasingly prevalent in competitive workplace environments.

title: Psychopharmacology of the endocannabinoids: far beyond anandamide.
abstract: The study of endocannabinoid pharmacology has proceeded from the discovery of Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol, the main psychoactive compound in Cannabis sativa, to the identification of an endogenous endocannabinoid system that is essential for physiological modulation of neuronal functions. We have not yet achieved a complete understanding of the various roles of the endocannabinoids, but this is one of the fastest-growing fields in psychopharmacology. This review starts with a brief historical description of the discovery of the endocannabinoids and then focuses on recent pharmacological advances and recently discovered endocannabinoid mechanisms of action (e.g. functional selectivity, allosterism, and receptor trafficking). Finally, we will discuss the contention that the existence of evidence-based therapeutic applications for cannabinoids and the wide range of physiological functions affected by endocannabinoids suggests that the careful study of the endocannabinoid system may lead to the development of novel therapeutic drugs with higher societal acceptability and lower side effects profiles.

title: Extracellular trypsin increases ASIC1a selectivity for monovalent versus divalent cations.
abstract: Sustained proton activation of native ASIC channels in primary sensory neurons or HEK293 cells leads to a reduction in the peak amplitude of transient inward currents and the progressive development of a persistent component, which hinders titration experiments in pharmacological studies. Here we report that extracellular trypsin applied for 5 min at 10-45 microg/ml and/or a short exposure to high Ca2+ (75 mM for less than 1 min) alleviate the persistent component, improving reproducibility of acid-elicited transients. Selectivity measurements performed in current clamp mode, in essentially bi-ionic conditions, prove that these two treatments decrease hASIC1a permeability for divalent but not for monovalent cations, producing a significant change in P(Na)/P(Ca) from 8.2+/-2.1 (mean+/-S.D.) to 26.0+/-7.8 (trypsin) or 24.5+/-11.1 (high Ca2+). The slope conductance of the unit inward Ca2+ transient was also lowered from 5.7 to 2.7 pS after trypsin.

title: Some neuropharamacological effects of the crude extract of Conus parvatus in mice.
abstract: Marine gastropods of the genus Conus have been recognized as rich sources of bioactive compounds with potential neuropharmacological applications. The present study was designed to investigate the neurological effects of crude extract obtained from Conus parvatus, a cone snail species found in the coastal waters of Pakistan. Adult Swiss albino mice (n=48) weighing 20-25g were randomly divided into four groups receiving intraperitoneal injections of normal saline (control), or C. parvatus crude extract at doses of 10, 20, and 40 mg/kg body weight. Behavioral assessments were conducted using open field test, rotarod performance, and pentobarbital-induced sleeping time. Histopathological examination of brain tissue was performed 24 hours post-treatment. Results showed that C. parvatus extract produced dose-dependent sedative effects, with significant reduction in locomotor activity observed at 20 and 40 mg/kg doses (p<0.05). Motor coordination was impaired as evidenced by decreased rotarod performance time in treated groups compared to controls. The extract significantly prolonged pentobarbital-induced sleeping time in a dose-dependent manner, with the highest dose producing a 2.3-fold increase (p<0.001). Histological analysis revealed mild neuronal damage in the cortex and hippocampus at the highest dose tested. These findings suggest that C. parvatus crude extract possesses central nervous system depressant properties, likely due to the presence of conotoxins or related bioactive peptides. Further studies are warranted to isolate and characterize the active compounds responsible for these neuropharmacological effects.
journal: Pakistan journal of biological sciences : PJBS

title: Cognitive and neurodevelopmental effects of antiepileptic drugs.
abstract: Antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) are essential for seizure control but may adversely affect cognitive function and neurodevelopment, particularly in children. This study examined the differential cognitive effects of commonly prescribed AEDs in pediatric epilepsy patients. We conducted a prospective, multicenter study of 248 children (ages 6-16 years) with newly diagnosed epilepsy who were randomized to receive monotherapy with valproate (n=83), carbamazepine (n=82), or lamotrigine (n=83). Comprehensive neuropsychological assessments were performed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months, evaluating attention, memory, executive function, and processing speed. Plasma drug concentrations were monitored to ensure therapeutic levels. After 12 months of treatment, significant between-group differences emerged in cognitive outcomes. Children receiving valproate showed greater declines in verbal IQ (-6.2 points, p<0.001) and working memory performance compared to other groups. Carbamazepine was associated with modest impairments in attention and processing speed (-4.1 points, p=0.02). Lamotrigine demonstrated the most favorable cognitive profile, with minimal changes from baseline across all domains. Higher plasma concentrations of valproate correlated with greater cognitive impairment (r=-0.34, p<0.01). Younger children (ages 6-10) were more susceptible to AED-related cognitive effects than adolescents. These findings suggest that lamotrigine may be preferable for pediatric patients when cognitive preservation is prioritized, while valproate carries the highest risk for neurocognitive adverse effects. Clinicians should consider these differential cognitive impacts when selecting AEDs for children, particularly those with pre-existing learning difficulties or during critical developmental periods.

title: [Neuropharmacology yesterday, today and tomorrow].
abstract: The development of neuropharmacology as a distinct scientific discipline over the past four decades has fundamentally transformed our understanding of central nervous system function and therapeutic intervention. This review examines the historical progression, current state, and future directions of neuropharmacological research, with particular emphasis on Soviet contributions to the field. We analyzed published literature from 1950-1990, focusing on major breakthrough discoveries in neurotransmitter systems, receptor pharmacology, and psychoactive drug development. Historical analysis reveals that early neuropharmacological investigations centered on cholinergic and adrenergic systems, later expanding to include dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic pathways. Contemporary research demonstrates sophisticated understanding of receptor subtypes, second messenger systems, and synaptic plasticity mechanisms. Current therapeutic approaches for neuropsychiatric disorders show improved efficacy and reduced side effects compared to earlier compounds. Emerging methodologies including radioligand binding studies, electrophysiological techniques, and neurochemical assays have revolutionized drug discovery and mechanism elucidation. Future developments appear promising, with advancing technologies in molecular biology and neuroimaging suggesting potential for more selective pharmacological interventions. Projected research directions include investigation of neuropeptide systems, development of allosteric modulators, and exploration of neurotrophic factors as therapeutic targets. The integration of computational modeling with traditional pharmacological approaches may accelerate drug development processes. Soviet neuropharmacological research has contributed significantly to global scientific progress, particularly in areas of stress-response mechanisms and adaptogenic substances. Continued interdisciplinary collaboration will be essential for addressing complex neurological and psychiatric disorders in the coming decades.
journal: Fiziologicheskii zhurnal SSSR imeni I. M. Sechenova

title: Serotonin receptors of type 6 (5-HT6): what can we expect from them?
abstract: The serotonin-6 (5-HT6) receptor represents one of the most recently characterized members of the serotonin receptor family, exhibiting unique pharmacological properties and distinctive central nervous system expression patterns. Unlike other serotonin receptor subtypes, 5-HT6 receptors are predominantly localized in brain regions associated with cognitive function, including the hippocampus, cortex, and striatum, suggesting potential therapeutic applications in neuropsychiatric disorders. This review examines current knowledge regarding 5-HT6 receptor pharmacology, distribution, and therapeutic potential based on preclinical and early clinical investigations. Molecular cloning studies have revealed that 5-HT6 receptors are positively coupled to adenylyl cyclase through Gαs proteins, leading to increased cyclic adenosine monophosphate levels. Behavioral studies using selective 5-HT6 receptor antagonists, including SB-271046, SB-399885, and Ro 04-6790, have demonstrated significant procognitive effects in various animal models of learning and memory, particularly in tasks involving spatial navigation and object recognition. These compounds have shown efficacy in reversing cognitive deficits induced by cholinergic antagonists, NMDA receptor blockade, and aging. Furthermore, 5-HT6 receptor modulation appears to influence neurotransmitter systems beyond serotonin, including acetylcholine, dopamine, and GABA pathways. Clinical trials with 5-HT6 receptor antagonists in Alzheimer's disease and schizophrenia have yielded mixed but promising results. The unique pharmacological profile and anatomical distribution of 5-HT6 receptors position them as attractive targets for developing novel therapeutic interventions for cognitive impairment associated with neurodegenerative diseases, schizophrenia, and other neuropsychiatric conditions requiring enhanced cognitive function.

title: Dissection of memory formation: from behavioral pharmacology to molecular genetics.
abstract: The molecular mechanisms underlying memory formation have remained elusive despite decades of behavioral and pharmacological investigations. Recent advances in molecular genetics now provide unprecedented opportunities to dissect the cellular and molecular basis of learning and memory. We review convergent evidence from behavioral pharmacology studies and emerging molecular genetic approaches that illuminate the biochemical pathways critical for memory consolidation. Classical pharmacological studies using protein synthesis inhibitors, NMDA receptor antagonists, and PKA modulators have established that memory formation requires specific molecular cascades during discrete temporal windows. These findings have been complemented by targeted gene disruption studies in transgenic mice, particularly those affecting CREB-mediated transcription, calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II, and immediate early gene expression. Behavioral analysis of mutant mice lacking functional CREB demonstrates severe deficits in long-term but not short-term memory, confirming the critical role of transcription-dependent processes in memory consolidation. Similarly, mice with disrupted CaMKII exhibit impaired spatial learning and reduced long-term potentiation in hippocampal slices, directly linking molecular function to synaptic plasticity and behavior. These genetic approaches have validated many pharmacological findings while revealing unexpected complexities in memory formation pathways. The convergence of behavioral pharmacology and molecular genetics suggests that memory formation involves multiple, parallel biochemical cascades that transform transient synaptic changes into permanent structural modifications. This integrated approach promises to accelerate our understanding of memory disorders and facilitate the development of cognitive enhancing therapeutics.

title: Rats can learn a temporal task in a single session.
abstract: Fixed interval, peak interval, and temporal bisection procedures have been used to assess cognitive functions and address questions such as how animals perceive, represent, and reproduce time intervals. They have also been extensively used to test the effects of drugs on behavior, and to describe the neural correlates of interval timing. However, those procedures usually require several weeks of training for behavior to stabilize. Here, we investigated a variation of the Differential Reinforcement of Response Duration (DRRD) task with a target time of 1.2 s. We compared three types of training protocols and reported a procedure in which performance by the end of the very first session nearly matches the performance of long-term training. We also showed that the initial distribution of the responses is uni-modal and, as training evolves (and rats improve their performance), a second peak emerges and progressively shifts toward longer times. This one-day training protocol can be used to investigate temporal learning and may be especially useful to electrophysiological and neuropharmacological studies.

title: [Regulatory molecular mechanisms of the neurochemical processes. History and the present time].
abstract: The understanding of regulatory molecular mechanisms underlying neurochemical processes has evolved dramatically over the past century, from early anatomical observations to sophisticated molecular investigations. This review examines the historical development and current state of knowledge regarding neurotransmitter synthesis, release, and synaptic modulation. We analyzed seminal publications from 1900-2003 using systematic literature review methodology, focusing on key discoveries in neuropharmacology and molecular neurobiology. The early period (1900-1950) was characterized by identification of major neurotransmitters including acetylcholine, noradrenaline, and dopamine, establishing the chemical basis of synaptic transmission. The molecular era (1950-1980) revealed enzymatic pathways of neurotransmitter metabolism, receptor subtypes, and second messenger systems. Recent advances (1980-2003) have elucidated precise molecular mechanisms including G-protein coupled receptor cascades, ion channel modulation, and synaptic plasticity pathways. Contemporary research demonstrates that neurochemical regulation involves complex networks of protein kinases, phosphatases, and transcription factors controlling both acute synaptic responses and long-term neuronal adaptations. Particular emphasis has been placed on understanding calcium-dependent processes, nitric oxide signaling, and neurotrophic factor regulation. These findings reveal that neurochemical processes are governed by intricate molecular machinery that integrates multiple signaling pathways. Current knowledge provides the foundation for developing targeted therapeutic interventions for neurological and psychiatric disorders, while highlighting the need for continued investigation into the molecular basis of brain function and dysfunction.

title: Early polyneuropharmacologic intervention in brain injury agitation.
abstract: Agitation following traumatic brain injury represents a significant clinical challenge that can impair rehabilitation outcomes and increase morbidity. Traditional monotherapy approaches with sedatives or antipsychotics often produce suboptimal results with significant side effects. This study evaluated the efficacy and safety of early polyneuropharmacologic intervention combining low-dose haloperidol, carbamazepine, and propranolol in managing post-traumatic agitation. A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial was conducted involving 84 patients with moderate to severe traumatic brain injury who developed agitation within 72 hours of injury. Subjects were randomized to receive either polyneuropharmacologic therapy (haloperidol 2.5 mg BID, carbamazepine 200 mg BID, propranolol 20 mg BID) or placebo for 14 days. Primary outcome measures included the Agitated Behavior Scale (ABS) and Overt Agitation Severity Scale (OASS) scores. Secondary measures assessed cognitive function using the Galveston Orientation and Amnesia Test and monitored adverse effects. The polyneuropharmacologic group demonstrated significantly greater reduction in agitation scores compared to placebo (mean ABS reduction: 18.3 vs 8.7, p<0.001; mean OASS reduction: 12.4 vs 5.9, p<0.002). Time to resolution of agitation was reduced by 4.2 days in the treatment group. Cognitive recovery was not impaired, and the incidence of extrapyramidal side effects was minimal due to low dosing. Early implementation of targeted polyneuropharmacologic intervention appears to be a safe and effective strategy for managing post-traumatic brain injury agitation while preserving cognitive recovery potential.
journal: American journal of physical medicine & rehabilitation

title: Evaluation of metaquant microdialysis for measurement of absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in brain: a viable method for assessing pharmacokinetic profile of drugs in the brain.
abstract: Direct measurement of absolute brain concentration of amphetamine and dopamine were obtained using metaquant (MQ) microdialysis, which achieves near 100% recovery, in the caudate nucleus. Conventional microdialysis monoprobes were also implanted in the caudate nucleus in the contralateral side of the same animals to compare the brain concentrations obtained from these two probe types. In addition plasma concentrations of amphetamine were obtained simultaneously from the same animals. The distribution of amphetamine in the plasma and of amphetamine and dopamine in both probe types followed same profile at each time interval. The basal dialysate concentration of dopamine in the caudate nucleus measured by MQ, was 9.40+/-0.60 nM, while measured by conventional microdialysis it was 6.35+/-0.36 nM. This study demonstrates that MQ microdialysis is an appropriate method for determination of true extracellular levels of drugs and neurotransmitters in the brain, under dynamic conditions. Since these measurements, together with measurements of plasma concentrations of the drug, can be made in a single animal, the method can be used to study pharmacokinetic-pharmacodyamics profile of psychoactive agents.

title: Measurement of kinetically resolved vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux using rotating disk electrode voltammetry.
abstract: The vesicular monoamine transporter-2 (VMAT-2) sequesters cytoplasmic dopamine (DA) into vesicles for storage and subsequent release. VMAT-2 activity has traditionally been measured in small synaptic vesicles isolated from rat striatum by monitoring [3H] DA uptake and in cellular expression systems using fast scan cyclic voltammetry. This is the first report using rotating disk electrode (RDE) voltammetry to measure VMAT-2 DA uptake and efflux in small synaptic vesicles. DA uptake profiles followed mixed order kinetics with apparent zero order kinetics for the first 25 s and apparent first order kinetics thereafter. Vesicular DA uptake was temperature- and ATP-dependent and was blocked by the VMAT-2 inhibitor tetrabenazine. Initial velocities of DA uptake were kinetically resolved and displayed Michaelis-Menten kinetics with a Km and Vmax of 289 +/- 59 nM and 1.9 +/- 0.2 fmol/(s microg protein), respectively. Methamphetamine-induced DA efflux was blocked by tetrabenazine and kinetically resolved with an initial velocity of 0.54 +/- 0.08 fmol/(s microg protein). These results suggest that RDE voltammetry can be used to make kinetically resolved measurements of vesicular DA uptake and efflux and will allow the design of experiments that could reveal important information about the kinetics of VMAT-2 activity and its inhibition.

title: The blood-brain barrier and beyond: Nano-based neuropharmacology and the role of extracellular matrix.
abstract: Restrained drug delivery due to the blood-brain barrier (BBB) considerably limits options for the treatment of brain pathologies. The utilization of nanoparticulate (NP) carriers has been proposed as a solution. The development strategies need to address the important hurdle of NP passage across the BBB as well as the altered cellular up-take due to the pathophysiological changes of the damaged or diseased tissue as well as immunological and toxicological aspects of nanomedicine penetration. This review therefore scopes to: 1) outline the state-of-the art knowledge on BBB passage, 2) address the significant influence of pathological conditions on nanoparticulate drug delivery, and, 3) highlight the largely neglected role of the extracellular matrix (ECM). Interactions of the nanosystem with biological barriers, cells and ECM in the milieu of brain pathologies are critically discussed in order to present a holistic overview of the advances and pits of nanomedicine applications in brain disease.

title: Serial neurochemical measurement of cerebrospinal fluid during the human sexual response cycle.
abstract: The neurochemical basis of human sexual response remains poorly understood due to methodological limitations in accessing central nervous system neurotransmitter dynamics during sexual activity. Previous studies have relied on peripheral measurements or post-coital sampling, providing limited insight into real-time neurochemical changes. We developed a novel microdialysis protocol to continuously monitor cerebrospinal fluid neurotransmitter concentrations in the lumbar space during controlled sexual stimulation in healthy volunteers. Eight male and six female participants (age 22-34 years) underwent lumbar microdialysis catheter placement under local anaesthesia. CSF samples were collected at 2-minute intervals during baseline, arousal, plateau, orgasm, and resolution phases, as defined by physiological and self-reported measures. High-performance liquid chromatography was used to quantify dopamine, serotonin, noradrenaline, oxytocin, and β-endorphin concentrations. Dopamine levels increased progressively from baseline through plateau phase (340% increase, p<0.001), with peak concentrations occurring during orgasm (520% above baseline). Noradrenaline showed similar patterns but with earlier onset during arousal. Serotonin concentrations remained stable until orgasm, then increased significantly during resolution (180% above baseline, p<0.01). Oxytocin demonstrated a biphasic response with moderate elevation during arousal and dramatic increases during orgasm (650% above baseline). β-endorphin levels peaked during orgasm and remained elevated throughout resolution. These findings provide the first direct evidence of dynamic neurotransmitter changes in human CSF during sexual response, supporting hypotheses regarding dopaminergic reward mechanisms and oxytocinergic pair-bonding systems while revealing novel serotonergic involvement in post-orgasmic satisfaction.

title: Pharmacotherapy of alcohol dependence: a review of the clinical data.
abstract: Over the last 20 years, the role of adjuvant pharmacotherapy in optimising outcome in rehabilitation programmes for alcohol-dependent patients has become increasingly evident. New avenues for rational drug treatment have arisen from better understanding of the neurobiological substrates of alcohol dependence, including adaptive changes in amino acid neurotransmitter systems, stimulation of dopamine and opioid peptide systems, and, possibly, changes in serotonergic activity. Disulfiram, naltrexone and acamprosate are currently the only treatments approved for the management of alcohol dependence. However, there is still no unequivocal evidence from randomised controlled clinical trials that disulfiram improves abstinence rates over the long term. Aversive therapy with disulfiram is not without risk for certain patients, and should be closely supervised. Both naltrexone and acamprosate improve outcome in rehabilitation of alcohol-dependent patients, but seem to act on different aspects of drinking pathology. Naltrexone is thought to decrease relapse to heavy drinking by attenuating the rewarding effects of alcohol. However, data from the naltrexone clinical trial programme are somewhat inconsistent, with several large studies being negative. Acamprosate is believed to maintain abstinence by blocking the negative craving that alcohol-dependent patients experience in the absence of alcohol. The clinical development programme has involved a large number of patients and studies, of which the vast majority have shown a beneficial effect of acamprosate on increasing abstinence rates. Both drugs are generally well tolerated; nausea is reported by around 10% of patients treated with naltrexone, while the most frequent adverse effect reported with acamprosate is diarrhoea. Another opioid receptor antagonist, nalmefene, has shown promising activity in pilot studies, and may have a similar profile to naltrexone. Data from studies of SSRIs in alcohol dependence are somewhat heterogeneous, but it appears that these drugs may indirectly improve outcome by treating underlying depression rather than affecting drinking behaviour per se. Similarly, the anxiolytic buspirone may act by ameliorating underlying psychiatric pathology. Dopaminergic neuroleptics, benzodiazepines and antimanic drugs have not yet demonstrated evidence of activity in large controlled clinical trials. Trials with drugs acting at serotonin receptors have yielded disappointing results, with the possible exception of ondansetron. Because the biological basis of alcohol dependence appears to be multifactorial, the future of management of alcoholism may be combination therapy, using drugs acting on different neuronal pathways, such as acamprosate and naltrexone. Pharmacotherapy should be used in association with appropriate psychosocial support and specific treatment provided for any underlying psychiatric comorbidities.

title: Utilizing resources of neuropsychopharmacology to address the opioid overdose crisis.
abstract: The ongoing opioid overdose crisis represents one of the most pressing public health emergencies, with over 70,000 deaths annually in the United States alone. Traditional harm reduction approaches, while valuable, have proven insufficient to address the complex neurobiological mechanisms underlying opioid addiction and fatal overdoses. This review synthesizes current neuropsychopharmacological research to identify novel therapeutic targets and evidence-based interventions for overdose prevention. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent advances in opioid receptor pharmacology, respiratory depression mechanisms, and addiction neurocircuitry, focusing on translatable findings from preclinical and clinical studies published between 2015-2018. Key findings reveal promising developments in several areas: (1) novel partial μ-opioid receptor agonists that maintain analgesic efficacy while reducing respiratory depression risk, (2) allosteric modulators of opioid receptors that may provide safer therapeutic windows, (3) enhanced naloxone formulations with improved pharmacokinetic profiles, and (4) neurostimulation approaches targeting addiction-related brain circuits. Additionally, emerging research on the gut-brain axis suggests microbiome-based interventions may modulate opioid tolerance and withdrawal severity. Neuroimaging studies have identified biomarkers for overdose risk assessment, potentially enabling personalized treatment approaches. These neuropsychopharmacological advances offer evidence-based strategies that could significantly impact overdose prevention when integrated with existing public health measures. Implementation of these findings into clinical practice requires coordinated efforts between researchers, clinicians, and policymakers to translate laboratory discoveries into life-saving interventions for individuals with opioid use disorders.

title: High temporal resolution for in vivo monitoring of neurotransmitters in awake epileptic rats using brain microdialysis and capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection.
abstract: A method for high temporal resolution monitoring of five neurotransmitters, dopamine (DA), noradrenaline (NA), gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), glutamate (Glu), l-aspartate (L-Asp), in freely-moving rats using microdialysis and capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection (CE-LIFD) was developed. An on-line device, including microdialysis and derivatization with naphthalene-2,3-dicarboxaldehyde, mixes the dialysate with derivatization reagents directly in the collection tube, i.e. with no reactor. Thereafter, collected derivatized samples are analyzed off-line with an automated CE system coupled to a LIFD using a 442 nm excitation. The sampling time was limited by the minimal volume required for the analysis by the automated CE system used: neurotransmitters could be determined in 667 nl dialysates (940 nl after derivatization), i.e. in samples collected every 20 s with a flow rate of 2 microl/min. The detection limits at the dialysis probe were 3 x 10(-9), 1 x 10(-9), 1.9 x 10(-8), 4.2 x 10(-7), 2.1 x 10(-7) mol/l for DA, NA, GABA, Glu and L-Asp, respectively. The protocol was validated using in vitro/in vivo tests and the performances--repeatability, linearity, characteristics of the probes--were determined. Finally, the high temporal resolution allowed the simultaneous monitoring of these neurotransmitters in rats with genetic absence epilepsy and revealed, for the first time, increases in GABA concentrations concomitantly with the seizures, detected when our new microdialysis method was combined to electroencephalographic recordings.

title: Antidepressant-related sexual dysfunction - perspectives from neuroimaging.
abstract: Antidepressant-induced sexual dysfunction (AISD) affects 30-70% of patients treated with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study employed functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to investigate brain activation patterns during visual sexual stimuli in patients experiencing AISD compared to healthy controls and depressed patients with preserved sexual function. Twenty-four medication-free depressed patients, 22 SSRI-treated patients with sexual dysfunction, 18 SSRI-treated patients without sexual dysfunction, and 26 healthy controls underwent fMRI scanning while viewing erotic visual stimuli. Sexual function was assessed using the Changes in Sexual Functioning Questionnaire and Arizona Sexual Experience Scale. Blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signal changes were analyzed in regions of interest including the anterior cingulate cortex, insula, hypothalamus, and reward-related structures. SSRI-treated patients with sexual dysfunction showed significantly reduced activation in the ventral striatum, anterior cingulate cortex, and hypothalamic regions compared to all other groups (p<0.05, corrected). Additionally, these patients demonstrated increased activation in the lateral prefrontal cortex, suggesting compensatory cognitive control mechanisms. Correlation analyses revealed significant negative associations between SSRI plasma concentrations and BOLD signal in reward-processing regions (r=-0.58, p<0.01). These findings provide the first neuroimaging evidence that AISD involves disrupted reward processing and altered hypothalamic function during sexual arousal. The results suggest that serotonergic modulation of dopaminergic reward circuits may underlie SSRI-induced sexual side effects, offering potential targets for therapeutic intervention and informing treatment strategies for this prevalent clinical problem.

title: Can voltammetry measure nitrogen monoxide (NO) and/or nitrites?
abstract: Nitrogen monoxide (NO) plays crucial roles as a neurotransmitter and signaling molecule in the central nervous system, yet direct electrochemical detection remains challenging due to its short half-life and low concentrations. This study evaluated the feasibility of using fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) to detect NO and its stable metabolite nitrite (NO2-) in brain tissue preparations. Carbon fiber microelectrodes (7 μm diameter) were fabricated and characterized using standard techniques. Voltammetric measurements were performed in artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF) containing known concentrations of NO (10 nM to 10 μM) generated by NONOate donors and sodium nitrite (1 μM to 1 mM). Rat hippocampal slices were used to assess detectability in biological preparations following stimulation with N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA, 100 μM). Direct NO detection proved difficult with FSCV due to rapid oxidation and interference from oxygen reduction currents. However, DPV successfully detected nitrite with a detection limit of 2.1 μM and linear response up to 500 μM (R² = 0.94). In hippocampal slices, NMDA stimulation produced detectable increases in nitrite-like signals (127 ± 23% of baseline, n=8, p<0.01) that were blocked by the NOS inhibitor L-NAME (100 μM). Background subtraction algorithms improved signal resolution but could not overcome fundamental limitations for direct NO detection. These findings suggest that while direct voltammetric NO measurement remains problematic, nitrite detection may serve as a viable indirect approach for monitoring NO production in neural tissue preparations.

title: Updating neuropathology and neuropharmacology of monoaminergic systems.
abstract: This article is part of a themed section on Updating Neuropathology and Neuropharmacology of Monoaminergic Systems. To view the other articles in this section visit http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bph.v173.13/issuetoc.

title: Targeted disruption of cocaine-activated nucleus accumbens neurons prevents context-specific sensitization.
abstract: Learned associations between effects of abused drugs and the drug administration environment are important in drug addiction. Histochemical and electrophysiological studies suggest that these associations are encoded in sparsely distributed nucleus accumbens neurons that are selectively activated by drugs and drug-associated cues. Although correlations have been observed between nucleus accumbens neuronal activity and responsivity to drugs and drug cues, no technique exists for selectively manipulating these activated neurons and establishing their causal role in behavioral effects of drugs and drug cues. Here we describe a new approach, which we term the 'Daun02 inactivation method', that selectively inactivates a minority of neurons previously activated by cocaine in an environment repeatedly paired with cocaine to demonstrate a causal role for these activated neurons in context-specific cocaine-induced psychomotor sensitization in rats. This method provides a new tool for studying the causal roles of selectively activated neurons in behavioral effects of drugs and drug cues and in other learned behaviors.

title: Trends in use of psychotropic medications among patients treated with cholinesterase inhibitors in Japan from 2002 to 2010.
abstract: We aimed to examine trends in the use of psychotropic medications among elderly outpatients with dementia in Japan between 2002 and 2010. We used data from the 2002-2010 Survey of Medical Care Activities in Public Health Insurance (SMCA-PHI), a nationally representative cross-sectional survey of claims data for the month of June in every year. We included ambulatory care visits by patients aged 65 years or older who were prescribed cholinesterase inhibitors (n = 15,591), and identified use of any psychotropic medications during the survey month. In 2008–2010, the most prevalently prescribed psychotropic medications to patients with dementia were sedatives-hypnotics (27.3%), antipsychotics (21.3%), antidepressants (11.4%), and mood-stabilizers(2.8%). Between 2002–2004 and 2008–2010, use of second-generation antipsychotics increased from 4.9%to 11.2%, while use of first-generation antipsychotics decreased from 17.4% to 12.1% [corrected].These numbers resulted in a 1.1-fold increase in the adjusted prevalence of the overall use of antipsychotics. Quetiapine and risperidone use showed a 4.8- and 1.8-fold increase, respectively, while haloperidol use showed a 2.3-fold decrease. Despite safety warnings against the use of antipsychotics for patients with dementia in several countries, our study revealed a slight increase in the extensive use of off-label antipsychotics over time in Japan. This finding indicates an urgent need for evaluation of the efficacy of antipsychotics for the approved treatment of severe agitation, aggression, and psychosis associated with dementia. Moreover, psychosocial interventions and antipsychotic withdrawal strategies are needed in order to reduce the overall prevalence of antipsychotic use.

title: Neuroscience networking: linking discovery to drugs.
abstract: The translation of neuroscience discoveries into therapeutic interventions remains a significant challenge in drug development, with high attrition rates and lengthy timelines characterizing the current paradigm. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of neuropharmacological research networks to identify factors that facilitate successful translation from preclinical findings to clinical applications. Using bibliometric analysis and network mapping, we examined 2,847 publications from 1995-2010 spanning basic neuroscience research to Phase III clinical trials across major neuropsychiatric disorders including depression, schizophrenia, and Alzheimer's disease. We quantified collaboration patterns, citation networks, and temporal relationships between discovery and therapeutic development. Our analysis revealed that successful drug development programs exhibited significantly higher cross-disciplinary collaboration indices (p<0.001) and more extensive industry-academia partnerships compared to failed programs. Notably, therapeutic candidates that progressed beyond Phase II trials originated from research networks with 3.2-fold higher connectivity scores and demonstrated earlier engagement of translational researchers in the discovery phase. Time-to-clinic was reduced by an average of 2.4 years in well-connected research networks compared to isolated research groups. Additionally, we identified specific network topology features predictive of translational success, including the presence of "bridge" researchers linking basic and clinical domains. These findings suggest that fostering collaborative networks and early integration of translational perspectives significantly enhance the probability of successful neuropharmaceutical development. Our results provide a framework for optimizing research network structure to accelerate the translation of neuroscience discoveries into therapeutic interventions for neuropsychiatric disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Laboratory of receptor pharmacology.
abstract: G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) represent the largest class of drug targets, yet their complex pharmacological profiles remain incompletely understood. We investigated the functional selectivity and allosteric modulation properties of metabotropic glutamate receptor subtype 5 (mGluR5) using novel pharmacological tools in recombinant cell systems. Chinese hamster ovary cells stably expressing human mGluR5 were treated with the orthosteric agonist DHPG (10-100 μM) and the negative allosteric modulator MPEP (1-10 μM) either alone or in combination. Intracellular calcium mobilization was measured using Fura-2 fluorescence imaging, while cAMP accumulation was quantified by radioimmunoassay. Receptor internalization was assessed through confocal microscopy following fluorescent ligand binding. DHPG produced concentration-dependent increases in both calcium release (EC₅₀ = 15.2 ± 2.1 μM) and cAMP inhibition (EC₅₀ = 23.7 ± 3.4 μM). MPEP demonstrated pathway-selective antagonism, completely blocking calcium signaling while only partially inhibiting cAMP responses. Co-application studies revealed that MPEP binding caused a 4.2-fold rightward shift in DHPG calcium dose-response curves but only 1.8-fold shift for cAMP inhibition. Receptor internalization assays showed that MPEP reduced agonist-induced endocytosis by 65% at 30 minutes post-treatment. These findings demonstrate that mGluR5 exhibits significant functional selectivity between calcium and cAMP signaling pathways, with allosteric modulators capable of biasing receptor responses toward specific downstream effectors. Understanding such pathway-selective pharmacology may enable development of more precise therapeutics targeting neurological and psychiatric disorders involving glutamatergic dysfunction.

title: Principles of motivation revealed by the diverse functions of neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates underlying feeding behavior.
abstract: Circuits that participate in specific subcomponents of feeding (e.g., gustatory perception, peripheral feedback relevant to satiety and energy balance, reward coding, etc.) are found at all levels of the neural axis. Further complexity is conferred by the wide variety of feeding-modulatory neurotransmitters and neuropeptides that act within these circuits. An ongoing challenge has been to refine the understanding of the functional specificity of these neurotransmitters and circuits, and there have been exciting advances in recent years. We focus here on foundational work of Dr. Ann Kelley that identified distinguishable actions of striatal opioid peptide modulation and dopamine transmission in subcomponents of reward processing. We also discuss her work in overlaying these neuropharmacological effects upon anatomical pathways that link the telencephalon (cortex and basal ganglia) with feeding-control circuits in the hypothalamus. Using these seminal contributions as a starting point, we will discuss new findings that expand our understanding of (1) the specific, differentiable motivational processes that are governed by central dopamine and opioid transmission, (2) the manner in which other striatal neuromodulators, specifically acetylcholine, endocannabinoids and adenosine, modulate these motivational processes (including via interactions with opioid systems), and (3) the organization of the cortical-subcortical network that subserves opioid-driven feeding. The findings discussed here strengthen the view that incentive-motivational properties of food are coded by substrates and neural circuits that are distinguishable from those that mediate the acute hedonic experience of food reward. Striatal opioid transmission modulates reward processing by engaging frontotemporal circuits, possibly via a hypothalamic-thalamic axis, that ultimately impinges upon hypothalamic modules dedicated to autonomic function and motor pattern control. We will conclude by discussing implications for understanding disorders of "non-homeostatic" feeding.

title: Serial neurochemical measurement of cerebrospinal fluid during the human sexual response cycle.
abstract: Recent studies examining the neuroendocrine response pattern underlying the human sexual response cycle revealed transient activation of the sympathoadrenal system and a substantial, long-lasting increase in plasma prolactin concentrations following orgasm in men and women. Prolactin has been discussed as being part of a feedback mechanism that signals centers in the central nervous system, such as the dopaminergic system controlling sexual arousal. To further elucidate the central role of neuropeptides, biogenic monoamines and neurotransmitters in human sexual behavior, a serial cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)-sampling technique was implemented using a previously established experimental paradigm for sexual activity in a laboratory setting. In parallel with peripheral endocrine measures, lumbar CSF was drawn via an indwelling spinal catheter during the sexual response cycle in 10 healthy males and 10 age-matched controls, and analysed for prolactin, oxytocin, biogenic monoamines and/or their metabolites as well as inhibitory and excitatory neurotransmitter concentrations. Parallel to raised peripheral sympathetic activity, norepinephrine also increased in CSF during audiovisual, masturbation-induced sexual arousal and orgasm, and remained elevated for the remainder of the session (F(4,72) = 8.79, P = 0.000). In contrast, none of the other measures, in particular prolactin and dopamine or its metabolites, reflected significant alteration. In conclusion, the human sexual response cycle is characterized by an increase in sympathetic activity in plasma and CSF, and by pronounced secretion of plasma prolactin after orgasm. However, alterations in dopaminergic or peptidergic activity are not found in lumbar CSF, possibly due to local and restricted release in diencephalic and mesencephalic brain regions.

title: Affinity labeling the dopamine transporter ligand binding site.
abstract: Photoaffinity labeling is a positive function approach that has been used in an effort to identify the cocaine-binding site on the dopamine transporter (DAT). Radioactive and non-radioactive analogs of cocaine and other dopamine uptake blockers are used to irreversibly label the DAT ligand-binding site and the protein is subjected to chemical or enzymatic treatments that cleave at specific amino acid residues. Analysis of cleavage products from radioactively photolabeled DAT using epitope-specific immunoprecipitation, gel electrophoresis, and autoradiography has identified the site of origin in the primary sequence of labeled fragments as small as 4 kDa. More precise localization of the site of labeling is done by subjecting photolabeled DAT to parallel or serial digestion with multiple cleavage methods, followed by analysis of radiolabeled peptides by reverse-phase HPLC. Fragment retention times are compared to calculated retention times of predicted digest peptides and to chemically or photochemically labeled synthetic peptides. The presence of authentic DAT sequence in HPLC fractions of digests from DAT labeled with non-radioactive ligands is further supported by MALDI and nanoelectrospray mass spectrometry. Using these methods we have identified two distinct regions of DAT that interact with multiple structurally related and diverse irreversible ligands, suggesting that these regions may be involved in the formation of ligand binding sites.

title: An improved method for analyzing coenzyme Q homologues and multiple detection of rare biological samples.
abstract: We have developed a simple method for the estimation of coenzyme Q homologues, neurotransmitters, metal ions, lipid peroxidation, gene expression, and DNA fragmentation simultaneously from genetically engineered mice brain regions and cultured neurons. The primary objective of this study was to improve conventional time-consuming, cumbersome, and less efficient procedures, and reduce the cost of conducting kinetic studies in rare biological samples. The improved method is novel, precise, efficient, accurate, sensitive, economical, versatile, and highly reproducible. The recovery and shelf life of coenzyme Q homologues was significantly increased and the chromatograms exhibited reduced background and retention times. It is envisaged that in addition to coenzyme Q homologues, the improved method could be utilized for the multiple analyses of DNA, RNA and proteins from clinically significant biopsy and autopsy samples.

title: The psychopharmacology of violence: making sensible decisions.
abstract: Violent behavior associated with mental disorders is a common reason for admission to a psychiatric inpatient unit. Once hospitalized, patients may continue to be intermittently agitated and have persistent aggressive behaviors, preventing their discharge back into the community. Managing agitation quickly with effective pharmacological agents can avoid further escalation to aggression and violence. In the acute setting, this usually involves the parenteral use of antipsychotics, with or without benzodiazepines. Within the past decade, short-acting intramuscular formulations of second-generation antipsychotics have become available and provide a means to induce calm with a substantially lower risk of acute dystonia or akathisia compared with haloperidol. New alternative formulations that avoid injections include inhalation and sublingual administration. Longer-term management of persistent aggressive behavior by reducing the frequency and intensity of future episodes of agitation is more complex. In contrast to agitation associated with schizophrenia or bipolar mania, no agents have yet been approved by regulatory agencies for the treatment of persistent aggressive behavior. The strongest evidence supports the use of clozapine as an antihostility agent, followed by olanzapine. Adjunctive strategies with anticonvulsants and beta-adrenergic agents may also be worthwhile to consider.

title: Microelectrode array-based system for neuropharmacological applications with cortical neurons cultured in vitro.
abstract: In vitro neuronal network models have emerged as valuable tools for investigating neuropharmacological mechanisms and drug screening applications. However, conventional electrophysiological techniques often limit simultaneous multi-site recordings from cultured neuronal preparations. We developed and characterized a microelectrode array (MEA)-based system capable of long-term, non-invasive monitoring of spontaneous and evoked electrical activity from primary rat cortical neurons in culture. The system consists of 60-electrode planar arrays with 30 μm diameter titanium nitride electrodes spaced at 200 μm intervals, integrated with custom amplification and data acquisition hardware. Primary cortical neurons from embryonic day 18 rats were cultured directly on MEA substrates and maintained for up to 28 days in vitro. Spontaneous network activity developed after 7-10 days, characterized by synchronized burst firing patterns with amplitudes ranging from 50-500 μV and durations of 100-2000 ms. Network maturation was accompanied by increased burst frequency and improved signal-to-noise ratios. Pharmacological validation was performed using GABAA receptor antagonist bicuculline (10-50 μM), which significantly increased burst frequency and duration, and glutamate receptor antagonist CNQX (10-40 μM), which dose-dependently suppressed network activity. The system demonstrated excellent long-term stability with <5% electrode degradation over 4 weeks. This MEA-based platform provides a robust, high-throughput approach for real-time assessment of compound effects on neuronal network function, offering significant advantages for neuropharmacological research and drug discovery applications requiring simultaneous multi-site electrophysiological monitoring.

title: Neuropharmacology and drug development.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) and allied non-invasive imaging techniques are being increasingly embraced by the pharmaceutical industry. These imaging modalities allow the assessment of novel drug action in man at a very early stage of the drug's discovery and development process; in turn, this enables earlier decision making about the developmental potential of novel and potential therapeutics. The in vivo characterisation of novel molecular targets and disease mechanisms in man is intimately connected with future developments in the diagnosis, management and treatment of human disease. The utility of non-invasive imaging modalities within the pharmaceutical industry is discussed with particular reference to the use of PET in drug discovery and development in the 21st century.

title: Psychedelic Psychiatry's Brave New World.
abstract: After a legally mandated, decades-long global arrest of research on psychedelic drugs, investigation of psychedelics in the context of psychiatric disorders is yielding exciting results. Outcomes of neuroscience and clinical research into 5-Hydroxytryptamine 2A (5-HT2A) receptor agonists, such as psilocybin, show promise for addressing a range of serious disorders, including depression and addiction.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of aqueous extract of Anaphe venata larva (Notondotidae) in rats.
abstract:
**Background:** Anaphe venata (Notondotidae) larvae are traditionally consumed as food and used in folk medicine across West Africa for treating neurological disorders including epilepsy, anxiety, and memory impairment. Despite widespread ethnopharmacological use, the neuropharmacological properties of A. venata have not been scientifically validated. This study investigated the central nervous system effects of aqueous extract of A. venata larvae in experimental rats.

**Methods:** Adult Wistar rats (180-220g) were randomly assigned to control and treatment groups (n=8 per group). Aqueous extract was prepared by standard decoction method and administered orally at doses of 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg body weight. Neuropharmacological evaluation included open field test for locomotor activity, elevated plus maze for anxiety-like behavior, Morris water maze for spatial memory, pentylenetetrazol-induced seizure model, and barbiturate-induced sleeping time. Phytochemical screening was performed using standard methods.

**Results:** Phytochemical analysis revealed presence of alkaloids, flavonoids, saponins, and tannins. A. venata extract significantly reduced locomotor activity (p<0.01) and increased time spent in open arms of elevated plus maze (p<0.05), indicating anxiolytic effects. Memory performance was enhanced in Morris water maze at 200 mg/kg dose (p<0.05). The extract demonstrated anticonvulsant activity by significantly delaying seizure onset (p<0.01) and reducing seizure duration. Barbiturate-induced sleeping time was prolonged in a dose-dependent manner.

**Conclusion:** These findings provide scientific support for traditional use of A. venata larvae in neurological disorders, demonstrating significant anxiolytic, memory-enhancing, anticonvulsant, and sedative properties warranting further investigation for potential therapeutic applications.
journal: African journal of traditional, complementary, and alternative medicines : AJTCAM

title: High frequency, real-time neurochemical and neuropharmacological measurements in situ in the living body.
abstract: Recent advances in neuropharmacology have been limited by the temporal resolution of traditional analytical methods for measuring neurotransmitter dynamics in vivo. Existing microdialysis techniques, while valuable, provide measurements averaged over minutes rather than the millisecond timescales relevant to synaptic transmission and drug action. We developed and validated a novel fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) approach combined with carbon fiber microelectrodes to achieve high-frequency, real-time neurochemical measurements in living brain tissue. Using anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24), we implanted carbon fiber electrodes (7 μm diameter) into the nucleus accumbens and monitored dopamine release with 100 Hz temporal resolution during pharmacological challenges. We administered cocaine (10 mg/kg, i.v.), amphetamine (2 mg/kg, i.v.), and quinpirole (1 mg/kg, i.v.) while simultaneously recording neurochemical changes and corresponding behavioral responses. Our system successfully detected dopamine transients with sub-second resolution, revealing previously unobserved rapid fluctuations in neurotransmitter concentrations following drug administration. Cocaine produced a characteristic 300% increase in extracellular dopamine within 5 seconds, while amphetamine showed a more gradual but sustained elevation over 15 minutes. Quinpirole administration resulted in biphasic dopamine responses not detectable with conventional sampling methods. These findings demonstrate that high-frequency neurochemical monitoring can reveal previously hidden temporal dynamics of neuropharmacological responses, providing critical insights into drug mechanisms of action and potentially improving therapeutic drug monitoring for neuropsychiatric disorders.
journal: Translational research : the journal of laboratory and clinical medicine

title: A novel technique for simultaneous bilateral brain infusions in a mouse model of neurodegenerative disease.
abstract: Simultaneous bilateral brain infusions are critical for studying symmetric neurodegenerative processes, yet current methodologies often require sequential procedures that introduce temporal variability and increased surgical trauma. We developed a novel dual-cannula system enabling synchronized bilateral intracerebroventricular (ICV) infusions in transgenic mouse models of Alzheimer's disease. The system consists of two custom-fabricated 26-gauge stainless steel cannulae connected via a Y-junction to a single Hamilton microsyringe, allowing precise volumetric control. Thirty-six APP/PS1 transgenic mice (8-10 weeks old) underwent stereotactic implantation of bilateral guide cannulae targeting the lateral ventricles (coordinates: ±1.0 mm lateral, -0.3 mm posterior, -2.5 mm ventral to bregma). Following one-week recovery, simultaneous bilateral infusions of fluorescent microspheres (0.5 μL per ventricle) were performed over 2 minutes. Histological analysis revealed successful bilateral ventricular targeting in 94% of animals, with symmetric distribution patterns confirmed by fluorescence microscopy. The coefficient of variation between hemispheres for tracer distribution was significantly reduced compared to sequential infusion controls (8.2% vs 24.7%, p<0.001). Post-surgical mortality was 2.8%, comparable to unilateral procedures. Behavioral assessment showed no significant differences in locomotor activity or anxiety-related behaviors compared to sham-operated controls at 48 hours post-procedure. This dual-cannula system provides a reliable, efficient method for simultaneous bilateral brain infusions, reducing experimental variability and surgical time while maintaining animal welfare standards. The technique has broad applications for bilateral drug delivery studies in neurodegenerative disease models.

title: Pharmacological regulation of gene expression.
abstract: Transcriptional regulation represents a fundamental mechanism through which pharmacological agents exert therapeutic effects in the central nervous system. While acute drug actions on neurotransmitter systems are well-characterized, the molecular pathways governing drug-induced changes in gene expression remain incompletely understood. This study investigated the temporal dynamics and signaling mechanisms underlying pharmacologically-induced transcriptional responses in primary cortical neurons and hippocampal tissue. Primary rat cortical neurons were treated with clinically relevant concentrations of fluoxetine (10 μM), diazepam (1 μM), or vehicle control for 1, 6, and 24 hours. Gene expression profiles were analyzed using quantitative RT-PCR and Western blotting, while chromatin immunoprecipitation assays examined histone modifications at target promoters. Pharmacological inhibitors of CREB, PKA, and MAPK pathways were employed to elucidate signaling mechanisms. Fluoxetine treatment significantly upregulated BDNF, c-fos, and CREB-1 mRNA expression in a time-dependent manner, with peak induction observed at 6 hours (2.8-fold, 4.2-fold, and 1.9-fold increases, respectively; p<0.01). Diazepam enhanced GABAA receptor α1 subunit expression while suppressing immediate early gene transcription. Chromatin analysis revealed increased H3K4me3 and H3K27ac marks at BDNF promoter regions following fluoxetine exposure. PKA inhibition with H-89 abolished fluoxetine-induced transcriptional changes, implicating cAMP-dependent mechanisms. These findings demonstrate that psychoactive drugs rapidly engage distinct transcriptional programs through specific epigenetic modifications and kinase cascades, providing molecular insight into delayed therapeutic responses and suggesting novel targets for neuropsychiatric drug development.

title: Glia and immune cell signaling in bipolar disorder: insights from neuropharmacology and molecular imaging to clinical application.
abstract: Bipolar disorder (BD) is a debilitating mental illness characterized by severe fluctuations in mood, sleep, energy and executive functioning. Pharmacological studies of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and the monoamine system have helped us to clinically understand bipolar depression. Mood stabilizers such as lithium and valproic acid, the first-line treatments for bipolar mania and depression, inhibit glycogen synthase kinase-3 beta (GSK-3β) and regulate the Wnt pathway. Recent investigations suggest that microglia, the resident immune cells of the brain, provide a physiological link between the serotonin system and the GSK-3β/Wnt pathway through neuroinflammation. We review the pharmacological, translational and brain imaging studies that support a role for microglia in regulating neurotransmitter synthesis and immune cell activation. These investigations provide a model for microglia involvement in the pathophysiology and phenotype of BD that may translate into improved therapies.

title: 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline antagonizes a rise in brain dopamine metabolism, glutamate release in frontal cortex and locomotor hyperactivity produced by MK-801 but not the disruptions of prepulse inhibition, and impairment of working memory in rat.
abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with NMDA antagonists like MK-801 producing schizophrenia-like behavioral and neurochemical alterations in rodents. 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (1MeTIQ), an endogenous amine with neuroprotective properties, has shown potential therapeutic effects in various neurological disorders. This study investigated whether 1MeTIQ could attenuate MK-801-induced behavioral and neurochemical changes in rats. Male Wistar rats received MK-801 (0.3 mg/kg, i.p.) with or without 1MeTIQ (25 mg/kg, i.p.) pretreatment. Locomotor activity was assessed in automated chambers, prepulse inhibition (PPI) of acoustic startle was measured, and working memory was evaluated using the T-maze alternation task. Brain dopamine metabolism was determined by HPLC analysis of striatal DOPAC and HVA levels, while glutamate release in frontal cortex was measured using in vivo microdialysis. MK-801 administration significantly increased locomotor activity, dopamine turnover, and cortical glutamate efflux, while disrupting PPI and impairing working memory performance. 1MeTIQ pretreatment effectively prevented MK-801-induced locomotor hyperactivity, normalized dopamine metabolism, and blocked excessive glutamate release in frontal cortex. However, 1MeTIQ failed to restore MK-801-disrupted PPI or improve working memory deficits. These findings suggest that 1MeTIQ selectively modulates certain aspects of NMDA receptor hypofunction, particularly those involving dopaminergic and glutamatergic neurotransmission, while cognitive and sensorimotor gating impairments remain unaffected. The differential protective profile of 1MeTIQ indicates distinct neurobiological mechanisms underlying various NMDA antagonist-induced behavioral phenotypes.

title: From the search for a molecular code of memory to the role of neurotransmitters: a historical perspective.
abstract: The history of the neurochemistry of mnesic processes can be divided into two main periods: the first (1946-1978) was inspired by the results of molecular genetics, providing evidence for storage of hereditary information in the DNA of genes. Therefore, the chemical bases for memory were investigated in the macromolecules of the brain. Such attempts were relatively unsuccessful, which led to a second period (starting in 1978) with the research emphasizing, in a less ambitious way, the role of the molecular correlates of mnesic processes, in particular in the main transmitter systems of the brain.

title: Commentary: physician-scientist's frustrations fester.
abstract: A growing problem of major proportions had been confronting biomedical scientists for many decades. Until solved, this long-neglected problem, the abject failure of the American health care system, presents a gigantic obstacle to the application of the discoveries flowing from neuropsychopharmacological research into deliverable medications utilized by medical practitioners. Although it is recognized that such advances could benefit all of society, both in the United States and globally, progress toward this important goal has not happened. As I noted 5 years ago, 'Unless steps are taken soon to undertake a comprehensive restoration of our system, the profound advances in bio-medical research so rapidly accruing today may never be effectively transformed into meaningful advances in health care for society.' I remain perplexed and frustrated by the reluctance of scientific research societies such as our ACNP to engage their energies and intellect into this most serious issue.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Recent advances in the neurobiology and neuropharmacology of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is an age-related neurodegenerative disorder characterized by progressive deterioration of cognitive functions. The pathological hallmarks are extracellular deposits of amyloid plaques and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles of tau protein. The cognitive deficits seen are thought to be due to synaptic dysfunction and neurochemical deficiencies. Various neurochemical abnormalities have been observed during progressive ageing, and are linked to cognitive abnormalities as seen with the sporadic form of AD. Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors are one of the major therapeutic strategies used for the treatment of AD. During the last decade, various new therapeutic strategies have shown beneficial effects in preclinical studies and under clinical development for the treatment of AD. The present review is aimed at discussing the neurobiology of AD and association of neurochemical abnormalities associated with cognitive deterioration and new therapeutic strategies for the treatment of AD.

title: The role of 5-HT₁A receptors in fish oil-mediated increased BDNF expression in the rat hippocampus and cortex: a possible antidepressant mechanism.
abstract: Epidemiological and dietary studies show that nutritional deficit of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (ω-3 PUFA) is directly related to the prevalence and severity of depression. Supplementation with docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) during critical periods of development (pregnancy and lactation) is essential for cortical maturation, synaptogenesis and myelination, and may also mitigate the risk for cognitive deficits and psychopathologies in young adults. The present study was performed to evaluate the involvement of serotonin (5-HT) receptors, particularly of 5-HT(1A), and hippocampal brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) expression in the antidepressant effect of ω-3 PUFA supplementation. In Experiment 1, the antidepressant effects of fish oil were assessed by the modified forced swim test in adult rats. The data indicated a robust antidepressant effect produced by this supplementation and that treatment of the rats with WAY 100135 reversed this effect. In Experiment 2, cortical and hippocampal contents of BDNF, 5-HT, dopamine (DA) and its metabolites, 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), and 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC), were determined in animals subjected to the same protocol. Increased BDNF expression in the cortex and hippocampus of both age groups was detected. In 90 day-old rats, 5-HT content in the hippocampus was increased, whereas 5-HIAA formation was diminished in the fish oil group. We suggest the occurrence of a reciprocal involvement of 5-HT(1A) receptors activation and the hippocampal BDNF-increased expression mediated by fish oil supplementation. These data corroborate and expand the notion that supplementation with ω-3 PUFA produces antidepressant effects mediated by an increase in serotonergic neurotransmission, particularly in the hippocampus. This article is part of a Special Issue entitled 'Anxiety and Depression'.

title: Automated discrimination of psychotropic drugs in mice via computer vision-based analysis.
abstract: Behavioral phenotyping of psychotropic drugs in rodent models remains a critical component of preclinical screening, yet traditional manual scoring methods are time-intensive and subject to observer bias. We developed and validated an automated computer vision system for real-time discrimination of psychotropic drug effects in mice based on locomotor patterns and stereotypic behaviors. Adult male C57BL/6J mice (n=120) received intraperitoneal injections of saline, amphetamine (2.5 mg/kg), diazepam (2 mg/kg), haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg), or fluoxetine (20 mg/kg) and were monitored for 60 minutes in standard open-field chambers equipped with overhead digital cameras. Custom software algorithms extracted 47 behavioral parameters including velocity profiles, turning angles, rearing frequency, and grooming episodes. Machine learning classifiers were trained on 80% of the dataset and tested on the remaining 20%. The automated system achieved 89.3% accuracy in discriminating between drug classes, with particularly robust identification of stimulant (94.1% sensitivity) and anxiolytic (91.7% sensitivity) effects. Antipsychotic and antidepressant discrimination showed moderate accuracy (82.4% and 79.6%, respectively). Principal component analysis revealed that velocity variance, spatial distribution patterns, and repetitive movement sequences were the most discriminative features.
