Manual validation by trained observers showed 92.1% concordance with automated classifications. This computer vision approach provides an objective, high-throughput method for psychotropic drug screening that reduces labor costs and eliminates rater bias while maintaining comparable accuracy to traditional behavioral assessment methods. The system offers significant advantages for large-scale pharmacological studies and drug discovery applications.

title: Electrophysiological and pharmacological validation of vagal afferent fiber type of neurons enzymatically isolated from rat nodose ganglia.
abstract: The nodose ganglion contains heterogeneous populations of vagal afferent neurons that mediate diverse physiological functions, yet reliable methods for identifying specific fiber types in isolated preparations remain limited. This study aimed to develop and validate electrophysiological and pharmacological criteria for classifying vagal afferent fiber types in enzymatically dissociated rat nodose ganglion neurons. Nodose ganglia were harvested from adult Sprague-Dawley rats and neurons isolated using sequential collagenase and trypsin digestion. Whole-cell patch-clamp recordings were performed to characterize action potential properties, including duration, amplitude, and afterhyperpolarization kinetics. Neurons were classified as A- or C-type based on action potential duration (<2 ms or >2 ms, respectively) and tetrodotoxin sensitivity. Pharmacological validation employed capsaicin (1 μM), ATP (100 μM), and bradykinin (1 μM) to assess nociceptive C-fiber properties, while baroreceptor A-fibers were identified by their sensitivity to phenylephrine (10 μM) and insensitivity to capsaicin. Of 347 recorded neurons, 62% exhibited C-fiber characteristics with broad action potentials (mean duration 4.8 ± 0.3 ms) and capsaicin sensitivity, while 38% showed A-fiber properties with narrow spikes (1.4 ± 0.1 ms) and capsaicin insensitivity. Pharmacological responses correlated strongly with electrophysiological classification (r = 0.89, p < 0.001). These findings establish reliable electrophysiological and pharmacological markers for identifying vagal afferent fiber types in vitro, providing a valuable tool for investigating the cellular mechanisms underlying visceral sensory transduction and enabling targeted studies of specific vagal afferent populations.

title: Corticotropin-Releasing Factor (CRF) Neurocircuitry and Neuropharmacology in Alcohol Drinking.
abstract: Alcohol use is pervasive in the United States. In the transition from nonhazardous drinking to hazardous drinking and alcohol use disorder, neuroadaptations occur within brain reward and brain stress systems. One brain signaling system that has received much attention in animal models of excessive alcohol drinking and alcohol dependence is corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF). The CRF system is composed of CRF, the urocortins, CRF-binding protein, and two receptors - CRF type 1 and CRF type 2. This review summarizes how acute, binge, and chronic alcohol dysregulates CRF signaling in hypothalamic and extra-hypothalamic brain regions and how this dysregulation may contribute to changes in alcohol reinforcement, excessive alcohol consumption, symptoms of negative affect during withdrawal, and alcohol relapse. In addition, it summarizes clinical work examining CRF type 1 receptor antagonists in humans and discusses why the brain CRF system is still relevant in alcohol research.

title: Comparative analysis of inhibitory effects of caged ligands for the NMDA receptor.
abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors play critical roles in synaptic plasticity, learning, and memory, making them important targets for neuropharmacological intervention. Caged compounds that can be rapidly photoreleased provide temporal precision for studying NMDA receptor function, yet systematic comparisons of their inhibitory properties remain limited. We evaluated the comparative efficacy of four commercially available caged NMDA receptor antagonists: caged MK-801, caged APV, caged CNQX, and caged kynurenic acid. Using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from cultured rat hippocampal neurons (DIV 14-21), we measured NMDA-evoked currents before and after UV photolysis (365 nm, 1-5 ms pulses) of each caged compound (10-100 μM). Concentration-response relationships were established for each antagonist, and kinetics of inhibition onset were analyzed. Caged MK-801 demonstrated the highest potency (IC₅₀ = 12.3 ± 2.1 μM) and fastest inhibition kinetics (τ = 185 ± 23 ms), while caged APV showed intermediate potency (IC₅₀ = 34.7 ± 4.8 μM) with slower onset (τ = 420 ± 67 ms). Caged CNQX and caged kynurenic acid exhibited lower potencies and considerable variability in photolysis efficiency across preparations. All compounds showed minimal effects on AMPA receptor-mediated currents at concentrations producing 80% NMDA receptor inhibition. These findings establish caged MK-801 as the most effective tool for rapid, selective NMDA receptor inhibition in acute experimental paradigms, while highlighting important pharmacokinetic differences among available caged antagonists that should guide experimental design decisions.

title: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology, yesterday, today and tomorrow. A conversation with Michel Hamon.
abstract: For this issue, Luc Zimmer, professor of pharmacology and chair of the Neuropsychopharmacology Committee of the French Society of Pharmacology and Therapeutics, talked with Michel Hamon, honorary director of research at the French National Institute of Health and Medical Research (INSERM) and honorary professor of neuropharmacology at Paris-Sorbonne (Pierre et Marie Curie) University. Some of the leading names in neuropsychopharmacology research are mentioned, pointing to significant conceptual advances that founded this discipline. The links between psychopharmacology and neuropharmacology are also discussed in the light of past collaborations. Finally, priorities are proposed for the emergence of the psychopharmacology of the future.

title: The fly as a model for neurodegenerative diseases: is it worth the jump?
abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases represent a growing challenge in an aging population, yet progress in developing effective therapeutics remains limited due to the complexity and cost of mammalian model systems. Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a promising alternative model organism for studying neurodegeneration, offering genetic tractability, short generation times, and conserved fundamental cellular mechanisms. We conducted a comprehensive analysis comparing the utility of Drosophila models versus traditional rodent models across five major neurodegenerative conditions: Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and spinocerebellar ataxia. Our systematic review examined 127 studies published between 2000-2005, evaluating translational success rates, screening throughput, and mechanistic insights. Drosophila models successfully recapitulated key pathological features in 78% of cases, including protein aggregation, neuronal loss, and behavioral deficits. Drug screening studies in flies demonstrated 3.2-fold higher compound throughput compared to mouse models, with 64% concordance in therapeutic efficacy when compounds advanced to mammalian testing. Notably, flies proved particularly valuable for dissecting cellular pathways involved in protein misfolding and mitochondrial dysfunction. Cost analysis revealed 15-fold reduction in experimental expenses compared to equivalent mouse studies. However, limitations included inability to model complex cognitive functions and species-specific differences in blood-brain barrier properties. These findings demonstrate that Drosophila represents a valuable complement to mammalian models in neurodegenerative disease research, particularly for high-throughput genetic and pharmacological screening, while emphasizing the continued necessity of validation in mammalian systems for therapeutic development.

title: Epigenetic Neuropharmacology: Drugs Affecting the Epigenome in the Brain.
abstract: The emerging field of epigenetic neuropharmacology represents a paradigm shift in understanding how pharmacological interventions can modulate gene expression in the central nervous system through chromatin modifications. Epigenetic mechanisms, including DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNA regulation, play crucial roles in synaptic plasticity, memory formation, and neuropsychiatric disorders. This review synthesizes recent advances in drugs that target epigenetic machinery in neural tissues, examining their therapeutic potential and mechanistic actions. We analyzed preclinical and clinical studies from 2015-2020 involving epigenetic modulators including histone deacetylase inhibitors (HDACi), DNA methyltransferase inhibitors, histone methyltransferase inhibitors, and bromodomain inhibitors. Particular attention was given to their applications in treating neurodegenerative diseases, addiction, depression, and cognitive disorders. HDAC inhibitors such as vorinostat and sodium butyrate demonstrated significant neuroprotective effects in Alzheimer's disease models by enhancing memory-related gene expression. DNA methylation modulators like 5-azacytidine showed promise in reversing addiction-related epigenetic modifications in reward circuitry. Bromodomain inhibitors exhibited antidepressant-like effects through chromatin remodeling in stress-responsive brain regions. However, challenges remain regarding blood-brain barrier penetration, off-target effects, and optimal dosing strategies. The specificity of epigenetic modifications across different brain regions and cell types presents both opportunities for targeted therapies and complications for systemic drug delivery. Future developments in brain-penetrant, region-specific epigenetic drugs hold considerable promise for treating previously intractable neurological and psychiatric conditions through precise modulation of neural gene expression programs.
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology

title: Computational models of neuronal biophysics and the characterization of potential neuropharmacological targets.
abstract: The development of effective neurotherapeutics remains challenging due to the complex biophysical properties of neuronal networks and the difficulty in predicting drug-target interactions in vivo. Traditional experimental approaches, while essential, are limited in their ability to systematically explore the vast parameter space of potential neuropharmacological interventions. This study presents a comprehensive computational framework integrating Hodgkin-Huxley type models with Monte Carlo simulations to characterize potential therapeutic targets in neuronal excitability disorders. We developed multi-compartmental models of cortical pyramidal neurons incorporating detailed kinetics of voltage-gated sodium, potassium, and calcium channels, along with ligand-gated receptors. Drug-target interactions were modeled using binding kinetics derived from patch-clamp electrophysiology data for fifteen clinically relevant compounds, including anticonvulsants, antipsychotics, and neuroprotective agents. Our simulations identified three critical parameter domains where pharmacological modulation significantly altered neuronal firing patterns: (1) persistent sodium current density (threshold for burst firing), (2) A-type potassium channel inactivation kinetics (spike frequency adaptation), and (3) L-type calcium channel voltage-dependence (dendritic integration). Notably, combined modulation of sodium channel fast inactivation and potassium channel activation produced synergistic effects in suppressing hyperexcitability while preserving physiological firing patterns. These findings were validated against experimental data from hippocampal slice preparations. Our computational approach provides a systematic framework for identifying novel neuropharmacological targets and predicting optimal drug combinations, potentially accelerating the development of more effective neurotherapeutics with improved therapeutic indices.

title: The neuropharmacology of L-theanine(N-ethyl-L-glutamine): a possible neuroprotective and cognitive enhancing agent.
abstract: L-theanine (N-ethyl-L-glutamine) or theanine is a major amino acid uniquely found in green tea. L-theanine has been historically reported as a relaxing agent, prompting scientific research on its pharmacology. Animal neurochemistry studies suggest that L-theanine increases brain serotonin, dopamine, GABA levels and has micromolar affinities for AMPA, Kainate and NMDA receptors. In addition has been shown to exert neuroprotective effects in animal models possibly through its antagonistic effects on group 1 metabotrophic glutamate receptors. Behavioural studies in animals suggest improvement in learning and memory. Overall, L-theanine displays a neuropharmacology suggestive of a possible neuroprotective and cognitive enhancing agent and warrants further investigation in animals and humans.

title: Acute treatment of migraine headaches.
abstract: Optimum acute treatment of migraine requires prevention of headache as a top priority. Recognition of the multitude of migraine presentations, the frequency of total headache attacks, and number of days of headache disability are critical. Successful treatment requires excellent patient-clinician communication enhancing confidence and mutual trust based on patient needs and preferences. Optimum management of acute migraine nearly always requires pharmacologic treatment for rapid resolution. Migraine-specific triptans, dihydroergotamine, and several antiinflammatories have substantial empirical clinical efficacy. Older nonspecific drugs, particularly butalbital and opioids, contribute to medication overuse headache and are to be avoided. Clinicians should utilize evidence-based acute migraine-specific therapy stressing the imperative acute treatment goal of early intervention, but not too often with the correct drug, formulation, and dose. This therapy needs to provide cost-effective fast results, meaningful to the patient while minimizing the need for additional drugs. Migraine-ACT evaluates 2-hour pain freedom with return to normal function, comfort with treatment, and consistency of response. Employ a thoroughly educated patient, formulary, testimonials, stratification, and rational cotherapy against the race to central sensitization for optimum outcomes.

title: Optofluidic control of rodent learning using cloaked caged glutamate.
abstract: Precise spatiotemporal control of neurotransmitter release remains a critical challenge for understanding neural circuit function and behavior. Here, we present an optofluidic approach using "cloaked" caged glutamate compounds that enable selective neurotransmitter delivery with minimal off-target effects during learning paradigms. We synthesized novel ruthenium-based caged glutamate derivatives with red-shifted activation spectra and reduced tissue scattering properties, then developed a miniaturized fiber-optic microfluidic device for concurrent compound delivery and optical uncaging in freely moving rodents. Adult C57BL/6 mice were implanted with optofluidic probes targeting the dorsal hippocampus CA1 region and trained in a Morris water maze task. Controlled glutamate release was triggered during specific learning phases using 660 nm light pulses. Electrophysiological recordings confirmed spatially restricted glutamate uncaging within a 200 μm radius, with peak concentrations reaching 50-100 μM lasting 2-3 seconds. Behavioral analysis revealed that optically triggered glutamate release during memory encoding phases significantly enhanced spatial learning performance compared to control groups receiving either vehicle or caged compound without light activation (p < 0.001, n = 12 per group). Post-training immunohistochemistry showed increased c-Fos expression and enhanced long-term potentiation in stimulated regions. Importantly, the cloaked compounds exhibited 95% reduced background activation compared to conventional caged glutamate, minimizing artifacts. This optofluidic platform provides unprecedented precision for dissecting glutamatergic signaling contributions to learning and memory, with broad applications for investigating causal relationships between neurotransmitter dynamics and behavior.
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

title: [Psychopharmacological Studies in Mice].
abstract: Since 1998, when the laboratory of Medicinal Pharmacology was established in the Graduate School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Osaka University, I have been interested in psychopharmacological research topics. During this period, we identified a number of novel regulatory mechanisms that control the prefrontal dopamine system through functional interaction between serotonin1A and dopamine D2 receptors or between serotonin1A and σ1 receptors. Our findings suggest that strategies that enhance the prefrontal dopamine system may have therapeutic potential in the treatment of psychiatric disorders. We also found that environmental factors during development strongly impact the psychological state in adulthood. Furthermore, we clarified the pharmacological profiles of the acetylcholinesterase inhibitors donepezil, galantamine, and rivastigmine, providing novel insights into their mechanisms of action. Finally, we developed the female encounter test, a novel method for evaluating motivation in mice. This simple method should help advance future psychopharmacological research. In this review, we summarize the major findings obtained from our recent studies in mice.
journal: Yakugaku zasshi : Journal of the Pharmaceutical Society of Japan

title: Applications of fMRI in translational medicine and clinical practice.
abstract: Functional MRI (fMRI) has had a major impact in cognitive neuroscience. fMRI now has a small but growing role in clinical neuroimaging, with initial applications to neurosurgical planning. Current clinical research has emphasized novel concepts for clinicians, such as the role of plasticity in recovery and the maintenance of brain functions in a broad range of diseases. There is a wider potential for clinical fMRI in applications ranging from presymptomatic diagnosis, through drug development and individualization of therapies, to understanding functional brain disorders. Realization of this potential will require changes in the way clinical neuroimaging services are planned and delivered.

title: The roles of dopamine and serotonin in decision making: evidence from pharmacological experiments in humans.
abstract: Decision-making processes involve complex interactions between neurotransmitter systems, particularly dopamine and serotonin pathways. While animal studies have established fundamental roles for these systems in reward processing and behavioral control, their specific contributions to human decision-making remain incompletely understood. We conducted a double-blind, placebo-controlled study examining the effects of selective pharmacological manipulation of dopamine and serotonin systems on decision-making performance in healthy volunteers. Forty-eight participants completed three experimental sessions, receiving either placebo, the dopamine precursor L-DOPA (100mg), or the serotonin reuptake inhibitor citalopram (20mg) in randomized order. Decision-making was assessed using the Iowa Gambling Task (IGT) and a probabilistic reversal learning paradigm. Computational modeling was applied to extract learning rate parameters and decision consistency measures. L-DOPA administration significantly enhanced performance on the IGT, increasing advantageous deck selections by 23% compared to placebo (p<0.01) and improving learning rates for positive outcomes (p<0.05). Conversely, citalopram impaired reversal learning performance, reducing adaptability to contingency changes (p<0.01) while increasing perseverative responding. Computational analysis revealed that dopaminergic enhancement primarily affected reward prediction error signaling, whereas serotonergic modulation influenced behavioral flexibility and response inhibition. These findings provide direct evidence for dissociable roles of dopamine and serotonin in human decision-making: dopamine facilitates reward-based learning and approach behavior, while serotonin modulates behavioral control and adaptation to changing environmental contingencies. Understanding these neurotransmitter-specific contributions has important implications for treating decision-making impairments in psychiatric and neurological disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Sidney George Shaw, DPhil (1948-2017).
abstract: On March 4, 2017 at the age of 68, Sidney George Shaw (Sid) unexpectedly died from complications following surgery, only four years after retiring from the University of Bern. Trained in biochemistry at Oxford University, Sid had quickly moved into molecular pharmacology and became a key investigator in the field of enzyme biochemistry, vasoactive peptide research, and receptor signaling. Sid spent half his life in Switzerland, after moving to the University of Bern in 1984. This article, written by his friends and colleagues who knew him and worked with him during different stages of his career, summarizes his life, his passions, and his achievements in biomedical research. It also includes personal memories relating to a dear friend and outstanding scientist whose intellectual curiosity, humility, and honesty will remain an example to us all.

title: Strains of rodents and the pharmacology of learning and memory.
abstract: Mendelian genetic tools have extensively been used to improve the description of the pharmacological mechanisms involved in learning and memory. The first part of this short review describes experiments involving the bidirectional selection of rats or mice for extreme behavioral characteristics or for sensitivity to pharmacological treatments. The second part focuses specifically on in-breeding. In conclusion, the advantages and the limits of a Mendelian pharmacogenetic approach of learning and memory are discussed.

title: Anxiolytics antagonize yohimbine-induced central noradrenergic activity: a concomitant in vivo voltammetry-electrophysiology model of anxiety.
abstract: It has been shown that increased cerebral noradrenergic activity is closely related to anxiety-related behaviours. Furthermore, the noradrenergic alpha-2 receptor antagonist yohimbine (YOH) increases noradrenaline- (NA) cell firing and release in discrete brain areas involved in the modulation of anxiety such as the locus coeruleus (LC) and amygdala (AMY). In addition, YOH is highly anxiogenic in both humans and laboratory animals. Here we used an in vivo dual methodology to measure concomitantly the extracellular levels of catecholamines in AMY with voltammetry and the cell firing rate in LC with field electrophysiology in anaesthetized rats to characterize the pharmacological effects of YOH. We found that systemic administration of YOH increases both noradrenergic cell firing in the LC and catecholamines release in the AMY. Prior administration of the alpha-2 agonist clonidine prevents these effects on both the electrophysiological and the voltammetric signals, suggesting that YOH effects are mainly mediated by the alpha-2 adrenoceptors. In addition, we found that pre-treatment with the behaviourally active doses of the benzodiazepine (BDZ) anxiolytic chlordiazepoxide (CDE, Librium: BDZ very similar to diazepam) and of the non-BDZ buspirone counteract the effects of YOH on catecholamines release and cell firing. This suggests that the neurochemical and physiological effects of YOH measured in this study might be related to the anxiogenic effects of the drug. Thus, the presented electrophysiological-voltammetric-YOH study may be proposed as in vivo real time model of anxiety and this approach might be suitable for the evaluation of the neurochemical and physiological effects of new anxiolytic compounds.

title: The neurochemistry of agitation in Alzheimer's disease: a systematic review.
abstract: To provide an up-to-date systematic review of the characteristics, methodology and findings of studies that have investigated the neurochemistry of agitation in Alzheimer's disease (AD). Electronic databases were searched for published peer-reviewed articles which provided data on any neurotransmitter system in relation to agitation in AD. Screening of titles and abstracts and data extraction from full texts were conducted in duplicate. Forty-five studies were included. Monoamines (serotonin, dopamine and noradrenaline) were most commonly investigated. A variety of methods were used to investigate the neurochemistry underlying agitation in AD and, although there were several conflicting findings, there was evidence of serotonergic deficit, relatively preserved dopaminergic function and compensatory overactivity of postsynaptic noradrenergic neurons in agitation in AD. Disruption of the dynamic balance between multiple neurotransmitter systems could impair functional neural networks involved in affective regulation and executive function. Differences in study design and methodology may have contributed to conflicting findings. Future studies that overcome these limitations (e.g. using standardized criteria to define agitation) and employ neuroimaging methods such as MRI/PET to investigate specific neural networks are needed to clarify the role of neurotransmitter alterations in these patients.

title: Psychopharmacology and memory.
abstract: Psychotropic and other drugs can alter brain mechanisms regulating the formation, storage, and retrieval of different types of memory. These include "off label" uses of existing drugs and new drugs designed specifically to target the neural bases of memory. This paper discusses the use of beta-adrenergic antagonists to prevent or erase non-conscious pathological emotional memories in the amygdala. It also discusses the use of novel psychopharmacological agents to enhance long term semantic and short term working memory by altering storage and retrieval mechanisms in the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex. Although intervention in the brain to alter memory as therapy or enhancement holds considerable promise, the long term effects of experimental drugs on the brain and memory are not known. More studies are needed to adequately assess the potential benefits and risks of these interventions.

title: Endocannabinoids and the neurochemistry of gluttony.
abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical regulator of feeding behavior and energy homeostasis, with CB1 receptors widely distributed throughout hypothalamic nuclei controlling appetite. While previous studies have established the orexigenic effects of cannabinoid receptor activation, the neurochemical mechanisms underlying pathological overeating remain poorly understood. We investigated the role of endocannabinoids in mediating excessive food consumption using a rodent model of binge-like feeding behavior. Male Wistar rats were subjected to intermittent food restriction followed by unlimited access to palatable high-fat diet to induce gluttonous feeding episodes. Brain tissue from hypothalamic paraventricular nucleus, arcuate nucleus, and lateral hypothalamic area was analyzed for endocannabinoid content using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. Expression of CB1 receptors and key orexigenic neuropeptides was assessed using quantitative PCR and immunohistochemistry. Animals exhibiting binge-like feeding showed significantly elevated levels of anandamide (2.3-fold increase, p<0.01) and 2-arachidonoylglycerol (1.8-fold increase, p<0.05) in hypothalamic regions compared to controls. CB1 receptor expression was upregulated by 34% in the arcuate nucleus, coinciding with increased neuropeptide Y and agouti-related peptide mRNA levels. Pretreatment with the CB1 antagonist rimonabant significantly attenuated binge-like feeding episodes by 62%. These findings demonstrate that endocannabinoid signaling is dysregulated during pathological overeating, suggesting that the endocannabinoid system represents a promising therapeutic target for treating disorders characterized by excessive food intake and loss of feeding control.

title: Lactate dehydrogenase release is facilitated by brief sonication of rat hippocampal slices and isolated retinas following acute neuronal damage.
abstract: Assessment of neuronal viability following acute injury requires reliable and quantitative methods for detecting cell death in brain tissue preparations. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release has emerged as a useful marker of cellular membrane integrity, but conventional protocols may underestimate total damage due to incomplete enzyme release from compromised cells. We investigated whether brief sonication enhances LDH release detection in two commonly used ex vivo preparations: rat hippocampal slices and isolated retinas. Adult Sprague-Dawley rats were sacrificed and 400-μm hippocampal slices or whole retinas were prepared using standard techniques. Acute neuronal damage was induced by oxygen-glucose deprivation (OGD) for 30 or 60 minutes, or by exposure to 100 μM glutamate for 2 hours. Following injury protocols, tissues were either processed immediately for LDH assay or subjected to brief sonication (3 × 10-second pulses at 20% amplitude) before enzyme measurement. Sonication significantly increased detectable LDH release in both preparations compared to non-sonicated controls. In hippocampal slices, sonication revealed 2.3-fold higher LDH levels following 60-minute OGD and 1.8-fold higher levels after glutamate exposure. Similarly, sonicated retinas showed 2.7-fold increased LDH release following OGD treatment. Microscopic examination confirmed that sonication parameters did not cause additional tissue damage in uninjured preparations. These findings demonstrate that brief sonication substantially improves the sensitivity of LDH-based viability assays by facilitating complete enzyme release from damaged neurons while preserving tissue architecture, providing a more accurate assessment of neuronal injury in slice preparations.

title: Recent developments in oculomotor neurophysiology.
abstract: Recent advances in oculomotor neurophysiology have significantly enhanced our understanding of the neural mechanisms controlling eye movements. This review examines developments over the past five years in identifying the cellular and molecular basis of oculomotor control, with particular emphasis on brainstem circuitry and neurotransmitter systems. Single-unit recording studies in alert primates have revealed distinct populations of burst neurons, pause neurons, and omnipause neurons within the paramedian pontine reticular formation that precisely coordinate horizontal saccadic eye movements. Immunohistochemical and lesion studies have demonstrated that glycinergic inhibition plays a crucial role in reciprocal innervation of extraocular motor neurons, while GABAergic mechanisms modulate burst neuron activity. Recent work has identified acetylcholine as a key neurotransmitter in the neural integrator circuits responsible for maintaining steady gaze position. Voltage-clamp studies of oculomotor neurons have characterized calcium-dependent potassium channels that contribute to the discharge patterns underlying different types of eye movements. Additionally, tract-tracing experiments using retrograde and anterograde transport techniques have mapped previously unknown connections between the superior colliculus, cerebellum, and brainstem oculomotor areas. These findings provide new insights into the pathophysiology of ocular motility disorders and suggest potential therapeutic targets. The emerging picture reveals a highly organized network of specialized neuronal populations utilizing specific neurotransmitter systems to generate the precise motor commands required for accurate eye movements and stable vision.

title: Automated analysis of antidepressants' effect in the forced swim test.
abstract: The forced swim test (FST) is a widely used behavioral paradigm for screening antidepressant compounds, traditionally relying on manual scoring of immobility time which is labor-intensive and subject to inter-observer variability. We developed an automated video-tracking system to objectively quantify behavioral parameters in the FST and evaluated its sensitivity to detect antidepressant effects. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) were treated with vehicle, imipramine (10, 20 mg/kg), fluoxetine (10, 20 mg/kg), or bupropion (10, 20 mg/kg) 30 minutes prior to a 6-minute FST session. Our custom software analyzed high-resolution video recordings to automatically distinguish between immobility, swimming, and climbing behaviors based on pixel displacement algorithms and movement pattern recognition. The system demonstrated excellent inter-session reliability (r=0.94) and strong correlation with manual scoring (r=0.89). Automated analysis detected significant dose-dependent reductions in immobility time for all tested antidepressants compared to vehicle controls (p<0.01). Imipramine and bupropion preferentially increased climbing behavior, while fluoxetine primarily enhanced swimming activity, consistent with their distinct mechanisms of action. The automated system also revealed subtle behavioral parameters not captured by traditional scoring, including movement velocity profiles and spatial distribution patterns within the cylinder. Processing time was reduced from 2 hours to 5 minutes per session. This automated approach provides a reliable, objective, and time-efficient method for FST analysis that enhances reproducibility and reveals additional behavioral dimensions relevant to antidepressant screening, potentially improving translational validity in preclinical research.

title: Pharmacological imaging as a tool to visualise dopaminergic neurotoxicity.
abstract: Dopaminergic neurotoxicity represents a critical pathological mechanism underlying Parkinson's disease and drug-induced parkinsonism, yet current methods for visualizing neuronal damage in vivo remain limited. This study evaluated the utility of pharmacological imaging approaches to detect and quantify dopaminergic neurotoxicity in real-time. We employed a combination of [¹¹C]raclopride PET imaging and [¹²³I]FP-CIT SPECT to assess dopaminergic function in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) following administration of 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP, 20 mg/kg, i.p.) or 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA, 8 μg, intrastriatal). Imaging was performed at baseline, 24 hours, 7 days, and 28 days post-lesion. Amphetamine challenge (2 mg/kg, i.v.) was used to assess presynaptic dopamine release capacity. Immunohistochemical analysis of tyrosine hydroxylase expression and striatal dopamine content measurement via HPLC served as validation endpoints. MPTP treatment resulted in progressive decline in [¹²³I]FP-CIT binding (65% reduction at 28 days, p<0.001) and blunted amphetamine-induced [¹¹C]raclopride displacement (42% vs 78% in controls, p<0.001). 6-OHDA lesions produced more focal but severe deficits, with near-complete loss of tracer binding in the lesioned hemisphere. Strong correlations were observed between imaging measures and post-mortem dopamine content (r=0.87-0.92). These findings demonstrate that pharmacological imaging provides sensitive, quantitative assessment of dopaminergic neurotoxicity with superior temporal resolution compared to conventional methods. This approach offers significant potential for monitoring disease progression and evaluating neuroprotective interventions in preclinical and clinical settings.

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: III. Actions in models of potential antidepressive and anxiolytic activity in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: Dopamine D3/D2 receptor agonists have shown promise as novel therapeutic agents for mood and anxiety disorders, yet their comparative efficacy remains poorly characterized. S32504, a novel naphtoxazine derivative with high affinity for dopamine D3 and D2 receptors, was evaluated for potential antidepressive and anxiolytic properties in comparison with the established D2/D3 agonist ropinirole. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received S32504 (0.04-2.5 mg/kg, s.c.), ropinirole (0.16-10 mg/kg, s.c.), or vehicle 30 minutes prior to behavioral testing. Antidepressive-like activity was assessed using the forced swim test and chronic mild stress paradigm, while anxiolytic potential was evaluated in the elevated plus maze and Vogel conflict test. S32504 demonstrated dose-dependent antidepressive-like effects, significantly reducing immobility time in the forced swim test (ED50 = 0.63 mg/kg) and reversing anhedonic behavior in chronically stressed animals. These effects were more potent than those observed with ropinirole (ED50 = 3.2 mg/kg). In anxiety models, S32504 (0.16-0.63 mg/kg) increased open arm exploration in the elevated plus maze and enhanced punished drinking in the Vogel test, indicating anxiolytic activity. Ropinirole showed similar but less pronounced anxiolytic effects at higher doses. The antidepressive and anxiolytic actions of S32504 were attenuated by the D2/D3 antagonist raclopride but not by the selective D2 antagonist L-741,626, suggesting preferential involvement of D3 receptors. These findings indicate that S32504 possesses superior antidepressive and anxiolytic potential compared to ropinirole, supporting the therapeutic relevance of selective D3 receptor modulation in mood disorders.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Neuropharmacology of Antiseizure Drugs.
abstract: Antiseizure drugs (ASDs) are the primary therapy for epilepsy, with more than 20 drugs introduced into clinical practice to date. These drugs are typically grouped by their mechanisms of action and therapeutic spectrum. This article aims to educate non-neurologists and medical students about the new frontiers in the pharmacology of ASDs and presents the current state of the literature on the efficacy and tolerability of these agents. Randomized controlled trials, observational studies, and evidence-based meta-analyses of ASD efficacy and tolerability as initial monotherapy for epileptic seizures and syndromes were identified in PubMed, EMBASE, the Cochrane Library, and Elsevier Clinical Pharmacology. The choice of ASD varies primarily according to the seizure type. Practical guidelines for ASD selection in patients with new-onset and drug-resistant epilepsy were recently published. The guidelines have shown that the newer-generation drugs, which have unique mechanistic and pharmacokinetic properties, are better tolerated but have similar efficacy compared with the older drugs. Several ASDs are effective as first-line monotherapy in focal seizures, including lamotrigine, carbamazepine, phenytoin, levetiracetam, and zonisamide. Valproate remains the first-line drug for many patients with generalized and unclassified epilepsies. However, valproate should be avoided, if possible, in women of childbearing potential because of teratogenicity. Toxicity profile precludes several drugs from use as first-line treatment, for example, vigabatrin, felbamate, and rufinamide. Antiseizure drugs have different pharmacologic profiles that should be considered when selecting and prescribing these agents for epilepsy. These include pharmacokinetic properties, propensity for drug-drug interactions, and adverse effects.

title: Pathological laughing and crying : epidemiology, pathophysiology and treatment.
abstract: Pathological laughing and crying (PLC), also known as pseudobulbar affect, represents a debilitating neuropsychiatric syndrome characterized by involuntary, excessive, and contextually inappropriate emotional outbursts. While classically associated with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and multiple sclerosis, PLC occurs across diverse neurological conditions including stroke, traumatic brain injury, and neurodegenerative diseases. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of epidemiological studies published between 1990-2007 and analyzed pathophysiological mechanisms underlying PLC manifestations. Epidemiological data reveal PLC prevalence rates of 10-52% in multiple sclerosis patients, 15-25% in stroke survivors, and up to 50% in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis cohorts, with significant underdiagnosis contributing to reduced quality of life measures. Neuroanatomical evidence implicates disruption of cortico-pontine-cerebellar pathways regulating emotional expression, particularly involving bilateral lesions affecting the internal capsule, brainstem tegmentum, and cerebellum. Neurotransmitter dysfunction appears central to PLC pathogenesis, with dysregulated serotonergic and glutamatergic signaling identified through neuroimaging and cerebrospinal fluid studies. Current therapeutic approaches demonstrate variable efficacy, with tricyclic antidepressants showing modest benefit in small controlled trials. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors exhibit promising results, while dextromethorphan-quinidine combination therapy has emerged as a novel treatment modality targeting NMDA receptor antagonism. Future research priorities include standardized diagnostic criteria development, larger randomized controlled trials, and investigation of targeted neurotransmitter-specific interventions. Enhanced recognition and treatment of PLC could significantly improve patient outcomes and caregiver burden across multiple neurological populations.

title: Nystagmus.
abstract: Nystagmus, characterized by involuntary rhythmic oscillations of the eyes, represents a complex oculomotor disorder with diverse etiologies ranging from congenital abnormalities to acquired vestibular and central nervous system pathology. Despite advances in understanding the neural circuitry controlling eye movements, pharmacological interventions for nystagmus remain limited and often ineffective. This review examines current neuropharmacological approaches to nystagmus treatment, focusing on recent developments in GABAergic, cholinergic, and adrenergic modulation of oculomotor control. A comprehensive analysis of published clinical trials and case series from 1990-1996 was conducted, evaluating the efficacy of various therapeutic agents including baclofen, gabapentin, clonazepam, scopolamine, and beta-blockers in reducing nystagmus amplitude and improving visual function. Results indicate that GABAergic agents, particularly baclofen at doses of 10-80 mg daily, demonstrate modest efficacy in acquired pendular nystagmus, with approximately 40% of patients showing clinically significant improvement in oscillopsia and visual acuity. Gabapentin shows promise for acquired nystagmus, with preliminary studies suggesting dose-dependent reduction in nystagmus intensity at 900-3600 mg daily. Anticholinergic agents provide limited benefit primarily in vestibular nystagmus, while adrenergic modulators show inconsistent results. Congenital nystagmus remains largely refractory to pharmacological intervention. Current evidence suggests that while no single agent provides universal efficacy, individualized pharmacotherapy based on nystagmus type and underlying pathophysiology may offer symptomatic relief. Future research should focus on novel targets within the oculomotor neural networks and combination therapeutic strategies.

title: History of modern psychopharmacology: a personal view with an emphasis on antidepressants.
abstract: The development of modern psychopharmacology has transformed psychiatric treatment over the past five decades, with antidepressant medications representing one of the most significant therapeutic advances. This review examines the historical evolution of antidepressant pharmacotherapy from the serendipitous discovery of imipramine and monoamine oxidase inhibitors in the 1950s through the introduction of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in the 1980s and 1990s. We analyzed pivotal clinical trials, regulatory milestones, and conceptual shifts in understanding depression neurobiology that shaped drug development strategies. The tricyclic era established the monoamine hypothesis and demonstrated that depression could be effectively treated pharmacologically, though significant side effects limited tolerability. The subsequent development of SSRIs, including fluoxetine, sertraline, and paroxetine, represented a paradigm shift toward more selective receptor targeting with improved safety profiles. Clinical trial data spanning four decades reveal a progressive increase in treatment response rates from 60% with early tricyclics to 65-70% with newer agents, while dropout rates decreased from 30-40% to 15-20%. The introduction of atypical antidepressants targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems, such as venlafaxine and nefazodone, reflects growing appreciation for depression's neurochemical complexity. These advances have fundamentally altered psychiatric practice, enabling outpatient management of severe depression and reducing hospitalization rates by approximately 50% since 1970. Future developments will likely focus on personalized medicine approaches and novel targets beyond traditional monoamine systems to address the substantial proportion of treatment-resistant patients.

title: Academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry.
abstract: The relationship between academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry has evolved significantly over the past two decades, raising concerns about potential conflicts of interest and their impact on research integrity and clinical practice. This study examined the extent and nature of financial relationships between academic psychiatrists and pharmaceutical companies through a comprehensive analysis of disclosure statements, research funding patterns, and publication trends from 1995-2005. We surveyed 847 academic psychiatrists from 125 medical schools across North America and Europe, analyzing their industry relationships, research outputs, and clinical recommendations. Additionally, we reviewed 2,340 published studies in major psychiatric journals to assess funding sources and potential bias indicators. Results revealed that 78% of surveyed academic psychiatrists reported some form of financial relationship with pharmaceutical companies, including research grants (52%), consulting fees (41%), speaker honoraria (38%), and equity holdings (12%). Department chairs and senior faculty showed higher rates of industry involvement (89%) compared to junior faculty (65%). Publications with industry funding were 2.3 times more likely to report outcomes favorable to the sponsor's product compared to independently funded studies. However, methodological quality scores did not differ significantly between industry-funded and independent research. These findings highlight the pervasive nature of academic-industry relationships in psychiatry and suggest the need for enhanced disclosure requirements, institutional oversight mechanisms, and standardized conflict-of-interest policies to maintain research objectivity while preserving beneficial collaborations that advance psychiatric therapeutics.

title: Cluster headache: diagnosis and treatment.
abstract: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder characterized by severe, unilateral periorbital pain occurring in cyclical patterns with associated autonomic features. Despite being one of the most painful conditions known to medicine, cluster headache remains underdiagnosed and undertreated due to limited physician awareness and diagnostic challenges. This comprehensive review examines current diagnostic criteria and evidence-based treatment approaches for cluster headache management. We analyzed recent clinical trials, case series, and systematic reviews published between 2000-2010 to evaluate diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic efficacy. The International Classification of Headache Disorders (ICHD-2) criteria demonstrate high specificity (>95%) when properly applied, though initial misdiagnosis rates remain elevated at 75-80%, often mistaken for migraine or trigeminal neuralgia. Neuroimaging is typically unnecessary except to exclude secondary causes in atypical presentations. For acute treatment, high-flow oxygen (12-15 L/min) provides relief in 78% of attacks within 15 minutes, while subcutaneous sumatriptan 6mg demonstrates 75% efficacy at 15 minutes. Prophylactic therapy with verapamil (240-960mg daily) shows superior efficacy compared to placebo, with 71% of patients achieving >50% reduction in attack frequency. Lithium carbonate and topiramate represent second-line options. Deep brain stimulation of the posterior hypothalamus has emerged as a promising treatment for medically refractory cases. Early recognition and appropriate treatment significantly improve quality of life and reduce disability in cluster headache patients. Increased physician education and adherence to established guidelines remain critical for optimal patient outcomes.

title: [S. V. Anichkov--man and scientist (on the centenary of his birth)].
abstract: The paper is devoted to a brief review of the research and public activities of Professor Sergeĭ V. Anichkov; the prominent Soviet pharmacologist. He was Hero of Socialist Labour, V. I. Lenin and State Prize winners and Academician of the USSR Academy of Medical Sciences. It also summaries the main biographic data on the scientist and his theoretical and practical achievements in various areas of pharmacology. Great emphasis is laid on S. V. Anichkov's scientific school and his work in the Department of Pharmacology (he was head of the Department from 1948 to 1981), Institute for Experimental Medicine, USSR Academy of Medical Sciences.

title: Pitfalls in presenting and interpreting clinical trial data.
abstract: Clinical trials in psychopharmacology have become increasingly sophisticated, yet systematic biases in data presentation and interpretation continue to compromise the validity of research findings. This review examines common methodological pitfalls encountered in the analysis and reporting of clinical trial outcomes in neuropsychiatric drug development. We analyzed 147 published clinical trials of antidepressants, anxiolytics, and antipsychotics from 1990-1994, identifying recurring patterns of data misrepresentation and interpretive errors. Systematic evaluation revealed several critical issues: selective reporting of favorable outcome measures while omitting negative findings (observed in 34% of studies), inappropriate use of multiple statistical comparisons without correction (28%), premature termination of trials showing unfavorable trends (12%), and misleading graphical representations that exaggerate treatment effects (41%). Post-hoc subgroup analyses were frequently employed to salvage non-significant primary endpoints, with insufficient justification in 23% of reviewed studies. Additionally, clinically insignificant but statistically significant differences were often overinterpreted as evidence of therapeutic efficacy. Industry-sponsored trials showed higher rates of these problematic practices compared to investigator-initiated studies (p<0.01). These findings highlight the need for more rigorous standards in clinical trial design, analysis, and reporting within psychopharmacology research. We recommend mandatory registration of study protocols, standardized outcome measures, and independent statistical review to minimize bias and improve the reliability of clinical evidence used to guide therapeutic decisions in psychiatric medicine.

title: Psychopharmacology and ethnicity: A comparative study on Senegalese and Italian men.
abstract: Objectives: Ethnicity represents a crucial factor in influencing response to psychotropic drugs. Some data indicate that functional polymorphisms of two candidate genes of the serotonin (5-HT) transporter (SERT) may affect the response to selective 5-HT reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). The present study aimed to compare the platelet SERT, through the specific [3H]paroxetine ([3H]Par) binding, and plasma oxytocin (OT) levels in 20 Senegalese and in 20 Italian men.Methods: No subjects had family or personal history of any major psychiatric disorder, or had ever regularly taken psychotropic drugs, or were suffering from any physical illness.Results: Senegalese men showed statistically significant higher density (Bmax, fmol/mg protein, mean ± SD) of [3H]Par binding sites (2105.00 ± 473.15 vs 1139.85 ± 213.58, P < 0.001), as well as more elevated plasma OT levels (pg/ml, mean ± SD) (OT: 18.08 ± 4.46 vs 6.62 ± 2.91) than Italian men.Conclusions: These differences, possibly due to genetic or dietary reasons, or even to gender, might affect the response to psychopharmacological compounds. Our findings would suggest specific caution when administering psychotropic compounds to non-European individuals, and the need of further studies in this emerging field of neuropsychopharmacology.
journal: The world journal of biological psychiatry : the official journal of the World Federation of Societies of Biological Psychiatry

title: Investigation of Potential Antioxidant, Thrombolytic and Neuropharmacological Activities of Homalomena aromatica Leaves Using Experimental and In Silico Approaches.
abstract: The leaves of Homalomena aromatica are traditionally used in Bangladesh for the treatment of different chronic ailments. The purpose of this study was to explore in vitro antioxidant, thrombolytic activities, and in vivo neuropharmacological effects of methanolic extract of Homalomena aromatica (MEHA) leaves. Antioxidant activity of MEHA was assessed by a DPPH free radical scavenging assay and total phenolics content, total flavonoids content were also measured. The thrombolytic activity was determined by percentage of clot lysis and neuropharmacological activities by hole board, tail suspension, forced swimming and elevated plus maze tests. The results showed that the IC50 value of the extract against DPPH was 199.51 μg/mL. Quantitative analysis displayed higher contents of phenolics and flavonoids (147.71 mg gallic acid equivalent/g & 66.65 mg quercetin equivalent/g dried extract, respectively). The extract also showed a significant clot lysis (33.31%) activity. In case of anxiolytic activity, the elevate plus maze (EPM) test demonstrated an increase in time spent in open arms, and in case of hole board test, the number of head dipping was also significantly increased (p < 0.05). All the test compared with control (1% Tween in water) and standard (diazepam 1 mg/kg), significant dose (200 & 400 mg/kg) dependent anxiolytic activity was found. In antidepressant activity, there was a significant decrease in period of immobility in both test models (tail suspension and forced swimming) (p < 0.05). Moreover, 13 compounds were identified as bioactive, showed good binding affinities to xanthine oxidoreductase, tissue plasminogen activator receptor, potassium channel receptor, human serotonin receptor targets in molecular docking experiments. Furthermore, ADME/T analysis revealed their drug-likeness, likely pharmacological actions and non-toxic upon consumption. Taken together, our finding support the traditional medicinal use of this plant, which may provide a potential source for future drug discovery.

title: [Integrative assessment of the developmental pharmacology and developmental toxicology, with special reference to the brain].
abstract: Increasing numbers of neurotoxins or therapeutic agents that have specific target cells or receptors can be used to assess the developmental correlation between the structure and function of various organs including the brain. Patients with chronic diseases are now able to maintain their social activities but still must be medicated for a long period of their life. This might increase the potential hazard of prenatal drug exposure in the progeny. Functional teratology is quite a new concept in neuroscience. Recent observations of our laboratory and those of others suggest that the sensitive period for functional teratology might encompass the whole stage of fetal life in animals and humans. The shortage of precise information on the developmental integration of the structure and function of the neurons with different properties is a problem to be solved for the further progress of developmental pharmacology and toxicology. Single exposures to drugs at a different stage during the gestational period of rats or mice might provide more useful information on the relationship between the lesioned area and related functional disorders manifested postnatally. This paper reviews recent advances in developmental neuropharmacology and functional neuroteratology including beneficial points of the short-term exposures to drugs.
journal: Nihon yakurigaku zasshi. Folia pharmacologica Japonica

title: Cannabinoids: Emerging developments in neuropsychopharmacology and biological psychiatry.
abstract: Cannabinoids from the cannabis plant were one of the earliest psychoactive phytochemicals harnessed by humanity for their medicinal properties and remain one of the most frequently used and misused classes of chemicals in the world. Despite our long-standing history with cannabinoids, much more is said than is known regarding how these molecules influence the brain and behavior. We are in a rapidly evolving discovery phase regarding the neuroscience of cannabinoids. This period of insight began in the mid-1990s when it was discovered that phytocannabinoids (e.g., delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol) act on G protein-coupled receptors (i.e., CB1/CB2) in the brain to produce their psychoactive effects. Shortly thereafter, it was discovered that endogenous ligands (i.e., endocannabinoids) exist for these receptor targets and, that they are synthetized on demand under a variety of physiological conditions. Thus, we can now study how phytochemicals, endogenous ligands, and synthetic/metabolic enzymes of the endocannabinoid system influence the brain and behavior by activating known receptor targets. Our increased ability to study cannabinoid interactions with the brain and behavior coincides with an increase in international interest in utilizing cannabinoids as a medicine. At the same time, the potency of, and administration routes by which cannabinoids are used is rapidly changing. And, these trends in cannabinoid misuse are producing lasting neural adaptations that have implications for mental health. In this special edition, we will summarize our recent period of discovery regarding how: 1) phytocannabinoids, synthetic cannabinoids and endocannabinoids act on the brain to produce behavioral effects; 2) cannabinoids can be harnessed to produce pharmacotherapeutic utility in the field of medicine; and 3) use of increasingly more cannabinoid variants through unique routes of administration alter the brain and behavior, especially when used in critical developmental periods like pregnancy and adolescence.

title: Molecular Signaling Pathways Behind the Biological Effects of Salvia Species Diterpenes in Neuropharmacology and Cardiology.
abstract: The genus Salvia, from the Lamiaceae family, has diverse biological properties that are primarily attributable to their diterpene contents. There is no comprehensive review on the molecular signaling pathways of these active components. In this review, we investigated the molecular targets of bioactive Salvia diterpenes responsible for the treatment of nervous and cardiovascular diseases. The effects on different pathways, including apoptosis signaling, oxidative stress phenomena, the accumulation of amyloid beta plaques, and tau phosphorylation, have all been considered to be mechanisms of the anti-Alzheimer properties of Salvia diterpenes. Additionally, effects on the benzodiazepine and kappa opioid receptors and neuroprotective effects are noted as neuropharmacological properties of Salvia diterpenes, including tanshinone IIA, salvinorin A, cryptotanshinone, and miltirone. Tanshinone IIA, as the primary diterpene of Salvia miltiorrhiza, has beneficial activities in heart diseases because of its ability to scavenge free radicals and its effects on transcription factors, such as nuclear transcription factor-kappa B (NF-κB) and the mitogen-activated protein kinases (MAPKs). Additionally, tanshinone IIA has also been proposed to have cardioprotective properties including antiarrhythmic activities and effects on myocardial infarction. With respect to the potential therapeutic effects of Salvia diterpenes, comprehensive clinical trials are warranted to evaluate these valuable molecules as lead compounds. Copyright © 2016 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

title: Fundamental and clinical neuropharmacology, a terra incognita with constantly expanding frontiers.
abstract: The field of neuropharmacology has witnessed unprecedented growth over the past decade, yet significant knowledge gaps persist in our understanding of drug-brain interactions and their clinical translation. This comprehensive review synthesizes recent advances in fundamental neuropharmacology research and examines the evolving landscape of therapeutic interventions for neurological and psychiatric disorders. We conducted a systematic analysis of published literature from 2010-2021, encompassing preclinical studies, clinical trials, and emerging therapeutic modalities. Database searches yielded 2,847 relevant publications, which were evaluated for methodological rigor, translational potential, and clinical impact. Our analysis reveals three major frontiers driving neuropharmacological innovation: precision medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenomics and biomarker-guided therapy, novel drug delivery systems enabling targeted central nervous system penetration, and the emergence of neuromodulatory techniques as pharmacological adjuncts. Despite remarkable progress in molecular neurobiology and drug discovery platforms, the failure rate for neurotherapeutic development remains disproportionately high at 92% compared to other therapeutic areas. Key challenges include the complexity of blood-brain barrier penetration, interspecies translational gaps, and the heterogeneous nature of neuropsychiatric phenotypes. However, breakthrough technologies including optogenetics, CRISPR-based therapeutics, and artificial intelligence-driven drug repurposing strategies offer unprecedented opportunities for therapeutic advancement. The neuropharmacological landscape continues to expand rapidly, demanding interdisciplinary collaboration and innovative methodological approaches to bridge the persistent gap between preclinical promise and clinical efficacy in treating disorders of the central nervous system.

title: Getting a Handle on Neuropharmacology by Targeting Receptor-Associated Proteins.
abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches have predominantly focused on direct modulation of neurotransmitter receptors, yet mounting evidence suggests that receptor-associated proteins (RAPs) represent untapped therapeutic targets with enhanced specificity and reduced side effects. Here, we systematically investigated the druggability of RAPs using a combination of structural biology, pharmacological screening, and behavioral analysis across multiple neurotransmitter systems. We employed high-throughput screening of 15,000 small molecules against purified protein complexes containing AMPA receptors with their auxiliary subunits, GABA_A receptors with associated scaffolding proteins, and dopamine D2 receptors coupled to their regulatory partners. Structural determination by cryo-electron microscopy revealed distinct allosteric binding sites on stargazin, gephyrin, and β-arrestin2 that modulate receptor function without directly competing with endogenous ligands. Lead compounds targeting these sites demonstrated nanomolar potency and remarkable selectivity profiles in patch-clamp electrophysiology experiments. In vivo validation using rodent models of depression, epilepsy, and Parkinson's disease showed therapeutic efficacy comparable to existing medications but with significantly improved side effect profiles. Notably, compound RAP-347, targeting the AMPA receptor-stargazin interface, produced antidepressant effects without the gastrointestinal and sexual dysfunction associated with conventional SSRIs. These findings establish RAPs as a new frontier in neuropharmacology, offering precision medicine approaches that exploit the molecular diversity of native receptor complexes. This paradigm shift from receptor-centric to complex-centric drug discovery may accelerate development of safer, more effective neurotherapeutics.

title: The cell cycle in Alzheimer disease: a unique target for neuropharmacology.
abstract: Several hypotheses have been proposed attempting to explain the pathogenesis of Alzheimer disease including, among others, theories involving amyloid deposition, tau phosphorylation, oxidative stress, metal ion dysregulation and inflammation. While there is strong evidence suggesting that each one of these proposed mechanisms contributes to disease pathogenesis, none of these mechanisms are able to account for all the physiological changes that occur during the course of the disease. For this reason, we and others have begun the search for a causative factor that predates known features found in Alzheimer disease, and that might therefore be a fundamental initiator of the pathophysiological cascade. We propose that the dysregulation of the cell cycle that occurs in neurons susceptible to degeneration in the hippocampus during Alzheimer disease is a potential causative factor that, together with oxidative stress, would initiate all known pathological events. Neuronal changes supporting alterations in cell cycle control in the etiology of Alzheimer disease include the ectopic expression of markers of the cell cycle, organelle kinesis and cytoskeletal alterations including tau phosphorylation. Such mitotic alterations are not only one of the earliest neuronal abnormalities in the disease, but as discussed herein, are also intimately linked to all of the other pathological hallmarks of Alzheimer disease including tau protein, amyloid beta protein precursor and oxidative stress, and even risk factors such as mutations in the presenilin genes. Therefore, therapeutic interventions targeted toward ameliorating mitotic changes would be predicted to have a profound and positive impact on Alzheimer disease progression.

title: Blended learning: how can we optimise undergraduate student engagement?
abstract: Blended learning is a combination of online and face-to-face learning and is increasingly of interest for use in undergraduate medical education. It has been used to teach clinical post-graduate students pharmacology but needs evaluation for its use in teaching pharmacology to undergraduate medical students, which represent a different group of students with different learning needs. An existing BSc-level module on neuropharmacology was redesigned using the Blended Learning Design Tool (BLEnDT), a tool which uses learning domains (psychomotor, cognitive and affective) to classify learning outcomes into those taught best by self-directed learning (online) or by collaborative learning (face-to-face). Two online courses were developed, one on Neurotransmitters and the other on Neurodegenerative Conditions. These were supported with face-to-face tutorials. Undergraduate students' engagement with blended learning was explored by the means of three focus groups, the data from which were analysed thematically. Five major themes emerged from the data 1) Purpose and Acceptability 2) Structure, Focus and Consolidation 3) Preparation and workload 4) Engagement with e-learning component 5) Future Medical Education. Blended learning was acceptable and of interest to undergraduate students learning this subject. They expressed a desire for more blended learning in their courses, but only if it was highly structured, of high quality and supported by tutorials. Students identified that the 'blend' was beneficial rather than purely online learning.

title: Movement disorders and new azapirone anxiolytic drugs.
abstract: Dopamine is a neurotransmitter affecting motor function, and the dopamine system is the focus of abnormality in dyskinetic diseases. An important side effect of many psychotropic drugs possessing dopamine activity is the production of movement disorders; this potential problem needs to be examined carefully with any new central nervous system drug, such as the azapirone anxiolytic drugs. The pharmacology of the azapirones, represented by the prototype, buspirone, involves several different neuroreceptor systems. Current evidence indicates that buspirone's anxiolytic activity is mediated through serotonergic mechanisms. It also displays some binding affinity for dopamine receptors, which necessitates comparisons between buspirone and neuroleptic agents in preclinical tests, clinical studies, and case reports. Although similarities occurred in some biochemical studies, neuroleptics, and not buspirone, caused an increase in dopamine receptors with chronic use. Neuroleptics typically produce this change after long-term use, which is thought to be a key factor leading to tardive dyskinesia. In standard animal behavior studies, buspirone's profile failed to match neuroleptic agents. Most notably, neuroleptics produce catalepsy; however, buspirone failed to induce catalepsy and instead reversed this effect. Isolated case reports have described buspirone as causing movement disorders, but these patients were previously exposed to neuroleptic agents. Furthermore, buspirone has been used successfully to treat anxiety in parkinsonism without exacerbating existing movement problems. In psychotropic drug use, careful patient monitoring, complete drug histories, and a full appreciation for a drug's pharmacology are necessary to decipher any likelihood of a drug relation with an adverse effect.
journal: The Journal of the American Board of Family Practice

title: Neuroethics and psychiatry.
abstract: The intersection of neuroscience and ethics has emerged as a critical domain in contemporary psychiatric practice, particularly as neuroimaging technologies and psychopharmacological interventions become increasingly sophisticated. This review examines the ethical implications of recent advances in neuropharmacology and neuroimaging within psychiatric contexts, analyzing literature published between 2005-2008. We conducted a comprehensive review of peer-reviewed articles, clinical guidelines, and case studies addressing neuroethical considerations in psychiatric treatment, focusing on cognitive enhancement, neuroimaging for diagnostic purposes, and informed consent in vulnerable populations. Key findings reveal significant ethical tensions surrounding the use of psychostimulants for cognitive enhancement in healthy individuals, with 34% of surveyed psychiatrists reporting requests for "cosmetic psychopharmacology." Neuroimaging studies raise concerns about genetic discrimination and privacy, particularly when structural abnormalities are detected incidentally. The concept of mental capacity and autonomous decision-making becomes complex when treating conditions that directly affect cognition and judgment. Additionally, the medicalization of normal human experiences through expanding diagnostic categories presents fundamental questions about the nature of mental illness. Our analysis identifies urgent needs for updated ethical frameworks addressing: enhancement versus treatment distinctions, confidentiality of neuroimaging data, and protection of vulnerable populations in research settings. As neurotechnology continues advancing, psychiatric practice must develop robust ethical guidelines that balance therapeutic innovation with patient autonomy and societal well-being. Professional training programs should integrate neuroethical education to prepare clinicians for these emerging challenges.

title: An integrative dynamic model of brain energy metabolism using in vivo neurochemical measurements.
abstract: An integrative, systems approach to the modelling of brain energy metabolism is presented. Mechanisms such as glutamate cycling between neurons and astrocytes and glycogen storage in astrocytes have been implemented. A unique feature of the model is its calibration using in vivo data of brain glucose and lactate from freely moving rats under various stimuli. The model has been used to perform simulated perturbation experiments that show that glycogen breakdown in astrocytes is significantly activated during sensory (tail pinch) stimulation. This mechanism provides an additional input of energy substrate during high consumption phases. By way of validation, data from the perfusion of 50 microM propranolol in the rat brain was compared with the model outputs. Propranolol affects the glucose dynamics during stimulation, and this was accurately reproduced in the model by a reduction in the glycogen breakdown in astrocytes. The model's predictive capacity was verified by using data from a sensory stimulation (restraint) that was not used for model calibration. Finally, a sensitivity analysis was conducted on the model parameters, this showed that the control of energy metabolism and transport processes are critical in the metabolic behaviour of cerebral tissue.

title: Principles of motivation revealed by the diverse functions of neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates underlying feeding behavior.
abstract: The neural mechanisms underlying feeding behavior represent a complex interplay of motivational, reward, and homeostatic systems that have evolved to ensure energy balance and survival. While extensive research has identified key neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates of feeding, the fundamental principles governing motivation across these diverse systems remain poorly understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge of feeding-related neural circuits to extract overarching motivational principles applicable to broader behavioral contexts. We analyzed findings from pharmacological studies targeting dopaminergic, serotonergic, GABAergic, and neuropeptidergic systems, alongside neuroanatomical investigations of hypothalamic, limbic, and brainstem structures involved in feeding regulation. Our analysis reveals three core motivational principles: (1) hierarchical processing, wherein homeostatic signals from hypothalamic nuclei modulate reward-processing regions including the ventral tegmental area and nucleus accumbens; (2) opponent-process dynamics, demonstrated by the reciprocal relationship between orexigenic and anorexigenic peptide systems; and (3) contextual integration, whereby environmental cues are processed through prefrontal-limbic circuits to modify feeding motivation. Pharmacological manipulations consistently show that disruption of any single system produces compensatory changes in others, suggesting robust motivational redundancy. These findings demonstrate that feeding behavior exemplifies fundamental organizational principles of motivated behavior, including distributed processing, dynamic opponent systems, and adaptive flexibility. Understanding these principles provides a framework for interpreting motivation across diverse behavioral domains and offers insights for developing therapeutic interventions for disorders of motivation and reward processing.

title: Annual Research Review: New frontiers in developmental neuropharmacology: can long-term therapeutic effects of drugs be optimized through carefully timed early intervention?
abstract: Mounting evidence suggests that the developing brain exhibits unique periods of heightened neuroplasticity that may be leveraged to optimize long-term therapeutic outcomes through strategically timed pharmacological interventions. This review examines emerging research on critical developmental windows and their implications for pediatric psychopharmacology. We systematically reviewed preclinical and clinical studies published between 2005-2011 investigating age-dependent drug effects, focusing on interventions targeting neurodevelopmental disorders including ADHD, autism spectrum disorders, and early-onset mood disorders. Preclinical studies consistently demonstrate that identical pharmacological agents produce markedly different long-term neurobiological and behavioral outcomes when administered during specific developmental epochs versus later time points. For example, early selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor exposure during critical periods of serotonergic system maturation (postnatal weeks 2-3 in rodents) results in persistent alterations in anxiety-like behavior and stress responsivity that differ substantially from adult treatment effects. Clinical data, while more limited, suggest similar phenomena in human populations. Early intervention with stimulant medications during preschool years appears to produce more robust and sustained improvements in executive function compared to treatment initiation during school age. Conversely, some interventions show enhanced efficacy when delayed until after critical developmental transitions. These findings suggest that optimizing pediatric pharmacotherapy requires moving beyond simple dose-scaling approaches to embrace developmentally-informed treatment strategies. Future research should prioritize identification of biomarkers for critical periods and development of age-specific therapeutic protocols that capitalize on developmental neuroplasticity while minimizing potential disruption of normal maturation processes.
journal: Journal of child psychology and psychiatry, and allied disciplines

title: Emergency Neuropharmacology.
abstract: Management of acute neurologic disorders in the emergency department is multimodal and may require the use of medications to decrease morbidity and mortality secondary to neurologic injury. Clinicians should form an individualized treatment approach with regard to various patient specific factors. This review article focuses on the pharmacotherapy for common neurologic emergencies that present to the emergency department, including traumatic brain injury, central nervous system infections, status epilepticus, hypertensive emergencies, spinal cord injury, and neurogenic shock.
journal: Emergency medicine clinics of North America

title: Neuropharmacological studies on Wedelia calendulacea Less stem extract.
abstract: The neuropharmacological activities of the methanolic and aqueous extract of Wedelia calendulacea stem were screened in rats and mice. The extracts effect on pentobarbital-induced sleeping time, pentylenetetrazole- and styrychnine-induced seizure, spontaneous motor activity, exploratory behaviour, and rota-rod performance (motor coordination) were evaluated. The methanolic extract (20 and 50 mg/kg, i.p.) and aqueous extract (200 and 500 mg/kg, i.p.) produced a significant (p<0.001) prolongation of pentobarbital-induced sleeping time, and reduced the SMA and exploratory behaviour. The extract prolonged onset of the phases of seizure activity but did not protect mice against lethality induced by pentylenetetrazole and strychnine.
