It also failed to affect the motor coordination test. These results suggest that the extract contained an agent with neuropharmacological activity that may be sedative in nature. In addition, from the crude methanolic extract of Wedelia calendulacea stem a HPLC fingerprint profile and liquid chromatography/sequential mass spectrometry (LC/MS) were performed.
journal: Phytomedicine : international journal of phytotherapy and phytopharmacology

title: Zebrafish assessment of cognitive improvement and anxiolysis: filling the gap between in vitro and rodent models for drug development.
abstract: Zebrafish can provide a valuable animal model to screen potential cognitive enhancing and anxiolytic drugs. They are economical and can provide a relatively quick indication of possible functional efficacy. In as much as they have a complex nervous system and elaborate behavioral repertoire, zebrafish can provide a good intermediate model between in vitro receptor and cell-based assays and classic mammalian models for drug screening. In addition, the variety of molecular tools available in zebrafish makes them outstanding models for helping to determine the neuromolecular mechanisms for psychoactive drugs. However, to use zebrafish as a translational model we must have validated, sensitive and efficient behavioral tests. In a series of studies, our lab has developed tests of cognitive function and stress response, which are sensitive to drug effects in a similar manner as rodent models and humans for cognitive enhancement and alleviating stress response. In particular, the three-chamber task for learning and memory was shown to be sensitive to the cognitive enhancing effects of nicotine and has been useful in helping to determine neural mechanisms crucial for nicotinic-induced cognitive enhancement. The novel tank diving test was shown to be a valid and efficient test of stress response. It is sensitive to the reduction in stress-related behaviors due to the amxiolytic drugs diazepam and buspirone but not chlordiazepoxide. Nicotine also causes stress alleviating effects which can be interpreted as anxiolytic effects. Zebrafish models of behavioral pharmacology can be useful to efficiently screen test compounds for drug development and can be useful in helping to determine the mechanisms crucial for new therapeutic treatments of neurobehavioral impairments.

title: Subcellular probes for neurochemical recording from multiple brain sites.
abstract: Dysregulation of neurochemicals, in particular, dopamine, is epitomized in numerous debilitating disorders that impair normal movement and mood aspects of our everyday behavior. Neurochemical transmission is a neuron-specific process, and further exhibits region-specific signaling in the brain. Tools are needed to monitor the heterogeneous spatiotemporal dynamics of dopamine neurotransmission without compromising the physiological processes of the neuronal environment. We developed neurochemical probes that are ten times smaller than any existing dopamine sensor, based on the size of the entire implanted shaft and its sensing tip. The microfabricated probe occupies a spatial footprint (9 μm) coordinate with the average size of individual neuronal cells (∼10 μm). These cellular-scale probes were shown to reduce inflammatory response of the implanted brain tissue environment. The probes are further configured in the form of a microarray to permit electrochemical sampling of dopamine and other neurotransmitters at unprecedented spatial densities and distributions. Dopamine recording was performed concurrently from up to 16 sites in the striatum of rats, revealing a remarkable spatiotemporal contrast in dopamine transmission as well as site-specific pharmacological modulation. Collectively, the reported platform endeavors to enable high density mapping of the chemical messengers fundamentally involved in neuronal communication through the use of minimally invasive probes that help preserve the neuronal viability of the implant environment.

title: Neurochemically distinct myenteric neurone populations containing calbindin have specific distribution patterns around the circumference of the gastric corpus.
abstract: The myenteric plexus of the gastric corpus contains morphologically and neurochemically heterogeneous populations of neurons that regulate gastric motility and secretion. While previous studies have identified calbindin-immunoreactive neurons within the enteric nervous system, their precise distribution patterns and neurochemical phenotypes remain poorly characterized. We investigated the circumferential organization and neurochemical properties of calbindin-positive myenteric neurons in the rat gastric corpus using immunohistochemistry and confocal microscopy. Whole-mount preparations of gastric muscle layers from adult Wistar rats (n=8) were processed for multiple immunofluorescence labeling using antibodies against calbindin-D28K, choline acetyltransferase, nitric oxide synthase, vasoactive intestinal peptide, and substance P. Quantitative analysis revealed that calbindin-immunoreactive neurons comprised 23.4 ± 2.1% of total myenteric neurons and exhibited distinct regional variations around the gastric circumference. The highest density occurred along the greater curvature (34.2 ± 3.8 neurons/cm²), while the lowest density was observed near the lesser curvature (18.7 ± 2.3 neurons/cm²). Double-labeling experiments demonstrated three neurochemically distinct calbindin-positive populations: cholinergic motor neurons (41.2%), nitrergic inhibitory neurons (35.8%), and a population co-expressing vasoactive intestinal peptide (23.0%). Each subpopulation showed characteristic circumferential distribution patterns that correlated with regional differences in gastric wall innervation density. These findings reveal a previously unrecognized organizational principle of the gastric myenteric plexus and suggest that calbindin-containing neurons contribute to region-specific control of gastric function through distinct neurochemical mechanisms.

title: Psychiatric safety of ketamine in psychopharmacology research.
abstract: **Background:** Ketamine, an NMDA receptor antagonist, has gained increasing attention as a research tool in psychopharmacology studies investigating glutamatergic mechanisms of psychiatric disorders. However, concerns regarding ketamine's psychiatric safety profile, particularly its potential to induce transient psychotomimetic effects and dissociative symptoms, have raised questions about its appropriate use in research populations. **Objective:** To systematically evaluate the psychiatric safety profile of ketamine administration in controlled psychopharmacology research settings. **Methods:** We conducted a comprehensive review of 47 published studies (n=1,247 subjects) utilizing ketamine in psychopharmacology research between 1995-2006. Studies employed subanesthetic doses (0.1-0.5 mg/kg IV bolus or infusion) in healthy volunteers and psychiatric patients. Primary safety outcomes included psychotic symptoms, dissociative effects, mood changes, and serious adverse events requiring intervention. **Results:** Transient perceptual alterations and mild dissociative symptoms were reported in 68% of subjects, typically resolving within 2 hours post-administration. Clinically significant psychotic symptoms occurred in 3.2% of healthy volunteers and 8.1% of psychiatric patients, with no episodes lasting beyond 24 hours. Two subjects (0.16%) required anxiolytic intervention for severe anxiety. No instances of persistent psychosis, suicidal ideation, or psychiatric hospitalization were observed. Pre-existing psychotic disorders and higher doses (>0.4 mg/kg) were associated with increased risk of adverse psychiatric effects. **Conclusions:** When administered under appropriate medical supervision with proper subject screening, ketamine demonstrates an acceptable psychiatric safety profile for psychopharmacology research. Careful exclusion of high-risk populations and adherence to established dosing protocols are essential for minimizing psychiatric adverse events.

title: The Neuropharmacology of Impulsive Behaviour, an Update.
abstract: Impulsive behavior, characterized by premature responses and poor inhibitory control, represents a core symptom across numerous psychiatric disorders including ADHD, substance use disorders, and personality disorders. While previous research has established the fundamental role of monoaminergic systems in impulse control, recent advances in neuropharmacology have revealed more complex mechanistic pathways involving glutamatergic, GABAergic, and cholinergic neurotransmission. This review synthesizes current understanding of the neurochemical basis of impulsivity, with particular emphasis on findings from the past decade. We examined preclinical and clinical studies investigating pharmacological interventions targeting impulsive behaviors, focusing on novel therapeutic approaches beyond traditional monoamine-based treatments. Key findings indicate that impulsivity involves dysregulation within prefrontal-striatal circuits, with distinct neurochemical profiles underlying different impulsivity subtypes. Dopaminergic signaling in the nucleus accumbens and ventral tegmental area remains central to reward-driven impulsivity, while serotonergic modulation in orbitofrontal and anterior cingulate cortices governs response inhibition. Emerging evidence highlights the therapeutic potential of glutamate modulators, particularly mGluR5 antagonists and AMPA receptor positive allosteric modulators, in reducing impulsive responding. Additionally, cholinergic enhancement through nicotinic α7 receptor agonists shows promise for improving attentional control. Novel targets including endocannabinoid system modulators and neuropeptide Y receptors represent promising avenues for future drug development. These advances provide a foundation for developing more targeted pharmacological interventions for impulse control disorders, moving toward personalized treatment approaches based on specific neurochemical phenotypes.

title: The ethics of elective psychopharmacology.
abstract: Pharmacological cognitive enhancers (PCEs) are used to improve cognitive functions, such as attention, learning, memory and planning in patients with impairments in cognition resulting from traumatic brain injury (TBI) or from neuropsychiatric disorders such as Alzheimer's disease (AD), mild cognitive impairment, schizophrenia, and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Moreover, PCEs have been shown to improve cognition in healthy volunteers with no psychiatric disorders. This article describes the rationale behind the need for their use in neuropsychiatric patients and illustrates how PCEs can ameliorate cognitive impairments, improve quality of life and wellbeing, and therefore reduce the economic burden associated with these disorders. We also describe evidence that PCEs are being used as cognitive enhancers by healthy people. Crucially, as the lifestyle use of these drugs becomes very popular in the healthy population, a final aim is to present an overview of the current and future neuroethical considerations of enhancing the healthy brain. As information regarding their actual use, benefits and harms in various healthy populations is currently lacking, we propose research that aims to obtain relevant empirical data, monitor the short- and long-term effectiveness and side-effects, and initiate accurate surveys to determine current patterns and quantity of usage of PCE drugs by healthy people. Furthermore, in order to instigate a dialogue between neuroethics and neuropsychopharmacology, we urge scientists to explore and communicate the social and ethical implications of their research to the public. Finally, we discuss and highlight other means of enhancing cognition in both patients and healthy adults, including education and physical exercise.

title: Neuropsychiatric aspects of Parkinson disease psychopharmacology: Insights from circuit dynamics.
abstract: Neuropsychiatric complications affect up to 90% of Parkinson disease (PD) patients and represent major contributors to disability and caregiver burden. While dopaminergic therapies effectively address motor symptoms, their impact on non-motor neuropsychiatric manifestations remains complex and poorly understood. This review synthesizes current evidence on PD psychopharmacology through the lens of cortico-basal ganglia circuit dysfunction. We analyzed published literature from 2000-2018 examining pharmacological interventions for depression, anxiety, psychosis, and impulse control disorders in PD, with particular focus on circuit-based mechanisms underlying therapeutic responses and adverse effects. Dopaminergic medications, while improving motor function via nigrostriatal pathway modulation, paradoxically worsen psychosis through excessive stimulation of relatively preserved mesolimbic circuits. Conversely, antipsychotics effective for hallucinations often exacerbate motor symptoms by further blocking already depleted striatal dopamine. Depression and anxiety in PD respond variably to conventional antidepressants, likely reflecting disrupted serotonergic and noradrenergic innervation of limbic circuits. Impulse control disorders emerge from hyperdopaminergic stimulation of reward circuits, particularly ventral striatal regions. Circuit-informed approaches reveal why selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show modest efficacy, atypical antipsychotics provide optimal psychosis management with minimal motor worsening, and dopamine agonist reduction effectively treats behavioral addictions. Understanding neuropsychiatric symptoms through disrupted circuit dynamics offers a rational framework for personalized PD psychopharmacology. Future therapeutic development should target circuit-specific dysfunction rather than employing broad neurotransmitter modulation, potentially improving both motor and non-motor outcomes while minimizing treatment-related complications in this vulnerable population.

title: Genomics and proteomics: the next generation of health care.
abstract: Recent advances in genomic sequencing and proteomic analysis have created unprecedented opportunities for personalized medicine approaches in neuropharmacology, with significant implications for employee health benefit programs. This study examined the feasibility and cost-effectiveness of implementing pharmacogenomic screening in corporate health plans to optimize psychotropic medication selection. We conducted a 12-month prospective analysis of 847 employees across three Fortune 500 companies who were prescribed antidepressants or anxiolytics. Participants were randomized to receive either standard care or genomics-guided therapy based on CYP2D6, CYP2C19, and serotonin transporter polymorphisms. Primary outcomes included medication adherence, clinical response rates, and healthcare utilization costs. Secondary measures assessed workplace productivity metrics and employee satisfaction scores. Genomics-guided therapy resulted in 34% fewer medication switches (p<0.001), 28% improvement in clinical response at 8 weeks (p<0.01), and 41% reduction in adverse events requiring medical intervention (p<0.001). Total healthcare costs decreased by $1,247 per employee annually, primarily due to reduced emergency department visits and psychiatric hospitalizations. Workplace productivity increased by 12% in the genomics group, with significant improvements in absenteeism and disability claims. Employee satisfaction with mental health benefits improved significantly (p<0.05). These findings suggest that integration of pharmacogenomic testing into employee benefit programs represents a cost-effective strategy for optimizing neuropsychiatric care while reducing overall healthcare expenditures and improving workforce productivity in the emerging genomics era.

title: Neuropharmacological studies on Wedelia calendulacea Less stem extract.
abstract:
**Background and Purpose:** Wedelia calendulacea Less (Asteraceae) is traditionally used in folk medicine for treating neurological disorders, including epilepsy and anxiety. Despite its ethnopharmacological significance, limited scientific evidence exists regarding its neuropharmacological properties. This study investigated the neuropharmacological activities of W. calendulacea stem extract in experimental models.

**Methods:** The methanolic stem extract of W. calendulacea was prepared and subjected to preliminary phytochemical screening. Acute toxicity was evaluated using OECD guidelines. Neuropharmacological activities were assessed using established behavioral models in Swiss albino mice. Anticonvulsant activity was evaluated using maximal electroshock seizure (MES) and pentylenetetrazol (PTZ)-induced seizure models at doses of 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg, i.p. Anxiolytic activity was assessed using elevated plus maze and open field tests. CNS depressant activity was evaluated through pentobarbital-induced sleeping time and locomotor activity tests. Diazepam and phenytoin served as standard drugs.

**Results:** Phytochemical analysis revealed the presence of flavonoids, saponins, and terpenoids. The extract showed significant dose-dependent anticonvulsant activity in both MES and PTZ models, with maximum protection (78.3% and 71.6%, respectively) at 400 mg/kg. In the elevated plus maze test, the extract significantly increased the percentage of open arm entries and time spent, indicating anxiolytic activity. The extract also demonstrated CNS depressant effects by prolonging pentobarbital-induced sleeping time and reducing locomotor activity.

**Conclusion:** W. calendulacea stem extract exhibits significant neuropharmacological activities, supporting its traditional use in neurological disorders. Further studies are warranted to identify active compounds and elucidate mechanisms of action.
journal: Phytomedicine : international journal of phytotherapy and phytopharmacology

title: Psychopharmacology of autistic spectrum disorders in children and adolescents.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorders (ASD) affect approximately 1 in 110 children and are characterized by impaired social communication, restricted interests, and repetitive behaviors. While behavioral interventions remain the cornerstone of treatment, psychopharmacological approaches have emerged as important adjunctive therapies for managing associated symptoms and comorbidities. This comprehensive review examines current evidence for medication use in pediatric ASD populations. A systematic literature search was conducted using PubMed, Cochrane Database, and PsycINFO for studies published between 2000-2011, focusing on randomized controlled trials and open-label studies in children and adolescents with ASD. Primary outcome measures included behavioral symptom reduction, functional improvement, and adverse event profiles. Evidence supports the use of risperidone and aripiprazole for irritability, aggression, and self-injurious behaviors, with both agents receiving FDA approval for these indications in pediatric ASD. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show mixed efficacy for repetitive behaviors and anxiety, with sertraline and fluoxetine demonstrating modest benefits in some studies. Methylphenidate appears effective for comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms, though response rates may be lower than in typically developing children. Alpha-2 agonists like guanfacine show promise for hyperactivity and impulsivity. Melatonin effectively addresses sleep disturbances common in ASD. However, medication responses are often heterogeneous, and careful monitoring for adverse effects is essential. Psychopharmacological interventions should be considered as part of comprehensive, individualized treatment plans that prioritize behavioral and educational approaches while addressing specific symptom domains that significantly impair functioning.

title: Tobacco psychopharmacology and public health policy: it takes a community.
abstract: The public health burden of tobacco use remains substantial despite decades of policy interventions, suggesting that traditional approaches may benefit from enhanced understanding of nicotine's neuropharmacological mechanisms and their interaction with social-environmental factors. This review synthesizes current evidence on tobacco dependence neurobiological pathways and examines how community-level interventions can leverage pharmacological insights to improve cessation outcomes. We conducted a systematic analysis of randomized controlled trials (n=47) published between 2005-2011 that combined pharmacotherapeutic approaches with community-based interventions, alongside neuroimaging studies (n=23) examining nicotine's effects on reward circuitry in social contexts. Pharmacological treatments showed significantly enhanced efficacy when implemented through community networks compared to clinical settings alone (OR=1.64, 95% CI 1.31-2.05, p<0.001). Varenicline and bupropion demonstrated superior outcomes in community pharmacy programs versus standard medical care (quit rates 28.3% vs 19.7%, p=0.003). Neuroimaging data revealed that social environmental cues modulate nicotinic receptor availability and dopaminergic signaling in ways that influence medication response patterns. Community-delivered nicotine replacement therapy combined with peer support networks achieved 6-month abstinence rates of 31.2% compared to 22.1% for individual treatment approaches. These findings suggest that effective tobacco control requires integration of neuropharmacological knowledge with community-level implementation strategies. The complex interplay between nicotine's neurobiological effects and social determinants of smoking behavior necessitates collaborative approaches spanning clinical pharmacology, public health infrastructure, and community engagement to optimize population-level cessation outcomes.

title: Strategy for utilization of new antiepileptic drugs.
abstract: The paper reviews strategies to incorporate new antiepileptic drugs into the treatment arsenal for patients with epilepsy. Ten new antiepileptic drugs have been developed in the last two decades, making selection of optimal therapy complex; they have not been shown to have better efficacy but generally seem to be better tolerated. Newer antiepileptic drugs offer new opportunities to patients who have not had a favorable response to the old ones. Many new antiepileptic drugs exhibit a broad spectrum of activity while only one of the older ones (valproate) has a broad-spectrum profile. There are therefore more choices when trying to match treatment with epileptic seizures and syndromes. The side-effect profiles of the newer antiepileptic drugs differ from the older ones with fewer systemic reactions and better pharmacokinetics for the most part. Comparative studies are needed to elucidate the specific weaknesses and strengths of each of the new antiepileptic drugs compared with the older ones. Most clinical trials do not help the physician in deciding drug, dose, or titration schedules. Thus the physician needs to understand efficacy spectrum and side-effect profiles of each new antiepileptic drug in order to be able to treat each patient optimally.

title: From real-world events to psychosis: the emerging neuropharmacology of delusions.
abstract: Delusions represent a core feature of psychotic disorders, yet their neurobiological underpinnings remain poorly understood. Recent evidence suggests that delusional beliefs may emerge from dysregulated neurotransmitter systems that normally process environmental salience and prediction errors. This study investigated the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying delusion formation using a novel translational approach combining clinical assessment, neuroimaging, and pharmacological challenge paradigms. We recruited 45 patients with schizophrenia experiencing active delusions, 32 patients in remission, and 38 healthy controls. Participants underwent functional magnetic resonance imaging during a probabilistic learning task designed to elicit prediction errors, followed by acute administration of either haloperidol (2mg), amisulpride (400mg), or placebo in a double-blind, randomized design. Delusional severity was assessed using the Peters Delusions Inventory and correlated with neural activation patterns. Patients with active delusions showed hyperactivation in the ventral striatum and reduced prefrontal cortical engagement during unexpected events compared to controls (p<0.001). Haloperidol administration normalized striatal hyperactivity and improved performance on the prediction error task, while amisulpride selectively enhanced prefrontal activation. Critically, the degree of striatal normalization following D2 blockade correlated significantly with reductions in delusional conviction scores (r=0.67, p<0.001). These findings provide direct evidence that delusions arise from aberrant dopaminergic signaling of environmental salience, leading to inappropriate assignment of significance to neutral stimuli. The differential effects of D2 versus D2/D3 antagonism suggest multiple therapeutic targets for delusion-specific interventions, potentially informing more precise pharmacological approaches to psychotic symptoms.

title: Neuropharmacology, pharmacogenetics and pharmacogenomics of aggression: The zebrafish model.
abstract: The zebrafish (Danio rerio) is increasingly utilized as a powerful new model organism in neurobehavioral research. Aggression is a common symptom of many CNS disorders, has some genetic determinants and can be modulated pharmacologically in humans and animal model species. Mounting evidence suggests zebrafish as a useful tool to study neurobiology of aggression, and its pharmacological and genetic regulation. Here, we discuss mechanisms of zebrafish aggression and their pharmacological, pharmacogenetic and pharmacogenomic models, as well as recent developments and existing challenges in this field. We also emphasize the growing utility of zebrafish models in translational neuropharmacological research of aggression, fostering future discoveries of potential therapeutic agents for aggressive behavior.

title: Considerations in the choice of an antiepileptic drug in the treatment of epilepsy.
abstract: The advent of multiple new antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) in the past 15 years has provided new options for clinicians treating patients with epilepsy, but has also created a need for guidelines in AED use. For selection of the most appropriate AED, the neurologist must know the pharmacological properties of the AEDs and has to balance their benefits and potential risks for the individual patient. A key consideration in AED selection is spectrum of efficacy for seizure types and epileptic syndrome. Other factors in AED selection include safety and tolerability, ease of use and titration rate, potential interactions, and efficacy in coexisting nonepileptic conditions. Comorbid conditions may favor or exclude the use of certain AEDs in a particular patient. Special considerations apply in specific epilepsy populations, such as women of childbearing age, children, and the elderly. This article analyzes the various factors involved in choosing an AED.

title: Operant self-administration models for testing the neuropharmacological basis of ethanol consumption in rats.
abstract: Operant self-administration procedures are used to assess the neural basis of ethanol-seeking behavior under a wide range of experimental conditions. In general, rats do not spontaneously self-administer ethanol in pharmacologically meaningful amounts. This unit provides a step-by-step guide for training rats to self-administer quantities of ethanol that produce moderate to high blood-alcohol content. Different protocols are used for rats that are genetically heterogeneous versus rats that are selectively bred for high alcohol preference. Also, these protocols have different sets of advantages and disadvantages in terms of the ability to control for caloric intake and taste of solutions in operant testing. Basic self-administration protocols can also be altered to focus on different aspects of the motivational properties of ethanol (for example, those related to dependence). This unit provides multiple protocols that lead to alcohol intake in rats, which can be pharmacologically probed relative to a variety of control conditions.

title: Acquiring local field potential information from amperometric neurochemical recordings.
abstract: Simultaneous acquisition of in vivo electrophysiological and neurochemical information is essential for understanding how endogenous neurochemicals modulate the dynamics of brain activity. However, up to now such a task has rarely been accomplished due to the major technical challenge of operating two independent recording systems simultaneously in real-time. Here we propose a simpler solution for achieving this goal by using only a standard electrochemical technique--amperometry. To demonstrate its feasibility, we compared amperometric signals with simultaneously recorded local field potential (LFP) signals. We found that the high frequency component (HFC) of the amperometric signals did not reflect neurochemical fluctuations, but instead it resembled LFPs in several aspects, including: (1) coherent spectral fluctuations; (2) clear characterization of different brain states; (3) identical hippocampal theta depth profile. As such, our findings provide the first demonstration that both LFP and local neurochemical information can be simultaneously acquired from electrochemical sensors alone.

title: Love and attachment: the psychobiology of social bonding.
abstract: Social bonding represents a fundamental aspect of human behavior with profound implications for mental health and wellbeing. While the neurobiological mechanisms underlying pair bonding have been extensively studied in prairie voles, the translational relevance to human attachment remains unclear. This review synthesizes current evidence examining the psychobiology of love and attachment across species, with particular emphasis on oxytocin and vasopressin signaling pathways. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of neuroimaging, pharmacological, and genetic studies published between 1990-2008, focusing on the neural circuitry and neurochemical systems mediating social bonding behaviors. Converging evidence indicates that the mesolimbic dopamine system, in concert with oxytocinergic and vasopressinergic neurotransmission, mediates the formation and maintenance of pair bonds. Functional neuroimaging studies in humans reveal activation of reward-related brain regions, including the ventral tegmental area and nucleus accumbens, during early romantic love. Genetic polymorphisms in oxytocin and vasopressin receptor genes correlate with individual differences in attachment styles and relationship quality. Additionally, intranasal oxytocin administration enhances trust, empathy, and social recognition in healthy individuals. These findings suggest that social bonding utilizes evolutionarily conserved neural mechanisms originally involved in maternal behavior and social recognition. Understanding the neurobiological substrates of attachment has important implications for treating psychiatric disorders characterized by social dysfunction, including autism spectrum disorders, social anxiety, and borderline personality disorder. Future therapeutic interventions targeting the oxytocin system may prove beneficial for enhancing social functioning in clinical populations.

title: Pharmacotherapies for alcoholism: the old and the new.
abstract: Alcoholism remains a significant public health burden with limited pharmacological treatment options and suboptimal clinical outcomes. While traditional therapies including disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate have shown modest efficacy, novel therapeutic targets are urgently needed. This comprehensive review examines established pharmacotherapies alongside emerging treatment approaches based on recent advances in understanding alcohol addiction neurobiology. We conducted a systematic analysis of clinical trial data from 1990-2009, encompassing 127 randomized controlled trials involving 18,456 patients with alcohol use disorders. Traditional medications demonstrated variable efficacy: naltrexone reduced heavy drinking days by 17-25%, acamprosate increased abstinence rates by 13-18%, while disulfiram showed benefits primarily in supervised settings. Emerging pharmacotherapies targeting glutamatergic, GABAergic, and dopaminergic systems show promise. Topiramate demonstrated significant reductions in drinking frequency and quantity, while baclofen showed efficacy in maintaining abstinence, particularly in patients with hepatic complications. Novel approaches targeting the endocannabinoid system, neuropeptide Y, and corticotropin-releasing factor pathways are in early development stages. Pharmacogenomic studies revealed potential biomarkers for treatment response, including OPRM1 polymorphisms affecting naltrexone efficacy and ALDH2 variants influencing disulfiram response. Despite advances, effect sizes remain moderate, with most medications showing 15-30% improvement over placebo. Combination therapies and personalized medicine approaches based on genetic profiling and neurobiological phenotyping represent promising future directions. Integration of pharmacotherapy with behavioral interventions continues to optimize treatment outcomes in alcohol use disorders.

title: Psychopharmacological profile of the alkaloid psychollatine as a 5HT2A/C serotonin modulator.
abstract: The alkaloid psychollatine, isolated from the leaves of *Psychotria collantha* (Rubiaceae), has been traditionally used by indigenous populations of the Colombian Amazon for its psychoactive properties. Previous phytochemical studies identified this indole alkaloid as a major constituent, yet its neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly characterized. The present study investigated the psychopharmacological profile of psychollatine with particular emphasis on serotonergic receptor interactions. Psychollatine was isolated using standard chromatographic techniques and its purity confirmed by NMR and mass spectrometry. Radioligand binding assays were performed using [³H]ketanserin and [³H]mesulergine to determine affinity at 5-HT₂A and 5-HT₂C receptors in rat cortical and choroid plexus membranes, respectively. Functional activity was assessed using phosphoinositide hydrolysis assays in transfected HEK-293 cells expressing human 5-HT₂A or 5-HT₂C receptors. Behavioral studies employed the head-twitch response paradigm in mice following intraperitoneal administration (0.1-10 mg/kg). Psychollatine demonstrated high affinity for 5-HT₂A receptors (Ki = 47 ± 8 nM) and moderate affinity for 5-HT₂C receptors (Ki = 185 ± 23 nM). Functional assays revealed partial agonist activity at both receptor subtypes, with maximal efficacies of 65% and 42% relative to serotonin at 5-HT₂A and 5-HT₂C receptors, respectively. Psychollatine induced dose-dependent head-twitch responses (ED₅₀ = 2.3 mg/kg), which were blocked by the selective 5-HT₂A antagonist MDL-100907. These findings establish psychollatine as a novel 5-HT₂A/C receptor partial agonist with predominant 5-HT₂A selectivity, providing neurochemical validation for its traditional psychoactive use

title: Pharmacological enhancement of memory and executive functioning in laboratory animals.
abstract: Investigating how different pharmacological compounds may enhance learning, memory, and higher-order cognitive functions in laboratory animals is the first critical step toward the development of cognitive enhancers that may be used to ameliorate impairments in these functions in patients suffering from neuropsychiatric disorders. Rather than focus on one aspect of cognition, or class of drug, in this review we provide a broad overview of how distinct classes of pharmacological compounds may enhance different types of memory and executive functioning, particularly those mediated by the prefrontal cortex. These include recognition memory, attention, working memory, and different components of behavioral flexibility. A key emphasis is placed on comparing and contrasting the effects of certain drugs on different cognitive and mnemonic functions, highlighting methodological issues associated with this type of research, tasks used to investigate these functions, and avenues for future research. Viewed collectively, studies of the neuropharmacological basis of cognition in rodents and non-human primates have identified targets that will hopefully open new avenues for the treatment of cognitive disabilities in persons affected by mental disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Cocaine increases stimulated dopamine release more in periadolescent than adult rats.
abstract: The neural mechanisms responsible for the enhanced adolescent vulnerability for initiating drug abuse are unclear. We investigated whether age differences in dopamine neurotransmission could explain cocaine's enhanced psychomotor effects in the periadolescent rat. Electrical stimulation of the medial forebrain bundle of anesthetized post-natal age 28 days (PN28) and PN65 rats elicited dopamine release in caudate nucleus and nucleus accumbens core before and after 15 mg/kg cocaine i.p. Extracellular dopamine concentrations were greater in PN65 than PN28 caudate following 20 and 60 Hz stimulations and in the PN65 nucleus accumbens following 60 Hz stimulations. Cocaine increased dopamine concentrations elicited by 20 Hz stimulations 3-fold in the adult, but almost 9-fold in periadolescent caudate. Dopamine release rate was lower in the periadolescent caudate although total dopamine clearance was similar to that of adults. The periadolescent caudate achieved adult levels of clearance by compensating for a lower V(max) with higher uptake affinity. Tighter regulation of extracellular dopamine by the higher uptake/release ratio in periadolescents led to greater increases after cocaine. In nucleus accumbens, dopamine release and V(max) were lower in periadolescents than adults, but uptake affinity and cocaine effects were similar. Immaturity of dopamine neurotransmission in dorsal striatum may underlie enhanced acute responses to psychostimulants in adolescent rats and suggests a mechanism for the greater vulnerability of adolescent humans to drug addiction.

title: Neuropharmacology of cognition and memory: a unifying theory of neuromodulator imbalance in psychiatry and amnesia.
abstract: Cognitive dysfunction and memory impairments represent core features across diverse psychiatric disorders and amnestic syndromes, yet the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. Current fragmented approaches focusing on individual neurotransmitter systems have failed to provide a comprehensive framework explaining the overlapping symptomatology observed in conditions ranging from schizophrenia and depression to Alzheimer's disease and temporal lobe amnesia. We propose a unifying hypothesis based on neuromodulator imbalance theory, wherein disrupted ratios between acetylcholine, dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin create distinct cognitive phenotypes through their differential effects on hippocampal-cortical circuits. This theoretical framework synthesizes existing literature demonstrating that memory consolidation requires optimal cholinergic-dopaminergic balance in the hippocampus, while executive attention depends on noradrenergic-serotonergic equilibrium in prefrontal regions. We postulate that psychiatric disorders represent specific neuromodulator imbalance patterns: excessive dopamine with deficient acetylcholine in psychosis, reduced norepinephrine with elevated serotonin in depression, and global cholinergic decline with compensatory monoaminergic hyperactivation in dementia. This model predicts that therapeutic interventions should target neuromodulator ratios rather than individual systems, explaining why combination therapies often prove more effective than monotherapy. Furthermore, the theory suggests that cognitive enhancement strategies should focus on restoring optimal neuromodulator balance within hippocampal-cortical networks. If validated, this framework could revolutionize psychiatric pharmacotherapy by shifting focus from symptom-based treatment to mechanism-based neuromodulator rebalancing, potentially improving both cognitive outcomes and quality of life across multiple neuropsychiatric conditions.

title: The future of acute care and prevention in headache.
abstract: Headache disorders represent a major public health burden, with migraine and tension-type headache affecting over 40% of the global population. Current therapeutic approaches often rely on non-specific analgesics and prophylactic agents with limited efficacy and significant adverse effects. This review examines emerging pharmacological strategies and preventive interventions that may revolutionize headache management in the coming decade. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent clinical trials and preclinical studies investigating novel therapeutic targets, including calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) antagonists, 5-HT1F receptor agonists, and neuronal nitric oxide synthase inhibitors. Additionally, we evaluated the potential of personalized medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenomic profiling and biomarker identification. Current evidence suggests that CGRP receptor antagonists demonstrate superior efficacy compared to traditional triptans in acute migraine treatment, with reduced cardiovascular contraindications. Lasmiditan, a selective 5-HT1F receptor agonist, shows promise for patients with coronary artery disease who cannot use conventional vasoconstrictive agents. Preventive strategies incorporating continuous monitoring devices and predictive algorithms may enable pre-emptive treatment initiation before headache onset. Pharmacogenomic testing for CYP2D6 polymorphisms could optimize prophylactic medication selection and dosing. The integration of targeted molecular therapies, personalized treatment algorithms, and real-time monitoring systems represents a paradigm shift toward precision medicine in headache care. These advances promise to improve treatment outcomes while minimizing adverse effects, ultimately reducing the substantial socioeconomic impact of headache disorders.
journal: Neurological sciences : official journal of the Italian Neurological Society and of the Italian Society of Clinical Neurophysiology

title: Advances in paediatric neuropsychopharmacology: an overview.
abstract: The field of paediatric neuropsychopharmacology has experienced significant growth over the past decade, yet substantial gaps remain in our understanding of psychotropic medication effects in developing populations. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic considerations unique to children and adolescents, examining recent advances in treatment of major psychiatric disorders including attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, depression, anxiety disorders, and early-onset psychosis. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases (1990-2001), focusing on controlled clinical trials, pharmacokinetic studies, and safety reports in patients under 18 years of age. Particular attention was given to developmental neurobiology considerations and age-related differences in drug metabolism. Recent findings demonstrate that children exhibit distinct pharmacokinetic profiles compared to adults, with generally faster hepatic metabolism and altered distribution patterns affecting most psychotropic agents. Controlled trials have established efficacy for stimulants in ADHD, though long-term growth effects require continued monitoring. Emerging evidence supports selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor use in paediatric anxiety and obsessive-compulsive disorder, while antidepressant efficacy in childhood depression remains controversial. Atypical antipsychotics show promise for early-onset schizophrenia but raise concerns regarding metabolic side effects. Critical knowledge gaps persist regarding optimal dosing strategies, long-term safety profiles, and effects on normal brain development. Future research priorities must include systematic age-stratified pharmacokinetic studies, longer-term safety monitoring, and investigation of neurodevelopmental impacts to ensure safe and effective psychopharmacological interventions in this vulnerable population.

title: A Focused Review on the Treatment of Pediatric Patients with Atypical Antipsychotics.
abstract: The use of atypical antipsychotic medications in pediatric patients has become more prevalent in recent years. The purpose of this review is to provide a clinically relevant update of recent selected key publications regarding the use of atypical antipsychotics in this population. Studies reviewed included randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled medication trials conducted within the past 5 years. A PubMed search was conducted for each of the 11 second-generation antipsychotic medications currently approved by the Food and Drug Administration for use in the United States: clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, ziprasidone, paliperidone, asenapine, iloperidone, lurasidone, and cariprazine. Trials published in English with subjects 18 years of age and younger were included in this review. Additional studies, chosen for their significance to clinical practice, were also included at the discretion of the authors. This review demonstrates that more empiric data are available regarding both the acute efficacy and, to a lesser extent, the longer-term efficacy and tolerability for several of the considered antipsychotic medications. The clinical conditions for which these medications have been studied include schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, Tourette's disorder, and autism spectrum disorder. They have also been used as an adjunctive treatment for disruptive behavior disorders with aggression, which have not responded to treatment with stimulants. Evidence regarding the efficacy and tolerability of antipsychotic medications for mental health disorders in children and adolescents has expanded exponentially in recent years. However, more information is needed so that evidence-based comparisons between medications can be made. In the future, data enabling the selection of medications based upon individual patient characteristics could potentially lead to greater efficacy and efficiency in treating what are frequently debilitating medical conditions. Maladaptive aggression in children, often treated with antipsychotics, is one such area in which there is a dearth of actual information available to the clinician. It is to be hoped that additional, longer-term studies of these medications will further inform evidence-based practice in clinical settings.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychopharmacology

title: Synthesis and pharmacological evaluation of 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines.
abstract: The novel 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines 6a-c and their rigid congeners 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydro-chromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines 7a,b were synthesized in order to obtain dopamine D2-like receptor ligands. The new compounds were evaluated for their in vitro binding affinities, in vivo behavioral activities on rats, and for their effects on rat brain neurochemistry. Compounds 6b (toward both D2 and D3 dopamine receptors) and 7a,b (toward D3 only dopamine receptors) showed the most significant affinities. However none of the new compounds was able to stimulate behavioral activity in non pre-treated rats, nor to influence brain neurochemistry.

title: Effects of soman (pinacolyl methylphosphonofluoridate) on coronary blood flow and cardiac function in swine.
abstract: The effects of soman (pinacolyl methylphosphonofluoridate) on coronary blood flow, the electrocardiogram, and cardiac function were measured in alpha-chloralose-anesthetized swine. Coronary blood flow (CBF), mean arterial blood pressure (MAP), peak systolic left ventricular pressure (IVP), maximum rate of left ventricular pressure development (dP/dtmax), cardiac output, and the ECG were monitored continuously. A dose of 2X LD50 of soman (1 LD50 = 4.6 micrograms/kg) was given at 1 LD50/min in the femoral vein, which produced an increase in coronary sinus plasma acetylcholine (ACh) from a control of 0.7 +/- 0.01 nmol/ml to a maximum 314% of control at 15 min and a decrease in CBF from a control of 99 +/- 13 ml/min/100 g to a minimum 55% of control at 15 min. The increase in ACh in the coronary sinus was significantly correlated with a decrease in CBF (r = -0.87, p < 0.001). The fall in CBF was accompanied by concomitant decreases in IVP, MAP, and dP/dtmax, with S-T segment elevation and ventricular fibrillation. The increase in coronary sinus acetylcholine concentration was significantly correlated with a 10-fold fall in coronary sinus acetylcholinesterase levels from a control of 2.47 +/- 0.97 mol acetylcholine hydrolyzed/ml blood/min and was consistent with the time course for the reduced hemodynamic measurements. These studies support the hypothesis that acetylcholine increases following soman toxicity may decrease coronary blood flow, thereby initiating ischemic electrocardiographic changes and reducing cardiac function.
journal: Fundamental and applied toxicology : official journal of the Society of Toxicology

title: Pathological laughing and crying : epidemiology, pathophysiology and treatment.
abstract: Pathological laughing and crying (PLC) is characterized by frequent, brief, intense paroxysms of uncontrollable crying and/or laughing due to a neurological disorder. When sufficiently frequent and severe, PLC may interfere with the performance of activities of daily living, interpersonal functioning, or both, and is a source of distress for affected patients and their families. PLC is also often misunderstood by patients and their families, and is under-recognized by the clinicians caring for patients with this disorder. However, this syndrome is easily recognized when understood properly and is highly responsive to treatment with a variety of pharmacological agents. This review aims to facilitate the diagnosis and treatment of patients with PLC, and begins by providing definitions of mood and affect that will help clinicians distinguish between mood disorders, such as major depression and mania, and disorders of affect, such as PLC. In addition, the various terms used to describe this syndrome are reviewed and a recommendation for the use of the term PLC is made. The core clinical features of PLC are also presented and the epidemiology of this syndrome is reviewed. A discussion of the pathophysiology of PLC, including the neuroanatomic and neurochemical bases, is provided. Finally, the evaluation and treatment of patients with PLC is described. Based on the pathophysiology of PLC and on a detailed review of published treatment studies, SSRIs are recommended as first-line pharmacotherapy for this disorder. When SSRIs are ineffective or poorly tolerated, other treatment options, including TCAs, noradrenergic reuptake inhibitors, novel antidepressants, dopaminergic agents and uncompetitive NMDA receptor antagonists may be useful second-line treatments.

title: Clinical practice with anti-dementia drugs: A revised (third) consensus statement from the British Association for Psychopharmacology.
abstract: The British Association for Psychopharmacology coordinated a meeting of experts to review and revise its previous 2011 guidelines for clinical practice with anti-dementia drugs. As before, levels of evidence were rated using accepted standards which were then translated into grades of recommendation A-D, with A having the strongest evidence base (from randomised controlled trials) and D the weakest (case studies or expert opinion). Current clinical diagnostic criteria for dementia have sufficient accuracy to be applied in clinical practice (B) and both structural (computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging) and functional (positron emission tomography and single photon emission computerised tomography) brain imaging can improve diagnostic accuracy in particular situations (B). Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, and galantamine) are effective for cognition in mild to moderate Alzheimer's disease (A), memantine for moderate to severe Alzheimer's disease (A) and combination therapy (cholinesterase inhibitors and memantine) may be beneficial (B). Drugs should not be stopped just because dementia severity increases (A). Until further evidence is available other drugs, including statins, anti-inflammatory drugs, vitamin E, nutritional supplements and Ginkgo biloba, cannot be recommended either for the treatment or prevention of Alzheimer's disease (A). Neither cholinesterase inhibitors nor memantine are effective in those with mild cognitive impairment (A). Cholinesterase inhibitors are not effective in frontotemporal dementia and may cause agitation (A), though selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors may help behavioural (but not cognitive) features (B). Cholinesterase inhibitors should be used for the treatment of people with Lewy body dementias (both Parkinson's disease dementia and dementia with Lewy bodies), and memantine may be helpful (A). No drugs are clearly effective in vascular dementia, though cholinesterase inhibitors are beneficial in mixed dementia (B). Early evidence suggests multifactorial interventions may have potential to prevent or delay the onset of dementia (B). Though the consensus statement focuses on medication, psychological interventions can be effective in addition to pharmacotherapy, both for cognitive and non-cognitive symptoms. Many novel pharmacological approaches involving strategies to reduce amyloid and/or tau deposition in those with or at high risk of Alzheimer's disease are in progress. Though results of pivotal studies in early (prodromal/mild) Alzheimer's disease are awaited, results to date in more established (mild to moderate) Alzheimer's disease have been equivocal and no disease modifying agents are either licensed or can be currently recommended for clinical use.

title: Electrophysiological and pharmacological validation of vagal afferent fiber type of neurons enzymatically isolated from rat nodose ganglia.
abstract: An unavoidable consequence of enzymatic dispersion of sensory neurons from intact ganglia is loss of the axon and thus the ability to classify afferent fiber type based upon conduction velocity (CV). An intact rat nodose ganglion preparation was used to randomly sample neurons (n=76) using the patch clamp technique. Reliable electrophysiological and chemophysiological correlates of afferent fiber type were established for use with isolated neuron preparations. Myelinated afferents (approximately 25%) formed two groups exhibiting strikingly different functional profiles. One group (n=10) exhibited CVs in excess of 10 m/s and narrow (<1 ms) action potentials (APs) while the other (n=9) had CVs as low as 4m/s and broad (>2 ms) APs that closely approximated those identified as unmyelinated afferents (n=57) with CVs less than 1m/s. A cluster analysis of select measures from the AP waveforms strongly correlated with CV, producing three statistically unique populations (p<0.05). These groupings aligned with our earlier hypothesis (Jin et al., 2004) that a differential sensitivity to the selective purinergic and vanilloid receptor agonists can be used as reliable pharmacological indicators of vagal afferent fiber type. These metrics were further validated using an even larger population of isolated (n=240) nodose neurons. Collectively, these indicators of afferent fiber type can be used to provide valuable insight concerning the relavence of isolated cellular observations to integrated afferent function of visceral organ systems.

title: William L. Woolverton: a case history in unraveling the behavioral pharmacology of stimulants.
abstract: The behavioral pharmacology of psychomotor stimulants has been fundamentally shaped by the pioneering work of William L. Woolverton over three decades of research. This retrospective analysis examines Woolverton's contributions to understanding stimulant mechanisms through systematic application of operant conditioning paradigms and drug discrimination procedures. We reviewed 127 peer-reviewed publications (1982-2013) from Woolverton's laboratory, categorizing methodological approaches and key findings that advanced stimulant pharmacology. His early work established the predictive validity of rhesus monkey self-administration models for assessing abuse liability of novel stimulants, demonstrating that compounds sharing dopamine transporter binding properties with cocaine maintained robust self-administration behavior. Subsequent studies revealed critical distinctions between dopamine and norepinephrine transporter selectivity in determining reinforcing efficacy. Woolverton's drug discrimination studies provided the first systematic characterization of stimulant interoceptive effects, establishing cocaine, d-amphetamine, and methylphenidate as prototypical training drugs with distinct subjective profiles. His laboratory demonstrated that DAT/NET selectivity ratios could predict both discriminative stimulus effects and abuse potential. Later investigations examined environmental and pharmacological factors modulating stimulant self-administration, including alternative reinforcer availability and pretreatment with monoamine uptake inhibitors. These findings directly informed medication development strategies for cocaine use disorder. Woolverton's systematic approach exemplifies how rigorous behavioral pharmacology can elucidate drug mechanisms while maintaining translational relevance. His work established fundamental principles linking neurochemical selectivity to behavioral outcomes that continue to guide contemporary stimulant research and therapeutic development efforts.

title: New treatment strategies in multiple sclerosis.
abstract: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system affecting over 2.5 million people worldwide. Current therapies primarily target inflammatory processes but provide limited neuroprotection and functional recovery. This study investigated novel therapeutic approaches combining immunomodulation with neuroprotective agents in experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE), the principal animal model of MS. Female C57BL/6 mice were immunized with myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein peptide 35-55 and treated with either conventional interferon-β, a combination of low-dose interferon-β plus the neuroprotective agent erythropoietin (EPO), or EPO alone. Clinical scores, histopathological analysis, and magnetic resonance imaging were performed over 30 days post-immunization. Flow cytometry assessed peripheral immune cell populations, while Western blot analysis evaluated myelin proteins and neurotrophin expression in spinal cord tissue. Combined interferon-β/EPO treatment significantly reduced clinical severity compared to interferon-β monotherapy (mean maximum clinical score 1.8 ± 0.4 vs 3.2 ± 0.6, p<0.01). This combination preserved myelin basic protein levels, increased brain-derived neurotrophic factor expression by 2.3-fold, and promoted oligodendrocyte survival. EPO monotherapy showed modest clinical benefit but enhanced remyelination markers. The combination therapy reduced CD4+ T cell infiltration while maintaining regulatory T cell populations in the CNS. These findings suggest that combining traditional immunomodulatory approaches with neuroprotective agents represents a promising strategy for MS treatment, potentially addressing both inflammatory and degenerative disease components simultaneously.

title: Validation of a fluorescence-based high-throughput assay for the measurement of neurotransmitter transporter uptake activity.
abstract: Current methods for measuring neurotransmitter transporter uptake activity rely primarily on radioactive substrates, which present safety concerns and disposal challenges while limiting throughput capacity. We developed and validated a fluorescence-based assay for high-throughput measurement of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine transporter activity using the fluorescent substrate 4-(4-(dimethylamino)styryl)-N-methylpyridinium (ASP+). HEK293 cells stably expressing human DAT, SERT, or NET were cultured in 96-well plates and incubated with ASP+ in the presence or absence of specific transporter inhibitors. Fluorescence accumulation was measured using a microplate reader with excitation at 475 nm and emission at 605 nm. The assay demonstrated excellent correlation with traditional [³H]-substrate uptake methods (r² > 0.92 for all three transporters). IC₅₀ values for cocaine, GBR12909, paroxetine, and desipramine obtained using the fluorescence assay were within 2-fold of published radioligand binding values. The assay exhibited high signal-to-background ratios (>10-fold), low inter- and intra-assay variability (CV < 15%), and Z'-factors consistently above 0.6, indicating excellent assay quality for screening applications. Linear uptake kinetics were observed for 30 minutes at 37°C, with Km values of 8.2 ± 1.4 μM, 12.1 ± 2.3 μM, and 9.7 ± 1.8 μM for DAT, SERT, and NET, respectively. This fluorescence-based method provides a safer, more cost-effective alternative to radioactive approaches while enabling high-throughput screening of neurotransmitter transporter modulators for drug discovery applications.

title: The engagement of brain cytochrome P450 in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive substrates: a possible role in mental disorders.
abstract: The cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzyme system, traditionally studied for xenobiotic metabolism, has emerged as a critical mediator of endogenous neuroactive compound biotransformation within the central nervous system. While peripheral CYP enzymes are well-characterized, the functional significance of brain-expressed CYP isoforms in neuropsychiatric pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding brain CYP-mediated metabolism of endogenous substrates including neurosteroids, arachidonic acid derivatives, and monoamine neurotransmitters, with emphasis on their potential involvement in mental disorders. Literature analysis reveals that CYP2D6, CYP2E1, and CYP46A1 are prominently expressed in neurons and glial cells across limbic and cortical regions implicated in mood and cognitive disorders. These enzymes metabolize key neuromodulators: CYP46A1 converts cholesterol to 24S-hydroxycholesterol affecting synaptic plasticity, CYP2D6 participates in dopamine and serotonin catabolism, and CYP2E1 generates reactive oxygen species influencing neuroinflammation. Dysregulation of brain CYP activity correlates with altered neurosteroid profiles in depression, modified eicosanoid signaling in schizophrenia, and disrupted cholesterol homeostasis in Alzheimer's disease. Genetic polymorphisms in brain-expressed CYP genes associate with differential treatment responses and disease susceptibility across psychiatric populations. These findings suggest brain CYP enzymes function as endogenous neuromodulatory switches, with their dysregulation contributing to neurochemical imbalances characteristic of mental disorders. Further investigation of brain CYP-substrate interactions may reveal novel therapeutic targets and biomarkers for precision psychiatry approaches.

title: A novel method for monitoring the cell surface expression of heteromeric protein complexes in dispersed neurons and acute hippocampal slices.
abstract: The subunit composition of multimeric protein complexes is critical in determining their trafficking and functional properties. Despite there being multiple techniques to investigate the trafficking events of individual subunits there are currently limited means to monitor the trafficking properties of heteromeric protein complexes. Here, we combine surface biotinylation with co-immunoprecipitation to monitor the cell surface expression of native, heteromeric AMPA receptor complexes. Using this method, we demonstrate that the surface levels of GluR1/2 and GluR2/3 complexes are reduced following NMDA-evoked long-term depression (NMDA-LTD) in acute hippocampal slices. Finally, we discuss how this method can be adapted to monitor the cell surface expression of other heteromeric protein complexes.

title: Applications of fMRI in translational medicine and clinical practice.
abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has emerged as a pivotal neuroimaging technique with significant potential for translational medicine and clinical applications. While traditionally confined to basic neuroscience research, recent technological advances and methodological refinements have positioned fMRI as a valuable tool for understanding disease mechanisms, evaluating therapeutic interventions, and informing clinical decision-making. This review examines current applications of fMRI across multiple clinical domains, including psychiatry, neurology, and neurosurgery. We analyzed 127 clinical studies published between 2000-2006 that utilized task-based and resting-state fMRI paradigms to investigate brain function in patient populations. Our analysis reveals substantial progress in several key areas: presurgical mapping for tumor resection and epilepsy surgery, with sensitivity rates exceeding 85% for language and motor localization; pharmacological studies demonstrating drug-induced neural changes in depression, schizophrenia, and Alzheimer's disease; and diagnostic applications showing promise for early detection of cognitive decline and psychiatric disorders. Resting-state fMRI has proven particularly valuable, revealing altered default mode networks in conditions ranging from autism to chronic pain. However, significant challenges remain, including standardization of acquisition protocols, validation of clinical biomarkers, and integration with existing diagnostic frameworks. Despite these limitations, fMRI's non-invasive nature and ability to probe neural circuits in vivo position it as an increasingly important bridge between basic neuroscience discoveries and clinical practice, with potential to revolutionize personalized medicine approaches in neurological and psychiatric care.

title: Closed-loop Neuropharmacology for Epilepsy: Distant Dream or Future Reality?
abstract: Epilepsy affects over 65 million people worldwide, with approximately 30% of patients remaining refractory to conventional antiepileptic drugs. Current pharmacological approaches rely on continuous systemic drug delivery, leading to dose-limiting side effects and suboptimal seizure control. Closed-loop neuropharmacology represents an emerging paradigm that combines real-time neural signal detection with automated, on-demand drug delivery systems. This review examines recent technological advances and clinical developments in closed-loop antiepileptic drug delivery systems. We analyzed peer-reviewed literature from 2010-2019, focusing on implantable drug delivery devices, seizure prediction algorithms, and biomarker-driven pharmacological interventions. Key developments include miniaturized intracerebroventricular pumps capable of delivering precise drug volumes within seconds of seizure detection, machine learning algorithms achieving 85-92% sensitivity in seizure prediction with false positive rates below 0.5 per hour, and novel rapid-acting antiepileptic compounds optimized for focal delivery. Preclinical studies demonstrate that closed-loop systems reduce seizure frequency by 70-85% while minimizing systemic drug exposure by up to 90% compared to oral administration. Early clinical trials in 23 patients with refractory temporal lobe epilepsy showed promising safety profiles, though long-term efficacy data remain limited. Current challenges include device miniaturization, battery longevity, biocompatibility of chronic implants, and regulatory approval pathways. While significant technical hurdles remain, convergent advances in neural engineering, pharmacokinetics, and seizure prediction suggest that closed-loop neuropharmacology may transition from experimental concept to clinical reality within the next decade, offering new hope for patients with drug-resistant epilepsy.

title: Cell death and proliferation in acute slices and organotypic cultures of mammalian CNS.
abstract: The precise characterization of cell death and proliferation mechanisms in ex vivo brain preparations remains critical for advancing our understanding of CNS pathophysiology and evaluating neuroprotective interventions. While acute brain slices and organotypic cultures are widely utilized experimental models, the temporal dynamics and molecular pathways underlying cellular responses in these preparations have not been systematically compared. We investigated cell death and proliferation patterns in acute hippocampal slices (400 μm) and organotypic slice cultures from postnatal day 7-10 rats over 72 hours using propidium iodide uptake, TUNEL staining, and BrdU incorporation assays. Caspase-3 activation, PARP cleavage, and expression of proliferation markers (Ki-67, PCTN-1) were assessed by immunofluorescence and Western blotting. Acute slices exhibited rapid cell death onset within 2-4 hours, predominantly in CA1 pyramidal neurons, with peak caspase-3 activation at 6 hours and minimal proliferative activity. In contrast, organotypic cultures showed delayed but sustained cell death over 24-48 hours, primarily affecting dentate granule cells, accompanied by robust glial proliferation and increased Ki-67 expression in the subgranular zone. NMDA receptor antagonists (MK-801, 10 μM) significantly reduced cell death in both preparations, while PI3K/Akt pathway inhibition (LY294002, 25 μM) selectively suppressed proliferation in organotypic cultures. These findings demonstrate distinct spatiotemporal profiles of cell death and proliferation between acute and organotypic preparations, suggesting different underlying mechanisms. This characterization provides essential baseline data for interpreting pharmacological interventions and highlights the importance of model selection in neuropharmacological research.

title: The engagement of brain cytochrome P450 in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive substrates: a possible role in mental disorders.
abstract: The current state of knowledge indicates that the cerebral cytochrome P450 (CYP) plays an important role in the endogenous metabolism in the brain. Different CYP isoenzymes mediate metabolism of many endogenous substrates such as monoaminergic neurotransmitters, neurosteroids, cholesterol, vitamins and arachidonic acid. Therefore, these enzymes may affect brain development, susceptibility to mental and neurodegenerative diseases and may contribute to their pathophysiology. In addition, they can modify the therapeutic effects of psychoactive drugs at the place of their target action in the brain, where the drugs can act by affecting the metabolism of endogenous substrates. The article focuses on the role of cerebral CYP isoforms in the metabolism of neurotransmitters, neurosteroids, and cholesterol, and their possible involvement in animal behavior, as well as in stress, depression, schizophrenia, cognitive processes, learning, and memory. CYP-mediated alternative pathways of dopamine and serotonin synthesis may have a significant role in the local production of these neurotransmitters in the brain regions where the disturbances of these neurotransmitter systems are observed in depression and schizophrenia. The local alternative synthesis of neurotransmitters may be of great importance in the brain, since dopamine and serotonin do not pass the blood-brain barrier and cannot be supplied from the periphery. In vitro studies indicate that human CYP2D6 catalyzing dopamine and serotonin synthesis is more efficient in these reactions than the rat CYP2D isoforms. It suggests that these alternative pathways may have much greater significance in the human brain but confirmation of these assumptions requires further studies.

title: Movement disorders and new azapirone anxiolytic drugs.
abstract: Movement disorders have emerged as a significant concern with traditional anxiolytic medications, particularly benzodiazepines, which can cause sedation, ataxia, and motor impairment in susceptible patients. The azapirones represent a novel class of anxiolytic agents that act as partial agonists at 5-HT1A receptors rather than enhancing GABA-mediated neurotransmission. This study evaluated the motor safety profile of buspirone and related azapirone compounds in patients with pre-existing movement disorders. We conducted a double-blind, placebo-controlled trial involving 48 patients with mild to moderate anxiety disorders who also presented with either essential tremor (n=18), early Parkinson's disease (n=16), or tardive dyskinesia (n=14). Subjects received either buspirone (15-30 mg daily), ipsapirone (10-20 mg daily), or placebo for 8 weeks. Motor function was assessed using standardized rating scales including the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale motor subsection and computerized tremor analysis. Anxiety levels were measured using the Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale. Both azapirone compounds significantly reduced anxiety scores compared to placebo (p<0.01) without exacerbating existing movement disorders. No patients experienced worsening of tremor, rigidity, or dyskinetic movements. Three subjects in the buspirone group reported mild dizziness, but no motor side effects were observed. These findings suggest that azapirone anxiolytics may offer a safer therapeutic option for anxiety management in patients with movement disorders, avoiding the motor complications associated with conventional benzodiazepine therapy.
journal: The Journal of the American Board of Family Practice

title: Neuropharmacology of Antiseizure Drugs.
abstract: The mechanisms of action of antiseizure drugs (ASDs) have evolved significantly over the past decade, yet our understanding of their diverse neuropharmacological profiles remains incomplete. This study aimed to comprehensively characterize the molecular targets and network effects of both traditional and novel ASDs using contemporary electrophysiological and molecular approaches. We employed whole-cell patch-clamp recordings in cultured hippocampal neurons from C57BL/6 mice to examine the effects of twelve clinically relevant ASDs on voltage-gated sodium channels (Nav1.1, Nav1.2, Nav1.6), calcium channels (Cav2.1, Cav3.2), and GABA-A receptors. Additionally, we used calcium imaging and multi-electrode arrays to assess network-level activity modulation. Molecular docking studies were performed to predict binding affinities and selectivity profiles. Traditional ASDs including phenytoin and carbamazepine demonstrated preferential inhibition of Nav1.2 channels (IC50 values of 18.7 ± 2.3 μM and 22.4 ± 3.1 μM, respectively), while newer agents lacosamide and eslicarbazepine showed enhanced selectivity for slow-inactivated sodium channels. Levetiracetam uniquely modulated synaptic vesicle protein SV2A with minimal direct ion channel effects, correlating with distinct network synchronization patterns. Perampanel exhibited dual mechanisms through AMPA receptor antagonism and indirect GABAergic enhancement. These findings reveal that modern ASDs demonstrate increasingly selective target engagement compared to traditional agents, with implications for improved therapeutic indices. The diverse mechanisms identified support rational polytherapy approaches and highlight the potential for precision medicine strategies in epilepsy treatment based on individual channel expression profiles.

title: Neurotoxicology: The Cinderella of neuroscience.
abstract: The field of neurotoxicology has historically received limited attention within mainstream neuroscience despite mounting evidence of environmental neurotoxicant exposure and its clinical significance. This review examines the current status of neurotoxicology research relative to other neuroscience disciplines through bibliometric analysis and survey data. We analyzed publication trends from 1980-1995 in major neuroscience journals using MEDLINE searches, comparing neurotoxicology articles to those in neurophysiology, neurochemistry, and behavioral neuroscience. Additionally, we surveyed 312 neuroscience researchers regarding funding priorities, career prospects, and perceived importance of neurotoxicology research. Our analysis revealed that neurotoxicology publications comprised only 3.2% of total neuroscience articles during this period, with funding allocation representing 2.8% of NIH neuroscience budgets. Survey results indicated that 73% of respondents considered neurotoxicology research "important" or "very important," yet only 18% would recommend it as a primary career path due to perceived limited opportunities and resources. Paradoxically, public health data suggest that environmental neurotoxicant exposure affects millions globally, with particular vulnerability in developing populations. The disconnect between scientific need and institutional support reflects historical biases favoring basic mechanistic research over applied toxicological studies. We conclude that neurotoxicology requires enhanced integration within neuroscience curricula, increased funding initiatives, and greater recognition of its critical role in understanding environmentally-mediated neurological disorders. Elevating neurotoxicology from its current marginalized status is essential for addressing emerging public health challenges in an increasingly industrialized world.

title: Artifact processing in topographic mapping of electroencephalographic activity in neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The application of computerized topographic mapping techniques to electroencephalographic data has become increasingly important in neuropsychopharmacological research for visualizing drug effects on brain electrical activity. However, the presence of artifacts in EEG recordings significantly compromises the validity of topographic maps and subsequent pharmacological interpretations. This study examined the efficacy of various artifact processing methods in improving the quality of EEG topographic maps used in psychopharmacological investigations. EEG data were recorded from 24 healthy volunteers during baseline conditions and following administration of diazepam (10 mg p.o.) using a 16-channel montage. Raw EEG signals were processed using three different artifact rejection algorithms: manual visual inspection, automated amplitude thresholding, and a hybrid approach combining spectral analysis with template matching for eye movement and muscle artifact detection. Topographic maps of absolute and relative power in delta, theta, alpha, and beta frequency bands were generated before and after artifact processing.
