Results demonstrated that automated hybrid processing reduced artifact contamination by 78% compared to visual inspection alone, while preserving 92% of artifact-free data. Drug-induced changes in alpha activity topography showed significantly improved signal-to-noise ratios and more consistent spatial distributions across subjects when hybrid processing was employed. The findings suggest that systematic artifact processing is essential for reliable interpretation of pharmacological effects in EEG topographic studies. Implementation of automated hybrid algorithms may enhance the sensitivity and reproducibility of neuropsychopharmacological research utilizing brain mapping techniques.

title: Evaluation of an osmotic pump for microdialysis sampling in an awake and untethered rat.
abstract: Microdialysis sampling in freely moving animals remains technically challenging due to the need for continuous perfusion while maintaining behavioral mobility. Traditional tethering systems limit natural movement patterns and may introduce stress artifacts that confound neurochemical measurements. We developed and validated an osmotic pump-based microdialysis system for continuous sampling in awake, untethered rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with guide cannulae targeting the nucleus accumbens shell. Alzet osmotic pumps (model 2ML1, 10 μL/h) containing artificial cerebrospinal fluid were subcutaneously implanted and connected via polyethylene tubing to CMA/11 microdialysis probes. Dialysate collection was achieved using miniaturized fraction collectors attached to the animals. The system was validated by measuring basal extracellular dopamine and dopamine metabolite levels over 24-hour periods and comparing responses to systemic amphetamine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) in both tethered and untethered conditions. The osmotic pump system maintained stable flow rates (9.8 ± 0.4 μL/h) throughout the experimental period. Basal dopamine levels in untethered animals (2.1 ± 0.3 nM) were significantly lower than in tethered controls (3.7 ± 0.5 nM, p<0.01), suggesting reduced stress-induced release. Amphetamine-stimulated dopamine release showed similar peak responses between conditions but more rapid recovery in untethered animals. Locomotor activity patterns were markedly more natural in untethered subjects. This osmotic pump-based approach provides a reliable method for extended microdialysis sampling in freely moving rats, reducing stress-related confounds while maintaining temporal resolution suitable for pharmacological studies.

title: Can psychopharmacological treatment change personality traits in patients with panic disorder?
abstract: **Background:** Panic disorder (PD) is frequently associated with specific personality traits, particularly neuroticism and harm avoidance, which may influence treatment outcomes and long-term prognosis. While psychopharmacological interventions effectively reduce panic symptoms, their impact on underlying personality dimensions remains poorly understood. **Objective:** To investigate whether successful pharmacological treatment of panic disorder produces measurable changes in personality traits. **Methods:** We conducted a 12-week prospective study of 84 patients diagnosed with PD according to DSM-IV criteria. Participants were randomized to receive either sertraline (n=42, 50-200mg/day) or clonazepam (n=42, 1-4mg/day). Personality traits were assessed using the NEO-PI-R and Temperament and Character Inventory (TCI) at baseline, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks. Panic severity was measured using the Panic Disorder Severity Scale (PDSS). **Results:** Both treatment groups showed significant reductions in PDSS scores (p<0.001). Patients receiving sertraline demonstrated significant decreases in neuroticism scores (baseline: 142.3±18.7 vs. 12-week: 128.6±20.1, p=0.003) and TCI harm avoidance (baseline: 22.8±5.2 vs. 12-week: 18.9±6.1, p=0.007). The clonazepam group showed modest but non-significant personality changes. Improvement in panic symptoms correlated with personality trait changes (r=0.52, p<0.01). Patients achieving clinical remission (PDSS≤7) showed greater personality trait improvements compared to partial responders. **Conclusions:** Successful antidepressant treatment of panic disorder appears to facilitate adaptive changes in personality traits, particularly reducing neuroticism and harm avoidance. These findings suggest that effective pharmacotherapy may produce benefits extending beyond symptom reduction to fundamental aspects of personality structure.
journal: Revista brasileira de psiquiatria (Sao Paulo, Brazil : 1999)

title: Quantitative assessment of the microstructure of rat behavior: I, f(d), the extension of the scaling hypothesis.
abstract: The scaling hypothesis proposes that temporal patterns in operant behavior exhibit fractal-like properties across multiple time scales, suggesting underlying neural mechanisms may operate according to power-law distributions. Previous qualitative analyses have supported this framework, but quantitative methods for characterizing behavioral microstructure remain limited. We developed a novel analytical approach, f(d), to quantify the fractal dimension of inter-response time distributions in rat operant behavior and test extensions of the scaling hypothesis. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were trained on variable-interval schedules (VI-30, VI-60, VI-120 seconds) in operant chambers. Inter-response times were recorded with millisecond precision across 2-hour sessions. The f(d) function was calculated using box-counting algorithms applied to log-log plots of inter-response time distributions across temporal scales from 100 ms to 10 minutes. Statistical analysis employed ANOVA and regression techniques to assess scaling exponents. Results demonstrated robust fractal scaling across all VI schedules, with dimension values ranging from 1.23 to 1.67. The f(d) function revealed self-similar patterns extending across four orders of magnitude in time scale. VI-30 schedules produced significantly higher fractal dimensions than VI-120 schedules (p<0.01), indicating schedule-dependent modulation of temporal organization. Within-subject variability in scaling exponents was minimal (CV<0.15), suggesting stable individual differences in behavioral microstructure. These findings provide quantitative support for the extended scaling hypothesis and establish f(d) as a sensitive measure of temporal organization in operant behavior, with potential applications for characterizing drug effects on behavioral dynamics.

title: I Believe I Can Fly!: Use of Drosophila as a Model Organism in Neuropsychopharmacology Research.
abstract: The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a powerful model organism for neuropsychopharmacological research, offering unique advantages including genetic tractability, conserved neurotransmitter systems, and well-characterized behaviors. Despite extensive use in basic neuroscience, systematic evaluation of Drosophila's utility for testing psychoactive compounds remains limited. We conducted a comprehensive assessment of behavioral and molecular responses to clinically relevant neuropsychiatric drugs in wild-type Canton-S flies and mutant strains with altered dopaminergic and serotonergic signaling. Adult flies were exposed to therapeutic concentrations of antidepressants (fluoxetine, imipramine), antipsychotics (haloperidol, risperidone), anxiolytics (diazepam), and psychostimulants (cocaine, amphetamine) via feeding protocols. Behavioral outcomes were measured using locomotor activity assays, negative geotaxis climbing tests, and novel courtship paradigms. Drug effects on neurotransmitter levels were quantified using HPLC analysis of whole-brain extracts. Pharmacological treatments produced dose-dependent behavioral changes remarkably similar to mammalian responses, including psychostimulant-induced hyperlocomotion, antipsychotic-mediated motor suppression, and anxiolytic effects on stress-related behaviors. Neurotransmitter analyses revealed drug-specific alterations in dopamine, serotonin, and GABA levels consistent with known mechanisms of action. Genetic mutants showed predictable alterations in drug sensitivity, validating target specificity. These findings demonstrate that Drosophila recapitulates key aspects of mammalian psychopharmacology, supporting its use as a cost-effective, high-throughput screening platform for neuropsychiatric drug discovery and mechanistic studies of psychoactive compounds.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Volume versus wiring transmission in the brain: a new theoretical frame for neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The traditional view of neural communication has focused primarily on point-to-point synaptic transmission, yet accumulating evidence suggests that diffuse chemical signaling plays an equally important role in brain function. We propose a comprehensive theoretical framework distinguishing between "wiring transmission" (classical synaptic communication) and "volume transmission" (extrasynaptic diffusion of neurotransmitters through brain parenchyma). This framework was developed through integration of neuroanatomical, neurochemical, and pharmacological data from multiple experimental paradigms, including microdialysis studies, electron microscopy of synaptic and non-synaptic release sites, and analysis of neurotransmitter diffusion patterns in brain tissue. Our analysis reveals that monoaminergic, cholinergic, and peptidergic systems operate predominantly through volume transmission, with neurotransmitters diffusing distances of 10-100 micrometers from release sites to reach extrasynaptic receptors. In contrast, glutamatergic and GABAergic systems rely primarily on wiring transmission for rapid, precise signaling. Pharmacokinetic modeling demonstrates that drugs affecting volume transmission systems exhibit distinct dose-response relationships and temporal dynamics compared to those targeting wiring transmission. This dual-mode framework provides new insights into psychopharmacological mechanisms, explaining why certain therapeutic agents require days to weeks for clinical efficacy and why regional brain concentrations often correlate poorly with synaptic activity. The volume/wiring transmission paradigm offers a novel conceptual basis for understanding drug action in the central nervous system and may guide development of more selective neuropsychiatric therapeutics targeting specific transmission modes.

title: [The way out from crisis--from the closing of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) to Semmelweis University].
abstract: The closure of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) in 2007 created an unprecedented crisis in Hungarian psychiatric research and clinical care, threatening decades of accumulated expertise in neuropharmacological research. This study examines the transitional process and outcomes following the integration of OPNI's psychiatric departments into Semmelweis University's medical faculty between 2007-2009. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of research productivity, patient care metrics, and institutional capacity before, during, and after the transition period. Data were collected from publication databases, clinical records, and structured interviews with 45 faculty members and researchers. Patient treatment continuity was assessed through medical records analysis of 1,247 patients with major psychiatric disorders. Results demonstrated an initial 40% decline in research output during 2007-2008, with publications dropping from 89 to 53 annually. However, by 2009, research productivity recovered to 78% of pre-crisis levels, accompanied by improved funding acquisition (€2.3M vs. €1.8M in 2006). Patient care quality indicators remained stable throughout the transition, with readmission rates showing no significant increase (p>0.05). The integration preserved critical neuropharmacological research programs, including ongoing clinical trials for novel antipsychotic compounds. Most importantly, the transition resulted in enhanced academic infrastructure and expanded international collaborations. The successful transformation from OPNI to Semmelweis University demonstrates that institutional crises, while initially disruptive, can catalyze positive structural changes that ultimately strengthen psychiatric research capacity and clinical excellence in Hungary.
journal: Psychiatria Hungarica : A Magyar Pszichiatriai Tarsasag tudomanyos folyoirata

title: An Update on the Neurochemistry of Essential Tremor.
abstract: The pathophysiology and neurochemical mechanisms of essential tremor (ET) are not fully understood, because only a few post-mortem studies have been reported, and there is a lack of good experimental model for this disease. The main aim of this review is to update data regarding the neurochemical features of ET. Alterations of certain catecholamine systems, the dopaminergic, serotonergic, GABAergic, noradrenergic, and adrenergic systems have been described, and are the object of this revision. For this purpose, we performed a literature review on alterations of the neurotransmitter or neuromodulator systems (catecholamines, gammaaminobutyric acid or GABA, excitatory amino acids, adenosine, T-type calcium channels) in ET patients (both post-mortem or in vivo) or in experimental models resembling ET. The most consistent data regarding neurochemistry of ET are related with the GABAergic and glutamatergic systems, with a lesser contribution of adenosine and dopaminergic and adrenergic systems, while there is not enough evidence of a definite role of other neurotransmitter systems in ET. The improvement of harmaline-induced tremor in rodent models achieved with T-type calcium channel antagonists, cannabinoid 1 receptor, sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor agonists, and gap-junction blockers, suggests a potential role of these structures in the pathogenesis of ET.

title: The nature of dopamine dysfunction in schizophrenia and what this means for treatment.
abstract: Current drug treatments for schizophrenia are inadequate for many patients, and despite 5 decades of drug discovery, all of the treatments rely on the same mechanism: dopamine D(2) receptor blockade. Understanding the pathophysiology of the disorder is thus likely to be critical to the rational development of new treatments for schizophrenia. To investigate the nature of the dopaminergic dysfunction in schizophrenia using meta-analysis of in vivo studies. The MEDLINE, EMBASE, and PsycINFO databases were searched for studies from January 1, 1960, to July 1, 2011. A total of 44 studies were identified that compared 618 patients with schizophrenia with 606 controls, using positron emission tomography or single-photon emission computed tomography to measure in vivo striatal dopaminergic function. Demographic, clinical, and imaging variables were extracted from each study, and effect sizes were determined for the measures of dopaminergic function. Studies were grouped into those of presynaptic function and those of dopamine transporter and receptor availability. Sensitivity analyses were conducted to explore the consistency of effects and the effect of clinical and imaging variables. There was a highly significant elevation (P.<001) in presynaptic dopaminergic function in schizophrenia with a large effect size (Cohen d=0.79). There was no evidence of alterations in dopamine transporter availability. There was a small elevation in D(2/3) receptor availability (Cohen d=0.26), but this was not evident in drug-naive patients and was influenced by the imaging approach used. The locus of the largest dopaminergic abnormality in schizophrenia is presynaptic, which affects dopamine synthesis capacity, baseline synaptic dopamine levels, and dopamine release. Current drug treatments, which primarily act at D(2/3) receptors, fail to target these abnormalities. Future drug development should focus on the control of presynaptic dopamine synthesis and release capacity.

title: Quantitative in vitro phosphor imaging using [3H] and [18F] radioligands: the effects of chronic desipramine treatment on serotonin 5-HT2 receptors.
abstract: Chronic antidepressant treatment is known to induce adaptive changes in serotonin receptor systems, yet quantitative assessment of these alterations remains technically challenging. The present study describes a novel dual-radioligand phosphor imaging approach to examine 5-HT2 receptor binding changes following chronic desipramine administration. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) received either desipramine (10 mg/kg, i.p.) or saline vehicle daily for 21 days. Brain sections (20 μm) were prepared and incubated with either [3H]ketanserin (2.5 nM) or [18F]setoperone (1.8 nM) to label 5-HT2A receptors, with parallel sections processed for 5-HT2C receptor binding using [3H]mesulergine (3.2 nM). Sections were exposed to phosphor imaging plates for quantitative autoradiography, with binding densities measured in frontal cortex, parietal cortex, caudate-putamen, and hippocampus. Both radioligands demonstrated excellent correlation (r=0.94) in control conditions, validating the dual-tracer methodology. Chronic desipramine treatment significantly reduced 5-HT2A receptor binding in frontal cortex (23% decrease, p<0.01) and parietal cortex (18% decrease, p<0.05) compared to controls, with no significant changes observed in subcortical regions. 5-HT2C receptor binding remained unchanged across all brain regions examined. Scatchard analysis revealed decreased Bmax values without alterations in KD, indicating reduced receptor density rather than altered affinity. These findings demonstrate that chronic tricyclic antidepressant treatment selectively downregulates cortical 5-HT2A receptors and establish phosphor imaging with multiple radioligands as a valuable quantitative approach for neuropharmacological investigations of receptor plasticity.

title: Chapter 5 - Development of iron chelator-nanoparticle conjugates as potential therapeutic agents for Alzheimer disease.
abstract: Oxidative stress is known to play a key role in the initiation and promotion of the neurodegeneration that characterizes the pathogenesis of Alzheimer disease (AD). An accumulation of redox active transition metals, including iron and copper, is likely a major generator of reactive oxidative species and other free radicals and is thought to induce a detrimental cycle of oxidative stress, amyloid-beta aggregation, and neurodegeneration. As such, metal chelators may provide an alternative therapeutic approach to sequester redox active metals and prevent the onslaught of oxidative damage. Unfortunately, however, metal chelation approaches are currently limited in their potential, since many cannot readily pass the blood-brain barrier (BBB), due to their hydrophilicity, and many are neurotoxic at high concentrations. To circumvent such issues, here we describe the development of iron chelator-nanoparticle conjugation that allows delivery of target chelator to the brain in the absence of neurotoxicity. Such nanoparticle delivery of iron chelators will likely provide a highly advantageous mode of attack on the oxidative stress that plagues AD as well as other conditions characterized by excess metal accumulation.

title: Pharmacological findings on the biochemical bases of memory processes: a general view.
abstract: We have advanced considerably in the past 2 to 3 years in understanding the molecular mechanisms of consolidation, retrieval, and extinction of memories, particularly of fear memory. This advance was mainly due to pharmacological studies in many laboratories using localized brain injections of molecularly specific substances. One area in which significant advances have been made is in understanding that many different brain structures are involved in different memories, and that often several brain regions are involved in processing the same memory. These regions can cooperate or compete with each other, depending on circumstances that are beginning to be identified quite clearly. Another aspect in which major advances were made was retrieval and post-retrieval events, especially extinction, pointing to new therapeutic approaches to fear-motivated mental disorders.

title: The first Insubria autumn school on neuroimmunopharmacology: challenging paradigms beyond boundaries.
abstract: This issue of the Journal of Neuroimmune Pharmacology is dedicated, in part, to the proceedings of the First Insubria Autumn School on Neuroimmunopharmacology, organized by Marco Cosentino, Georges J.M. Maestroni, Franca Marino (University of Insubria, Varese, Italy), Manfred Schedlowsky (University of Duisburg-Essen, Germany) and Rainer H. Straub (University of Regensburg, Germany), which took place in November 2011 in Varese, Italy. The School was attended by 50 graduate students and postdoctoral fellows in medical and biological disciplines from 16 european universities and 25 faculties/departments.
journal: Journal of neuroimmune pharmacology : the official journal of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology

title: The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
abstract: The review presents evidence that 5-HT3 receptors within the brain may contribute to the control of behavior. 5-HT3 receptor antagonists GR38032F, zacopride, ICS 205-930 and other agents are very potent in reducing mesolimbic dopamine hyperactivity caused by the injection of amphetamine or infusion of dopamine into the rat nucleus accumbens and amygdala, and the ventral striatum of the marmoset. Such actions are distinguished from those of neuroleptic agents by a failure to reduce normal levels of activity or to induce a rebound hyperactivity after discontinuation of treatment. Indeed, the 5-HT3 receptor antagonists can prevent the neuroleptic-induced rebound hyperactivity. Further evidence that 5-HT3 receptors moderate limbic dopamine function is shown by their ability to reduce both the behavioral hyperactivity and changes in limbic dopamine metabolism caused by DiMe-C7 injection into the ventral tegmental area. The 5-HT3 receptor antagonists also have an anxiolytic profile in the social interaction test in the rat, the light/dark exploration test in the mouse, the marmoset human threat test and behavioral observations in the cynomolgus monkey. They differ from the benzodiazepines by an absence of effect in the rat water lick conflict test and a withdrawal syndrome. Importantly, the 5-HT3 receptor antagonists are highly effective to prevent the behavioral syndrome following withdrawal from treatment with diazepam, nicotine, cocaine and alcohol. Intracerebral injection techniques in the mouse indicate that the dorsal raphe nucleus and amygdala may be important sites of 5-HT3 receptor antagonist action to inhibit aversive behavior. Studies with GR38032F indicate an additional effect in reducing alcohol consumption in the marmoset. The identification and distribution of 5-HT3 receptors in the brain using a number of 5-HT3 receptor ligands, [3H]65630, [3H]zacopride and [3H]ICS 205-930 correlates between studies, and the 5-HT3 recognition sites in cortical, limbic and other areas meet the criteria for 5-HT3 receptors to mediate the above behavioral effects. Thus the use of 5-HT3 receptor antagonists reveals an important role for 5-hydroxytryptamine in the control of disturbed behavior in the absence of effect on normal behavior. The profile of action of the 5-HT3 receptor antagonists has generated a major clinical interest in their potential use for schizophrenia, anxiety and in the control of drug abuse.

title: In vivo monitoring of extracellular glutamate in the brain with a microsensor.
abstract: Glutamate is the primary excitatory neurotransmitter in the mammalian central nervous system, and alterations in extracellular glutamate concentrations are implicated in various neurological disorders including stroke, epilepsy, and neurodegenerative diseases. Traditional methods for measuring extracellular glutamate, such as microdialysis, suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage. We developed and characterized a novel glutamate microsensor based on glutamate oxidase immobilized on a platinum microelectrode for real-time monitoring of extracellular glutamate in the living brain. The microsensor exhibited high sensitivity (detection limit 0.5 μM), selectivity for glutamate over other amino acids (>95%), and rapid response time (<2 seconds). In anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8), we implanted the microsensor into the hippocampal CA1 region and monitored basal glutamate levels and stimulus-evoked responses. Basal extracellular glutamate concentrations were 2.3 ± 0.4 μM, consistent with previous microdialysis studies. High-frequency electrical stimulation of the Schaffer collaterals produced rapid, transient increases in glutamate concentration (peak amplitude 8.2 ± 1.1 μM, duration 12.4 ± 2.1 seconds). Administration of the glutamate uptake inhibitor PDC (1 mM) via reverse microdialysis resulted in a 3-fold increase in both basal and evoked glutamate responses. The microsensor remained stable for recordings lasting up to 6 hours with minimal tissue reaction. These results demonstrate that glutamate microsensors provide a valuable tool for investigating glutamatergic neurotransmission with high spatial and temporal resolution in vivo, offering new opportunities for understanding glutamate dynamics in physiological and pathological conditions.

title: 5-HT in the enteric nervous system: gut function and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT) serves as a critical neurotransmitter and signaling molecule within the enteric nervous system (ENS), where it regulates diverse gastrointestinal functions including motility, secretion, and visceral sensation. The gut contains approximately 95% of the body's total 5-HT, produced primarily by enterochromaffin cells in the intestinal mucosa and enteric neurons. We review current understanding of 5-HT signaling pathways in the ENS and their pharmacological modulation. Multiple 5-HT receptor subtypes (5-HT1P, 5-HT3, 5-HT4, and 5-HT7) are expressed throughout enteric neural circuits, mediating both excitatory and inhibitory responses. 5-HT3 receptors on intrinsic primary afferent neurons initiate peristaltic and secretory reflexes, while 5-HT4 receptors facilitate acetylcholine release from motor neurons, promoting propulsive motility. The 5-HT reuptake transporter (SERT) terminates synaptic 5-HT signaling and represents a key regulatory mechanism. Dysfunction of enteric 5-HT systems contributes to gastrointestinal disorders including irritable bowel syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease, and gastroparesis. Therapeutic agents targeting enteric 5-HT signaling show considerable promise: 5-HT4 receptor agonists enhance gastric emptying and colonic transit, while 5-HT3 receptor antagonists reduce visceral hypersensitivity and diarrhea. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors can modulate gut function independently of their central nervous system effects. Understanding the complex pharmacology of enteric 5-HT systems provides new opportunities for developing targeted therapies for functional and inflammatory gastrointestinal diseases, potentially offering improved efficacy and reduced systemic side effects compared to conventional approaches.

title: A novel technique for simultaneous bilateral brain infusions in a mouse model of neurodegenerative disease.
abstract: A common problem faced by researchers using transgenic models to study disease is the phenotypic variability that exists within a group or colony of animals. Significant pathological analyses thus often require large numbers of mice to perform. Many lines of transgenic mice harboring the gene for human amyloid precursor protein (APP) with different mutations causing familial Alzheimer's disease have been developed over the past decade to study plaque deposition and other aspects of AD. However, variations in size, density, plaque number, and total amyloid load between animals of the same age and genotype have been identified by our lab and others. Therefore, to study the effects of compounds on amyloid pathology, it was imperative to develop a technique that would allow each brain hemisphere to receive different infusions. We have developed catheters that facilitate simultaneous bilateral infusion in mouse brains, thereby using the contralateral hemisphere of the same animal as an internal control while studying, for example, the effect of compounds on amyloid plaques, a pathological hallmark of the progression of Alzheimer's disease (AD). Several molecules have been identified within the plaques including the major component, the Abeta peptide, and two inflammation-related proteins, apolipoprotein E (apoE) and the serine protease inhibitor alpha-1-antichymotrypsin (ACT). In these experiments, ACT was infused unilaterally over a period of 28 days into the parenchyma and lateral ventricles of PS/APP mice and observed to associate with amyloid plaques, with minimal mortality. Utilizing the ACT/Abeta interaction, details of this procedure are discussed here in detail.

title: Automated discrimination of psychotropic drugs in mice via computer vision-based analysis.
abstract: We developed an inexpensive computer vision-based method utilizing an algorithm which differentiates drug-induced behavioral alterations. The mice were observed in an open-field arena and their activity was recorded for 100 min. For each animal the first 50 min of observation were regarded as the drug-free period. Each animal was exposed to only one drug and they were injected (i.p.) with either amphetamine or cocaine as the stimulant drugs or morphine or diazepam as the inhibitory agents. The software divided the arena into virtual grids and calculated the number of visits (sojourn counts) to the grids and instantaneous speeds within these grids by analyzing video data. These spatial distributions of sojourn counts and instantaneous speeds were used to construct feature vectors which were fed to the classifier algorithms for the final step of matching the animals and the drugs. The software decided which of the animals were drug-treated at a rate of 96%. The algorithm achieved 92% accuracy in sorting the data according to the increased or decreased activity and then determined which drug was delivered. The method differentiated the type of psychostimulant or inhibitory drugs with a success ratio of 70% and 80%, respectively. This method provides a new way to automatically evaluate and classify drug-induced behaviors in mice.

title: An in vivo technique for pharmacological manipulation of Drosophila brain during optical recording.
abstract: The combination of pharmacological manipulation with simultaneous optical recording in living Drosophila represents a powerful approach for dissecting neural circuit function, yet technical challenges have limited its application. Traditional methods for drug delivery to the adult fly brain require invasive procedures that disrupt normal physiology and are incompatible with high-resolution imaging. Here we describe a novel in vivo preparation that enables controlled pharmacological manipulation of the Drosophila central nervous system during concurrent optical recording of neural activity. We developed a semi-intact preparation where the head capsule is opened and the brain exposed while maintaining physiological saline perfusion and minimizing mechanical damage to neural tissue. A custom perfusion chamber allows rapid exchange of solutions containing neuroactive compounds while the preparation remains stable under a two-photon microscope. We validated this technique using genetically-encoded calcium indicators (GCaMP2.0) expressed in specific neuronal populations and tested responses to GABA, acetylcholine, dopamine, and octopamine at physiologically relevant concentrations (10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁴ M). Pharmacological responses were detected within 30-60 seconds of compound application, with full reversibility achieved within 5-10 minutes of washout. The preparation remained viable for optical recordings up to 4 hours, enabling detailed pharmacological dose-response studies. This method provides temporal and spatial resolution superior to feeding-based drug delivery while preserving the genetic advantages of the Drosophila model system. The technique should facilitate mechanistic studies of neurotransmitter signaling and screening of neuroactive compounds in an intact nervous system context.

title: Effects of soybean peptide on immune function, brain function, and neurochemistry in healthy volunteers.
abstract: Soybeans, an excellent source of dietary peptides, have beneficial effects on health. We investigated the effect of the soybean peptide on immune function, brain function, and neurochemistry in healthy volunteers. Near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) was used to analyze brain cerebral blood flow. The A and DA levels in the serum were analyzed by ELISA kit. The total number of leukocytes was recorded with a standard counter. Flow cytometry was used to assess lymphocyte subset levels. Cell numbers were upregulated in the group that had fewer leukocytes but downregulated in the group with more leukocytes. For the lymphocyte-rich type, lymphocyte counts tended to decrease, accompanied by an increase in granulocyte numbers. For the granulocyte-rich type, granulocyte counts tended to increase, but lymphocyte counts also increased. The numbers of CD11b(+) cells and CD56(+) cells increased significantly. Soybean peptide decreased the adrenalin level in plasma but increased the level of dopamine. Near-infrared spectroscopy showed significant increases in the amplitudes of θ, α-2, and β-L frequency bands after the ingestion of peptides. Soybean peptides can modulate cellular immune systems, regulate neurotransmitters, and boost brain function.

title: Early polyneuropharmacologic intervention in brain injury agitation.
abstract: To evaluate clinical outcomes observed in the treatment of posttraumatic brain injury agitation with multiple medications. Longitudinal evaluation of effect of treatment intervention on patients admitted to a level 1 trauma center with traumatic brain injury. Eleven adult patients referred for physiatry consultation to manage posttraumatic brain injury agitation were treated with a combination of medications and other agents (antipsychotics and sedatives) were discontinued. The Rancho Los Amigos scale was rated on initial consultation and follow-up, ranging from 1 to 3 wk. All 11 patients demonstrated an average improvement of three Rancho Los Amigos levels within an average treatment duration of 10 days. Simultaneously, 9 of 11 patients were discontinued from treatment and prescribed antipsychotic and sedative agents. The findings suggest that administration of multiple neuropharmacologic agents used early in the treatment of posttraumatic brain injury agitation may be an effective therapeutic intervention in which behavioral and, possibly, cognitive benefits occur. These results were noted as patients were discontinued from other agents more commonly employed in the acute care setting for the treatment of posttraumatic brain injury agitation. Further research is needed to determine the most effective medication combinations and the best time to initiate treatment.
journal: American journal of physical medicine & rehabilitation

title: [Use of the antisense oligonucleotide technique in neuropsychopharmacology research].
abstract: The technique using antisense oligonucleotides has been widely used in the study of specific functions of targeted protein molecules. This technique has enabled us to perform experiments which were previously impossible in the field of neuropsychopharmacology. Some chemically modified oligonucleotide analogues (i.e., phosphorothioate, S-oligo), which are relatively nuclease-resistant, are now available. However, the molecular mechanisms of action, pharmacokinetics, and kinetics of cellular uptake of antisense oligonucleotides, have not yet been fully understood. Recently, a number of reports have described the use of antisense oligonucleotides in inhibiting gene expression in the living brain, using intact laboratory animals. In the present article, after a brief discussion of mechanisms of action of antisense oligonucleotides, the methodological aspects of antisense experiments using the "mini osmotic pump" as the oligonucleotide delivery system are reviewed. The choice, design, mode of delivery, and dose are also discussed. In particular, we address the advantages and disadvantages of the antisense oligonucleotide technique as well as the control experiments required. This technique is a powerful method and one of the most important tools in neuropsychopharmacology research.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: PET in psychopharmacology.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful neuroimaging technique for investigating the pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics of psychotropic medications in living human brain. This review examines the current applications and methodological considerations of PET in psychopharmacological research. PET studies utilizing various radioligands have provided unprecedented insights into drug occupancy at neurotransmitter receptors, regional brain metabolism, and cerebral blood flow changes following psychoactive drug administration. Dopamine D2 receptor occupancy studies using [11C]raclopride have demonstrated that therapeutic efficacy of antipsychotic drugs occurs at 60-80% striatal receptor occupancy, while extrapyramidal side effects emerge above 80% occupancy. Similarly, serotonin transporter occupancy studies with [11C]McN5652 have shown that antidepressant efficacy requires >80% transporter blockade. PET investigations of glucose metabolism using [18F]FDG have revealed drug-induced alterations in prefrontal cortex activity in patients with schizophrenia and depression. Recent developments in PET methodology, including improved spatial resolution and novel radioligands targeting GABA, glutamate, and other neurotransmitter systems, are expanding research possibilities. However, limitations include radiation exposure constraints, high costs, and the need for on-site cyclotron facilities. Despite these challenges, PET remains an invaluable tool for dose optimization, understanding mechanisms of action, and predicting therapeutic response and adverse effects. Future applications may include personalized medicine approaches and the development of novel therapeutic targets in psychiatric disorders.

title: The future of pharmacotherapy for paediatric anxiety.
abstract: Anxiety disorders affect approximately 10-20% of children and adolescents, yet pharmacological treatment options remain limited due to insufficient pediatric clinical trial data and concerns regarding developmental neurotoxicity. Current off-label prescribing practices rely heavily on adult efficacy data, despite significant differences in pediatric pharmacokinetics and neurobiological development. This review examines emerging pharmacotherapeutic approaches and regulatory developments that may transform pediatric anxiety treatment over the next decade. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases (1990-2002), focusing on pediatric clinical trials, developmental neuropharmacology studies, and regulatory policy analyses. Recent developments include the FDA's proposed Pediatric Exclusivity provisions, which provide market incentives for conducting pediatric trials of existing medications. Promising pharmacological advances include selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors with improved pediatric safety profiles, novel neurokinin-1 receptor antagonists, and gamma-aminobutyric acid modulators specifically designed for developing neural systems. Preliminary data suggest that buspirone analogues and certain atypical antipsychotics may offer anxiolytic benefits with reduced metabolic side effects in children. Additionally, pharmacogenomic approaches are beginning to identify genetic polymorphisms affecting drug metabolism in pediatric populations, potentially enabling personalized dosing strategies. The integration of neuroimaging biomarkers with clinical outcomes may facilitate more targeted drug development. Future pediatric anxiety pharmacotherapy will likely emphasize mechanism-specific agents with established developmental safety profiles, supported by robust clinical trial infrastructure and regulatory frameworks specifically designed for vulnerable populations. Enhanced collaboration between academic researchers, pharmaceutical companies, and regulatory agencies remains essential for advancing this critical therapeutic area.

title: Compact 256-channel multi-well microelectrode array system for in vitro neuropharmacology test.
abstract: High-throughput screening of neuroactive compounds requires scalable platforms that can simultaneously monitor neuronal activity across multiple conditions while maintaining physiological relevance. Current microelectrode array (MEA) systems are limited by low channel density, bulky instrumentation, or inadequate throughput for pharmacological screening applications. We developed a compact 256-channel multi-well MEA system specifically designed for in vitro neuropharmacology testing. The system integrates 16 independent wells, each containing a 4×4 array of titanium nitride microelectrodes (30 μm diameter, 200 μm spacing) fabricated on glass substrates using standard photolithography. Custom low-noise amplification circuits and multiplexed data acquisition enable simultaneous recording from all channels at 20 kHz sampling rate. We validated the platform using primary rat cortical cultures and human iPSC-derived neurons, demonstrating stable recordings of spontaneous network activity for over 21 days in vitro. Pharmacological validation included dose-response characterization of GABA_A receptor modulators (muscimol, bicuculline), glutamate receptor antagonists (CNQX, APV), and sodium channel blockers (TTX, lidocaine). The system successfully detected compound effects with EC50 values consistent with literature reports and showed excellent inter-well reproducibility (CV < 15%). Signal-to-noise ratios exceeded 8:1 for 89% of electrodes. The compact footprint (15×10 cm) and integrated design enable deployment in standard laboratory settings. This platform addresses the growing need for medium-throughput electrophysiological screening in drug discovery, offering a practical solution for neuropharmacology laboratories requiring reliable, cost-effective neuronal activity monitoring across multiple experimental conditions.

title: Transgenic animal models for neuropharmacology.
abstract: Transgenic animal models have emerged as powerful tools for investigating the molecular mechanisms underlying neurological and psychiatric disorders, offering unprecedented opportunities to study gene function in vivo and evaluate novel therapeutic interventions. This review examines the current state of transgenic technology in neuropharmacological research, focusing on mouse models of major neurological conditions including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, and epilepsy. We analyzed over 200 published studies utilizing transgenic approaches, including knockout, knock-in, and overexpression models, to assess their contribution to drug discovery and mechanistic understanding of brain disorders. Transgenic models have successfully recapitulated key pathological features of human diseases, including amyloid plaque formation in APP/PS1 mice, dopaminergic neurodegeneration in α-synuclein overexpressing animals, and polyglutamine aggregation in Huntington's disease models. These models have facilitated the identification of novel drug targets and enabled preclinical testing of therapeutic compounds, leading to several clinical trials. However, significant limitations remain, including incomplete phenotypic penetrance, strain-dependent variability, and challenges in translating findings to human patients. Recent advances in tissue-specific and inducible expression systems have improved model precision and temporal control. Despite these limitations, transgenic animal models continue to provide invaluable insights into disease pathogenesis and remain essential for neuropharmacological research. Future developments in transgenic technology, including the integration of multiple genetic modifications and improved behavioral phenotyping, promise to enhance the predictive validity of these models for human therapeutic applications.

title: Dorsal raphe vs. median raphe serotonergic antagonism. Anatomical, physiological, behavioral, neuroendocrinological, neuropharmacological and clinical evidences: relevance for neuropharmacological therapy.
abstract: Monoaminergic neurons located in the central nervous system (CNS) are organized into complex circuits which include noradrenergic (NA), adrenergic (Ad), dopaminergic (DA), serotonergic (5-HT), histaminergic (H), GABA-ergic and glutamatergic systems. Most of these circuits are composed of more than one and often several types of the above neurons. Such physiologically flexible circuits respond appropriately to both external and internal stimuli which, if not modulated adequately, can trigger pathophysiologic responses. A great deal of research has been devoted to mapping the multiple functions of the CNS circuitry, thereby forming the basis for effective neuropharmacological therapeutic approaches. Such lineal strategies that seek to normalize complex and mixed physiological disorders, however, meet only partial therapeutic success and are often followed by undesirable side effects and/or total failure. In light of these, we have worked to develop possible models of CNS circuitry that are less affected by physiological interaction using the models to design more effective therapeutic approaches. In the present review, we cite and present evidence supporting the dorsal raphe versus median raphe serotonergic circuitry as one model of a reliable paradigm, necessary to the clear understanding and therapy of many psychiatric and even non-psychiatric disturbances.

title: Neuropharmacology of drugs and alcohol in mother and fetus.
abstract: Epidemiological evidence suggests that an adverse prenatal environment can have profound long-term health consequences throughout postnatal life. This chapter discusses the underlying mechanisms implicated in the consumption of mood-altering recreational drugs and teratogenicity in the fetus. The way metabolic parameters in pregnancy influence the pharmacokinetic characteristics of drugs and alcohol and the developmental stage of neurotoxicity are reviewed. The general underlying mechanisms that link multifaceted interactions between drug characteristics, gene polymorphisms, dietary deficiencies, changed endocrine indices and fetal programming are outlined, with specific examples throughout the text. As developmental injury is of significant social concern, the final section questions whether society provides adequate support for making appropriate and informed lifestyle choices to alleviate preventable transgenerational harm.

title: Validation of a fluorescence-based high-throughput assay for the measurement of neurotransmitter transporter uptake activity.
abstract: Pre-synaptic dopamine, norepinephrine and serotonin transporters (DAT, NET and SERT) terminate synaptic catecholamine transmission through reuptake of released neurotransmitter. Common approaches for studying these transporters involve radiolabeled substrates or inhibitors which, however, have several limitations. In this study we have used a novel neurotransmitter transporter uptake assay kit. The assay employs a fluorescent substrate that mimics the biogenic amine neurotransmitters and is taken up by the cell through the specific transporters, resulting in increased fluorescence intensity. In order to validate the assay, a variety of reference and proprietary neurotransmitter transporter ligands from a number of chemical and pharmacological classes were tested. The ability of these compounds to inhibit the selective transporter-mediated uptake demonstrated a similar rank order of potency and IC(50) values close to those obtained in radiolabeled neurotransmitter uptake assays. The described assay enables monitoring of dynamic transport activity of DAT, NET and SERT and is amenable for high-throughput screening and compound characterization.

title: A method for the evaluation of intracranial tetrodotoxin injections.
abstract: Tetrodotoxin (TTX) is widely used in neuroscience research to selectively block voltage-gated sodium channels and create reversible neural lesions. However, current methods for evaluating the efficacy and spatial extent of intracranial TTX injections rely primarily on behavioral assessments or post-mortem histological analysis, limiting real-time verification of injection success. We developed a novel electrophysiological approach to assess TTX delivery and effectiveness in vivo using multi-electrode arrays positioned adjacent to injection sites in anesthetized rats. Following stereotaxic placement of injection cannulae in the motor cortex (n=18) or hippocampus (n=12), we recorded local field potentials and multi-unit activity before, during, and after TTX administration (10-50 ng in 0.5 μL artificial cerebrospinal fluid). TTX injection produced rapid suppression of neural activity within 2-5 minutes, with complete silencing achieved at distances up to 800 μm from the injection site. The effective radius of neural suppression correlated positively with TTX concentration (r=0.78, p<0.001) and injection volume (r=0.71, p<0.01). Recovery of neural activity occurred 4-6 hours post-injection, following a predictable time course that varied with distance from the injection site. Control injections of vehicle alone produced minimal changes in neural activity. Post-mortem fluorescent tracer analysis confirmed the spatial distribution of injected solutions matched electrophysiological predictions. This method provides real-time verification of TTX injection efficacy and enables precise determination of affected tissue volume, offering significant advantages over existing approaches for studies requiring reversible neural inactivation.

title: Neurochemical substrates linked to impulsive and compulsive phenotypes in addiction: A preclinical perspective.
abstract: Impulsive and compulsive behaviors represent core features of substance use disorders, yet their underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain incompletely understood. While impulsivity typically precedes addiction onset, compulsivity emerges during chronic drug exposure and withdrawal phases. This study examined the neurochemical substrates mediating these distinct behavioral phenotypes using established rodent models. Male Sprout-Dawley rats (n=120) underwent behavioral characterization using the 5-choice serial reaction time task for impulsivity assessment and progressive ratio schedules for compulsive drug-seeking evaluation following chronic cocaine self-administration. Microdialysis, immunohistochemistry, and Western blot analyses quantified neurotransmitter levels and protein expression across key brain regions including the nucleus accumbens, prefrontal cortex, and dorsal striatum. High-impulsive animals exhibited reduced baseline dopamine D2 receptor expression in the nucleus accumbens core (p<0.01) and elevated extracellular glutamate in the prelimbic cortex compared to low-impulsive counterparts. Following chronic cocaine exposure, compulsive phenotypes correlated with decreased serotonin transporter density in the dorsal raphe (p<0.001) and enhanced AMPA receptor phosphorylation in the dorsolateral striatum. Acute N-acetylcysteine administration (100 mg/kg) attenuated compulsive responding while having minimal effects on impulsive choice behavior. These findings demonstrate distinct neurochemical profiles underlying impulsive versus compulsive addiction-related phenotypes, with impulsivity associated with dopaminergic and glutamatergic dysregulation in corticoaccumbal circuits, while compulsivity involves serotonergic deficits and enhanced striatal plasticity. Understanding these neurochemical distinctions may inform targeted therapeutic approaches for different stages of addiction vulnerability and progression.

title: Characterization of rat behavior in the elevated plus-maze using a directed graph.
abstract: The elevated plus-maze (EPM) is a widely used behavioral paradigm for assessing anxiety-like behavior in rodents, traditionally analyzed through time-based and frequency measures in open versus closed arms. However, conventional analyses may overlook important temporal dynamics and sequential patterns of exploratory behavior. We developed a novel analytical approach using directed graph theory to characterize the spatiotemporal organization of rat locomotor behavior in the EPM. Male Wistar rats (n=48) were subjected to standard EPM testing under control conditions or following administration of diazepam (1 mg/kg, i.p.) or yohimbine (3 mg/kg, i.p.). Behavioral trajectories were digitized and transformed into directed graphs where nodes represented spatial zones and edges indicated transition frequencies between zones. Graph-theoretical measures including node centrality, clustering coefficients, and path lengths were computed to quantify behavioral organization. Traditional anxiety measures correlated significantly with graph parameters: animals spending more time in open arms exhibited higher centrality values for open arm nodes (r=0.74, p<0.001) and increased inter-arm transition frequencies. Diazepam-treated rats showed enhanced graph connectivity and reduced path lengths compared to controls, while yohimbine produced the opposite pattern. Cluster analysis of graph metrics successfully classified animals into anxiety phenotypes with 87% accuracy, superior to conventional measures alone (69% accuracy). Our directed graph approach reveals previously undetected aspects of anxiety-related behavior, providing a more comprehensive framework for analyzing exploratory patterns in the EPM. This methodology may enhance sensitivity for detecting subtle behavioral changes in neuropharmacological studies and facilitate automated behavioral phenotyping.

title: [Atypical antipsychotic drugs].
abstract:
The introduction of atypical antipsychotic drugs has fundamentally changed the treatment of schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders. Unlike conventional neuroleptics that primarily block dopamine D2 receptors, atypical antipsychotics demonstrate a broader receptor binding profile with reduced risk of extrapyramidal side effects. This review examines the pharmacological properties, clinical efficacy, and safety profiles of the major atypical antipsychotics available in clinical practice.

We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published clinical trials and pharmacokinetic studies of clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine, and quetiapine from 1988 to 1999. Data were extracted from randomized controlled trials comparing atypical agents with conventional antipsychotics and placebo in patients with schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, and treatment-resistant psychosis. Primary endpoints included positive and negative symptom reduction measured by PANSS and BPRS scales, and incidence of movement disorders.

Atypical antipsychotics demonstrated superior efficacy in treating negative symptoms compared to haloperidol and other conventional agents. Clozapine showed particular benefit in treatment-resistant cases, with 30-60% response rates in previously refractory patients. The incidence of tardive dyskinesia was significantly reduced across all atypical agents (0.5-2% annually versus 5-7% with conventional drugs). However, metabolic side effects including weight gain, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia emerged as significant concerns, particularly with clozapine and olanzapine.

Atypical antipsychotics represent a major therapeutic advance, offering improved symptom control with reduced neurological side effects. However, careful monitoring for metabolic complications is essential. The improved tolerability profile may enhance long-term treatment adherence and functional outcomes in patients with chronic psychotic disorders.

title: Listening to the brain: microelectrode biosensors for neurochemicals.
abstract: Chemical signalling underlies every function of the nervous system, from those of which we are unaware, for example, control of the heart, to higher cognitive functions, such as emotions, learning and memory. Neurotransmitters and neuromodulators mediate communication between neurons and between neurons and non-neural cells such as glia and muscle. In the past, the means for studying the production and release of these signalling agents directly has been limited in its temporal and spatial resolution relative to the dynamics of chemical signalling and the structures of interest in the brain. Now microelectrode biosensors are becoming available that give unprecedented spatial and temporal resolution, enabling, for the first time, direct measurement in real time of the chemical conversations between cells in the nervous system.

title: Pharmacological treatment in behavioural medicine: the importance of neurochemistry, molecular biology and mechanistic hypotheses.
abstract: Psychopharmacology has become a popular, and sometimes mandatory addition to treatment regimes for canine and feline patients with behavioural problems; however, clients and practitioners should be dissuaded that behavioural drugs are 'quick fixes'. Veterinarians should only prescribe psychotropic medication when they have a specific idea of how the mechanism of action of the drug will affect the target behaviours associated with a specific diagnosis. The diagnosis must be treated rather than non-specific signs. Newer psychotropic medications demonstrate the extent to which truly abnormal behaviours are dysfunctions of neurochemistry; synaptic or cellular metabolism; or genetic encoding and 'learning', or LTP, hence there is a clear role for the interaction of neuropharmacology and behavioural and environmental modification.Future advances in treatment in behavioural medicine will be pharmacological and neurophysiological. As the field of behavioural medicine expands, its paradigm will enlarge to include combination therapy and the implementation of neuropharmacological intervention as a diagnostic tool. At present, the veterinary practitioner can effectively aid many common behavioural problems using extant drugs to treat animals with true behavioural pathology. Rational pharmacological therapy requires complete medical and behavioural histories, requisite laboratory work, complete client understanding and compliance, and an honest and ongoing dialogue between the client and veterinarian that includes frequent follow-ups and re-examinations.

title: Pharmacological regulation of gene expression.
abstract: Pharmacological regulation of gene expression was one of the top professional interests of Dr. Costa. He promoted the idea that drugs can improve the endogenous mechanisms of synaptic plasticity by modulating gene expression. In this article I reflect upon Dr. Costa's leadership in projects undertaken at FGIN that were aimed at elucidating how neurotransmitter receptor activation could affect brain function by modulating genes and their products. I will be presenting examples of how pharmacological tools can change gene expression. These include the ability of drugs of abuse to alter the synthesis of opioid peptides or an endogenous ligand for GABAA receptor. I will conclude with a brief summary of intriguing discoveries about the regulation of nerve growth factor (NGF) and its receptors by beta-receptor agonists, adrenal steroids and cytokines.

title: Contributions of different modes of TRPV1 activation to TRPV1 antagonist-induced hyperthermia.
abstract: Transient receptor potential vanilloid-1 (TRPV1) antagonists are widely viewed as next-generation pain therapeutics. However, these compounds cause hyperthermia, a serious side effect. TRPV1 antagonists differentially block three modes of TRPV1 activation: by heat, protons, and chemical ligands (e.g., capsaicin). We asked what combination of potencies in these three modes of TRPV1 activation corresponds to the lowest potency of a TRPV1 antagonist to cause hyperthermia. We studied hyperthermic responses of rats, mice, and guinea pigs to eight TRPV1 antagonists with different pharmacological profiles and used mathematical modeling to find a relative contribution of the blockade of each activation mode to the development of hyperthermia. We found that the hyperthermic effect has the highest sensitivity to the extent of TRPV1 blockade in the proton mode (0.43 to 0.65) with no to moderate sensitivity in the capsaicin mode (-0.01 to 0.34) and no sensitivity in the heat mode (0.00 to 0.01). We conclude that hyperthermia-free TRPV1 antagonists do not block TRPV1 activation by protons, even if they are potent blockers of the heat mode, and that decreasing the potency to block the capsaicin mode may further decrease the potency to cause hyperthermia.
journal: The Journal of neuroscience : the official journal of the Society for Neuroscience

title: Functional genomics in neuropsychiatric disorders and in neuropharmacology.
abstract: The rapidly accumulating amount of information concerning gene and protein expression patterns produced by functional genomics, proteomics and bioinformatics is presently providing new targets for drug development. Furthermore, the analysis of gene expression in cells and tissues affected by a disease may reveal the underlying metabolic pathways and cellular processes affected. Finally, changes in gene expression may be used in either diagnostics or the monitoring of drug responses. This review focuses on advances in the use of functional genomics in neurological and neuropsychiatric diseases and neuropsychopharmacology. Although the number of published studies in this field is still limited, it already appears that this strategy may become a fruitful means in the analysis of the aetiology of neuropsychiatric disorders and the search for novel neuropharmacological drugs.

title: Extracellular trypsin increases ASIC1a selectivity for monovalent versus divalent cations.
abstract: Acid-sensing ion channels (ASICs) are proton-gated sodium channels that play critical roles in synaptic transmission, pain perception, and neurodegeneration. ASIC1a, the predominant neuronal subtype, exhibits modest selectivity for monovalent over divalent cations under physiological conditions. Recent evidence suggests that extracellular proteases may modulate ASIC function, though the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. We investigated whether trypsin treatment alters the ionic selectivity properties of heterologously expressed ASIC1a channels in Chinese hamster ovary cells using whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology. ASIC1a currents were recorded in response to pH drops from 7.4 to 6.0 before and after exposure to 10 μg/ml trypsin for 5 minutes. Ion selectivity was assessed by measuring reversal potentials and relative permeabilities using bi-ionic substitution protocols with Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+. Untreated ASIC1a channels showed PNa/PCa and PNa/PMg ratios of 3.2 ± 0.4 and 4.1 ± 0.5, respectively (n=12). Following trypsin treatment, these ratios increased significantly to 8.7 ± 1.2 and 10.3 ± 1.4 (p<0.001), indicating enhanced discrimination against divalent cations. Trypsin also reduced steady-state inactivation and increased the pH50 for activation from 6.2 to 6.0. The selectivity changes were prevented by the serine protease inhibitor PMSF and were irreversible upon washout. These findings demonstrate that extracellular trypsin proteolytically modifies ASIC1a channels to enhance monovalent cation selectivity, potentially representing a novel mechanism for activity-dependent modulation of acid-sensing channel function in the nervous system.

title: Neuronal and glial cell lines as model systems for studying P2Y receptor pharmacology.
abstract: Investigation of the role of extracellular nucleotides in nervous system has been one of the main topics of the P2Y receptor research throughout the years. In parallel to numerous studies on primary culture systems, various neuronal and non-neuronal cell lines have been used to model in vitro the processes mediated by extracellular nucleotides. In this review article, a survey of expression profiles of G protein-coupled P2Y receptor subtypes in nervous-system-derived cell lines is presented, by analysing the receptor expression at the mRNA, protein, and functional level. The variability of receptor expression profiles in established cell lines is further discussed, bringing forward some general properties for neuronal and glial malignant cell lines.

title: Measurement of plasmalemmal dopamine transport, vesicular dopamine transport, and K(+)-stimulated dopamine release in frozen rat brain tissue.
abstract: Measurement of dopamine transport and release mechanisms in brain tissue has traditionally required fresh tissue preparations, limiting experimental flexibility and sample throughput. This study evaluated whether frozen rat brain tissue retains sufficient functional integrity for quantitative assessment of plasmalemmal dopamine transporter (DAT) activity, vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) function, and K+-stimulated dopamine release. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were sacrificed and striatal tissue was either used fresh or frozen at -80°C for 1-4 weeks. DAT activity was measured using [3H]dopamine uptake assays in synaptosomal preparations, while VMAT2 function was assessed using [3H]dopamine uptake into isolated synaptic vesicles. K+-stimulated dopamine release was evaluated using superfusion chambers with HPLC detection. Fresh tissue demonstrated robust DAT-mediated uptake (Vmax = 2.14 ± 0.18 pmol/mg protein/min), VMAT2 activity (Km = 0.82 ± 0.11 μM), and K+-stimulated release (348 ± 29% of baseline). Frozen tissue retained 78 ± 6% of DAT activity, 71 ± 8% of VMAT2 function, and 82 ± 7% of stimulated release capacity compared to fresh controls. Pharmacological profiles remained unchanged, with GBR-12909 inhibiting DAT (IC50 = 12.3 nM fresh vs. 15.7 nM frozen) and reserpine blocking VMAT2 (IC50 = 3.8 nM fresh vs. 4.9 nM frozen). These findings demonstrate that frozen rat brain tissue maintains sufficient dopaminergic transport and release functions for quantitative neuropharmacological studies, enabling improved experimental design and increased sample utilization efficiency.

title: Changes in striatal electroencephalography and neurochemistry induced by kainic acid seizures are modified by dopamine receptor antagonists.
abstract: We investigated the involvement of striatal dopamine release in electrographic and motor seizure activity evoked by kainic acid in the guinea pig. The involvement of the dopamine receptor subtypes was studied by systemic administration of the dopamine D(1) receptor antagonist, R(+)-7-chloro-8-hydroxy-3-methyl-1-phenyl-2,3,4,5-tetrahydro-1H-3-benzazepine hydrochloride (SCH 23390; 0.5 mg kg(-1)), or the dopamine D(2) antagonist, (5-aminosulphonyl)-N-[(1-ethyl-2-pyrrolidinyl)-methyl]-2-methoxybenzamide (sulpiride, 30 mg kg(-1)). Microdialysis and high performance liquid chromatography were used to monitor changes in extracellular levels of striatal dopamine and its metabolites, glutamate, aspartate and gamma-amino-butyric acid (GABA). These data were correlated with changes in the striatal and cortical electroencephalographs and clinical signs. We found that, although neither dopamine receptor antagonist inhibited behavioural seizure activity, blockade of the dopamine D(1)-like receptor with SCH 23390 significantly reduced both the 'power' of the electrical seizure activity and the associated change in extracellular striatal concentration of glutamate, whilst increasing the extracellular striatal concentration of GABA. In contrast, blockade of the dopamine D(2)-like receptor with sulpiride significantly increased the extracellular, striatal content of glutamate and the dopamine metabolites. These results confirm previous evidence in other models of chemically-evoked seizures that antagonism of the dopamine D(1) receptor tends to reduce motor and electrographic seizure activity as well as excitatory amino-acid transmitter activity, while antagonism of the dopamine D(2) receptor has relatively less apparent effect.

title: Stereochemistry in clinical medicine: a neurological perspective.
abstract: Stereoisomers are compounds that have identical sets of atoms configured in the same positions but are arranged differently spatially. Approximately 25% of contemporary drugs are marketed and used as racemates (i.e., as equimolar mixtures of stereoisomers). This may have major clinical implications, as stereoisomers may possess qualitative and/or quantitative differences in pharmacological effects, plasma protein and tissue binding, metabolic and renal clearance. There are many examples of racemic drugs manufactured and used as single stereoisomers in the field of neurology including the anti-Parkinsonian drugs levodopa, selegiline, apomorphine and entacapone, the antiepileptic drugs tiagabine and levetiracetam, the secondary stroke prevention agent clopidogrel and the acetylcholinesterase inhibitor rivastigmine. The role of drug stereochemistry in the re-evaluation of established drugs and the production of new agents is becoming increasingly important as pharmaceutical companies endeavour to show proof of "no penalty" for the introduction of a racemic new drug over one or other of its single stereoisomers.
journal: Journal of clinical neuroscience : official journal of the Neurosurgical Society of Australasia

title: The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
abstract: The 5-HT3 receptor represents a novel class of serotonin receptors that differs fundamentally from the previously characterized 5-HT1 and 5-HT2 receptor subtypes. Unlike these G-protein coupled receptors, the 5-HT3 receptor functions as a ligand-gated cation channel, mediating rapid synaptic transmission in the central and peripheral nervous systems. This review examines the emerging psychopharmacological profile of 5-HT3 receptors based on recent radioligand binding studies, electrophysiological recordings, and behavioral investigations using selective antagonists. Pharmacological characterization reveals high-affinity binding sites for [3H]GR65630 and [3H]zacopride in brain regions including the area postrema, nucleus tractus solitarius, and hippocampus. The selective 5-HT3 antagonists ondansetron, granisetron, and tropisetron demonstrate nanomolar affinities and exhibit distinct behavioral effects in animal models. Administration of these compounds produces anxiolytic-like effects in conflict paradigms, reduces ethanol consumption in alcohol-preferring rat strains, and blocks cocaine-induced locomotor activation without affecting basal motor activity. Electrophysiological studies confirm that 5-HT3 receptor activation produces rapid membrane depolarization through increased sodium and calcium conductance. The 5-HT3 receptor appears critically involved in mediating nausea and emesis, with clinical trials demonstrating remarkable efficacy of 5-HT3 antagonists in chemotherapy-induced emesis. These findings suggest that 5-HT3 receptors represent important therapeutic targets for anxiety disorders, substance abuse, and gastrointestinal dysfunction, warranting continued investigation of their psychopharmacological properties.

title: Role of neurologists in the pharmaceutical industry.
abstract: The increasing complexity of neurological disorders and the expansion of pharmaceutical research have created new opportunities for neurologists to contribute to drug development beyond traditional clinical practice. However, the extent and nature of neurologist involvement in the pharmaceutical industry remains poorly characterized. We conducted a comprehensive survey of 847 board-certified neurologists across the United States to assess their current and potential roles in pharmaceutical research and development. Questionnaires were distributed through the American Academy of Neurology membership database, examining current industry relationships, consulting activities, clinical trial participation, and attitudes toward industry collaboration. Of the 623 respondents (73.5% response rate), 34% reported current consulting relationships with pharmaceutical companies, while 52% had participated in industry-sponsored clinical trials within the past five years. Primary activities included protocol design consultation (28%), adverse event monitoring (41%), and educational program development (19%). Neurologists specializing in epilepsy, movement disorders, and multiple sclerosis showed significantly higher rates of industry involvement compared to general neurologists (p<0.001). Barriers to participation included time constraints (67%), concerns about conflict of interest (31%), and inadequate compensation structures (23%). Most respondents (78%) viewed industry collaboration as beneficial for advancing neurological care, though 45% expressed concerns about maintaining clinical objectivity. These findings suggest that neurologists play increasingly important roles in pharmaceutical development, particularly in specialized therapeutic areas. Enhanced guidelines for ethical industry relationships and improved integration of clinical expertise into drug development processes may optimize these collaborative efforts while preserving professional integrity.

title: Proteomic approaches in brain research and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Proteomic technologies have emerged as powerful tools for investigating complex molecular mechanisms underlying neurological disorders and drug actions in the central nervous system. Traditional approaches focusing on individual proteins have proven insufficient to understand the intricate networks governing brain function and pathology. We applied two-dimensional gel electrophoresis coupled with mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) to analyze protein expression profiles in rat cortical and hippocampal tissues following chronic administration of haloperidol (2 mg/kg/day for 21 days) compared to vehicle-treated controls. Additionally, we examined cerebrospinal fluid samples from patients with Parkinson's disease (n=24) and age-matched controls (n=20) using surface-enhanced laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (SELDI-TOF MS). In haloperidol-treated animals, we identified 47 differentially expressed proteins, including significant upregulation of heat shock protein 70 (2.3-fold), α-synuclein (1.8-fold), and downregulation of synapsin I (0.6-fold) and DARPP-32 (0.4-fold). Human CSF analysis revealed distinct proteomic signatures in Parkinson's patients, with decreased levels of DJ-1 protein and altered expression of several metabolic enzymes. Pathway analysis indicated disruption of synaptic transmission, protein folding mechanisms, and dopaminergic signaling cascades. These findings demonstrate that proteomic approaches can reveal novel therapeutic targets and biomarkers not detectable through conventional pharmacological studies. The identification of protein networks affected by neuroleptic treatment and neurodegeneration provides new insights into drug mechanisms and disease pathophysiology, potentially facilitating the development of more selective therapeutic interventions.

title: Discrete neurochemical coding of distinguishable motivational processes: insights from nucleus accumbens control of feeding.
abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAc) has been implicated in motivational control of feeding behavior, yet the specific neurochemical mechanisms that distinguish between different motivational processes remain poorly understood. Previous studies have suggested that dopamine and GABA systems within the NAc may differentially regulate appetitive versus consummatory phases of feeding. Here, we examined the distinct neurochemical profiles associated with food-seeking versus food-consumption behaviors using targeted microinjections and in vivo microdialysis in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Bilateral microinjections of the GABA-A agonist muscimol (0.25 μg) into the NAc shell dramatically increased food intake by 340% compared to vehicle controls, while the same treatment in the NAc core produced no significant effect. Conversely, intra-shell injections of the dopamine D1 antagonist SCH-23390 (1.0 μg) selectively impaired food-seeking behaviors without affecting consummatory responses once feeding was initiated. Microdialysis revealed that muscimol treatment increased extracellular dopamine levels by 180% specifically during active feeding periods, while SCH-23390 had no effect on dopamine release but reduced acetylcholine efflux by 45%. Double-label Fos immunohistochemistry demonstrated distinct patterns of neuronal activation, with GABA manipulations primarily affecting ventromedial shell neurons and dopamine manipulations engaging dorsolateral shell populations. These findings provide evidence for anatomically and neurochemically discrete coding mechanisms within the NAc shell that independently regulate motivational drive versus hedonic consumption. Understanding this neurochemical compartmentalization may inform therapeutic approaches for disorders involving dysregulated feeding motivation.

title: Pharmacological treatment in behavioural medicine: the importance of neurochemistry, molecular biology and mechanistic hypotheses.
abstract: Behavioural disorders in companion animals have traditionally been treated empirically, with limited understanding of underlying neurobiological mechanisms. This review examines the application of neurochemical principles and molecular biology to improve therapeutic outcomes in veterinary behavioural medicine. We analysed treatment responses in 156 dogs and 43 cats presenting with anxiety disorders, compulsive behaviours, and aggression over a 24-month period. Animals were treated with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (fluoxetine, sertraline), tricyclic antidepressants (clomipramine), or anxiolytics (alprazolam) based on mechanistic hypotheses derived from neurochemical dysfunction patterns. Therapeutic response was evaluated using standardised behavioural scoring systems and owner questionnaires at 4, 8, and 12 weeks post-treatment initiation. Dogs with separation anxiety showed 73% improvement with fluoxetine compared to 45% with traditional anxiolytics (P<0.01). Compulsive disorders responded favourably to clomipramine (68% improvement) versus fluoxetine (41%, P<0.05). Cats with urine marking behaviour demonstrated 82% response rates to fluoxetine. Molecular analysis of cerebrospinal fluid in a subset of animals revealed altered serotonin metabolite ratios correlating with treatment response (r=0.67, P<0.001). These findings demonstrate that mechanism-based therapeutic approaches, grounded in neurochemical understanding, significantly improve treatment outcomes compared to empirical methods.
