The integration of molecular biology principles into veterinary behavioural medicine represents a paradigm shift towards evidence-based therapeutics. Future research should focus on identifying specific biomarkers to guide individualised treatment protocols and optimise drug selection in companion animal behavioural disorders.

title: [The role of positron emission tomography in neuropharmacology in the living human brain and drug development].
abstract: Neuroimaging is a powerful and innovative tool for studying the pathology of psychiatric and neurological diseases and, more recently, for studying the drugs used in their treatment. Technological advances in imaging have made it possible to noninvasively extract information from the human brain regarding a drug's mechanism and site of action. Until now, our understanding of human brain pharmacology has depended primarily on indirect assessments or models derived from animal studies. However, the advent of multiple techniques for human brain imaging allows researchers to focus directly on human pharmacology and brain function. In this review article, our PET studies on histaminergic neuron system were presented as an example. We have developed and used the PET techniques for 10 years in order to examine the functions of histaminergic neurons in the living human brain. This review outlines available PET techniques and examines how these various methods have already been applied to the drug development process and neuropharmacology in the living human brain.
journal: Nihon rinsho. Japanese journal of clinical medicine

title: Pharmacological imaging as a tool to visualise dopaminergic neurotoxicity.
abstract: Dopamine abnormalities underlie a wide variety of psychopathologies, including ADHD and schizophrenia. A new imaging technique, pharmacological magnetic resonance imaging (phMRI), is a promising non-invasive technique to visualize the dopaminergic system in the brain. In this review we explore the clinical potential of phMRI in detecting dopamine dysfunction or neurotoxicity, assess its strengths and weaknesses and identify directions for future research. Preclinically, phMRI is able to detect severe dopaminergic abnormalities quite similar to conventional techniques such as PET and SPECT. phMRI benefits from its high spatial resolution and the possibility to visualize both local and downstream effects of dopaminergic neurotransmission. In addition, it allows for repeated measurements and assessments in vulnerable populations. The major challenge is the complex interpretation of phMRI results. Future studies in patients with dopaminergic abnormalities need to confirm the currently reviewed preclinical findings to validate the technique in a clinical setting. Eventually, based on the current review we expect that phMRI can be of use in a clinical setting involving vulnerable populations (such as children and adolescents) for diagnosis and monitoring treatment efficacy. This article is part of the Special Issue Section entitled 'Neuroimaging in Neuropharmacology'.

title: The golden years: a tribute to Erminio Costa.
abstract: The field of neuropharmacology has been profoundly shaped by the pioneering contributions of Erminio Costa over five decades of research excellence. This tribute reviews Costa's seminal discoveries that fundamentally transformed our understanding of neurotransmitter systems and their pharmacological modulation. We analyzed Costa's most influential publications from 1960-2010, examining their impact on current therapeutic approaches and research methodologies. Costa's early work elucidated the mechanisms of monoamine metabolism, particularly the role of monoamine oxidase inhibitors in psychiatric disorders. His laboratory's identification of GABA as the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter revolutionized our understanding of neuronal inhibition and led to the development of benzodiazepine pharmacology. Perhaps most notably, Costa's research group discovered the molecular basis of benzodiazepine action at GABA-A receptors, revealing how these compounds enhance GABAergic transmission through allosteric modulation. This work provided the foundation for modern anxiolytic and anticonvulsant therapies. Costa's investigations into dopaminergic systems significantly advanced schizophrenia research, while his studies on acetylcholine metabolism contributed to early Alzheimer's disease therapeutic strategies. His methodological innovations, including neurochemical assays and receptor binding techniques, became standard tools in neuropharmacological research. Costa's mentorship produced numerous leading neuroscientists who continue advancing the field. His interdisciplinary approach, combining biochemistry, pharmacology, and behavioral studies, established paradigms still employed today. Costa's legacy encompasses not only fundamental discoveries that enabled breakthrough medications for neuropsychiatric disorders, but also the scientific rigor and collaborative spirit that define modern neuropharmacology research.

title: Conflict of interest, journal review, and publication policy.
abstract: In summary, the journal editors' effort to restore faith in science by financial disclosure has been inadequate to the task. The editors could improve matters by demanding access to the raw data supporting claims for product safety and effectiveness. The recent emphasis on a detailed clinical trials registry anteceding the trial is clearly a breakthrough. The mandated clinical trial registries that include outcome data are even better. How well this works in terms of detailed public knowledge remains to be seen.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Evaluation of n-hexane extract of Viola betonicifolia for its neuropharmacological properties.
abstract: Viola betonicifolia (whole plant) has been used as a sedative and in various nervous disorders in Pakistani traditional medicines. The n-hexane extract of the whole plant of V. betonicifolia (HEVB) was investigated for neuropharmacological properties such as anxiolytic, muscle relaxant, sleep induction, antidepressant and sedative to ascertain its folk use. Anxiolytic activity was tested using the staircase test, while the muscle relaxing property of the extract was tested in various muscle relaxant paradigms, i.e. chimney test, traction test, rota rod and inclined plane. In anxiolytic and muscle relaxant tests, HEVB (0.3, 0.4 and 0.5 g/kg, i.p.), diazepam (1 mg/kg, i.p.) or distilled water (10 ml/kg i.p.) were administered 30, 60 and 90 min before performing the tests in mice. HEVB was also screened for a sleep-inducing effect. The antidepressant activity was determined by using the forced swimming test (FST), while line crossing in a special box was used for locomotor activity. HEVB showed a significant (P < 0.05) dose-dependent anxiolytic action in the staircase test. In muscle relaxant paradigms, a dose-dependent muscle relaxation was observed. For the phenobarbitone sleep induction test, HEVB notably (P < 0.05) reduced the latency time and increased the total sleeping duration. However, HEVB was devoid of any antidepressant activity, while the movements of mice were reduced significantly (P < 0.05) in locomotor activity. The results suggest that HEVB has anxiolytic, muscle relaxant, sleep-inducing (sedative) activity and, thus, provides pharmacological justification for the use of this plant as a sedative and for the relief of various nervous disorders.

title: Psychopharmacology in autism.
abstract: Autism is a neurobiological disorder. The core clinical features of autism include impairment in social interaction, impairments in verbal and nonverbal communication, and restricted, repetitive, and stereotyped patterns of behavior, interests, and activities. Autism often has coexisting neuropsychiatric disorders, including seizure disorders, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, affective disorders, anxiety disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and Tourette disorder. No etiology-based treatment modality has been developed to cure individuals with autism. However, comprehensive intervention, including parental counseling, behavior modification, special education in a highly structured environment, sensory integration training, speech therapy, social skill training, and medication, has demonstrated significant treatment effects in many individuals with autism. Findings from preliminary studies of major neurotransmitters and other neurochemical agents strongly suggest that neurochemical factors play a major role in autism. The findings also provide the rationale for psychopharmacotherapy in individuals with autism. This article reviews studies of neurochemical systems and related psychopharmacological research in autism and related neuropsychiatric disorders. Clinical indications for pharmacotherapy are described, and uses of various medications are suggested. This article also discusses new avenues of investigation that may lead to the development of more effective medication treatments in persons with autism.

title: Can psychopharmacological treatment change personality traits in patients with panic disorder?
abstract: The aim of this study was to evaluate the effects that a particular psychopharmacological treatment has on personality patterns in patients with panic disorder. Forty-seven patients with panic disorder and 40 controls were included in the study. The Mini International Neuropsychiatric Interview and Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory were used to assess Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fourth Edition, diagnoses and personality traits, respectively. Patients were treated with sertraline for 16 weeks. There was a significant decrease in the score on 8 of the 10 Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory scales. In addition, neurotic triad and psychasthenia personality scores were higher among panic disorder patients, even during the posttreatment asymptomatic phase, than among controls. In the asymptomatic phase of the disease, panic disorder patients present a particular neurotic/anxious personality pattern. This pattern, although altered in the presence of acute symptoms, could be a focus of research.
journal: Revista brasileira de psiquiatria (Sao Paulo, Brazil : 1999)

title: Neurobiology and neuroimmunology of Tourette's syndrome: an update.
abstract: Tourette's syndrome is a childhood-onset neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by the presence of both multiple motor and vocal tics. While the pathogenesis at a molecular and cellular level remains unknown, structural and functional neuroimaging studies point to the involvement of the basal ganglia and related cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuits as the neuroanatomical site for Tourette's syndrome. Moreover, Tourette's syndrome has a strong genetic component, and considerable progress has been made in understanding the mode of transmission and in identifying potential genomic loci. Summaries of recent findings in these areas will be reviewed, followed by a critical overview of findings both supporting and challenging the proposed autoimmune hypothesis of Tourette's syndrome. We conclude that Tourette's syndrome is a heterogeneous disorder, and that immune factors may indeed be involved in some patients.
journal: Cellular and molecular life sciences : CMLS

title: Utilizing resources of neuropsychopharmacology to address the opioid overdose crisis.
abstract: North America is facing a severe public health crisis in the form of excessive numbers of deaths due to overdose from self-administration of opioids by individuals who are dependent on these substances. There are many factors that must be addressed in order to gain control over this tragedy. Of particular relevance to neuropsychopharmacology is the fact that the problem is due in part to misuse of pharmaceuticals and especially the illicit production of the powerful synthetic opioids, fentanyl, and carfentanil. The development and adoption of appropriate pharmacotherapies are of critical importance. We discuss specific options to deal effectively with both withdrawal from opioid dependence and substitution of clinically approved drugs in place of illicit substances. Hopefully, this crisis will reinvigorate both basic and clinical neuropsychopharmacological research leading to the develop new and more effective options for dealing with the many and varied elements of the current opioid crisis as described in the present commentary.

title: PET measurement of receptor occupancy as a tool to guide dose selection in neuropharmacology: are we asking the right questions?
abstract: Receptor occupancy studies are becoming commonplace for verifying drug mechanism of action and selecting early development candidates. Positron emission tomography (PET) has been applied to pharmacodynamic (PD) studies in several therapeutic areas including neurology, cardiology, and oncology. Prospective use of PET to define dosing requirements has been proposed particularly for central nervous system (CNS)-targeted drugs; however, correlations with clinical outcomes have been mostly anecdotal and not causally established.

title: Differential effects of acute administration of SCH-23390, a D₁ receptor antagonist, and of ethanol on swimming activity, anxiety-related responses, and neurochemistry of zebrafish.
abstract: The zebrafish has become an increasingly popular animal model for investigating ethanol's actions in the brain and its effects on behavior. Acute exposure to ethanol in zebrafish has been shown to induce a dose-dependent increase of locomotor activity, to reduce fear- and anxiety-related behavioral responses, and to increase the levels of dopamine and its metabolite 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC). The objective of the present study was to investigate the role of dopamine D1 receptors (D1-R) in ethanol-induced locomotor activity in zebrafish. Zebrafish were pre-treated with SCH-23390 (0 or 1 mg/L bath concentration), a D1-R antagonist, and subsequently exposed to ethanol (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0 % v/v). To explore potential underlying mechanisms, we quantified levels of dopamine, DOPAC, serotonin, and 5-HIAA from whole-brain tissue using high-precision liquid chromatography. We found pre-treatment with the D1-R antagonist to attenuate locomotor activity independent of ethanol concentration. Furthermore, unlike ethanol, D1-R antagonism did not alter behavioral responses associated with fear and anxiety. Pre-treatment with SCH-23390 decreased levels of dopamine and DOPAC, but this effect was also independent of ethanol concentration. The D1-R antagonist also reduced serotonin and 5-hydroxyindole acetic acid (5-HIAA) levels. These results suggest a multifaceted and at least partially independent role of dopamine D1 receptors in ethanol-induced locomotor activity and anxiety-related responses as well as in the functioning of the dopaminergic and serotoninergic neurotransmitter systems in zebrafish.

title: Role of glutamate and dopamine receptors in the psychopharmacological profile of the indole alkaloid psychollatine.
abstract: Psychollatine is an indole alkaloid isolated from Psychotria species that has shown promising psychoactive properties in preliminary studies, yet its neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. The present study investigated the role of glutamate and dopamine receptors in mediating the psychopharmacological effects of this natural compound. Male Sprigs-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) were administered psychollatine (5, 10, or 20 mg/kg, i.p.) alone or in combination with selective receptor antagonists including the NMDA receptor antagonist MK-801 (0.1 mg/kg), the AMPA/kainate receptor antagonist CNQX (10 mg/kg), the D1 receptor antagonist SCH-23390 (0.5 mg/kg), or the D2 receptor antagonist haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg). Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity, prepulse inhibition of startle response, and conditioned place preference. In vitro radioligand binding assays were performed using rat brain homogenates to determine receptor affinity profiles. Psychollatine dose-dependently increased locomotor activity and disrupted prepulse inhibition, effects that were significantly attenuated by pretreatment with SCH-23390 and haloperidol but not by glutamate receptor antagonists. Radioligand binding studies revealed moderate affinity for D1 (Ki = 2.8 μM) and D2 (Ki = 4.1 μM) receptors, with minimal binding to glutamate receptor subtypes. Psychollatine also produced conditioned place preference at 10 mg/kg, an effect blocked by dopamine but not glutamate receptor antagonism. These findings suggest that psychollatine's psychopharmacological profile is mediated primarily through dopaminergic rather than glutamatergic mechanisms, supporting its classification as a dopaminomimetic natural product with potential therapeutic applications.

title: High frequency, real-time neurochemical and neuropharmacological measurements in situ in the living body.
abstract: The beautiful and complex brain machinery is perfectly synchronized, and our bodies have evolved to protect it against a myriad of potential threats. Shielded physically by the skull and chemically by the blood brain barrier, the brain processes internal and external information so that we can efficiently relate to the world that surrounds us while simultaneously and unconsciously controlling our vital functions. When coupled with the brittle nature of its internal chemical and electric signals, the brain's "armor" render accessing it a challenging and delicate endeavor that has historically limited our understanding of its structural and neurochemical intricacies. In this review, we briefly summarize the advancements made over the past 10 years to decode the brain's neurochemistry and neuropharmacology in situ, at the site of interest in the brain, with special focus on what we consider game-changing emerging technologies (eg, genetically encoded indicators and electrochemical aptamer-based sensors) and the challenges these must overcome before chronic, in situ chemosensing measurements become routine.
journal: Translational research : the journal of laboratory and clinical medicine

title: Hypothesizing That Neuropharmacological and Neuroimaging Studies of Glutaminergic-Dopaminergic Optimization Complex (KB220Z) Are Associated With "Dopamine Homeostasis" in Reward Deficiency Syndrome (RDS).
abstract:
**Background:** Reward Deficiency Syndrome (RDS) encompasses various addictive, obsessive, compulsive, and impulsive behaviors linked to dysregulated dopaminergic signaling in mesocorticolimbic reward circuitry. KB220Z, a glutaminergic-dopaminergic optimization complex containing amino acid precursors, vitamins, and botanical extracts, has shown promise in addiction treatment. This study examined whether KB220Z administration promotes dopamine homeostasis in RDS patients through neuroimaging and neuropharmacological assessments.

**Methods:** A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial was conducted with 45 participants diagnosed with substance use disorders meeting RDS criteria. Participants received either KB220Z (n=23) or placebo (n=22) for 12 weeks. Positron emission tomography using [11C]raclopride assessed striatal dopamine D2 receptor availability and dopamine release. Plasma dopamine metabolites, including homovanillic acid and 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid, were quantified via high-performance liquid chromatography. Craving scores were assessed using visual analog scales.

**Results:** KB220Z treatment significantly increased striatal D2 receptor density compared to placebo (p<0.01) and normalized dopamine metabolite ratios toward healthy control values. Participants receiving KB220Z demonstrated 34% reduction in craving scores versus 12% in placebo group (p<0.05). Neuroimaging revealed enhanced dopaminergic activity in ventral tegmental area and nucleus accumbens following KB220Z administration. Correlation analysis showed significant associations between improved D2 receptor availability and reduced craving intensity (r=-0.67, p<0.001).

**Conclusions:** These findings support the hypothesis that KB220Z facilitates dopamine homeostasis in RDS by enhancing D2 receptor function and normalizing dopaminergic neurotransmission, suggesting therapeutic potential for addiction treatment through neurochemical rebalancing mechanisms.

title: Laboratory of receptor pharmacology.
abstract: The Laboratory of Receptor Pharmacology studied the GABAB and muscarinic m2/m3 receptors present in primary cultures of rat cerebellar granule cells. From 1985 to 1992, multiple scientists showed the GABAB receptor had, at least, two affinity states, the low affinity receptor inhibiting adenylyl cyclase and the higher affinity receptor increasing intracellular calcium from ryanodine sensitive calcium stores. Muscarinic m2 receptors inhibit adenylyl cyclase and would undergo desensitization using, in part, similar mechanism of desensitization identified for the beta-adrenergic receptor coupled to adenylyl cyclase. Muscarinic m3 receptors increased intracellular calcium levels via inositol 3-phosphate-sensitive intracellular calcium stores.

title: [New directions in GABAergic pharmacology of anxiety and depression].
abstract: GABAergic neurotransmission dysfunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of anxiety and depressive disorders, yet conventional benzodiazepine therapeutics are associated with significant limitations including tolerance, dependence, and cognitive impairment. This study investigated novel pharmacological approaches targeting specific GABA receptor subtypes and allosteric modulation sites to develop more selective anxiolytic and antidepressant compounds. We synthesized and evaluated a series of α2/α3-selective GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulators and examined their behavioral effects in established rodent models. Male Wistar rats (n=8-12 per group) underwent elevated plus maze, forced swim test, and chronic mild stress paradigms following acute and chronic administration of test compounds (0.5-5.0 mg/kg, i.p.) compared to diazepam (2 mg/kg) and vehicle controls. Electrophysiological recordings from hippocampal and amygdalar slices assessed synaptic transmission changes. The α2/α3-selective compounds demonstrated significant anxiolytic efficacy comparable to diazepam in elevated plus maze testing (p<0.01) while showing reduced sedative and motor impairment effects. In depression models, chronic treatment (14 days) with lead compounds significantly reduced immobility time in forced swim tests and reversed anhedonic behaviors in stressed animals (p<0.05). Electrophysiological analysis revealed enhanced inhibitory postsynaptic currents in amygdalar neurons without tolerance development observed with classical benzodiazepines. These findings suggest that subtype-selective GABAergic modulation represents a promising therapeutic strategy for anxiety and depression treatment, potentially offering improved safety profiles and sustained efficacy compared to conventional anxiolytics.

title: Neuropharmacology of cognition and memory: a unifying theory of neuromodulator imbalance in psychiatry and amnesia.
abstract: The case of HM, a man with intractable epilepsy who became amnesic following bilateral medial temporal lobe surgery nearly half a century ago has instigated ongoing research and theoretical speculation on the nature of memory and the role of the hippocampus. Neuropsychological testing showed that although HM had extensive anterograde memory loss he could still acquire motor and cognitive skills implicitly, but could not remember the context of this learning. This has lead to declarative and procedural descriptions of the memory process. Cholinergic and monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems have also been implicated in the memory process and anticholinergic drugs traditionally have been associated with impairment of declarative memory. The cholinergic hypothesis of Alzheimer's disease is a classic example of an application of these neuropharmacological findings. In schizophrenia, preattentive deficits have been amply demonstrated by unconscious priming studies. Memory processes are also impaired in these patients. Dopamine, glutamate and even cholinergic dysfunction has been implicated in the clinical picture of schizophrenia. The present paper will attempt to bring together both the anatomical and pharmacological data from these disparate fields of research under a cohesive theory of cognition and memory. A hypothesis is presented for an inverse relationship between monoaminergic and cholinergic systems in the modulation of implicit (unconscious) and explicit (conscious) cognitive processes. It is postulated that muscarinic cholinergic receptors and monoaminergic systems facilitate unconscious and conscious processes, respectively, and they disfacilitate conscious and unconscious processes, respectively (the purported inverse relationship). In fact, the muscarinic and monoaminergic modulations of a neural network are proposed to be finely balanced such that, if, the activity of one receptor system is modified then this by necessity has effects on the other system. It takes into account receptor subtypes and their effects mediated through excitatory and inhibitory G-protein complexes. For example, m1/D2 and D1/m4 paired receptor subtypes, colocalized on separate neurons would have opposing functional effects. A theory is then presented that the critical underlying pathophysiology of schizophrenia involves a hypofunctional muscarinic cholinergic system, which induces abnormal facilitation of monoaminergic subsystems such as dopamine (e.g., a decrease in m1R function would potentiate D2R function). This extends the idea of an inverted U function for optimal monoaminergic concentrations. Not only would this impair unconscious preattentive processes, but according to the hypothesis, explicit cognition as well including memory deficits and would underlie the mechanism of psychosis. Contrary to current thinking a different view is also presented for the role of the hippocampus in the memory process. It is postulated that long-term explicit memory traces in the neocortex are laid down by phasic coactivation of forebrain projecting monoaminergic systems above some basal firing rate, such as the rostral serotonergic raphe, which projects diffusely to the cortex and according to a modified Hebbian principle. This is the proposed principal function of the hippocampal theta rhythm. The phasic activation of the cholinergic basal forebrain is mediated by projections from a separate cortical structure, possibly the lateral prefrontal cortex. Phasic muscarinic receptor activation is proposed to strengthen implicit memory traces (at a synaptic level) in the neocortex. Thus, the latter are spared by medial temporal surgery explaining the dissociation of explicit from implicit memory.

title: Systematic review of therapeutic interventions in human prion disease.
abstract: Human prion diseases represent a group of rare, invariably fatal neurodegenerative disorders characterized by the accumulation of misfolded prion protein (PrPSc). Despite decades of research, no curative treatments exist, and therapeutic options remain limited. We conducted a systematic review to evaluate the efficacy of pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions reported in human prion disease cases. We searched MEDLINE, EMBASE, and Cochrane databases from 1990 to 2007 for studies reporting therapeutic interventions in patients with confirmed or probable prion disease, including Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, fatal familial insomnia, and Gerstmann-Sträussler-Scheinker syndrome. Case reports, case series, and clinical trials were included. Data extraction focused on intervention type, dosage, duration, clinical outcomes, and survival. From 1,847 initially identified articles, 43 studies met inclusion criteria, encompassing 312 patients. Quinacrine was the most frequently reported intervention (18 studies, 156 patients), showing no significant improvement in survival or clinical progression. Other antiprion compounds including fluphenazine, chlorpromazine, and pentosan polysulfate demonstrated similarly disappointing results. Symptomatic treatments with antiepileptics, antipsychotics, and sleep medications provided modest symptomatic relief in some cases. Two small trials of immunomodulatory approaches showed preliminary signals of potential benefit but lacked statistical power. Our systematic review reveals that despite numerous therapeutic attempts, no intervention has demonstrated clear efficacy in altering the natural history of human prion diseases. Current evidence supports only symptomatic management. Further research into novel therapeutic targets and larger controlled trials are urgently needed for these devastating disorders.

title: Microbore liquid chromatography with UV detection to study the in vivo passage of compound 21, a non-peptidergic AT₂ receptor agonist, to the striatum in rats.
abstract: The angiotensin II type 2 (AT₂) receptor has emerged as a potential therapeutic target for neurodegenerative disorders, yet limited information exists regarding brain penetration of AT₂ receptor agonists. Compound 21, a novel non-peptidergic AT₂ receptor agonist, has shown neuroprotective properties in vitro, but its pharmacokinetic profile in brain tissue remains uncharacterized. We developed and validated a sensitive microbore liquid chromatography method coupled with UV detection to quantify compound 21 concentrations in rat striatal tissue following systemic administration. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=6 per time point) received intravenous bolus injections of compound 21 (10 mg/kg). Animals were sacrificed at 15, 30, 60, 120, and 240 minutes post-injection, and striatal tissue was rapidly extracted and homogenized. Chromatographic separation was achieved using a C18 microbore column (2.1 × 150 mm) with a mobile phase of acetonitrile-water (65:35, v/v) containing 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. UV detection was performed at 254 nm. The method demonstrated excellent linearity (r² = 0.998) over the range 0.1-50 μg/mL with a lower limit of quantification of 0.1 μg/mL. Compound 21 achieved peak striatal concentrations of 2.8 ± 0.4 μg/g tissue at 30 minutes, with a brain-to-plasma ratio of 0.35 ± 0.08. The elimination half-life from striatal tissue was 78 ± 12 minutes. This analytical method provides a reliable tool for studying AT₂ receptor agonist pharmacokinetics and confirms significant brain penetration of compound 21, supporting its potential for CNS therapeutic applications.

title: Evaluation of neuronal cell death by laser scanning cytometry.
abstract: We developed a method in which laser scanning cytometry (LSC) is applied to evaluate cell viability. Neuronal cell death induced by glutamic acid, serum potassium deprivation and 3-nitropropionic acid was studied in cerebellar granule cells by neutral red assay (NR) and LSC, using propidium iodide (PI) as fluorescent dye. PI labeled the nuclei of dead neurons and increased fluorescence was measured using a laser scanning cytometer. Similar levels of damage for each injury were detected by NR or LSC. The protocol presented here, provides a fast and sensitive assay for the analysis of neuronal viability using LSC, and can be used to study new neuroprotective drugs in neuronal cell cultures.

title: [The role of positron emission tomography in neuropharmacology in the living human brain and drug development].
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful neuroimaging technique for investigating neuropharmacological mechanisms in the living human brain, offering unprecedented opportunities for drug development and clinical research. This review examines the current applications and future potential of PET in neuropharmacology, with particular emphasis on receptor binding studies and drug occupancy measurements. We analyzed data from 127 PET studies conducted between 1995-1999 using various radioligands including [11C]raclopride for dopamine D2 receptors, [18F]fluorodopa for dopaminergic function, and [11C]flumazenil for GABA-A receptors. PET methodology allows quantitative assessment of neurotransmitter receptor density, binding affinity, and drug-receptor interactions with high spatial resolution and sensitivity. Results demonstrate that PET can effectively measure receptor occupancy by psychotropic medications, with typical antipsychotics showing 70-90% D2 receptor occupancy at therapeutic doses, while atypical antipsychotics exhibit lower occupancy levels (60-80%) correlating with reduced extrapyramidal side effects. Furthermore, PET studies have revealed significant correlations between receptor occupancy and clinical response in schizophrenia and depression. The technique has proven invaluable for determining optimal dosing regimens, predicting side effect profiles, and understanding individual variations in drug response. PET neuroimaging represents a critical bridge between preclinical pharmacology and clinical therapeutics, enabling rational drug development and personalized medicine approaches. Future applications include investigation of novel neurotransmitter systems and development of more specific radioligands for emerging therapeutic targets in neuropsychiatric disorders.
journal: Nihon rinsho. Japanese journal of clinical medicine

title: Neuropharmacology of the Neuropsychiatric Symptoms of Dementia and Role of Pain: Essential Oil of Bergamot as a Novel Therapeutic Approach.
abstract: Neuropsychiatric symptoms (NPS) including agitation, anxiety, and depression significantly impact quality of life in dementia patients and represent major clinical challenges. Emerging evidence suggests chronic pain may exacerbate NPS through shared neuroinflammatory pathways involving microglial activation and altered neurotransmitter signaling. Essential oil of bergamot (Citrus bergamia) contains bioactive compounds including linalool and linalyl acetate that demonstrate neuroprotective properties, yet its therapeutic potential for dementia-related NPS remains unexplored. We investigated bergamot essential oil's effects on NPS and pain-related behaviors using the 5xFAD transgenic mouse model of Alzheimer's disease. Twenty-four 8-month-old mice received either bergamot oil aromatherapy (0.1% v/v, 30 min daily) or control treatment for 4 weeks. Behavioral assessments included elevated plus maze, forced swim test, and von Frey mechanical sensitivity testing. Post-mortem analysis examined hippocampal and frontal cortex levels of inflammatory markers (IL-1β, TNF-α), neurotransmitters (serotonin, GABA), and pain-related neuropeptides. Bergamot treatment significantly reduced anxiety-like behaviors (p<0.01) and depressive symptoms (p<0.05) compared to controls. Mechanical pain sensitivity was notably decreased following treatment (p<0.01). Neurochemical analysis revealed reduced neuroinflammation with decreased IL-1β (-35%) and TNF-α (-28%) expression, alongside increased serotonergic signaling (+42%) and GABAergic activity (+31%). Substance P levels, associated with pain processing, were significantly diminished (-39%). These findings suggest bergamot essential oil ameliorates NPS through anti-inflammatory mechanisms and neurotransmitter modulation while addressing pain-related components, supporting its potential as a complementary therapeutic intervention for dementia-associated neuropsychiatric complications.

title: Combinatorial Oxytocin Neuropharmacology in Social Cognition.
abstract: Social cognition encompasses complex behavioral processes including empathy, trust, and social memory that are critically regulated by neuropeptide signaling systems. While oxytocin (OT) has emerged as a key modulator of prosocial behavior, recent evidence suggests that its effects depend heavily on interactions with other neurotransmitter systems. Here, we investigated the combinatorial effects of oxytocin with dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic signaling on social cognitive performance in healthy adults. Using a double-blind, placebo-controlled design, 120 participants received intranasal oxytocin (24 IU) combined with either L-DOPA (100mg), citalopram (20mg), or lorazepam (1mg) before completing validated social cognition tasks including emotion recognition, theory of mind, and trust paradigms. Neuroimaging data were acquired using task-based fMRI during social decision-making. Results revealed that oxytocin-dopamine co-administration significantly enhanced trust behavior (p<0.001) and increased activation in the ventral tegmental area and nucleus accumbens compared to oxytocin alone. The oxytocin-serotonin combination improved emotion recognition accuracy (p<0.01) with corresponding increases in amygdala-prefrontal connectivity. Conversely, oxytocin-GABA co-treatment impaired social memory performance and reduced anterior cingulate cortex activation. Computational modeling indicated that these combinatorial effects arise from synergistic modulation of prediction error signals during social learning. These findings demonstrate that oxytocin's prosocial effects emerge through dynamic interactions with classical neurotransmitter systems, suggesting that effective therapeutic interventions for social cognitive disorders may require multi-target pharmacological approaches rather than single-agent treatments.

title: Changes in cerebrospinal fluid neurochemistry during pregnancy.
abstract: Little is known about changes in brain function that may occur during pregnancy. Studies in rodents and sheep suggest that several brain neurotransmitter and neurohormonal systems known to modulate anxiety may be altered during pregnancy. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and plasma samples were obtained from 21 women (during weeks 38-39 of pregnancy) who were undergoing elective cesarean section and from 22 healthy nonpregnant women. The CSF levels of g-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycolwere reduced in pregnant women. There were no changes in CSF glutamate, 5-hydroxyindoleactic acid, and homovanillic acid. There was a large increase in CSF prolactin in pregnant women and also a trend toward an elevation in CSF oxytocin. Levels of prolactin, but not oxytocin, in CSF and plasma were correlated in pregnant women. These results suggest that pregnancy alters regulation of brain GABA, norepinephrine, and prolactin, which may play a role in changes in vulnerability to anxiety and depression during pregnancy and postpartum. Prolactin circulating in the bloodstream seems to be the major source of CSF prolactin during pregnancy.

title: Treating prolonged symptoms of mild traumatic brain injury: neuropharmacology.
abstract: Mild traumatic brain injury (mTBI) affects millions annually, with 10-15% of patients developing persistent symptoms including headache, cognitive dysfunction, and mood disturbances lasting beyond three months post-injury. Despite the prevalence of post-concussive syndrome, evidence-based pharmacological interventions remain limited. This systematic review and meta-analysis examined the efficacy of neuropharmacological treatments for prolonged mTBI symptoms. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of MEDLINE, EMBASE, and Cochrane databases from 2000-2013, identifying 23 randomized controlled trials and 31 observational studies involving 2,847 patients with persistent symptoms following mTBI. Primary outcome measures included cognitive performance, headache frequency, and validated symptom scales. Secondary analyses examined specific drug classes including cholinesterase inhibitors, dopamine agonists, stimulants, antidepressants, and anticonvulsants. Methylphenidate demonstrated significant improvement in attention and processing speed (effect size 0.72, p<0.001), while amantadine showed modest benefits for executive function and fatigue (effect size 0.43, p=0.02). Topiramate reduced headache frequency by 65% compared to placebo (p<0.001). SSRIs improved mood symptoms but showed no cognitive benefits. Cholinesterase inhibitors yielded inconsistent results across studies. Combination therapies targeting multiple symptom domains showed promise but require further investigation. Current evidence supports targeted pharmacological interventions for specific symptom clusters in persistent mTBI, though optimal treatment protocols remain to be established. Future research should focus on biomarker-guided therapy selection and standardized outcome measures to advance precision medicine approaches in this challenging patient population.

title: Neuropathology in Alzheimer's disease: target of pharmacotherapy.
abstract: The pathogenesis of AD is complex and not yet fully understood. A number of factors, including amyloid plaques, NFTs, and inflammatory processes, are likely to contribute to development of the disease. Acetylcholine and glutamate are intimately involved in learning and memory. Hypotheses implicating defects within both neurotransmitter systems in AI) are recognized. This knowledge coupled with ongoing discoveries about the multiple pathophysiologic pathways involved in development and progression of AD has given rise to several plausible therapeutic targets. Therapies addressing some of these targets (ie, acetylcholine, glutamate) have already shown clinical efficacy in treating AD while other targets continue to be investigated.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Nurse Practitioners

title: Drug-gut microbiota interactions: implications for neuropharmacology.
abstract: The fate and activity of drugs are frequently dictated not only by the host per se but also by the microorganisms present in the gastrointestinal tract. The gut microbiome is known to, both directly and indirectly, affect drug metabolism. More evidence now hints at the effects that drugs can have on the function and composition of the gut microbiome. Both microbiota-mediated alterations in drug metabolism and drug-mediated alterations in the gut microbiome can have beneficial or detrimental effects on the host. Greater insights into the mechanisms driving these reciprocal drug-gut microbiota interactions are needed to guide the development of microbiome-targeted dietary or pharmacological interventions, which may have the potential to enhance drug efficacy or reduce drug side effects. In this review, we explore the relationship between drugs and the gut microbiome, with a specific focus on potential mechanisms underpinning the drug-mediated alterations on the gut microbiome and the potential implications for psychoactive drugs. LINKED ARTICLES: This article is part of a themed section on When Pharmacology Meets the Microbiome: New Targets for Therapeutics? To view the other articles in this section visit http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bph.v175.24/issuetoc.

title: Synthesis and pharmacological evaluation of 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines.
abstract: Novel 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinoline derivatives were synthesized and evaluated for their potential neuropharmacological activities. The tetrahydroisoquinoline scaffold has garnered significant interest due to its presence in numerous biologically active compounds, particularly those targeting dopaminergic and adrenergic systems. A series of twenty-two compounds (8a-v) were prepared via Pictet-Spengler cyclization followed by phenoxy substitution at the 4-position, while eight chromeno-fused derivatives (12a-h) were synthesized through intramolecular cyclization of appropriately substituted precursors. All compounds were characterized by ¹H NMR, ¹³C NMR, IR spectroscopy, and mass spectrometry. In vitro binding assays were performed against dopamine D₁ and D₂ receptors, α₁- and α₂-adrenergic receptors, and 5-HT₂A receptors using rat brain membrane preparations. Selected compounds were further evaluated in behavioral models including locomotor activity, rotarod performance, and forced swim tests in male Wistar rats. Compounds 8f, 8m, and 12c exhibited notable affinity for D₂ receptors (Ki = 45-89 nM) with good selectivity over D₁ receptors. Compound 8m demonstrated significant antidepressant-like activity in the forced swim test without impairing motor coordination. The chromeno-fused derivatives showed moderate α₁-adrenergic antagonist activity. Structure-activity relationship analysis revealed that electron-withdrawing substituents on the phenoxy ring enhanced D₂ receptor affinity. These findings suggest that 4-phenoxy-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives represent promising lead compounds for developing novel antipsychotic and antidepressant agents with improved therapeutic profiles.

title: Neuroglialpharmacology: white matter pathophysiologies and psychiatric treatments.
abstract: Psychotropic treatments such as second generation or atypical antipsychotics are efficacious in a wide spectrum of psychiatric disorders ranging from schizophrenia to depression, bipolar disorder, and autism. These treatments are associated with peripheral metabolic derangements that are often also present in drug-naive patients. Furthermore, altering lipid composition/levels (with omega 3 fatty acids) and ameliorating oxidative toxicities may treat/prevent disease. The above observations are reexamined from the perspective of a myelin-centered model of the human brain. The model proposes that the human brain's extensive myelination required higher metabolic resources that caused evolutionary adaptations resulting in our quadratic (inverted U) myelination trajectory that peaks in the sixth decade of life. It further proposes that optimal brain function depends on exquisite action potential synchronization that myelin makes possible and that myelin's exceptional vulnerability to subtle metabolic/oxidative abnormalities may promote both developmental and degenerative diseases. Available data are integrated herein to suggest that widely used psychotropic treatments have under-appreciated CNS metabolic and neurotransmitter effects on myelination, its plasticity, and repair that may substantially contribute to their mechanisms of action.

title: The use of imaging in the early development of neuropharmacological drugs: a survey of approved NDAs.
abstract: The integration of neuroimaging techniques into early-phase drug development has emerged as a critical tool for advancing neuropharmacological therapeutics, yet systematic evaluation of its regulatory impact remains limited. We conducted a comprehensive survey of New Drug Applications (NDAs) approved by the FDA between 2000-2007 for central nervous system indications to assess the utilization and influence of imaging data in regulatory decision-making. All publicly available FDA review documents for approved neurological and psychiatric medications (n=47) were systematically analyzed for inclusion of neuroimaging studies, imaging modalities employed, and stated regulatory relevance. Positron emission tomography (PET) was incorporated in 68% of submissions, functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) in 34%, and single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) in 23%. Imaging data most frequently supported proof-of-mechanism (72% of cases), dose selection (45%), and biomarker validation (38%). PET studies demonstrating target engagement were particularly influential in antipsychotic and antidepressant approvals, with receptor occupancy data directly informing dosing recommendations in 15 applications. Imaging-derived biomarkers showed significant correlation with clinical efficacy measures in Phase II trials (r=0.67, p<0.001) and were associated with reduced late-stage attrition rates compared to programs without imaging components (18% vs. 34%, p=0.02). These findings demonstrate that neuroimaging has become integral to contemporary neuropharmaceutical development, providing mechanistic insights that enhance regulatory confidence and optimize clinical trial design. Strategic implementation of imaging biomarkers early in development programs may substantially improve success rates for neurotherapeutics.

title: The rotational model and microdialysis: Significance for dopamine signalling, clinical studies, and beyond.
abstract: The detailed anatomy of the monoamine pathways of the rat by the students of Nils-Ake Hillarp provided the basis for a neurocircuitry targeting pharmacology. Further progress was achieved by the introduction of 6-hydroxydopamine as a tool for performing specific lesions, leading to the first stereotaxic mapping of the monoamine pathways in the rat brain by Urban Ungerstedt at the Karolinska Institutet, Stockholm, Sweden. Unilateral intracerebral injections with 6-hydroxydopamine led to the proposal of 'Rotational Behaviour', as a classical model for screening drugs useful for alleviating Parkinson's disease and other neuropathologies. The direction of the rotational behaviour induced by drugs administrated to lesioned rats reveals their mechanisms of action on dopamine synapses, as demonstrated when rotational behaviour was combined with microdialysis. The model was useful for proposing a role of dopamine receptors in the gating of the flow of information through different efferent pathways of the basal ganglia. It is established now that the coupling of dopamine receptors is regulated by a number of proteins acting as GTPases, the regulators of G-protein signalling (RGS) family. More than 20 RGS proteins have been identified, organised into subfamilies based on structural features and specificity for different G-protein subunits. These protein subfamilies represent alternative pathways gating the flow of information generated in the basal ganglia. Microdialysis has been developed as a general tool for studying tissue and organ chemistry, leading to a truly translational venture as microdialysis is brought into clinical use, monitoring energy metabolism following global or focal ischemia in the neurosurgery and general medicine scenario.

title: Neurophamacology of yawning.
abstract: Yawning is a highly conserved behavioral phenomenon observed across vertebrate species, yet its neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. While traditionally associated with fatigue or boredom, emerging evidence suggests yawning involves complex neurotransmitter interactions within the hypothalamus, brainstem, and limbic structures. This study investigated the neuropharmacological basis of yawning using a rat model with selective pharmacological interventions. Adult Sprague-Dawley rats (n=96) were administered various neurotransmitter agonists and antagonists, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, cholinergic, and GABAergic compounds. Yawning frequency was quantified during 60-minute observation periods following drug administration. Apomorphine, a dopamine receptor agonist, significantly increased yawning frequency (p<0.001), with peak effects occurring 15-30 minutes post-injection. This response was completely blocked by haloperidol pretreatment, confirming dopaminergic mediation. Oxytocin administration also induced pronounced yawning, while the oxytocin receptor antagonist L-368,899 reduced baseline yawning behavior. Serotonin 5-HT2C receptor activation with mCPP enhanced yawning, whereas 5-HT1A agonist 8-OH-DPAT produced inhibitory effects. Cholinergic stimulation with physostigmine moderately increased yawning frequency, while muscarinic blockade with atropine showed minimal effects. GABA-A receptor activation suppressed yawning behavior. These findings demonstrate that yawning is regulated by a complex interplay of neurotransmitter systems, with dopaminergic and oxytocinergic pathways playing primary roles. Understanding yawning's neuropharmacology may provide insights into arousal mechanisms and have implications for neurological conditions characterized by excessive yawning.

title: Applying small quantities of multiple compounds to defined locations of in vitro brain slices.
abstract: When studying in vitro brain slices, rapidly applying multiple agonists, antagonists, drugs, or modulatory compounds is a significant technical problem. There are three major ways that multiple compounds are applied to slices: by bath, via a U-tube device, or by pressure application using a "puffer" pipette. Each of these methods has advantages and disadvantages, making each more appropriate for particular purposes. Because puffer pipettes have a small, sharp tip, they are best suited to apply a small quantity of a compound to a well-defined location within the slice. When used in this way, puffer pipettes have two shortcomings. Solution leaking from the tip of the pipette can contaminate the signal, and it is difficult to apply more than one test solution to exactly the same local area of the slice. We describe methods and newly designed devices aimed at overcoming those limitations. Relatively inexpensive approaches are described to apply eight different solutions to the same exact location deep within a brain slice. The validity of the approach is verified by measuring ligand-gated channel currents activated by glutamate (Glu), acetylcholine (ACh), and gamma-amino butyric acid (GABA).

title: Psychiatric safety of ketamine in psychopharmacology research.
abstract: A growing number of investigators are studying ketamine effects in healthy human subjects, but concerns remain about its safety as a research tool. Therefore, it is timely to revisit the safety of subanesthetic doses of ketamine in experimental psychopharmacology studies. To report on the safety of laboratory studies with subanesthetic doses of ketamine in healthy humans using an existing dataset. Medically healthy subjects with no personal or familial Axis I psychotic spectrum disorders were administered subanesthetic doses of ketamine by intravenous infusion in a series of clinical investigations from 1989 to 2005. The safety of ketamine administration was monitored in these subjects. Four hundred and fifty subjects received at least one dose of active ketamine. Eight hundred and thirty three active ketamine and 621 placebo infusions were administered. Ten adverse mental status events were documented in nine subjects/infusions that were deemed related to ketamine administration (2% of subjects, 1.45% of infusions). All but one adverse reaction resolved by the end of the test session. The side effects in the remaining individual were no longer clinically significant within 4 days of the test session. No residual sequelae were observed. Ketamine administration at subanesthetic doses appears to present an acceptable level of risk for carefully screened populations of healthy human subjects in the context of clinical research programs that intensively monitor subjects throughout their study participation.

title: Traditional Chinese medicine and Western psychopharmacology: building bridges.
abstract: Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) has been used for millennia to treat neuropsychiatric disorders, yet the underlying mechanisms of action remain poorly understood from a Western pharmacological perspective. This review synthesizes current evidence linking TCM formulations to established neurotransmitter pathways and explores opportunities for integrated therapeutic approaches. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of peer-reviewed studies published between 2000-2012 examining the neurochemical effects of commonly prescribed TCM herbs and formulations used in psychiatric treatment. Particular attention was given to compounds affecting monoaminergic, GABAergic, and cholinergic systems. Database searches identified 147 relevant studies, of which 89 met inclusion criteria for detailed analysis. Key findings demonstrate that several TCM herbs exhibit measurable effects on neurotransmitter metabolism: Ginkgo biloba extracts enhanced acetylcholine availability and showed neuroprotective properties; Panax ginseng modulated dopaminergic signaling and stress-related cortisol responses; and Schisandra chinensis demonstrated GABAergic activity comparable to mild anxiolytics. Polygala tenuifolia showed promising antidepressant-like effects through serotonin reuptake inhibition. However, significant methodological limitations were identified across studies, including inadequate standardization of herbal preparations and limited pharmacokinetic data. Despite these constraints, the evidence suggests that TCM approaches may complement conventional psychopharmacology through multi-target mechanisms that differ from single-compound Western medications. Future research should prioritize rigorous clinical trials with standardized preparations and systematic investigation of herb-drug interactions to establish safety profiles for integrated treatment protocols.

title: The fly as a model for neurodegenerative diseases: is it worth the jump?
abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases are responsible for agonizing symptoms that take their toll on the fragile human life. Aberrant protein processing and accumulation are considered to be the culprits of many classical neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, tauopathies, Parkinson's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, hereditary spastic paraplegia and various polyglutamine diseases. However, recently it has been shown that toxic RNA species or disruption of RNA processing and metabolism may be partly to blame as clearly illustrated in spinal muscular atrophy, spinocerebellar ataxia 8 and fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome. At the dawn of the twenty-first century, the fruit fly or Drosophila melanogaster has taken its place at the forefront of an uphill struggle to unveil the molecular and cellular pathophysiology of both protein- and RNA-induced neurodegeneration, as well as discovery of novel drug targets. We review here the various fly models of neurodegenerative conditions, and summarise the novel insights that the fly has contributed to the field of neuroprotection and neurodegeneration.

title: The Psychopharmacology of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Preclinical Roadmap.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) affects 2-3% of the global population and represents one of the most debilitating psychiatric conditions, yet current pharmacological interventions remain limited and poorly understood mechanistically. While selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) constitute first-line treatment, approximately 40-60% of patients show inadequate response, highlighting critical gaps in our understanding of OCD neurobiology. This review synthesizes current preclinical models and emerging pharmacological targets to provide a translational framework for OCD drug development. We systematically analyzed behavioral paradigms including marble burying, schedule-induced polydipsia, and signal attenuation models in rodents, alongside their neurobiological correlates in cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuitry. Beyond the classical serotonergic hypothesis, converging evidence implicates glutamatergic dysfunction, particularly NMDA receptor hypofunction and altered metabotropic glutamate receptor signaling within the orbitofrontal cortex and striatum. Preclinical studies demonstrate therapeutic potential for glutamate modulators including memantine, riluzole, and mGluR2/3 agonists. Additionally, emerging targets encompass GABAergic interneuron dysfunction, dopaminergic imbalances in reward processing circuits, and neuroimmune activation involving microglial cytokine release. Novel approaches targeting the endocannabinoid system, particularly CB1 receptor modulation, show promise in reversing compulsive-like behaviors. Optogenetic and chemogenetic studies have refined our understanding of specific neural populations driving repetitive behaviors, revealing distinct roles for direct versus indirect pathway medium spiny neurons. These preclinical insights provide a roadmap for developing more effective, mechanistically-informed treatments for OCD, emphasizing the need for circuit-specific therapeutic approaches beyond traditional monoaminergic strategies.

title: Traditional Chinese medicine and Western psychopharmacology: building bridges.
abstract: This paper demonstrates that in the treatment of psychiatric disorders, there are striking similarities between the mechanisms of psychoactive agents used in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) and those of western psychopharmacology. While western researchers search for new treatments and novel mechanisms of action, investigators in Asia are analyzing traditional remedies in order to understand the mechanisms responsible for their effectiveness. A review of contemporary pharmacologic studies of agents used in TCM for psychiatric indications reveals that virtually all of the active principles of drug action established in 20th century psychopharmacology were encountered empirically in Chinese herbal medicine over the past 2000 years. Building bridges between these two traditions may thus be of benefit to both cultures. In addition to providing western patients with a wider selection of treatment options, the effort may help Asian clinicians and researchers avoid some of the errors that have troubled their western counterparts.

title: Pharmacotherapy in children and adolescents with pervasive developmental disorders.
abstract: Children with autism and the related PDDs may benefit from serotonin reuptake inhibitors such as clomipramine, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, and sertraline for targeting repetitive thoughts and behaviors, anxiety, and depressed mood. To date, however, there are few controlled studies of these agents in children with PDD, so definitive evidence is lacking. Despite preliminary results in favor of naltrexone, neuroleptic medication appears to be effective for reducing aggression, self-injurious behavior, agitation, and stereotypies. The primary drawback with traditional neuroleptics is risk of short- and long-term side effects. The newer atypical neuroleptics have the potential for benefit with fewer extrapyramidal side effects, but more study is needed to establish their efficacy and safety. Children on neuroleptic medications should be started at the lowest possible dose, with gradual increases until clinical benefit is observed. The likelihood of untoward side effects is increased if the medication dose is increased rapidly. Baseline measurement of target behaviors can be aided by using standardized scales. The presence of abnormal movements should be assessed before initiating treatment and at regular intervals during the course of treatment--including after medication withdrawal. Weight gain is emerging as a recurrent side effect with the atypical neuroleptics. Thus, weight should be monitored, and the family should be advised about a diet baseline. As with all treatments of children with severe behavioral difficulties, pharmacotherapy should be instituted in the context of an integrated treatment plan.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychiatric nursing : official publication of the Association of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nurses, Inc

title: A rodent model of appetitive discrimination with concomitant evaluation of anxiety-like behavior.
abstract: Food-seeking behavior and anxiety represent interconnected behavioral domains that are frequently disrupted in neuropsychiatric disorders, yet few experimental paradigms allow simultaneous assessment of both processes. We developed and validated a novel behavioral protocol that combines appetitive discrimination learning with concurrent anxiety evaluation in rodents. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were trained in a modified Y-maze apparatus containing distinct visual and olfactory cues paired with either food reward or neutral outcomes. The maze incorporated an elevated central platform and open arms to assess anxiety-like behaviors during discrimination trials. Animals underwent 14 days of training followed by pharmacological validation using anxiogenic (yohimbine, 2mg/kg) and anxiolytic (diazepam, 1mg/kg) compounds. Behavioral measures included discrimination accuracy, response latency, center platform time, and open arm exploration. Results demonstrated robust appetitive learning, with discrimination accuracy reaching 78.3±4.2% by day 10. Anxiogenic treatment significantly impaired discrimination performance (62.1±5.8% vs. vehicle 76.9±3.9%, p<0.01) while reducing center platform time and open arm entries. Conversely, anxiolytic treatment maintained discrimination accuracy while increasing exploratory behavior in anxiety-provoking zones. Individual discrimination performance correlated negatively with baseline anxiety measures (r=-0.61, p<0.001), suggesting trait-dependent interactions between learning and emotional processing. This paradigm provides a time-efficient method for evaluating the bidirectional relationship between appetitive cognition and anxiety-related behaviors, offering applications for screening anxiolytic compounds and investigating neural mechanisms underlying motivation-anxiety interactions in neuropharmacological research.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of aqueous extract of Anaphe venata larva (Notondotidae) in rats.
abstract: Consumption of Anaphe larva had been reported to cause seasonal ataxia and impaired consciousness. Therefore this study examined the neuropharmacological and mechanism(s) of action of aqueous extract of Anaphe venata in rats. Behavioural effects namely rearing, stretching, sniffing and ataxia were determined after the intraperitoneal administration of aqueous extract of Anaphe larva in rats. Animals were divided into groups and graded doses (100, 200 and 400 mg/kg, i.p.) of extract were administered. The control group was administered normal saline (vehicle). The effects of scopolamine (3 mg/kg, i.p.), flumazenil (2 mg/kg, i.p.), naloxone (2.5 mg/kg, i.p.), and thiamine (1 mg/kg, i.p.) on the observed behavioral changes were also examined. The effects of the extract administered intraperitoneally at a dose of 200 mg/kg on the amphetamine-induced stereotypy and locomotion were evaluated. Aqueous anaphe extract induced significant (p< 0.01) stretching and ataxia behavioural effects while it inhibited rearing behaviour when compared with the vehicle-treated group. However, it had no significant effect on sniffing behaviour. Scopolamine reversed all the effects of the extract on rearing, stretching and ataxia. Both Flumazenil and naloxone only reversed the effects of the extract on stretching and ataxia-induced behaviours significantly. However, thiamine potentiated both stretching and ataxia-induced behaviours. The extract inhibited the amphetamine-induced stereotype behaviour and locomotion. In conclusion, these results showed that these anaphe-induced behavioural effects are mediated via cholinergic, GABAergic, opioidergic and dopaminergic receptor systems with strong muscarinic-cholinergic receptors involvement in ataxia-induced behaviour. We therefore suggest that muscranic-cholinergic like drugs may be of benefit in the management of patients that present with clinical condition of seasonal ataxia.
journal: African journal of traditional, complementary, and alternative medicines : AJTCAM

title: The neuropsychopharmacology of fronto-executive function: monoaminergic modulation.
abstract: Executive functions encompass higher-order cognitive processes including working memory, cognitive flexibility, and inhibitory control, which are critically dependent on prefrontal cortical circuits. Accumulating evidence indicates that monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems—dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin—play fundamental roles in modulating fronto-executive performance through distinct but overlapping mechanisms. This review synthesizes recent advances in understanding how monoaminergic signaling influences executive control processes. Dopaminergic modulation of prefrontal function follows an inverted-U shaped dose-response relationship, with optimal performance occurring at intermediate levels of D1 receptor activation. Working memory capacity and cognitive flexibility are particularly sensitive to dopaminergic tone, with both hypo- and hyperdopaminergic states impairing performance. Noradrenergic influences on executive function are mediated primarily through α2A-adrenoceptors in prefrontal cortex, where moderate activation enhances working memory and attentional control while excessive stimulation produces deficits. The norepinephrine system also modulates the balance between focused attention and cognitive flexibility through differential activation of α2- versus α1-adrenoceptors. Serotonergic modulation of executive function occurs principally through 5-HT2A and 5-HT1A receptors, influencing behavioral inhibition and cognitive flexibility. Interactions between monoaminergic systems add further complexity, with dopamine-norepinephrine and dopamine-serotonin interactions being particularly important for executive control. These findings have significant implications for understanding psychiatric disorders characterized by executive dysfunction, including schizophrenia, ADHD, and depression, and suggest that optimal executive performance requires precise balance among monoaminergic systems rather than simply increased or decreased activity in any single system.

title: The psychopharmacology of brain vascular disease/poststroke depression.
abstract: Poststroke depression (PSD) is a frequent complication and source of suffering among stroke survivors. Assessment of mood, suicidal ideation, and other neuropsychiatric disturbances that can co-occur or share similar features with depression are important aspects of clinical stroke care. Pharmacotherapy is the first-line treatment of PSD, with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) being agents of first choice. The evidence for SSRIs, newer generation antidepressants, and tricyclic antidepressants for PSD will be reviewed with consideration of their side effect profiles and use for other neuropsychiatric comorbidities. Additional therapeutic options, such as cognitive enhancing medications, brain stimulation, and psychotherapy can also be considered. Given the prevalence of PSD, preventive pharmacologic strategies may be effective alternatives. Ultimately, the choice of treatment will be determined by the type and severity of medical and psychiatric comorbidities as well as the preferences of the patient.

title: Imaging drugs with and without clinical analgesic efficacy.
abstract: The behavioral response to pain is driven by sensory and affective components, each of which is mediated by the CNS. Subjective pain ratings are used as readouts when appraising potential analgesics; however, pain ratings alone cannot enable a characterization of CNS pain circuitry during pain processing or how this circuitry is modulated pharmacologically. Having a more objective readout of potential analgesic effects may allow improved understanding and detection of pharmacological efficacy for pain. The pharmacological/functional magnetic resonance imaging (phMRI/fMRI) methodology can be used to objectively evaluate drug action on the CNS. In this context, we aimed to evaluate two drugs that had been developed as analgesics: one that is efficacious for pain (buprenorphine (BUP)) and one that failed as an analgesic in clinical trials aprepitant (APREP). Using phMRI, we observed that activation induced solely by BUP was present in regions with μ-opioid receptors, whereas APREP-induced activation was seen in regions expressing NK(1) receptors. However, significant pharmacological modulation of functional connectivity in pain-processing pathways was only observed following BUP administration. By implementing an evoked pain fMRI paradigm, these drugs could also be differentiated by comparing the respective fMRI signals in CNS circuits mediating sensory and affective components of pain. We report a correlation of functional connectivity and evoked pain fMRI measures with pain ratings as well as peak drug concentration. This investigation demonstrates how CNS-acting drugs can be compared, and how the phMRI/fMRI methodology may be used with conventional measures to better evaluate candidate analgesics in small subject cohorts.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Are You Ready for Change?
abstract: Under the direction of the Journal's new Psychopharmacology Section Editor, a different approach will be taken: psychotropic drugs will be classified based on their neurobiological mechanism of action in the brain. This approach focuses on how the drug acts on brain cells and neurotransmitters involved in the specific symptoms presented by patients. The current article introduces the approach and will help clinicians think about medications as they relate to the neurobiological functions they are trying to correct. [Journal of Psychosocial Nursing and Mental Health Services, 56(2), 11-13.].
journal: Journal of psychosocial nursing and mental health services

title: From the search for a molecular code of memory to the role of neurotransmitters: a historical perspective.
abstract: The quest to understand the molecular basis of memory formation has undergone significant paradigmatic shifts over the past five decades. Early investigations focused primarily on structural modifications at synapses, while contemporary research has increasingly emphasized the critical role of neurotransmitter systems in memory consolidation and retrieval. This historical review examines the evolution of memory research from initial attempts to identify a putative "molecular code" to current understanding of neurotransmitter-mediated mechanisms. We analyzed seminal publications from 1950-2003 using systematic literature review methodology, categorizing findings into distinct conceptual frameworks and identifying key theoretical transitions. Our analysis reveals three major phases: the initial search for memory-specific molecules (1950s-1970s), the emergence of synaptic plasticity models emphasizing structural changes (1980s-1990s), and the recent integration of neurotransmitter dynamics with molecular mechanisms (1990s-present). Particularly significant was the recognition that acetylcholine, dopamine, and glutamate systems provide both the immediate signaling required for memory formation and the modulatory influences necessary for memory strength and persistence. The convergence of molecular biology techniques with pharmacological approaches has demonstrated that memory formation involves complex interactions between neurotransmitter release, receptor activation, and downstream signaling cascades rather than simple structural encoding. These findings suggest that future therapeutic interventions for memory disorders should target neurotransmitter system dysfunction rather than solely focusing on synaptic structural modifications, representing a fundamental shift in translational memory research strategies.

title: A novel method for monitoring the cell surface expression of heteromeric protein complexes in dispersed neurons and acute hippocampal slices.
abstract: Understanding the cell surface expression and trafficking of heteromeric protein complexes is crucial for elucidating synaptic function and plasticity mechanisms. Current methodologies for monitoring surface expression of multisubunit receptors and channels in native neuronal preparations remain technically challenging and often lack the specificity required to distinguish heteromeric assemblies from homomeric complexes. Here we describe a novel dual-epitope labeling approach that enables real-time monitoring of heteromeric protein complex surface expression in both dispersed cultured neurons and acute brain slices. Our method utilizes simultaneous immunofluorescent detection of extracellular epitopes on different subunits of the target complex, combined with proximity-based fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) analysis to confirm heteromeric assembly at the cell surface. We validated this technique using NMDA receptors containing NR1 and NR2B subunits in primary hippocampal neurons and acute hippocampal slices from postnatal day 14-21 rats. Surface-expressed heteromeric NR1/NR2B complexes showed characteristic FRET efficiency of 0.23 ± 0.03, significantly higher than background levels (0.08 ± 0.02, p<0.001). The method successfully detected dynamic changes in surface expression following pharmacological treatments, including a 40% increase in surface heteromers after forskolin application and a 25% decrease following NMDA treatment. This approach provides superior spatial resolution compared to biochemical methods and maintains tissue architecture integrity compared to dissociated cell preparations. The technique is applicable to various heteromeric complexes and offers a valuable tool for investigating synaptic protein trafficking in physiologically relevant preparations.

title: The use of biomarkers in psychiatric research: how serotonin transporter occupancy explains the dose-response curves of SSRIs.
abstract: This column reviews why signal detection in psychiatric research has been problematic, how the use of biomarkers can help, how and why fixed dose studies are done, and how these studies differ from what clinicians do in practice. The fixed dose studies done with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are used to illustrate general points about clinical trial research methodology relevant to clinical practice. Studies with SSRIs have yielded flat dose-response curves with regard to efficacy but ascending dose-response curves with regard to discontinuation due to adverse effects. These clinical trial findings are explained by studies using serotonin transporter inhibition or occupancy as a surrogate marker for SSRI efficacy and tolerability. Initially, these studies were conducted ex vivo using human platelets as the model system; however, they have now been extended to in vivo measurement of serotonin transporter occupancy in patients using positron emission tomography. The conclusion from this work is that the usually effective, minimum dose of each marketed SSRI produces 70%-80% inhibition or occupancy (depending on the methodology used) of the serotonin transporter; higher rates of inhibition or occupancy do not on average increase efficacy but instead increase early discontinuation rates due to adverse effects. These increased discontinuation rates offset any gain in efficacy when the results are analyzed using the last-observation-carried-forward approach. An understanding of these principles also provides an explanation for what initially may appear to be a conundrum: why some patients can benefit from a dose increase even though, in fixed dose clinical trials, the drug had a flat dose-efficacy curve.

title: Conduction pathways in microtubules, biological quantum computation, and consciousness.
abstract: Technological computation is entering the quantum realm, focusing attention on biomolecular information processing systems such as proteins, as presaged by the work of Michael Conrad. Protein conformational dynamics and pharmacological evidence suggest that protein conformational states-fundamental information units ('bits') in biological systems-are governed by quantum events, and are thus perhaps akin to quantum bits ('qubits') as utilized in quantum computation.
