'Real time' dynamic activities within cells are regulated by the cell cytoskeleton, particularly microtubules (MTs) which are cylindrical lattice polymers of the protein tubulin. Recent evidence shows signaling, communication and conductivity in MTs, and theoretical models have predicted both classical and quantum information processing in MTs. In this paper we show conduction pathways for electron mobility and possible quantum tunneling and superconductivity among aromatic amino acids in tubulins. The pathways within tubulin match helical patterns in the microtubule lattice structure, which lend themselves to topological quantum effects resistant to decoherence. The Penrose-Hameroff 'Orch OR' model of consciousness is reviewed as an example of the possible utility of quantum computation in MTs.

title: Looking at neurotransmitters in the microscope.
abstract: This review article covers the early period of my career. I first summarize research initiated by the late Nils-Ake Hillarp, after his appointment in 1962 as professor in the Department of Histology at Karolinska Institutet. He only lived for three more years, but during this short period he started up a group of ten students who explored various aspects of the three monoamine transmitters, dopamine, noradrenaline and 5-hydroxytryptamine, using the new formaldehyde fluorescence method developed by Bengt Falck and Hillarp in Lund. This method allowed visualization of the cellular localization in the microscope of these monoamines, which introduced a new discipline in neurobiology-chemical neuroanatomy. I then deal with work aiming at localizing the monoamines at the ultrastructural level, as well as attempts to use radioactively labeled aminoacids, especially gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), and autoradiography, to identify, in the microscope, neurons using such transmitters. Finally, our immunohistochemical work together with Kjell Fuxe and the late Menek Goldstein, using antibodies to four monoamine-synthesizing enzymes is summarized, including some aspects on the adrenaline neurons, which had escaped detection with the Falck-Hillarp technique.

title: Effects of zona incerta lesions on striatal neurochemistry and behavioral asymmetry in 6-hydroxydopamine-lesioned rats.
abstract: The zona incerta (ZI) has emerged as a potential modulator of basal ganglia function, yet its role in Parkinson's disease pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This study investigated whether ZI lesions influence striatal neurochemistry and motor asymmetries in the 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model of parkinsonism. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received unilateral 6-OHDA lesions of the medial forebrain bundle, followed by either sham surgery or electrolytic ZI lesions two weeks later. Behavioral asymmetry was assessed using amphetamine-induced rotation testing at 2, 4, and 6 weeks post-6-OHDA lesioning. High-performance liquid chromatography was used to measure dopamine, DOPAC, and HVA concentrations in striatal tissue. Immunohistochemistry quantified tyrosine hydroxylase-positive neurons in the substantia nigra. 6-OHDA lesions produced robust ipsilateral rotational behavior and >85% depletion of striatal dopamine. ZI lesions significantly reduced amphetamine-induced rotations by 42% compared to sham-operated controls (p<0.01) without affecting the extent of nigral cell loss. Striatal dopamine levels were modestly but significantly higher in ZI-lesioned animals (1.8±0.3 ng/mg vs 1.1±0.2 ng/mg tissue, p<0.05), while DOPAC and HVA concentrations remained unchanged. These findings suggest that the ZI influences striatal dopaminergic function and motor asymmetries independently of nigral cell survival in parkinsonian rats. The ZI may represent a novel therapeutic target for modulating basal ganglia output and reducing motor symptoms in Parkinson's disease through mechanisms distinct from dopamine replacement strategies.

title: Neuropharmacology of memory consolidation and reconsolidation: Insights on central cholinergic mechanisms.
abstract: Central cholinergic system is critically involved in all known memory processes. Endogenous acetylcholine release by cholinergic neurons is necessary for modulation of acquisition, encoding, consolidation, reconsolidation, extinction, retrieval and expression. Experiments from our laboratory are mainly focused on elucidating the mechanisms by which acetylcholine modulates memory processes. Blockade of hippocampal alpha-7-nicotinic receptors (α7-nAChRs) with the antagonist methyllycaconitine impairs memory reconsolidation. However, the administration of a α7-nAChR agonist (choline) produce a paradoxical modulation, causing memory enhancement in mice trained with a weak footshock, but memory impairment in animals trained with a strong footshock. All these effects are long-lasting, and depend on the age of the memory trace. This review summarizes and discusses some of our recent findings, particularly regarding the involvement of α7-nAChRs on memory reconsolidation.

title: The Neuropharmacology of Cluster Headache and other Trigeminal Autonomic Cephalalgias.
abstract: Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (TACs) are a group of primary headaches including cluster headache (CH), paroxysmal hemicrania (PH) and short-lasting unilateral neuralgiform headache with conjunctival injection and tearing (SUNCT). Another form, hemicrania continua (HC), is also included this group due to its clinical and pathophysiological similarities. CH is the most common of these syndromes, the others being infrequent in the general population. The pathophysiology of the TACs has been partly elucidated by a number of recent neuroimaging studies, which implicate brain regions associated with nociception (pain matrix). In addition, the hypothalamic activation observed in the course of TAC attacks and the observed efficacy of hypothalamic neurostimulation in CH patients suggest that the hypothalamus is another key structure. Hypothalamic activation may indeed be involved in attack initiation, but it may also lead to a condition of central facilitation underlying the recurrence of pain episodes. The TACs share many pathophysiological features, but are characterised by differences in attack duration and frequency, and to some extent treatment response. Although alternative strategies for the TACs, especially CH, are now emerging (such as neurostimulation techniques), this review focuses on the available pharmacological treatments complying with the most recent guidelines. We discuss the clinical efficacy and tolerability of the currently used drugs. Due to the low frequency of most TACs, few randomised controlled trials have been conducted. The therapies of choice in CH continue to be the triptans and oxygen for acute treatment, and verapamil and lithium for prevention, but promising results have recently been obtained with novel modes of administration of the triptans and other agents, and several other treatments are currently under study. Indomethacin is extremely effective in PH and HC, while antiepileptic drugs (especially lamotrigine) appear to be increasingly useful in SUNCT. We highlight the need for appropriate studies investigating treatments for these rare, but lifelong and disabling conditions.

title: The Domino Effect: Ed Domino's early studies of Psychoactive Drugs.
abstract: University of Michigan Pharmacology Professor Ed Domino is an expert in the field of neuropsychopharmacology. For over six decades, Dr. Domino has made many contributions to our understanding of psychoactive drugs, but is most well-known for his role in the development of ketamine anesthesia. This article covers the story behind this discovery, along with many other fascinating personal and professional anecdotes, all of which provide insight into the career of a remarkable scientist.

title: The rotational model and microdialysis: Significance for dopamine signalling, clinical studies, and beyond.
abstract: The rotational model using unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) lesions has served as a cornerstone of Parkinson's disease research for over four decades, while in vivo microdialysis has emerged as the gold standard for monitoring extracellular neurotransmitter dynamics. However, the integration of these methodologies and their translational relevance to clinical dopaminergic dysfunction remains incompletely characterized. We systematically examined dopamine signaling patterns using concurrent rotational behavioral testing and striatal microdialysis in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) following unilateral 6-OHDA lesions. Microdialysis probes were implanted bilaterally in the dorsal striatum, and extracellular dopamine levels were monitored during baseline conditions and following pharmacological challenges with apomorphine (0.05-0.5 mg/kg, s.c.) and L-DOPA (25-100 mg/kg, i.p.). Lesioned animals exhibited >95% dopamine depletion on the lesioned side and developed robust ipsilateral rotation following apomorphine administration. Microdialysis revealed a 20-fold increase in dopamine sensitivity on the denervated side, with paradoxically enhanced extracellular dopamine responses despite massive terminal loss. Contralateral rotational behavior correlated strongly with the interhemispheric dopamine gradient (r=0.84, p<0.001) rather than absolute dopamine levels. L-DOPA treatment normalized both rotational asymmetry and dopamine imbalance in a dose-dependent manner. These findings demonstrate that the rotational model reflects dynamic dopaminergic imbalances rather than static dopamine deficiency, supporting its continued utility for evaluating therapeutic interventions. The enhanced dopamine sensitivity observed in denervated striatum parallels clinical observations of dyskinesia development, validating this model's translational relevance for understanding both Parkinson's disease pathophysiology and treatment-related complications.

title: Neuropharmacology of Synthetic Cathinones.
abstract: Synthetic cathinones, colloquially known as "bath salts," represent a rapidly expanding class of psychoactive substances that have emerged as significant public health concerns due to their widespread abuse and unpredictable neuropsychiatric effects. These β-ketone analogs of phenethylamine exhibit structural similarities to amphetamine and cathinone, the psychoactive component of the khat plant. To elucidate their neuropharmacological mechanisms, we conducted comprehensive in vitro and in vivo studies examining the effects of prominent synthetic cathinones including mephedrone, methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV), and α-pyrrolidinopentiophenone (α-PVP) on monoaminergic neurotransmission. Radioligand binding assays and uptake inhibition studies in HEK293 cells transfected with human dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin transporters revealed that synthetic cathinones function primarily as transporter blockers rather than substrates, with MDPV and α-PVP demonstrating particularly high selectivity for dopamine and norepinephrine transporters (DAT/NET selectivity ratios >100-fold over SERT). Microdialysis studies in freely-moving rats showed dose-dependent increases in extracellular dopamine concentrations in the nucleus accumbens following administration of all tested compounds, with MDPV producing the most sustained elevation. Behavioral assessments revealed hyperlocomotion, stereotypy, and conditioned place preference, consistent with abuse liability. These findings demonstrate that synthetic cathinones exert their psychoactive effects primarily through blockade of monoamine reuptake transporters, with selectivity profiles that may explain their distinct behavioral and abuse potential compared to classical stimulants.

title: Resting state functional connectivity: its physiological basis and application in neuropharmacology.
abstract: Resting state functional connectivity (rsFC) has emerged as a powerful neuroimaging approach for investigating intrinsic brain network organization and its modulation by pharmacological interventions. This review synthesizes current understanding of the physiological mechanisms underlying rsFC and examines its applications in neuropharmacological research. Resting state networks reflect spontaneous, low-frequency (<0.1 Hz) fluctuations in blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signal that are temporally correlated across spatially distributed brain regions. These intrinsic connectivity patterns arise from the interplay of neuronal oscillations, neurotransmitter systems, and structural white matter architecture. Growing evidence indicates that rsFC measurements are sensitive to acute and chronic drug effects across multiple neurotransmitter systems, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic pathways. Pharmacological neuroimaging studies utilizing rsFC have provided novel insights into drug mechanisms of action, dose-response relationships, and therapeutic efficacy in psychiatric and neurological disorders. For instance, antidepressant medications consistently alter connectivity within the default mode network, while antipsychotics modulate fronto-striatal and thalamo-cortical connectivity patterns. Additionally, rsFC serves as a translational bridge between preclinical animal models and human studies, enabling cross-species validation of drug effects. The non-invasive nature and high test-retest reliability of rsFC measures make them particularly suitable for longitudinal pharmacological studies and clinical trials. Future applications include the development of connectivity-based biomarkers for drug response prediction, optimization of dosing regimens, and identification of novel therapeutic targets. As methodological advances continue to improve the specificity and interpretability of rsFC measures, this approach promises to accelerate neuropharmacological discovery and personalized medicine approaches.

title: Interaction Between the Trace Amine-Associated Receptor 1 and the Dopamine D2 Receptor Controls Cocaine's Neurochemical Actions.
abstract: Recent evidence suggests that the trace amine-associated receptor 1 (TAAR1) plays a pivotal role in the regulation of dopamine (DA) transmission and cocaine's actions. However, the underlying mechanisms through which TAAR1 activation mediates these effects have not yet been elucidated. Here, we used fast-scan cyclic voltammetry to measure DA dynamics and explore such mechanisms. We show, first, that the full TAAR1 agonist, RO5256390, dose-dependently blocked cocaine-induced inhibition of DA clearance in slices of the nucleus accumbens. Second, subthreshold inhibition of PKA or PKC phosphorylation did not prevent TAAR1 suppression of cocaine effects whereas subeffective doses of the DA D2 receptor antagonist, L-741,626, rescued cocaine's ability to produce changes in DA uptake in the presence of full TAAR1 activation, thus indicating that TAAR1 modulation of cocaine effects requires simultaneous DA D2 receptor activation. Predictably, inhibition of glycogen synthase kinase-3 (GSK-3), which results from activation of D2/TAAR1 heterodimers, fully reproduced the inhibitory effects of TAAR1 activation on cocaine-induced changes in DA transmission. Collectively, the present observations reveal that the ability of TAAR1 to regulate cocaine effects is linked to cooperative interactions with D2 autoreceptors and associated downstream molecular targets converging on GSK-3 and suggest a new mechanism to disrupt cocaine neurochemical actions.

title: Volume versus wiring transmission in the brain: a new theoretical frame for neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: A volume transmission mode of communication in brain was implicit already in the early work of Golgi, who postulated the existence of electrical signals in the extracellular fluid (ECF) based on Volta's "wet conductor" made by solutions. The term volume transmission is taken from the term volume conduction describing the flow of ionic currents in the ECF as a basis for the electrocorticogram. The slow VT mode includes also chemical signals and is opposed to the fast synaptic (wiring) transmission. Every neuron may function in a dual mode, the synaptic and the volume transmission mode, when considering the autocrine and synaptic classes of communication. The paracrine- and neuroendocrine-like classes only involve the VT mode in the latter case including the CSF as a route. The chemical signals for VT are the neuropeptides, but also the classical transmitters, the monoamines, acetylcholine, GABA, and glutamate can participate, when they operate via slow, high affinity G protein coupled receptors. Ions such as K+, Ca++, and H+ also function as VT signals. The hypothesis is also introduced that CO2 can act as a multifacit long-distance VT and WT regulator besides being part of the CO2/HCO3 buffer. CO2 via regulating NMDA receptor sensitivity can also regulate NO formation, which represents a paracrine and fast VT signal. The therapy of CNS disorders is also discussed in the frame of the wiring and VT concept. Two therapeutical approaches can therefore be developed, one based on increasing WT and one based on increasing VT. In contrast to the WT therapy, which must preserve the electrotemporal code, the VT therapy can operate also with postsynaptic agonists. Therefore, a therapeutic effect with such a drug indicates that the deficiency in the communication process operates via VT. In view of the lack of very effective negative feedbacks in VT vs. WT, VT therapy may produce less tolerance and drug dependency.

title: Clinical neuropharmacology of drugs of abuse: a comparison of drug-discrimination and subject-report measures.
abstract: Drug discrimination procedures in laboratory animals and subjective self-report measures in humans represent two primary methodologies for evaluating the behavioral pharmacology of abused substances, yet systematic comparisons of these approaches remain limited. This review examines the concordance between drug discrimination data and human subjective effects for major classes of abused drugs, including psychostimulants, opioids, hallucinogens, and sedative-hypnotics. We analyzed published studies from 1980-2002 comparing discriminative stimulus effects in rodents and non-human primates with subjective ratings from controlled human laboratory studies. Drug discrimination procedures demonstrated high sensitivity in detecting pharmacologically relevant differences between compounds within drug classes, particularly for cocaine-like stimulants (>85% concordance with human "drug liking" ratings) and morphine-like opioids (>80% concordance). Hallucinogens showed moderate correspondence (65-75%) between animal discrimination and human perceptual ratings, while benzodiazepines exhibited variable concordance (45-85%) depending on the specific behavioral endpoint examined. Cross-tolerance studies in discriminating animals accurately predicted substitution patterns observed in human drug users for 78% of tested compounds. Notable discrepancies emerged for certain NMDA receptor antagonists and novel designer drugs, where animal models underestimated abuse liability compared to human subjective reports. These findings support the utility of drug discrimination as a translational bridge between preclinical research and clinical assessment of abuse potential, while highlighting the complementary value of human subjective measures in comprehensive abuse liability evaluation.

title: Smell and Stress Response in the Brain: Review of the Connection between Chemistry and Neuropharmacology.
abstract: The olfactory system represents a critical interface between environmental chemical stimuli and neurobiological stress responses, yet the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current evidence linking olfactory processing to hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activation and stress-related neurotransmitter systems. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 2000-2021, focusing on studies examining olfactory-induced stress responses, neuroanatomical pathways connecting the olfactory bulb to stress-regulatory brain regions, and pharmacological interventions targeting these circuits. Emerging evidence demonstrates that olfactory stimuli directly modulate cortisol release through projections from the olfactory bulb to the amygdala, hypothalamus, and hippocampus, bypassing thalamic processing typical of other sensory modalities. Specific odorants activate distinct neurotransmitter pathways: predator odors primarily engage GABAergic and glutamatergic signaling, while social chemosignals modulate dopaminergic and serotonergic transmission. Pharmacological studies reveal that selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and benzodiazepines can attenuate olfactory-mediated stress responses, while NMDA receptor antagonists show mixed efficacy depending on odor valence and concentration. Additionally, endocannabinoid signaling within the olfactory tubercle appears crucial for contextual fear memory formation following aversive odor exposure. These findings highlight the olfactory system as a promising therapeutic target for stress-related disorders and suggest that understanding odor-brain chemistry interactions may inform novel anxiolytic drug development. Further research is needed to elucidate species-specific differences and optimize translational applications for human neuropsychiatric conditions.

title: Psychedelic Psychiatry's Brave New World.
abstract: The resurgence of psychedelic research has transformed our understanding of psychiatric therapeutics, yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain poorly characterized. Here, we investigated the molecular and circuit-level changes induced by psilocybin treatment in a translational model of treatment-resistant depression. We employed a combination of two-photon calcium imaging, single-cell RNA sequencing, and behavioral assays in mice subjected to chronic unpredictable stress, alongside a double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial in 84 patients with major depressive disorder. Psilocybin administration (25 mg/kg in mice; 25 mg in humans) produced rapid and sustained increases in dendritic spine density within layer 5 pyramidal neurons of the medial prefrontal cortex, accompanied by enhanced long-term potentiation and increased expression of neuroplasticity genes including BDNF, Arc, and Egr1. Single-cell transcriptomics revealed preferential activation of cortical serotonin 2A receptor-expressing interneurons and a shift toward excitatory-inhibitory balance favoring cortical disinhibition. Behaviorally, both mice and humans showed significant improvements in depression-like symptoms that persisted for 4-6 weeks post-treatment, correlating with structural plasticity measures. Pretreatment with the 5-HT2A antagonist ketanserin blocked both molecular and behavioral effects, confirming receptor specificity. These findings demonstrate that psychedelic-assisted therapy promotes enduring neuroplasticity through 5-HT2A-dependent mechanisms, providing a neurobiological foundation for the clinical efficacy observed in psychiatric applications and supporting the therapeutic potential of this emerging pharmacological class.

title: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor redux: Discovery of accessories opens therapeutic vistas.
abstract: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) have long been considered promising therapeutic targets for neurological and psychiatric disorders, yet clinical translation has remained elusive due to limited subtype selectivity and complex pharmacology. Recent structural and functional studies have revealed that nAChR activity is profoundly modulated by a previously unrecognized class of accessory proteins, termed nicotinic receptor-associated proteins (NRAPs), which confer tissue-specific receptor properties and drug sensitivities. Here, we employed cryo-electron microscopy, electrophysiology, and high-throughput compound screening to characterize NRAP interactions across major nAChR subtypes. We identified three distinct NRAP families—NRAPs 1-3—that selectively associate with α4β2, α7, and α3β4 receptors, respectively, altering their agonist binding kinetics by 10-100 fold and dramatically shifting allosteric modulator potencies. Proteomics analysis revealed brain region-specific NRAP expression patterns that correlate with known cholinergic dysfunction in Alzheimer disease and schizophrenia. Leveraging this knowledge, we discovered compound series that exhibit >1000-fold selectivity for NRAP-containing versus NRAP-free receptors. Lead compounds demonstrated robust cognitive enhancement in mouse models of Alzheimer disease and antipsychotic-like effects in dopamine hyperfunction paradigms, with minimal peripheral side effects. Furthermore, NRAP expression was significantly altered in postmortem brain tissue from patients with these disorders. These findings fundamentally redefine nAChR pharmacology and provide a mechanistic framework for developing the next generation of subtype-selective cholinergic therapeutics with enhanced clinical translatability.

title: Treatment of hyperkinetic movement disorders.
abstract:
**Background** Hyperkinetic movement disorders, including chorea, dystonia, and myoclonus, represent a heterogeneous group of neurological conditions with limited therapeutic options. Current treatments rely primarily on dopamine receptor antagonists and anticonvulsants, which provide incomplete symptom control and substantial adverse effects. We investigated the efficacy and safety of combined levetiracetam and tetrabenazine therapy in patients with refractory hyperkinetic movement disorders.

**Methods** We conducted a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial at three tertiary movement disorder centres between January 2007 and August 2008. Adults aged 18-75 years with moderate to severe hyperkinetic movements (Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale motor score ≥15 or Burke-Fahn-Marsden Dystonia Rating Scale ≥10) despite optimal standard therapy were randomly assigned to receive combination therapy (levetiracetam 1000-3000 mg daily plus tetrabenazine 25-100 mg daily) or matched placebo for 12 weeks. Primary endpoint was reduction in movement severity scores at week 12. Secondary endpoints included functional disability measures and adverse events.

**Results** 84 patients were enrolled (42 per group). Mean age was 52·3 years (SD 14·8); 38 (45%) had Huntington's disease, 28 (33%) had dystonia, and 18 (21%) had other hyperkinetic disorders. At week 12, combination therapy produced significantly greater improvement in movement scores compared with placebo (mean difference -8·4 points, 95% CI -12·7 to -4·1; p=0·0003). Functional disability scores improved by 23% in the treatment group versus 4% with placebo (p=0·02). Sedation occurred in 18 (43%) patients receiving active treatment versus 6 (14%) receiving placebo.

**Interpretation** Combined levetiracetam and tetrabenazine therapy represents a promising treatment approach for refractory hyperkinetic movement disorders, warranting larger confirmatory studies.

title: Neuropharmacological basis of combining antidepressants.
abstract: To review the neuropharmacological basis of antidepressant combination therapy. Literature searches and other relevant material were obtained and reviewed. The overall clinical aim of combining antidepressants is to increase the efficacy whilst minimizing the side effects. Although such prescriptions are frequently based on the previous experience and knowledge, a sound neuropharmacological basis to support these combinations is desirable. When combining antidepressants, it is important to combine mechanisms of action, rather than simply one drug with another, and to aim for synergistic effects. The possibilities of combining mechanisms of action should also be exploited to the full if necessary, and the potential exists for combining two independent actions that have synergistic effects on the serotonergic, noradrenergic and even the dopaminergic systems. Unfortunately, there are still, as yet, insufficient data to categorically justify choosing one or other combination based only on the neuropharmacological evidence.

title: An integrative dynamic model of brain energy metabolism using in vivo neurochemical measurements.
abstract: Brain energy metabolism plays a crucial role in neuronal function, yet the dynamic relationships between glucose utilization, oxygen consumption, and neurotransmitter cycling remain incompletely understood. We developed an integrative computational model that incorporates real-time neurochemical measurements to simulate energy metabolism in the awake rat brain. Using microdialysis probes implanted in the striatum, we continuously monitored extracellular glucose, lactate, and glutamate concentrations during baseline conditions and following pharmacological challenges with 3-nitropropionic acid (10 mg/kg, i.p.) and glucose (2 g/kg, i.v.). Simultaneously, we measured local field potentials and tissue oxygen tension using chronically implanted electrodes. Our dynamic model integrated astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttle kinetics, mitochondrial respiratory chain function, and glutamate-glutamine cycling based on Michaelis-Menten enzyme kinetics and diffusion parameters derived from experimental data. The model successfully predicted 89% of variance in observed lactate fluctuations and 76% of glucose changes during metabolic perturbations. Following 3-nitropropionic acid administration, the model accurately reproduced the 34% decrease in tissue oxygen and corresponding 2.1-fold increase in lactate/glucose ratio observed experimentally. Sensitivity analyses revealed that astrocytic glucose transporter capacity and neuronal oxidative metabolism were the primary determinants of system stability. These findings demonstrate that integrative modeling combined with in vivo neurochemical monitoring provides novel insights into brain bioenergetics and may facilitate development of therapeutic strategies for metabolic disorders affecting the central nervous system.

title: Data collection and analysis strategies for phMRI.
abstract: Although functional MRI traditionally has been applied mainly to study changes in task-induced brain function, evolving acquisition methodologies and improved knowledge of signal mechanisms have increased the utility of this method for studying responses to pharmacological stimuli, a technique often dubbed "phMRI". The proliferation of higher magnetic field strengths and the use of exogenous contrast agent have boosted detection power, a critical factor for successful phMRI due to the restricted ability to average multiple stimuli within subjects. Receptor-based models of neurovascular coupling, including explicit pharmacological models incorporating receptor densities and affinities and data-driven models that incorporate weak biophysical constraints, have demonstrated compelling descriptions of phMRI signal induced by dopaminergic stimuli. This report describes phMRI acquisition and analysis methodologies, with an emphasis on data-driven analyses. As an example application, statistically efficient data-driven regressors were used to describe the biphasic response to the mu-opioid agonist remifentanil, and antagonism using dopaminergic and GABAergic ligands revealed modulation of the mesolimbic pathway. Results illustrate the power of phMRI as well as our incomplete understanding of mechanisms underlying the signal. Future directions are discussed for phMRI acquisitions in human studies, for evolving analysis methodologies, and for interpretative studies using the new generation of simultaneous PET/MRI scanners. This article is part of the Special Issue Section entitled 'Neuroimaging in Neuropharmacology'.

title: Integrative biological analysis for neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The complexity of neuropsychiatric disorders necessitates integrative approaches that combine multiple biological data types to understand drug mechanisms and identify novel therapeutic targets. Current neuropsychopharmacology research often relies on single-dimensional analyses, limiting our ability to capture the multifaceted nature of brain dysfunction and drug action. We developed a comprehensive analytical framework integrating genomics, proteomics, metabolomics, and neuroimaging data to investigate antidepressant mechanisms in major depressive disorder. Using samples from 156 treatment-naive patients and 89 healthy controls, we employed weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA) to identify molecular modules, followed by functional enrichment analysis and drug-target network construction. Pre- and post-treatment assessments included RNA sequencing from peripheral blood, plasma metabolite profiling, and resting-state fMRI. Our integrative analysis revealed three distinct molecular signatures associated with treatment response: a stress-response module enriched for glucocorticoid signaling genes, a neuroinflammatory module characterized by elevated cytokine pathways, and a neurotransmitter metabolism module showing altered monoamine biosynthesis. Critically, the combination of transcriptomic and metabolomic signatures predicted treatment outcome with 78% accuracy, significantly outperforming single-modality approaches (genomics: 61%, metabolomics: 65%). Neuroimaging data showed that molecular signatures correlated with altered connectivity in limbic-prefrontal circuits. These findings demonstrate that integrative biological analysis can reveal convergent pathways underlying neuropsychiatric disorders and drug action, potentially enabling personalized treatment selection. This multi-dimensional approach represents a paradigm shift toward precision neuropsychopharmacology, offering improved understanding of complex brain disorders and therapeutic mechanisms.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Neuronal and glial cell lines as model systems for studying P2Y receptor pharmacology.
abstract: P2Y receptors are G-protein coupled receptors activated by extracellular nucleotides that play crucial roles in neurotransmission, glial activation, and neuroinflammation. While native tissues provide physiological relevance, immortalized cell lines offer advantages for systematic pharmacological studies including homogeneous receptor expression and reproducible experimental conditions. We characterized P2Y receptor subtypes in commonly used neuronal and glial cell lines to evaluate their utility as model systems for neuropharmacological research. RT-PCR analysis and immunocytochemistry were performed on PC12, SH-SY5Y, N2a neuroblastoma cells, C6 glioma cells, and primary rat astrocytes. Functional responses were assessed using calcium imaging, cAMP measurements, and inositol phosphate accumulation following stimulation with selective P2Y agonists including ADP, UTP, and UDP. PC12 cells predominantly expressed P2Y₁ and P2Y₁₂ receptors with robust calcium responses to ADP (EC₅₀ = 145 ± 23 nM). SH-SY5Y cells showed high P2Y₂ expression and strong UTP-evoked responses (EC₅₀ = 78 ± 15 nM). C6 glioma cells expressed multiple P2Y subtypes (P2Y₁, P2Y₂, P2Y₄) with mixed pharmacological profiles, while primary astrocytes showed predominant P2Y₁ receptor-mediated responses. N2a cells exhibited minimal P2Y receptor expression and weak functional responses. These findings demonstrate that specific cell lines can serve as reliable models for distinct P2Y receptor subtypes, with PC12 cells optimal for P2Y₁/P2Y₁₂ studies and SH-SY5Y cells suitable for P2Y₂ receptor pharmacology. This characterization provides a framework for selecting appropriate cellular models in P2Y receptor drug discovery and mechanistic studies.

title: Corticotropin-Releasing Factor (CRF) Neurocircuitry and Neuropharmacology in Alcohol Drinking.
abstract: Corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF) signaling plays a critical role in stress-induced alcohol consumption and the development of alcohol use disorders. The extended amygdala, particularly the central nucleus of the amygdala (CeA) and bed nucleus of the stria terminalis (BNST), represents a key neuroanatomical substrate where CRF modulates alcohol-seeking behaviors. This review synthesizes current understanding of CRF neurocircuitry and pharmacological interventions targeting the CRF system in preclinical models of alcohol consumption. We examined evidence from rodent studies utilizing operant self-administration, conditioned place preference, and reinstatement paradigms, focusing on the effects of CRF receptor antagonists and genetic manipulations. CRF1 receptor antagonism consistently reduces stress-induced reinstatement of alcohol seeking and escalated drinking in dependent animals, while having minimal effects on baseline consumption in non-dependent subjects. Antalarmin, CP-154,526, and more selective compounds like R121919 demonstrate efficacy in attenuating alcohol-motivated behaviors across multiple laboratories. CRF2 receptor modulation shows more complex, context-dependent effects on alcohol consumption. Neuroanatomically, CRF release in the CeA and BNST increases during withdrawal and stress exposure, correlating with enhanced alcohol seeking. The CRF system appears to be particularly important during the transition from recreational to compulsive alcohol use. These findings support the therapeutic potential of CRF receptor antagonists for treating alcohol use disorders, particularly in individuals with comorbid stress-related pathology. Future directions include developing brain-penetrant CRF1 antagonists with improved pharmacokinetic properties and exploring combination therapies targeting multiple stress-related neurotransmitter systems.

title: A history of the Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (1957-2004).
abstract: Recognition that one of the essential prerequisites of successful neuro-psychopharmacological research is a continuous dialogue between clinicians and basic scientists led to the founding of the Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) in Zurich on September 2, 1957. At the time, CINP was the only association in neuro-psychopharmacology. It was hoped that by organizing biennial congresses, it would facilitate the interaction among the many disciplines involved in the new field. It was also envisaged that discussion of findings in translational research would provide guidance for developing more selective and thereby more effective psychotropic drugs. These expectations were not fulfilled. In the years that followed, major developments in neuropharmacology without parallel development in the methodology of clinical investigations, created a widening gap between neuro- and psychopharmacology. Clinical interpretations of neuropharmacological findings filled in the missing information from translational research. In the 1980s, to facilitate the dissemination of these interpretations, CINP established Travel Awards for Young Investigators to assist them in attending the biennial meetings. In the 1990s, the college extended its activities by organizing other meetings, including president's workshops, regional meetings, educational seminars and other programs. By the end of the 20th century CINP was a legal entity that was registered in Switzerland with domicile in Zurich. It had also become a financially secure organization with sufficient funds to support administration and coordination. In the early years of the 21st century CINP established a central office and a congress-organizing group. To meet the needs of its steadily growing membership from Asia, Australia and the Latin Americas, CINP has been regionalized. In order to regain its relevance to all its members it is hoped that CINP will return to its roots.

title: Combinatorial Oxytocin Neuropharmacology in Social Cognition.
abstract: The efficacy and reliability of using intranasal oxytocin (OT) to clinically enhance social functions remains undependable. We discuss the potential benefit of concurrent administration of OT and naloxone (NAL) to robustly modulate social behavior. We further suggest that combinatorial neuropharmacology approaches should exploit the interactions between OT and serotonin to regulate social functions.

title: New treatment strategies in multiple sclerosis.
abstract: Multiple sclerosis is the most common, non-traumatic, disabling neurological disease of young adults, affecting an estimated two million people worldwide. At onset multiple sclerosis can be categorised clinically into relapsing remitting MS (RRMS - 85-90% of patients) or primary progressive MS (PPMS). Relapses typically present sub-acutely over hours to days with neurological symptoms persisting for days to weeks before they gradually dissipate. At first full recovery is the norm, later patients accumulate deficits and ultimately most convert to a secondary progressive phase (SPMS), characterised by deficits that increase in the absence of further relapses. The clinical picture reflects the complex interplay of focal inflammation, demyelination and axonal degeneration occurring within the central nervous system. Since the introduction of a genuine disease-modifying drug, interferon-beta1b in 1993, there has been a growing interest from academia and pharmaceutical companies alike in multiple sclerosis therapy. In part this effort has focused on investigating the "window of therapeutic opportunity" within the natural history of the disease: it is becoming increasingly clear that immunotherapies are not useful in the secondary phase of the disease but may offer long-term benefit if given early in the relapsing-remitting phase. In part, attention is being paid to the details of dosing and administration of the various licensed therapies, but there is also a significant research effort to explore new ways to treat the disease. In this review, we first sketch the landscape of novel therapies in multiple sclerosis and then discuss in detail approaches which are likely to emerge over the next few years.

title: NMDA antagonists: antiepileptic-neuroprotective drugs with diversified neuropharmacological profiles.
abstract: In conclusion, NMDA antagonists as anticonvulsants are especially active in preventing the generalization of the behavioural and electrical seizures and display a typical spectrum of in vitro antiepileptiform activities. In addition, based on in vitro and in vivo limbic kindled studies, the drugs should be regarded more as an antiepileptiform than as an anticonvulsant drugs. As neuroprotective drugs, NMDA antagonists are effective against many types of neuronal injury and show a window of activity which does not exceed 1-2 h, thus suggesting an influence of NMDA receptors in the 'early' or 'acute' mechanisms of brain damage. Among NMDA antagonists, glycine antagonists or the morphinans dextromethorphan and dextrorphan showed a spectrum of antiepileptiform and neuroprotective activities broader than other NMDA antagonists. The primary pharmacological activities of NMDA antagonists are accompanied by some effects including perturbation of many sensory, psychological or motor processes. Typical behavioural and EEG changes were also induced by the drugs. In spite of the side-effects elicited by the drugs, differential effects detected among the various classes of NMDA antagonists (i.e. lack of induction of typical EEG-behavioural effects and of typical cortical neurotoxicity) might render some of these suitable for full clinical application as anticonvulsant-neuroprotective drugs.

title: [Pharmacotherapy of chronic neuropathic pain].
abstract: Chronic neuropathic pain has a prevalence of 6.9-10% in the general population. The current recommendations for treatment are presented based on a literature search. Neuropathic pain requires the use of co-analgesic, antidepressant, anticonvulsant drugs and topical agents because non-opioid analgesic drugs are usually ineffective. The use of meta-analyses tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin-norephinephrine reuptake inhibitors, and calcium channel anticonvulsants are recommended as the drugs of first choice. Under certain conditions chronic neuropathic pain can be treated with opioids. Topical therapeutics are only used to treat peripheral neuropathic pain. At present the use of drugs is independent of the etiology of the pain. Comorbidities, concomitant medication, potential side effects and patients' age have to be considered in treatment planning.

title: Evidence for improved neuropharmacological efficacy and decreased neurotoxicity in mice with traditional processing of Rhizoma Arisaematis.
abstract: Traditional Chinese medicine has long employed specific processing methods to enhance therapeutic efficacy and reduce toxicity of medicinal herbs. Rhizoma Arisaematis (RA), derived from Arisaema consanguineum, contains potent neurotoxic alkaloids that limit its clinical application despite documented neuroprotective properties. This study investigated whether traditional processing methods could improve the neuropharmacological profile of RA in experimental mice. Raw RA was processed using traditional methods including ginger juice treatment, steaming, and drying according to classical pharmaceutical protocols. Male ICR mice (n=8 per group) received either raw RA extract (50, 100, 200 mg/kg), traditionally processed RA extract (50, 100, 200 mg/kg), or vehicle control via oral gavage for 14 days. Neurotoxicity was assessed through behavioral testing, histopathological examination, and measurement of serum neurotoxicity markers. Neuroprotective efficacy was evaluated using the Morris water maze and measurement of brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) levels. Traditional processing significantly reduced acute neurotoxic symptoms including seizures, ataxia, and mortality compared to raw RA (p<0.01). Histological analysis revealed decreased neuronal damage in the hippocampus and cortex of mice receiving processed RA. Importantly, processed RA maintained superior cognitive enhancement effects, with improved spatial learning performance (p<0.05) and elevated BDNF expression (2.3-fold increase vs. control). Serum levels of neurotoxic alkaloids were reduced by 68% following traditional processing while maintaining bioactive compounds. These findings provide scientific validation for traditional processing methods of RA, demonstrating enhanced neuropharmacological efficacy with substantially reduced neurotoxicity, supporting its potential clinical application in neurodegenerative disorders.
journal: The American journal of Chinese medicine

title: On the prescribing analyst.
abstract: The value of medication for some patients in psychoanalysis serves to highlight the potential challenges of the medical analyst and invites exploration into possible motivations for assuming the prescribing role. Prescribing medication is one way in which the medical analyst integrates the dual identities of physician and analyst while dealing with significant cultural influences and intrapsychic tensions. Technical challenges posed by assuming the prescribing role are explored, as are the potential benefits of split treatment. The educational implications of this argument are discussed in relation to identity formation for candidates who are physicians.

title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning I: the degraded contingency effect is disrupted by atropine but reinstated by physostigmine.
abstract: The cholinergic system has been shown to modulate contextual fear conditioning. However, with the exception of trace conditioning studies, most of the available data have focussed on independent context, i.e., context that do not compete with the conditioned stimulus to control for the conditioned response (interactive context). In the present series of experiments, the effects of the muscarinic antagonist, atropine, were assessed when contextual fear memory interacts with cued fear memory to regulate conditioned response, using a Pavlovian degraded contingency preparation in rats. This preparation not only afforded an insight into simple Pavlovian associations but also enabled us to test for the processes of competition that made use of these associations to make an appropriate response to a stimulus [degraded contingency effect (DCE)]. In experiment 1, three doses of atropine [2.5, 5.0, and 10.0 mg/kg, intraperitoneally (i.p.)] were evaluated on male Sprague-Dawley rats. In experiment 2, physostigmine (0.037-0.3 mg/kg, i.p.) was injected after the administration of 5 mg/kg of atropine. Experiment 1A and its partial replication (experiment 1B) showed that at asymptotic level of training, atropine did not alter contextual and cued fear memories when the subjects were directly tested for them, whereas it suppressed the DCE for a 5 mg/kg dose. Indeed, atropine-induced suppression of the DCE was found to be an inverted U-shaped dose-response curve. Experiment 2 showed that physostigmine caused a dose-dependent reversal of the atropine-induced alleviation of the DCE, without altering the expression of simple cued and contextual fear memories. These results evidence at asymptotic level of training a cholinergic modulation of the processing of interactive context, but not of independent ones. They are discussed in the framework of the mechanisms that are involved in both types of contextual processing.

title: Attention: oscillations and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Neural oscillations in the gamma frequency range (30-100 Hz) have been implicated in attentional processes, yet the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying attention-related oscillatory activity remain poorly understood. We investigated how GABAergic and cholinergic modulation influences gamma oscillations during sustained attention in awake behaving rats. Local field potentials were recorded from the primary visual cortex of rats performing a visual discrimination task while administering pharmacological manipulations via reverse microdialysis. Muscimol (GABA-A receptor agonist, 1 μM) significantly reduced gamma power by 47% ± 8% and impaired task performance (correct response rate decreased from 78% ± 5% to 52% ± 7%, P < 0.001). Conversely, bicuculline (GABA-A antagonist, 10 μM) increased gamma power by 62% ± 12% but paradoxically decreased performance accuracy to 61% ± 9% (P < 0.01), suggesting that excessive gamma activity disrupts optimal attentional processing. Physostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, 50 μM) enhanced both gamma oscillations (32% ± 6% increase) and behavioral performance (89% ± 4% correct responses, P < 0.001). Scopolamine (muscarinic antagonist, 100 μM) abolished attention-related gamma enhancement and reduced performance to chance levels (49% ± 8%). Cross-correlation analysis revealed that cholinergic modulation specifically enhanced the coupling between gamma oscillations and behavioral response times (r = 0.73, P < 0.001). These findings demonstrate that optimal attentional performance requires a delicate balance of GABAergic inhibition to generate gamma rhythms and cholinergic facilitation to couple oscillatory dynamics with behavioral output, providing new insights into the neuropharmacological basis of attention.

title: An optimized recording method to characterize biophysical and pharmacological properties of acid-sensing ion channel.
abstract: To re-confirm and characterize the biophysical and pharmacological properties of endogenously expressed human acid-sensing ion channel 1a (hASIC1a) current in HEK293 cells with a modified perfusion methods. With cell floating method, which is separating the cultured cell from coverslip and putting the cell in front of perfusion tubing, whole cell patch clamp technique was used to record hASIC1a currents evoked by low pH external solution. Using cell floating method, the amplitude of hASIC1a currents activated by pH 5.0 in HEK293 cells is twice as large as that by the conventional method where the cells remain attached to coverslip. The time to reach peak at two different recording conditions is (21+/-5) ms and (270+/-25) ms, respectively. Inactivation time constants are (496+/-23) ms and (2284+/-120) ms, respectively. The cell floating method significantly increases the amiloride potency of block on hASIC1a [IC50 is (3.4+/-1.1) micromol/L and (2.4+/- 0.9) micromol/L, respectively]. Both recording methods have similar pH activation EC50 (6.6+/-0.6, 6.6+/-0.7, respectively). ASICs channel activation requires fast exchange of extracellular solution with the different pH values. With cell floating method, the presence of hASIC1a current was re-confirmed and the biophysical and pharmacological properties of hASIC1a channel in HEK293 cells were precisely characterized. This method could be used to study all ASICs and other ligand-gated channels that require fast extracellular solution exchange. 用一种改良的灌流方法研究和再次证实HEK 293细胞内源性酸敏感性离子通道的生物物理和药理学特性。 使用全细胞膜片钳技术, 将记录细胞与玻片分离, 并使细胞位于输液맜前, 呈漂浮状态, 以记录在低pH 值外液中, HEK293 细胞的酸敏感性1a 型离子通道的电流。 使用细胞漂浮方法, pH 5.0 的细胞外液诱发的酸敏感性1a 型离子通道电流是传统细胞附着法诱发的两倍。在两种不同的方法下, 通道电流达到峰值的时间分别是 (21±5) ms和 (270±25) ms, 失活的时间常数分别是(496±23) ms 和(2284±120) ms。细胞漂流法也明显增强amiloride 对酸敏感性1a型离子通道的阻断效能。两种方法具有相似的pH激活的EC50 (分别为6.6±0.6, 6.6±0.7)。 酸敏感性1a 型离子通道的激活需要细胞外液快速的交换。通过细胞漂浮的方法, 我们再次证明了酸敏感性1a型离子通道电流的存在, 更精确地分析了HEK 293 细胞内源性酸敏感型离子通道的生物物理和药理学特性。这一改良的方法能够用于研究所有的酸敏感型离子通道和需要快速细胞外液交换的配体门控通道。

title: Evidence for improved neuropharmacological efficacy and decreased neurotoxicity in mice with traditional processing of Rhizoma Arisaematis.
abstract: Rhizoma Arisaematis (RA, the rhizome of Pinellia pedatisecta Schott) is a traditional Chinese medicine commonly used in the treatment of convulsions, inflammation, and cancer. Despite the fact that it has been used for more than 2000 years, the pharmacological and toxic effects of traditionally processed products of RA are still unclear. In this study, we attempted to investigate the effects exerted by untreated crude RA and different preparations of RA treated with alumen in combination with ginger juice (Zhinanxing) or bile juice (Dannanxing) in ICR mice. The results showed that both the Zhinanxing and Dannanxing water extracts exerted significantly increased sedative effects, as indicated by the inhibitory effects on ambulatory distances, jumps, vertical-plane entries, and prolonged pentobarbital-induced sleeping time. The extracts also exerted significantly increased analgesic effects (increase of tail flick latency in nociceptive testing) in mice than did the unprocessed crude RA after oral administration for one to three days, and effects persisted 18 days after the cessation of treatment. By contrast, the toxic effects, such as an increase in stereotype-1 episodes of locomotor activities and reduction of the retention time on a rotating rod (motor equilibrium dysfunction), were observed only in mice treated with the unprocessed crude RA for three consecutive days, and effects persisted for 18 days after the cessation of treatment. These neurotoxic effects were accompanied by an increase in plasma lipid peroxidation (LPO), decrease in whole blood nitric oxide (NO(x)) levels, and inhibition of Na(+)/K(+)-ATPase activities in membrane fractions of erythrocytes and in the cerebral cortex. In conclusion, these findings provide scientific evidence that the processed RA indeed possesses not only enhanced neuropharmacological efficacy but also reduced neurotoxic effects as compared to the unprocessed crude RA. The signaling of NO(x)/oxidative stress/Na(+)-K(+)- ATPase activities played a role, at least in part, in the underlying mechanisms of neurotoxic effects induced by the crude RA.
journal: The American journal of Chinese medicine

title: Psychopharmacology: finding one's way.
abstract: The development of effective psychotropic medications has been largely serendipitous, with many breakthrough discoveries arising from unexpected clinical observations rather than rational drug design. This review examines the historical progression of psychopharmacology from the 1950s to present, analyzing the relationship between mechanistic understanding and therapeutic efficacy. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of major psychotropic drug classes, including antipsychotics, antidepressants, and anxiolytics, examining their discovery pathways, proposed mechanisms of action, and clinical validation timelines. Database searches of MEDLINE from 1950-1994 identified 847 relevant publications, which were categorized by drug class and discovery methodology. Our analysis reveals that 78% of currently approved psychotropic medications were discovered through phenotypic screening or clinical accident rather than target-based approaches. The monoamine hypothesis of depression emerged only after tricyclic antidepressants and MAOIs demonstrated clinical efficacy, while dopamine receptor antagonism was characterized years after chlorpromazine's antipsychotic properties were established. Despite limited mechanistic insight, these empirically-derived treatments have provided substantial clinical benefit and generated testable hypotheses about psychiatric pathophysiology. However, the gap between therapeutic efficacy and mechanistic understanding has hindered the development of more selective agents with improved side effect profiles. Future progress in psychopharmacology will likely require integration of emerging neuroscience technologies, including molecular biology and neuroimaging, with traditional pharmacological approaches to bridge the divide between empirical observation and rational drug design.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The pediatric psychopharmacology of autism spectrum disorder: A systematic review - Part I: The past and the present.
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a severe and lifelong neurodevelopmental disorder, with high social costs and a dramatic burden on the quality of life of patients and family members. Despite its high prevalence, reaching 1/54 children and 1/45 adults in the United States, no pharmacological treatment is still directed to core symptoms of ASD, encompassing social and communication deficits, repetitive behaviors, restricted interests, and abnormal sensory processing. The purpose of this review is to provide an overview of the state-of-the-art of psychopharmacological therapy available today for ASD in children and adolescents, in order to foster best practices and to organize new strategies for future research. To date, atypical antipsychotics such as risperidone and aripiprazole represent the first line of intervention for hyperactivity, impulsivity, agitation, temper outbursts or aggression towards self or others. Tricyclic antidepressants are less prescribed because of uncertain efficacy and important side effects. SSRIs, especially fluoxetine and sertraline, may be effective in treating repetitive behaviors (anxiety and obsessive-compulsive symptoms) and irritability/agitation, while mirtazapine is more helpful with sleep problems. Low doses of buspirone have shown some efficacy on restrictive and repetitive behaviors in combination with behavioral interventions. Stimulants, and to a lesser extent atomoxetine, are effective in reducing hyperactivity, inattention and impulsivity also in comorbid ASD-ADHD, although with somewhat lower efficacy and greater incidence of side effects compared to idiopathic ADHD. Clonidine and guanfacine display some efficacy on hyperactivity and stereotypic behaviors. For several other drugs, case reports and open-label studies suggest possible efficacy, but no randomized controlled trial has yet been performed. Research in the pediatric psychopharmacology of ASD is still faced with at least two major hurdles: (a) Great interindividual variability in clinical response and side effect sensitivity is observed in the ASD population. This low level of predictability would benefit from symptom-specific treatment algorithms and from biomarkers to support drug choice; (b) To this date, no psychoactive drug appears to directly ameliorate core autism symptoms, although some indirect improvement has been reported with several drugs, once the comorbid target symptom is abated.

title: Mildronate: an antiischemic drug for neurological indications.
abstract: Mildronate (3-(2,2,2-trimethylhydrazinium)propionate), originally developed as an antianginal agent, has emerged as a promising neuroprotective compound with potential applications in cerebral ischemia and neurodegenerative disorders. This comprehensive review examines the pharmacological profile, mechanisms of action, and clinical evidence supporting mildronate's use in neurological conditions. Mildronate's primary mechanism involves reversible inhibition of γ-butyrobetaine hydroxylase, the rate-limiting enzyme in L-carnitine biosynthesis, thereby shifting cellular metabolism from fatty acid β-oxidation toward more oxygen-efficient glucose utilization. Preclinical studies demonstrate that mildronate administration reduces infarct volume by 35-45% in middle cerebral artery occlusion models and improves neurological deficit scores. The compound exhibits multiple neuroprotective mechanisms including stabilization of mitochondrial membrane potential, reduction of oxidative stress markers, and preservation of ATP levels during hypoxic conditions. Clinical trials in acute ischemic stroke patients treated with mildronate (500-1000 mg daily) showed significant improvements in neurological outcomes at 30 and 90 days compared to standard therapy alone. Additionally, preliminary evidence suggests efficacy in vascular dementia and diabetic neuropathy. Mildronate demonstrates favorable safety profile with minimal adverse effects, primarily gastrointestinal disturbances occurring in <8% of patients. The drug's unique metabolic mechanism, combined with its established cardiovascular safety record and emerging neurological benefits, positions mildronate as a valuable addition to current stroke treatment protocols and warrants further investigation in broader neurological applications.

title: 3D-QSAR study of corticotropin-releasing factor 1 antagonists and pharmacophore-based drug design.
abstract: Corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF) plays a pivotal role in the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and stress response, making CRF1 receptor antagonists promising therapeutic targets for anxiety, depression, and stress-related disorders. Despite significant research efforts, the structural requirements for potent CRF1 antagonism remain poorly understood. In this study, we performed a comprehensive three-dimensional quantitative structure-activity relationship (3D-QSAR) analysis on a dataset of 87 structurally diverse CRF1 antagonists to elucidate key molecular determinants of binding affinity and selectivity. Comparative molecular field analysis (CoMFA) and comparative molecular similarity indices analysis (CoMSIA) models were constructed using binding affinity data (Ki values ranging from 0.8 to 2,450 nM) obtained from radioligand displacement assays. The optimized CoMFA model demonstrated excellent statistical parameters (r² = 0.891, q² = 0.667, r²pred = 0.724), while the CoMSIA model incorporating steric, electrostatic, hydrophobic, and hydrogen-bond donor fields yielded comparable predictive ability (r² = 0.903, q² = 0.671, r²pred = 0.738). Contour map analysis revealed that bulky hydrophobic substituents at specific positions enhance binding affinity, while positive electrostatic fields in the central aromatic region are detrimental to activity. Based on these findings, a comprehensive pharmacophore model was developed featuring four hydrophobic regions, two hydrogen-bond acceptors, and one aromatic ring. Virtual screening of commercial databases using this pharmacophore identified 23 novel compounds with predicted high CRF1 binding affinity. These results provide valuable insights for structure-based optimization of CRF1 antagonists and accelerate the discovery of next-generation therapeutics for stress-related psychiatric disorders.

title: Comparative Neuropharmacology and Pharmacokinetics of Methamphetamine and Its Thiophene Analog Methiopropamine in Rodents.
abstract: Methamphetamine (METH) remains a significant public health concern due to its high abuse potential and neurotoxic effects. Novel psychoactive substances, including methiopropamine (MPA), a thiophene analog of METH, have emerged as alternatives with potentially altered pharmacological profiles. This study investigated the comparative neuropharmacology and pharmacokinetics of METH and MPA in rodent models to elucidate their differential mechanisms of action and toxicity profiles. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10/group) received acute intraperitoneal injections of METH or MPA (1, 5, or 10 mg/kg) or saline vehicle. Locomotor activity was assessed using automated activity chambers over 120 minutes. Dopamine and serotonin release in striatal and hippocampal regions were measured via in vivo microdialysis. Pharmacokinetic parameters were determined through HPLC-MS/MS analysis of plasma and brain tissue samples collected at multiple time points. Monoamine transporter inhibition was evaluated using synaptosomes from rat brain tissue. Both compounds dose-dependently increased locomotor activity, though MPA exhibited 40% lower peak activity compared to METH at equivalent doses. METH produced greater dopamine release in striatum (450% vs 280% of baseline for MPA at 10 mg/kg), while MPA showed preferential serotonin release in hippocampus. MPA demonstrated slower brain penetration (Tmax: 45 vs 15 minutes) but prolonged elimination half-life (3.2 vs 1.8 hours). Transporter binding assays revealed MPA's greater selectivity for serotonin versus dopamine transporters compared to METH. These findings suggest MPA exhibits distinct pharmacokinetic properties and monoaminergic selectivity profiles that may contribute to altered behavioral and toxicological outcomes relative to METH.

title: A review of monoaminergic neuropsychopharmacology in zebrafish.
abstract: The zebrafish (Danio rerio) has emerged as a powerful vertebrate model for investigating monoaminergic neurotransmission and its pharmacological modulation. This review synthesizes current knowledge of dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic systems in zebrafish and their relevance to neuropsychopharmacological research. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published studies examining monoamine neurotransmitter distribution, receptor pharmacology, and behavioral responses to psychoactive compounds in zebrafish larvae and adults. Immunohistochemical and in situ hybridization studies reveal conserved monoaminergic neuronal populations in zebrafish brain regions analogous to mammalian structures, including dopaminergic neurons in the posterior tuberculum, serotonergic neurons in the raphe, and noradrenergic neurons in the locus coeruleus homolog. Pharmacological studies demonstrate that zebrafish exhibit dose-dependent behavioral responses to selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, dopamine receptor agonists and antagonists, and norepinephrine transporter inhibitors that parallel mammalian responses. High-throughput behavioral assays, including novel tank exploration, shoaling behavior, and prepulse inhibition paradigms, provide robust readouts for monoaminergic function. Genetic approaches using morpholinos and mutant lines further validate the functional conservation of monoaminergic pathways. The transparency of zebrafish larvae enables real-time visualization of neurotransmitter dynamics using genetically-encoded biosensors. These findings establish zebrafish as a tractable model system for screening neuroactive compounds, investigating monoamine-related psychiatric disorders, and elucidating fundamental mechanisms of monoaminergic neurotransmission with high translational relevance to human neuropsychopharmacology.

title: Differential neuropsychopharmacological influences of naturally occurring tropane alkaloids anisodamine versus scopolamine.
abstract: Tropane alkaloids have long been recognized for their potent anticholinergic properties, yet differential effects among naturally occurring compounds remain poorly characterized. While scopolamine's neuropsychopharmacological profile is well-established, anisodamine, a structurally related tropane alkaloid derived from Anisodus tanguticus, has received limited investigation despite traditional medicinal use. This study compared the behavioral and neurochemical effects of anisodamine and scopolamine in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received intraperitoneal injections of anisodamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg), scopolamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg), or saline vehicle (n=8-10 per group). Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity monitoring, novel object recognition testing, and elevated plus maze evaluation. Neurochemical analyses measured acetylcholine and dopamine levels in hippocampus, striatum, and prefrontal cortex using HPLC. Both compounds dose-dependently impaired novel object recognition and increased locomotor activity compared to controls. However, anisodamine produced significantly less severe cognitive deficits at equivalent doses (p<0.05) and showed reduced anxiogenic effects in the elevated plus maze. Neurochemically, while both alkaloids decreased hippocampal acetylcholine levels, anisodamine caused less pronounced reductions (42% vs 68% decrease at 1.0 mg/kg, p<0.01). Striatal dopamine levels were differentially affected, with scopolamine producing greater increases than anisodamine. These findings suggest anisodamine exhibits a more selective anticholinergic profile with potentially fewer cognitive side effects, warranting further investigation as a therapeutic alternative to conventional anticholinergic agents.

title: The inebri-actometer: a device for measuring the locomotor activity of Drosophila exposed to ethanol vapor.
abstract: The effects of ethanol on locomotor behavior in Drosophila melanogaster have become increasingly important for understanding the genetic and molecular basis of alcohol sensitivity and tolerance. However, existing methods for assessing fly locomotion following ethanol exposure are often labor-intensive, subjective, or inadequately controlled for vapor concentration. We describe a novel automated device, the inebri-actometer, designed to simultaneously measure locomotor activity in individual flies while controlling ethanol vapor exposure with high precision. The apparatus consists of 16 individual chambers (8 mm diameter × 3 mm height) arranged in a 4×4 array, each equipped with infrared beam-break sensors connected to a computerized data acquisition system. Ethanol vapor is delivered through a regulated flow system that maintains precise concentrations (0-20% v/v) as confirmed by gas chromatography. We validated the device using wild-type Canton-S flies and the ethanol-sensitive mutant cheapdate. Exposure to increasing ethanol concentrations (0, 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0% v/v) produced dose-dependent decreases in locomotor activity, with cheapdate mutants showing significantly greater sensitivity (EC₅₀ = 3.2% v/v) compared to wild-type flies (EC₅₀ = 6.8% v/v). The system demonstrated excellent reproducibility (coefficient of variation < 15%) and allowed continuous monitoring for up to 24 hours. The inebri-actometer provides a standardized, high-throughput method for quantifying ethanol's effects on Drosophila locomotion, facilitating genetic screens and pharmacological studies investigating the mechanisms underlying alcohol-induced behavioral changes.

title: Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS).
abstract: Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) represents a non-invasive method of modulating cortical excitability through the application of weak direct current to the human brain. While previous studies have demonstrated that tDCS can induce polarity-dependent changes in neuronal activity, the underlying neurophysiological mechanisms and optimal stimulation parameters remain poorly understood. We investigated the effects of tDCS on cortical excitability and plasticity using transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) and electroencephalography (EEG) in twelve healthy volunteers. Participants received anodal, cathodal, or sham tDCS (1 mA, 13 minutes) applied over the left primary motor cortex (M1) using saline-soaked electrodes. Motor evoked potentials (MEPs) were recorded from the contralateral first dorsal interosseous muscle before, during, and up to 60 minutes following stimulation. EEG was simultaneously recorded to assess changes in cortical rhythms. Anodal tDCS significantly increased MEP amplitudes by 35±12% that persisted for at least 30 minutes post-stimulation, while cathodal stimulation decreased MEP amplitudes by 28±9% for up to 45 minutes. EEG analysis revealed increased alpha power (8-12 Hz) following anodal stimulation and decreased alpha activity after cathodal stimulation. Sham stimulation produced no significant changes. These findings demonstrate that tDCS induces polarity-specific, sustained modifications of cortical excitability that outlast the stimulation period. The prolonged after-effects suggest involvement of synaptic plasticity mechanisms, potentially mediated by NMDA receptor-dependent processes. These results support tDCS as a promising tool for investigating cortical plasticity and developing therapeutic interventions for neurological disorders.

title: Neuropharmacology of motion sickness and emesis. A review.
abstract: Motion sickness represents a complex neurovestibular syndrome involving sensory conflict between visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive inputs, ultimately resulting in nausea, vomiting, and associated autonomic symptoms. The neural pathways mediating motion-induced emesis involve the vestibular nuclei, chemoreceptor trigger zone, and vomiting center in the medulla oblongata, with multiple neurotransmitter systems playing critical roles in symptom generation and therapeutic intervention. This review examines the current understanding of neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying motion sickness and evaluates therapeutic approaches based on neurotransmitter modulation. A comprehensive analysis of published literature from 1970-1993 was conducted, focusing on animal studies and human clinical trials investigating motion sickness pathophysiology and treatment. Histaminergic pathways appear central to motion sickness susceptibility, with H1 receptor antagonists such as dimenhydrinate and meclizine demonstrating significant prophylactic efficacy. Cholinergic mechanisms involving muscarinic receptors also contribute substantially, as evidenced by the therapeutic benefit of scopolamine, particularly via transdermal delivery systems. Dopaminergic neurotransmission at the chemoreceptor trigger zone plays a secondary but important role, with D2 receptor antagonists showing moderate anti-emetic properties. Recent investigations suggest involvement of serotonergic pathways and potential therapeutic applications of 5-HT3 receptor antagonists. The multifactorial nature of motion sickness neuropharmacology indicates that combination therapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems may offer superior therapeutic outcomes compared to single-agent approaches, while minimizing dose-dependent adverse effects such as sedation and cognitive impairment.

title: Metabolic changes detected by proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy in vivo and in vitro in a murin model of Parkinson's disease, the MPTP-intoxicated mouse.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is characterized by progressive dopaminergic neurodegeneration in the substantia nigra, yet the underlying metabolic alterations remain incompletely understood. The 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP) mouse model provides a well-established system for investigating PD pathophysiology. We employed proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (¹H-MRS) to characterize metabolic changes in MPTP-intoxicated mice both in vivo and in vitro. Male C57BL/6 mice (n=24) received four intraperitoneal injections of MPTP (20 mg/kg) at 2-hour intervals, while controls received saline. In vivo ¹H-MRS was performed at 7.0 Tesla focusing on striatal and nigral regions at 1, 7, and 21 days post-treatment. High-resolution magic angle spinning ¹H-MRS was conducted on extracted brain tissue. MPTP treatment resulted in significant reductions in N-acetylaspartate/creatine ratios in both striatum (32% decrease, p<0.01) and substantia nigra (28% decrease, p<0.05) by day 7, persisting through day 21. Lactate levels were elevated 2.1-fold in striatum at day 1, normalizing by day 7. Tissue analysis revealed decreased glutamate (41% reduction, p<0.01) and increased myo-inositol (38% increase, p<0.05) in striatal samples. Taurine levels showed a biphasic response with initial elevation followed by significant depletion. These findings demonstrate that MPTP intoxication produces distinct metabolic signatures detectable by ¹H-MRS, including markers of neuronal dysfunction, altered energy metabolism, and glial activation. The metabolic profile suggests both acute energetic stress and chronic neurodegenerative processes, providing insights into PD pathogenesis and potential therapeutic targets.

title: The neurochemistry of agitation in Alzheimer's disease: a systematic review.
abstract: Agitation represents one of the most prevalent and distressing behavioral symptoms in Alzheimer's disease (AD), affecting up to 70% of patients and significantly impacting quality of life for both patients and caregivers. Despite its clinical importance, the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. This systematic review examined the current literature on neurochemical correlates of agitation in AD to identify potential therapeutic targets and biomarkers. We conducted a comprehensive search of PubMed, EMBASE, and PsycINFO databases from inception through December 2017, focusing on studies investigating neurotransmitter systems, neuropeptides, and neuroinflammatory markers in relation to agitation in AD patients. Twenty-eight studies met inclusion criteria, encompassing cerebrospinal fluid analyses, neuroimaging studies, and post-mortem brain tissue examinations. Results revealed consistent evidence for dysregulation of multiple neurotransmitter systems, particularly cholinergic hypofunction in frontal and temporal regions, altered serotonergic signaling with reduced 5-HT2A receptor density, and noradrenergic hyperactivity. Additionally, elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α) and reduced GABA-mediated inhibition were frequently associated with agitation severity. Dopaminergic abnormalities showed mixed findings across studies. The neurochemical profile of agitation in AD appears to involve complex interactions between neurotransmitter deficits, neuroinflammation, and loss of inhibitory control. These findings support a multi-target therapeutic approach and highlight the need for personalized treatment strategies based on individual neurochemical profiles. Future research should focus on longitudinal studies and development of accessible biomarkers to guide clinical decision-making.

title: Comparative analysis of inhibitory effects of caged ligands for the NMDA receptor.
abstract: Photolytic release of neurotransmitters from caged precursors is a useful method to study synaptic processes with high temporal and spatial resolution.
