At present, the two most widely used classes of caged precursors for studies on glutamate receptors are based on derivatives of the 2-nitrobenzyl caging group (alpha-carboxy-2-nitrobenzyl, CNB) and the nitroindoline caging group (7-nitroindoline, NI, and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindoline, MNI). Besides NI- and MNI-caged amino acids being thermally more stable than the CNB-caged amino acids, there have been no other major advantages reported of using compounds from either of these two classes. Here, we show inhibitory effects of CNB-glutamate and a number of other CNB-caged agonists on N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors at non-saturating concentrations of the co-agonist glycine. In contrast, NI- and MNI-glutamate and most other NI-/MNI-caged agonists that we tested were inert under these conditions. Furthermore, we demonstrate that carboxynitroindoline-caged glycine (CNI-glycine), which was previously found to inhibit glycine receptors, has no such effect on NMDA receptors. Together, these findings underline the usefulness of NI- and MNI-caged ligands and show that CNB-caged compounds should be avoided in studies involving NMDA receptors.

title: Manipulating neuropeptidergic pathways in humans: a novel approach to neuropharmacology?
abstract: The manipulation of neuropeptidergic systems represents an emerging frontier in human neuropharmacology, yet direct intervention strategies remain limited by blood-brain barrier penetration and receptor selectivity issues. This study investigated the feasibility of modulating key neuropeptide pathways using a combination of peripherally administered peptide analogues and intranasal delivery systems in healthy volunteers. Twenty-four subjects (aged 22-35 years) received either substance P antagonist L-733,060 (0.5 mg intranasal), oxytocin analogue carbetocin (100 μg i.v.), or placebo in a randomized, double-blind crossover design. Outcome measures included cerebrospinal fluid neuropeptide concentrations, pain threshold testing, social cognition tasks, and neuroimaging with PET using [¹¹C]carfentanil. Intranasal L-733,060 administration resulted in significant reduction of CSF substance P levels (62% decrease at 2h, p<0.01) and increased thermal pain thresholds by 23% compared to placebo (p<0.05). Carbetocin treatment enhanced performance on emotional face recognition tasks (15% improvement in accuracy, p<0.001) and increased μ-opioid receptor availability in limbic regions by 18-25%. Both interventions were well-tolerated with minimal side effects. PET imaging confirmed central nervous system engagement of target pathways within 60 minutes of administration. These findings demonstrate that selective neuropeptidergic manipulation in humans is achievable using optimized delivery methods. This approach may offer therapeutic potential for pain disorders, psychiatric conditions, and social dysfunction, warranting further investigation in patient populations.

title: Microbore liquid chromatography with UV detection to study the in vivo passage of compound 21, a non-peptidergic AT₂ receptor agonist, to the striatum in rats.
abstract: A microbore liquid chromatography method coupled to UV detection was developed and validated in order to monitor the passage of compound 21 (C21), a non-peptide angiotensin II type 2 receptor agonist, to the striatum of rats. For this purpose, sampling from the striatum was performed using the in vivo microdialysis technique. Separations were performed on a C₁₈ microbore (1mm i.d.) column using gradient elution. The retention time for C21 was found to be 6.3 min. The calibration curve was linear between 10 and 200 ng/ml with a correlation coefficient ≥ 0.999. The limit of detection and the limit of quantification were 3 and 10 ng/ml respectively. The intra-day and the inter-day precision (RSD%) ranged between 0.5 and 4.6% with an average recovery of 101.5±10.0% (mean±SD, n=15). In vivo experiments were performed on rats to measure the concentration of C21 in striatal dialysates after intraperitoneal (10 or 50mg/kg) or intravenous injection (10 mg/kg or 20 mg/kg) of C21 and suggest minimal passage of the compound to the striatum.

title: Computational models of neuronal biophysics and the characterization of potential neuropharmacological targets.
abstract: The identification and characterization of potential pharmacological targets in neurology and psychiatry is a fundamental problem at the intersection between medicinal chemistry and the neurosciences. Exciting new techniques in proteomics and genomics have fostered rapid progress, opening numerous questions as to the functional consequences of ligand binding at the systems level. Psycho- and neuro-active drugs typically work in nerve cells by affecting one or more aspects of electrophysiological activity. Thus, an integrated understanding of neuropharmacological agents requires bridging the gap between their molecular mechanisms and the biophysical determinants of neuronal function. Computational neuroscience and bioinformatics can play a major role in this functional connection. Robust quantitative models exist describing all major active membrane properties under endogenous and exogenous chemical control. These include voltage-dependent ionic channels (sodium, potassium, calcium, etc.), synaptic receptor channels (e.g. glutamatergic, GABAergic, cholinergic), and G protein coupled signaling pathways (protein kinases, phosphatases, and other enzymatic cascades). This brief review of neuromolecular medicine from the computational perspective provides compelling examples of how simulations can elucidate, explain, and predict the effect of chemical agonists, antagonists, and modulators in the nervous system.

title: Neuropharmacology of the essential oil of bergamot.
abstract: Bergamot (Citrus bergamia, Risso) is a fruit most knowledgeable for its essential oil (BEO) used in aromatherapy to minimize symptoms of stress-induced anxiety and mild mood disorders and cancer pain though the rational basis for such applications awaits to be discovered. The behavioural and EEG spectrum power effects of BEO correlate well with its exocytotic and carrier-mediated release of discrete amino acids endowed with neurotransmitter function in the mammalian hippocampus supporting the deduction that BEO is able to interfere with normal and pathological synaptic plasticity. The observed neuroprotection in the course of experimental brain ischemia and pain does support this view. In conclusion, the data yielded so far contribute to our understanding of the mode of action of this phytocomplex on nerve tissue under normal and pathological experimental conditions and provide a rational basis for the practical use of BEO in complementary medicine. The opening of a wide venue for future research and translation into clinical settings is also envisaged.

title: Future neuroprotective strategies.
abstract: The development of effective neuroprotective therapies remains a critical challenge in treating neurodegenerative diseases and acute brain injuries. Traditional single-target approaches have largely failed in clinical trials, necessitating novel multi-modal strategies that address the complex pathophysiology of neuronal death. This review examines emerging neuroprotective paradigms based on recent advances in understanding cellular mechanisms of neurodegeneration, including mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, and protein aggregation. We analyzed preclinical and clinical data from 2005-2010 focusing on combination therapies, temporal therapeutic windows, and personalized treatment approaches. Promising strategies include: (1) multi-target compounds that simultaneously modulate excitotoxicity, inflammation, and apoptotic pathways; (2) stem cell-based therapies combined with growth factor delivery; (3) nanotechnology-mediated drug delivery systems that enhance blood-brain barrier penetration; (4) preconditioning paradigms that activate endogenous protective mechanisms; and (5) biomarker-guided patient stratification for optimized therapeutic selection. Recent failures of single-agent neuroprotectants like citicoline and NXY-059 in stroke trials highlight the importance of addressing multiple injury cascades simultaneously. Future successful neuroprotection will likely require personalized combination therapies administered within optimized time windows, guided by real-time biomarker monitoring and advanced neuroimaging. The integration of systems biology approaches with traditional pharmacology offers unprecedented opportunities to develop truly effective neuroprotective strategies. These emerging paradigms represent a fundamental shift from the current "one-size-fits-all" approach toward precision neuroprotection tailored to individual pathophysiological profiles and injury mechanisms.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology and the genetics of schizophrenia: a history of the diagnosis of schizophrenia.
abstract: The diagnostic conceptualization of schizophrenia has undergone substantial revision since Kraepelin's original description of dementia praecox, with implications for contemporary neuropsychopharmacological research and genetic studies. This historical analysis examines how evolving diagnostic criteria have influenced neurobiological investigations and therapeutic approaches from 1911 to 2004. We conducted a systematic review of diagnostic manuals, landmark clinical studies, and pharmacological trials, analyzing the relationship between diagnostic refinement and drug development. Additionally, we examined how changes in diagnostic boundaries affected genetic linkage studies and heritability estimates. Our analysis reveals three distinct phases in schizophrenia diagnosis: the broad Bleulerian concept (1911-1970), the restrictive neo-Kraepelinian approach embodied in DSM-III (1980), and the current dimensional considerations. The introduction of chlorpromazine in 1954 marked a pivotal shift toward symptom-based classification systems, while subsequent development of atypical antipsychotics challenged traditional positive-negative symptom dichotomies. Genetic studies show marked heterogeneity in findings across diagnostic eras, with heritability estimates ranging from 0.64 to 0.84 depending on diagnostic stringency. Linkage studies conducted under DSM-III-R criteria demonstrated improved reproducibility compared to earlier investigations using broader phenotypic definitions. The evolution of schizophrenia diagnosis reflects advancing neurobiological understanding while simultaneously constraining research methodologies. Current pharmacogenomic approaches must account for this diagnostic heterogeneity when interpreting treatment response variability. Future neuropsychopharmacological research would benefit from incorporating dimensional symptom measures alongside categorical diagnoses to optimize therapeutic targeting and genetic association studies.

title: Impairments in reflective cognitive functions in alcoholics: a neuropharmacological model.
abstract: The ability to make use of reflective cognitive operations in monitoring and evaluating remembered events is impaired in subgroups of nominally cognitively unimpaired, detoxified alcoholics. Alcoholics, relative to controls, make more errors in identifying the source of remembered information (i.e. whether a remembered word was self-generated or was a stimulus word presented by the experimenter), and are impaired in their ability to inhibit confabulatory errors (intrusions). The cognitive-memory impairment expressed in benzodiazepine-treated normal volunteers mimics this impairment in alcoholics. Disturbances in prefrontal and frontal lobe functions may be involved in this selective impairment in cognition in many alcoholics and may also contribute to what accounts for the failures in reflective cognitive operations observed in amnestic patients.

title: Role of neurologists in the pharmaceutical industry.
abstract: Important advances in the field of clinical neuropharmacology have created an increasing demand by the pharmaceutical industry for neurologists. As recently as a decade ago, neurologists were relatively rare in the pharmaceutical industry. I describe the roles played by industrial neurologists and review both the opportunities and risks in this career path.

title: Promising avenues of therapeutics for bipolar illness.
abstract: The treatment of bipolar disorder remains challenging, with current mood stabilizers providing incomplete symptom control and significant side effects in many patients. Recent advances in understanding the neurobiology of bipolar illness have identified novel therapeutic targets beyond traditional monoaminergic pathways. This review examines emerging pharmacological approaches that show promise for improving treatment outcomes. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and early clinical data for compounds targeting glutamatergic signaling, particularly NMDA receptor modulators and metabotropic glutamate receptor antagonists. Additionally, we evaluated agents affecting cellular resilience pathways, including glycogen synthase kinase-3 inhibitors, protein kinase C modulators, and compounds enhancing neuroplasticity through brain-derived neurotrophic factor upregulation. Anticonvulsants with novel mechanisms, such as lamotrigine's effects on voltage-gated sodium channels, demonstrate efficacy in bipolar depression with minimal switching risk. Preliminary evidence suggests that glutamate-modulating agents like riluzole and memantine may offer rapid mood stabilization, while omega-3 fatty acids show anti-inflammatory properties relevant to bipolar pathophysiology. Chromium supplementation has demonstrated modest benefits in atypical depression, and second-generation antipsychotics continue to expand therapeutic options for acute mania and maintenance therapy. These diverse mechanistic approaches reflect the complex, multifactorial nature of bipolar disorder. While traditional lithium and valproate remain cornerstone treatments, the integration of targeted therapies addressing specific neurobiological abnormalities holds considerable promise for personalized treatment strategies and improved long-term outcomes in bipolar illness.

title: CSF neurochemicals during tryptophan depletion in individuals with remitted depression and healthy controls.
abstract: The purpose of this study was to examine the differential effects of acute tryptophan (TRP) depletion vs. sham condition on plasma, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) biochemical parameters, and mood in the following three subject groups: (1) nine antidepressant-free individuals with remitted depression, (2) eight paroxetine-treated individuals with recently remitted depression, and (3) seven healthy controls. Plasma TRP decreased during TRP depletion and increased during sham condition (p<.01). CSF TRP and 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid were lower during TRP depletion than sham condition (p<.01 each). During TRP depletion, CSF TRP correlated significantly with the plasma sum of large neutral amino acids (SigmaLNAA) (R=-.52, p=.01), but did not significantly correlate with plasma TRP (R=.15, p=.52). The correlation between CSF TRP and ratio of TRP to SigmaLNAA was R=.41 and p=.06 during TRP depletion, and R=-.44 and p=.04 during sham condition. A negative correlation trend was observed between CSF-TRP levels and peak Hamilton Depression Rating Scale scores during TRP depletion in patients recovered from depression (R=-.45, p=.07), but not in healthy controls (R=-.01, p=.98). CSF neuropeptide Y was higher during TRP depletion than sham condition (t=1.75, p<.10). These results illustrate the importance of assessing plasma SigmaLNAA when using the TRP depletion paradigm. The use of a single CSF sampling technique although practical may result in data acquisition limitations.
journal: European neuropsychopharmacology : the journal of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Endocannabinoids and the neurochemistry of gluttony.
abstract: Cannabis exerts its actions by mimicking 'endocannabinoid' neuromodulators at CB1 receptors in brain and peripheral tissues. The endocannabinoids both stimulate appetite by increasing food craving and enjoyment and promote the deposition of energy as fat in adipose tissues. These findings have raised the possibility of CB1 antagonists as a novel class of anti-obesity agents.

title: Neuropharmacology of the mesolimbic system and associated circuits on social hierarchies.
abstract: Social hierarchies are fundamental organizational structures across species, yet the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying dominance behaviors and rank-related neuroplasticity remain poorly understood. The mesolimbic dopaminergic system, comprising projections from the ventral tegmental area (VTA) to nucleus accumbens (NAc) and associated limbic structures, has been implicated in reward processing and social behaviors. Here, we investigated how pharmacological manipulation of mesolimbic circuits affects social rank establishment and maintenance using a tube test paradigm in adult male C57BL/6J mice. Animals were housed in groups of four and subjected to daily social ranking assessments following bilateral microinfusions of vehicle, D1/D2 receptor antagonists (SCH23390/raclopride), or selective optogenetic stimulation of VTA-NAc projections. Chronic social defeat stress was used to model subordinate status. Results demonstrated that D1 receptor blockade in NAc significantly reduced dominance behaviors and decreased winning probability in tube tests (p<0.001), while D2 antagonism had minimal effects. Optogenetic activation of VTA-NAc terminals increased aggressive behaviors and social dominance rank. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed that dominant animals exhibited elevated c-Fos expression in NAc shell and decreased glucocorticoid receptor density compared to subordinates. RNA sequencing identified differential expression of genes involved in synaptic plasticity and stress response pathways in mesolimbic regions based on social rank. These findings indicate that dopaminergic signaling through D1 receptors in NAc critically regulates social hierarchy formation and maintenance, suggesting therapeutic targets for disorders characterized by altered social functioning and providing mechanistic insights into the neurobiological basis of social dominance behaviors.

title: Microdialysis in freely moving mice: determination of acetylcholine, serotonin and noradrenaline release in galanin transgenic mice.
abstract: Galanin, a 29-amino acid neuropeptide, has been implicated in the modulation of cholinergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic neurotransmission, but its precise role in regulating neurotransmitter release remains poorly understood. To investigate the effects of galanin overexpression on central neurotransmitter systems, we employed in vivo microdialysis to simultaneously monitor acetylcholine, serotonin, and noradrenaline release in freely moving transgenic mice expressing human galanin under the dopamine β-hydroxylase promoter. Concentric microdialysis probes were stereotaxically implanted into the hippocampus and frontal cortex of transgenic mice and wild-type littermates. Dialysate samples were collected every 20 minutes and analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection for monoamines and a chemiluminescent assay for acetylcholine. Baseline extracellular acetylcholine levels were significantly reduced by 35% in the hippocampus and 28% in the frontal cortex of galanin transgenic mice compared to controls. Potassium-evoked acetylcholine release was similarly attenuated in transgenic animals. Conversely, basal serotonin concentrations were elevated by 42% in the hippocampus of transgenic mice, while noradrenaline levels showed no significant differences between groups. Pharmacological challenge with the acetylcholinesterase inhibitor physostigmine produced a blunted response in transgenic mice, suggesting impaired cholinergic function. These findings demonstrate that chronic galanin overexpression selectively modulates neurotransmitter release in a region- and transmitter-specific manner, providing evidence for galanin's inhibitory influence on cholinergic transmission while facilitating serotonergic activity. This microdialysis approach in transgenic mice offers a valuable tool for investigating neuropeptide-neurotransmitter interactions in vivo.

title: Pharmacotherapy of alcohol dependence: a review of the clinical data.
abstract: Alcohol dependence represents a major public health concern affecting millions worldwide, yet pharmacological treatment options remain limited. Current therapeutic approaches have shown modest efficacy, highlighting the need for comprehensive evaluation of available agents. This review systematically examines clinical trial data for FDA-approved medications and emerging pharmacotherapies in alcohol dependence treatment. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of randomized controlled trials published between 1994-2004, focusing on naltrexone, acamprosate, and disulfiram, as well as investigational compounds including topiramate, ondansetron, and baclofen. Primary outcome measures included relapse rates, time to first drink, percentage of drinking days, and treatment retention. Naltrexone demonstrated consistent efficacy in reducing heavy drinking episodes and craving intensity across multiple studies, with effect sizes ranging from 0.2-0.4. Acamprosate showed superior efficacy in maintaining abstinence, particularly in European trials, with 13-23% improvement over placebo in abstinence rates at 6 months. Disulfiram effectiveness was highly dependent on supervised administration and patient compliance. Among investigational agents, topiramate showed promising results in reducing drinking frequency and promoting abstinence, while ondansetron demonstrated selective efficacy in early-onset alcoholics. Treatment response varied significantly based on patient characteristics, including genetic polymorphisms in opioid and dopamine systems, drinking patterns, and psychosocial factors. Current pharmacotherapies provide meaningful but modest benefits for alcohol dependence treatment. Optimal outcomes likely require personalized approaches combining medications with psychosocial interventions. Future research should focus on identifying predictors of treatment response and developing novel therapeutic targets.

title: Neuropharmacology of 3,4-Methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV), Its Metabolites, and Related Analogs.
abstract: 3,4-Methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV) is a synthetic cathinone that emerged as a prominent constituent of "bath salts" and has been associated with severe psychostimulant toxicity and abuse liability. Despite regulatory scheduling, MDPV analogs continue to appear in illicit drug markets, yet their pharmacological profiles remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the neuropharmacological properties of MDPV, its primary metabolites, and five structural analogs using in vitro transporter assays, microdialysis, and behavioral paradigms in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Radioligand binding assays revealed that MDPV potently inhibited dopamine (DAT) and norepinephrine (NET) transporters with IC₅₀ values of 4.2 and 26.1 nM, respectively, while showing minimal serotonin transporter activity. The 3,4-dihydroxy and 4-hydroxy-3-methoxy metabolites retained significant DAT inhibition (IC₅₀ 89-156 nM) but displayed reduced potency compared to the parent compound. Structural analogs α-PVP and MDPBP exhibited similar DAT/NET selectivity profiles, while pyrrolidinyl modifications substantially altered transporter selectivity. In vivo microdialysis demonstrated that MDPV (1-3 mg/kg, i.p.) produced dose-dependent increases in nucleus accumbens dopamine levels lasting 4-6 hours. Self-administration studies confirmed robust reinforcing effects, with MDPV maintaining higher response rates than cocaine at equivalent doses. These findings demonstrate that MDPV and related analogs function as potent DAT/NET inhibitors with high abuse potential. The persistent activity of MDPV metabolites may contribute to prolonged psychoactive effects observed clinically. These data provide crucial pharmacological insights for understanding synthetic cathinone toxicity and informing regulatory strategies for emerging analogs.

title: Traumatic brain injury: a review of practice management and recent advances.
abstract: This review of traumatic brain injury encompasses its impact on society, pathophysiology, and rehabilitative management. Topics include mild traumatic brain injury, outcomes, prognosis, cognitive rehabilitation, behavioral management, and neurologic and medical complications. Emphasis is placed on clinically relevant topics that have had recent developments or have been historically difficult to treat. Neurologic complications discussed include seizure, balance, visual dysfunction, and spasticity. Medical complications discussed include neuroendocrine and pain issues.
journal: Physical medicine and rehabilitation clinics of North America

title: "Novel Psychopharmacology for Depressive Disorders".
abstract: Major depressive disorder (MDD) affects over 300 million individuals worldwide, with current antidepressant therapies demonstrating limited efficacy and significant side effects. Traditional monoamine-based treatments show response rates of only 60-70%, necessitating novel therapeutic approaches. This study investigated the antidepressant potential of compound ZL-47, a selective modulator of synaptic plasticity targeting both AMPA receptor potentiation and mTOR pathway activation. Male C57BL/6J mice (n=48) were subjected to chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) for 6 weeks to induce depression-like behaviors. Animals received daily intraperitoneal injections of ZL-47 (5, 10, or 20 mg/kg), fluoxetine (20 mg/kg), or vehicle for 14 days. Behavioral assessments included forced swim test, sucrose preference test, and open field test. Hippocampal tissue was analyzed for BDNF expression, dendritic spine density, and synaptic protein levels using Western blot and Golgi staining. ZL-47 treatment dose-dependently reduced immobility time in the forced swim test (p<0.001) and restored sucrose preference (p<0.01) compared to vehicle controls. Notably, behavioral improvements emerged after 7 days of treatment, significantly faster than fluoxetine (14 days). Molecular analyses revealed increased BDNF expression (2.3-fold), enhanced dendritic spine density (34% increase), and elevated PSD-95 levels in ZL-47-treated animals. These findings suggest that dual AMPA/mTOR modulation represents a promising therapeutic strategy for depression, offering rapid onset and robust efficacy through synaptic plasticity enhancement. Further clinical investigation is warranted to evaluate translational potential.
journal: Advances in experimental medicine and biology

title: Towards a clinical methodology for neuropsychopharmacological research.
abstract: Neuropsychopharmacology is dedicated to the study of the pathophysiology and treatment of mental pathology with the employment of centrally acting drugs. In neuropsychopharmacological research the clinical effects of a psychotropic drug are linked to the effects of the substance on brain structures involved in its mode of action. It is assumed, that knowledge about the mode of action of a selectively effective psychotropic drug will provide clues about the pathophysiology of the illness, and conversely, that knowledge about the pathophysiology of an illness, will provide clues for developing clinically more effective psychotropic drugs. Since the currently employed clinical methodology for the demonstration of therapeutic efficacy links the mode of action of psychotropic drugs to pharmacologically heterogeneous populations, neuropsychopharmacological research does not provide the necessary feedback for developing more effective drugs. To resolve the pharmacological heterogeneity within currently used diagnoses, attempts were made to split syndrome-based psychiatric diagnoses into discrete neurobiological deficits, and to replace traditional psychiatric nosology by a genetic psychiatric nosology. Yet, to date, there is no alternative methodology to psychopathology-based psychiatric nosology for classifying mental pathology in a clinically relevant manner. As we are moving from the "neurotransmitter era" to a "genetic era" in neuropsychopharmacology, the need for identifying pharmacologically homogenous populations is becoming imminent. All primary targets of psychotropic drugs in the brain are encoded by genes which are identified, and any nosologic entity or psychiatric syndrome that corresponds with a treatment responsive population is a candidate for the generation of genetic hypotheses relevant to mental illness. Recognition that progress in neuropsychopharmacology, and molecular genetic research, depends on the speed clinical research can resolve the pharmacological heterogeneity within currently used diagnoses, led to the development of methodologies for the identification of treatment responsive form(s) of illness, such as the Composite Diagnostic Evaluation (CODE) System, and nosologic homotyping. The CODE System is a methodology for the identification of treatment responsive forms of illness if covered up by consensus-based diagnoses; it consists of a set of diagnostic algorithms that can assign simultaneously a diagnosis from several classifications to a patient. Nosologic homotypes are identical in elementary units of mental illness and are assigned the same position in the nosologic matrix, based on three "nosologic organizing principles. The empirically derived diagnostic categories are suitable for testing hypotheses relevant to the relationship between the "processing of mental events" and "signal transduction" in the central nervous system.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacologia Hungarica : a Magyar Pszichofarmakologiai Egyesulet lapja = official journal of the Hungarian Association of Psychopharmacology

title: The nature of dopamine dysfunction in schizophrenia and what this means for treatment.
abstract: Schizophrenia is characterized by complex dopaminergic abnormalities that have profound implications for therapeutic intervention. While the dopamine hypothesis has evolved significantly since its initial formulation, the precise nature of dopamine dysfunction across brain regions remains incompletely understood. We conducted a comprehensive meta-analysis of neuroimaging and postmortem studies published between 1990 and 2011, supplemented by pharmacokinetic data from 847 patients with schizophrenia and 423 healthy controls. Positron emission tomography studies using [¹¹C]raclopride and [¹⁸F]DOPA revealed region-specific patterns of dopamine dysfunction, with hyperactivity in mesolimbic circuits (striatum: effect size = 0.72, p < 0.001) and hypoactivity in mesocortical pathways (prefrontal cortex: effect size = -0.58, p < 0.001). Postmortem analysis demonstrated altered D2 receptor density and dopamine transporter expression that correlated with symptom severity. Pharmacological challenge studies indicated that conventional antipsychotics effectively address positive symptoms through mesolimbic D2 blockade but worsen cognitive deficits by further reducing mesocortical dopamine function. Conversely, partial D2 agonists showed superior cognitive outcomes while maintaining antipsychotic efficacy. These findings suggest that schizophrenia involves simultaneous dopamine excess and deficiency in distinct neural circuits. Optimal treatment strategies should selectively modulate regional dopamine function rather than providing uniform receptor blockade. Future therapeutic development should focus on circuit-specific interventions, including partial agonists and allosteric modulators, to address the heterogeneous nature of dopaminergic dysfunction in schizophrenia while minimizing cognitive and motor side effects.

title: From neuroscience to application in neuropharmacology: A generation of progress in electrophysiology.
abstract: A continuum from neuronal cellular/subcellular properties to system processes appears to exist in many instances and to allow privileged approaches in neuroscience and neuropharmacology research. Brain signals and the cholinergic and GABAergic systems, in vivo and in vitro evidence from studies on the retina, or the "gamma band" oscillations in neuron membrane potential/spiking rate and neuronal assemblies are examples in this respect. However, spontaneous and stimulus-event-related signals at any location and time point reflect brain state conditions that depend on neuromodulation, neurotransmitter interaction, hormones (e.g., glucocorticois, ACTH, estrogens) and neuroendocrine interaction at different levels of complexity, as well as on the spontaneous or experimentally-induced changes in metabolism (e.g., glucose, ammonia), blood flow, pO2, pCO2, acid/base balance, K activity, etc., that occur locally or systemically. Any of these factors can account for individual differences and/or changes over time that often are (or need to be) neglected in pharmaco-EEG studies or are dealt with statistically and by controlling the experimental conditions. As a result, the electrophysiological effects of neuroactive drugs are to an extent non-specific and require adequate modeling and precise correlation with independent parameters (e.g., drug kinetics, vigilance, hormonal profile or metabolic status, etc.) to avoid biased results in otherwise controlled studies.

title: A pre-clinical study showing how dopaminergic drugs administered during pre-exposure can impair or facilitate latent inhibition.
abstract: Latent inhibition (LI), the phenomenon whereby pre-exposure to a stimulus retards subsequent associative learning, serves as an important model for understanding attentional processes and schizophrenia-related cognitive deficits. Dopaminergic dysfunction has been implicated in LI disruption, yet the specific effects of dopaminergic manipulations during the pre-exposure phase remain poorly characterized. The present study examined how dopamine D1 and D2 receptor ligands administered during pre-exposure affect LI expression in rats. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received vehicle, the D1 agonist SKF-38393 (1.0 or 3.0 mg/kg), D1 antagonist SCH-23390 (0.01 or 0.03 mg/kg), D2 agonist quinpirole (0.5 or 1.0 mg/kg), or D2 antagonist haloperidol (0.05 or 0.1 mg/kg) 30 minutes before each pre-exposure session. Following standard conditioning and testing phases conducted drug-free, suppression ratios were calculated to assess LI. Control animals demonstrated robust LI, with pre-exposed subjects showing significantly less conditioned suppression than non-pre-exposed controls. High-dose SKF-38393 (3.0 mg/kg) and haloperidol (0.1 mg/kg) completely abolished LI, while low-dose quinpirole (0.5 mg/kg) enhanced LI expression beyond control levels. SCH-23390 had no significant effect at either dose tested. These findings demonstrate that dopaminergic manipulations during pre-exposure can bidirectionally modulate LI, with D1 receptor overstimulation and D2 receptor blockade impairing LI formation, while moderate D2 receptor activation facilitates it. These results provide important insights into the neurochemical mechanisms underlying attentional learning and may inform therapeutic approaches for disorders characterized by LI deficits.

title: Role of glutamate and dopamine receptors in the psychopharmacological profile of the indole alkaloid psychollatine.
abstract: Psychollatine (1), a new glycoside indole monoterpene alkaloid isolated from Psychotria umbellata, has shown an interesting psychopharmacological profile. This study aimed to investigate the role of NMDA glutamate and dopamine receptors in mediating the properties of 1. Psychollatine (1) was assessed for NMDA-induced seizures, MK-801-induced hyperlocomotion, amphetamine-induced lethality, and apomorphine-induced climbing behavior in mice. Psychollatine (1) (100 mg/kg) and MK-801 (0.3 mg/kg) prevented NMDA-induced seizures (P < 0.01), while 1 (100 mg/kg) attenuated the MK-801-induced hyperlocomotion (P < 0.05). Compound 1 (3 and 10 mg/kg), as well as chlorpromazine (4 mg/kg), prevented amphetamine-induced lethality (P < 0.05). Finally, 1 (10 mg/kg) (P < 0.05), MK-801 (0.2 mg/kg) (P < 0.01), and chlorpromazine (4 mg/kg) (P < 0.01) attenuated apomorphine-induced climbing behavior. The present results strongly support the involvement of NMDA glutamate receptors in the mode of action of psychollatine (1).

title: Beyond the reward hypothesis: alternative functions of nucleus accumbens dopamine.
abstract: According to the dopamine (DA) hypothesis of reward, DA systems in the brain, particularly in the nucleus accumbens, are thought to directly mediate the rewarding or primary motivational characteristics of natural stimuli such as food, water and sex, as well as various drugs of abuse. However, there are numerous problems associated with this hypothesis. Interference with accumbens DA transmission does not substantially blunt primary motivation for natural rewards such as food, but it does disrupt the propensity of animals to engage in effortful responding to obtain food. Electrophysiological and voltammetric studies indicate that novel stimuli, conditioned stimuli that predict reward, and instrumental behaviors that deliver natural rewards all act to stimulate DA activity. Accumbens DA acts as a modulator of several functions related to motivated behavior, and can influence normal and pathological cognitive function, activational aspects of motivation, anergia or psychomotor slowing in depression, the impact of conditioned stimuli, plasticity and a variety of sensorimotor functions.

title: Effects of triiodothyronine and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity in rat brain: regional differences.
abstract: The thyroid hormone triiodothyronine (T3) augments and accelerates the effects of antidepressant drugs. Although the majority of studies showing this have used tricyclics, a few studies have shown similar effects with the selective serotonin re-uptake inhibitor (SSRI) fluoxetine. In this study we investigated the effects of fluoxetine (5 mg/kg), T3 (20 microg/kg) and the combination of these drugs, each administered daily for 7 days, on serotonergic function in the rat brain, using in vivo microdialysis. Fluoxetine alone induced a trend towards desensitization of 5-HT1A autoreceptors as shown by a reduction in the effect of 8-OH-DPAT to lower 5-HT levels in frontal cortex, and desensitized 5-HT1B autoreceptors in frontal cortex. The combination of fluoxetine and T3 induced desensitization of 5-HT1B autoreceptors in hypothalamus. Since there is evidence linking hypothalamic function and depression, we suggest that this effect may partly account for the therapeutic efficacy of the combination of an SSRI and T3.

title: Tobacco psychopharmacology and public health policy: it takes a community.
abstract: This commentary is based upon the author's lecture given as the 2010 recipient of the award named in honor of Drs. Joseph V. Brady and Charles R. Schuster, given by the Psychopharmacology and Substance Abuse Division (Division 28) of the American Psychological Association (APA). The focus is on the contributions of many behavioral pharmacology researchers who collaborated very much in the spirit of an interactive community dedicated to the common cause of advancing science in service of public health. Division 28 and its members hold a prominent place in this account because, throughout the 1980s and 1990s, the Division was the lead scientific forum for bringing together researchers addressing the behavioral pharmacology of tobacco and nicotine. The commentary provides an overview of how advances utilizing animal and human models of dependence and withdrawal came to inform public health policy and more recently, tobacco product regulation. The commentary also recounts how efforts by the tobacco industry collided with those of nonindustry researchers, including Division 28 members, and how this was taken up in congressional hearings that addressed behavioral pharmacology research on tobacco. The review concludes with an overview of current challenges to behavioral pharmacology researchers to assist in guiding the regulation of tobacco products by the United States Food and Drug Administration and other national regulatory authorities, as well as guiding the implementation of the international tobacco treaty-the World Health Organization Framework Convention on Tobacco Control.

title: [The neurochemistry and neuropharmacology of frontotemporal dementia].
abstract: Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) represents a heterogeneous group of neurodegenerative disorders characterized by progressive deterioration of frontal and temporal cortices, leading to behavioral, executive, and language dysfunction. Despite growing clinical recognition, the underlying neurochemical mechanisms and therapeutic targets remain poorly understood. This review examines current knowledge of neurotransmitter systems alterations and pharmacological interventions in FTD. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published literature from 1995-2005, focusing on neurochemical findings from post-mortem studies, neuroimaging investigations, and clinical trials in FTD patients. Neurochemical studies reveal significant disruptions in multiple neurotransmitter systems, particularly reduced cholinergic function in frontal regions, with acetylcholinesterase activity decreased by 30-50% compared to controls. Serotonergic dysfunction is prominent, with decreased 5-HT2A receptor binding and reduced serotonin transporter availability correlating with behavioral symptoms. Dopaminergic abnormalities include striatal D2 receptor downregulation and altered dopamine metabolism in prefrontal areas. GABA-ergic interneuron loss contributes to disinhibition patterns. Pharmacological interventions show limited efficacy compared to Alzheimer's disease treatments. Cholinesterase inhibitors demonstrate modest benefits in some behavioral symptoms but lack consistent cognitive improvement. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show promise for managing behavioral disturbances, particularly disinhibition and compulsive behaviors. Atypical antipsychotics provide symptomatic relief but require careful monitoring due to sensitivity in this population. These findings highlight the complex neurochemical pathology underlying FTD and emphasize the need for targeted therapeutic approaches addressing specific neurotransmitter dysfunction patterns rather than broad-spectrum interventions.

title: The neuropsychopharmacology of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: More than three decades of research has attempted to elucidate the neuropsychopharmacology of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Stimulants, a principle treatment for the disorder, act on the norepinephrine (NE) and dopamine (DA) systems; this has led to a long-standing hypothesis of catecholamine dysfunction in ADHD. Animal studies show a clear role for NE and DA in the modulation of executive functions, which are often disturbed in persons with ADHD. Nonstimulant agents that are effective in the treatment of ADHD tend to affect the NE system, whereas those affecting only DA, or those that affect neither catecholamine, are less potent in reducing ADHD symptoms. Studies of the effects of NE and DA peripheral metabolites by ADHD pharmacotherapies show acute increases in levels of these catecholamines; however, their long-term turnover may be reduced. Imaging studies suggest stimulants increases DA levels in the brain, whereas some animal models of ADHD are more consistent with excessive DA activation in the disorder. Ultimately, ADHD therapy may modify activity in the NE and DA systems to a more optimal level, thus improving responses to environmental stimuli and enhancing working memory and executive function.

title: The psychopharmacology of violence: making sensible decisions.
abstract: Violence represents a complex behavioral phenotype with significant public health implications, yet the psychopharmacological approaches to its management remain fragmented and poorly systematized. Current treatment decisions often lack empirical foundation, particularly regarding the selection of appropriate interventions for different violence subtypes. This comprehensive review synthesizes evidence from randomized controlled trials, meta-analyses, and clinical case series published between 2000-2013 to establish evidence-based psychopharmacological strategies for violence management across various psychiatric conditions. We systematically analyzed 847 studies examining antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, anticonvulsants, and novel agents in treating aggressive behaviors associated with schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, personality disorders, dementia, and developmental disabilities. Atypical antipsychotics demonstrated superior efficacy compared to typical agents in reducing aggressive incidents (effect size d=0.68, p<0.001), with risperidone and olanzapine showing the strongest evidence base. Mood stabilizers, particularly lithium and valproate, proved effective for impulsive aggression in bipolar patients (response rates 65-78%). Anticonvulsants showed mixed results, with carbamazepine demonstrating modest benefits in organic brain disorders. Beta-blockers emerged as promising adjunctive treatments for treatment-refractory cases. Notably, benzodiazepines showed limited long-term utility and potential for paradoxical disinhibition. The evidence supports a diagnostic-specific, symptom-targeted approach rather than generic anti-aggressive prescribing. Future research should prioritize biomarker development and personalized treatment algorithms. Clinicians should consider violence phenomenology, underlying pathophysiology, and patient-specific factors when making psychopharmacological decisions for aggressive behaviors.

title: The future of pharmacotherapy for paediatric anxiety.
abstract: There is a growing body of data supporting the use of pharmacological agents in the treatment of paediatric anxiety. The most compelling data exists for the use of selective serotonin re-uptake inhibitors. However, questions the safe and efficacious use of medications with anxiolytic properties still remain. Although the field has progressed in the past several years, it is evident that more research needs to be done in the future.

title: Pharmacotherapy in children and adolescents with pervasive developmental disorders.
abstract: Pervasive developmental disorders (PDDs), including autistic disorder, Asperger's disorder, and pervasive developmental disorder not otherwise specified, affect approximately 0.7-2.1 per 1,000 children and present significant challenges in behavioral management and educational programming. While no medications directly treat the core symptoms of PDDs, pharmacological interventions may address associated behavioral symptoms including hyperactivity, aggression, self-injurious behaviors, and repetitive stereotypies. This retrospective chart review examined pharmacotherapy patterns and outcomes in 127 children and adolescents (ages 3-17 years, mean age 8.4 years) diagnosed with PDDs who received psychiatric services at three university-affiliated clinics between 1995-1998. Data were collected on medication types, dosages, duration of treatment, side effects, and clinical response as measured by the Clinical Global Impression-Improvement scale and standardized behavioral rating scales completed by parents and teachers. Results indicated that 78% of patients received at least one psychotropic medication during the study period. Stimulants were most frequently prescribed (42% of medicated patients), followed by selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (31%), typical antipsychotics (24%), and anticonvulsants (18%). Modest improvements in hyperactivity and aggression were observed in 65% and 58% of cases, respectively, while stereotypic behaviors showed limited response to pharmacological intervention. Common side effects included appetite suppression, sleep disturbances, and sedation. These findings suggest that while pharmacotherapy can provide symptomatic relief for certain behavioral manifestations in children with PDDs, careful monitoring and realistic expectations are essential components of comprehensive treatment planning.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychiatric nursing : official publication of the Association of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nurses, Inc

title: Dynamic of neurochemical alterations in striatum, hippocampus and cortex after the 6-OHDA mesostriatal lesion.
abstract: Parkinson's disease is characterized by progressive degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, leading to striatal dopamine depletion and motor dysfunction. While the primary pathology affects the nigrostriatal pathway, emerging evidence suggests broader neurochemical alterations may occur in interconnected brain regions. The present study investigated the temporal dynamics of neurochemical changes in striatum, hippocampus, and cortex following unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) lesions of the medial forebrain bundle in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received stereotaxic injections of 6-OHDA (8 μg/4 μl) or vehicle and were sacrificed at 1, 2, 4, and 8 weeks post-lesion (n=8-10/group). High-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection was used to quantify dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and their metabolites in dissected brain regions. 6-OHDA lesions produced >90% dopamine depletion in ipsilateral striatum by 1 week, which remained stable throughout the study period. Unexpectedly, hippocampal dopamine levels showed a biphasic response, with initial 40% reduction at 1 week followed by partial recovery by 4 weeks. Frontal cortex exhibited delayed dopamine loss (25% reduction) emerging only at 4-8 weeks post-lesion. Serotonin levels increased progressively in striatum (+35% at 8 weeks) and hippocampus (+20% at 4-8 weeks), while norepinephrine remained unchanged across all regions. These findings demonstrate that 6-OHDA mesostriatal lesions trigger complex, time-dependent neurochemical reorganization extending beyond the primary target region, suggesting compensatory mechanisms that may have therapeutic implications for Parkinson's disease treatment strategies.
journal: International journal of developmental neuroscience : the official journal of the International Society for Developmental Neuroscience

title: [Intervening in the neural basis of one's personality: a practice-oriented ethical analysis of neuropharmacology and deep-brain stimulation].
abstract: Through the rapid progress in neuropharmacology it seems to become possible to effectively improve our cognitive capacities and emotional states by easily applicable means. Moreover, deep-brain stimulation may allow an effective therapeutic option for those neurological and psychiatric diseases which still can not be sufficiently treated by pharmacological measures. So far, however, both the benefit and the harm of these techniques are only insufficiently understood by neuroscience and detailed ethical analyses are still missing. In this article ethical criteria and most recent empirical evidence are systematically brought together for the first time. This analysis shows that it is irrelevant for an ethical evaluation whether a drug or a brain-machine interface is categorized as "enhancement" or "treatment" or whether it changes "human nature". The only decisive criteria are whether the intervention (1.) benefits the patient, (2.) does not harm the patient and (3.) is desired by the patient. However, current empirical data in both fields, neuropharmacology and deep-brain stimulation are still too sparse to adequately evaluate these criteria. Moreover, the focus in both fields has been strongly misled by neglecting the distinction between "benefit" and "efficacy": In past years research and clinical practice have only focused on physiological effects, but not on the actual benefit to the patient.

title: TARPs differentially decorate AMPA receptors to specify neuropharmacology.
abstract: Transmembrane AMPA receptor regulatory proteins (TARPs) are the first identified auxiliary subunits for a neurotransmitter-gated ion channel. Although initial studies found that stargazin, the prototypical TARP, principally chaperones AMPA receptors, subsequent research demonstrated that it also regulates AMPA receptor kinetics and synaptic waveforms. Recent studies have identified a diverse collection of TARP isoforms--types Ia, Ib II--that distinctly regulate AMPA receptor trafficking, gating and neuropharmacology. These TARP isoforms are heterogeneously expressed in specific neuronal populations and can differentially sculpt synaptic transmission and plasticity. Whole-genome analyses also link multiple TARP loci to childhood epilepsy, schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. TARPs emerge as vital components of excitatory synapses that participate both in signal transduction and in neuropsychiatric disorders.

title: Demon voltammetry and analysis software: analysis of cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling using multiple kinetic measures.
abstract: The fast sampling rates of fast scan cyclic voltammetry make it a favorable method for measuring changes in brain monoamine release and uptake kinetics in slice, anesthetized, and freely moving preparations. The most common analysis technique for evaluating changes in dopamine signaling uses well-established Michaelis-Menten kinetic methods that can accurately model dopamine release and uptake parameters across multiple experimental conditions. Nevertheless, over the years, many researchers have turned to other measures to estimate changes in dopamine release and uptake, yet to our knowledge no systematic comparison amongst these measures has been conducted. To address this lack of uniformity in kinetic analyses, we have created the Demon Voltammetry and Analysis software suite, which is freely available to academic and non-profit institutions. Here we present an explanation of the Demon Voltammetry acquisition and analysis features, and demonstrate its utility for acquiring voltammetric data under in vitro, in vivo anesthetized, and freely moving conditions. Additionally, the software was used to compare the sensitivity of multiple kinetic measures of release and uptake to cocaine-induced changes in electrically evoked dopamine efflux in nucleus accumbens core slices. Specifically, we examined and compared tau, full width at half height, half-life, T₂₀, T₈₀, slope, peak height, calibrated peak dopamine concentration, and area under the curve to the well-characterized Michaelis-Menten parameters, dopamine per pulse, maximal uptake rate, and apparent affinity. Based on observed results we recommend tau for measuring dopamine uptake and calibrated peak dopamine concentration for measuring dopamine release.

title: [Current trends in clinical psychiatric research].
abstract: SCHIZOPHRENIA: The long-standing dopaminergic hypothesis is based on well-established findings, mainly related to a dysfunction of the mesolimbic C2 and D3 receptors, negative signs of prefrontal hypodopaminergia concerning the D2 and D4 receptors. Immune disorders are also involved as well as a possible neurodevelopmental component. Finally genetic factors play an important role. Three monoaminergic systems appear to be implicated: the catecholaminergic, serotaminergic and homovanilic systems. It is not known whether the disorder of the serotaminergic system results from pre-existing fragilization or a symptom-related modification. According to the chronobiology hypothesis, certain biological rhythms (cortisol, temperature, perhaps melatonin) are desynchronized, a disequilibrium which disappears after cure. The fragilization hypothesis is based on the progressive development of a stress-related pathological organization in the hippocampo-hypothalamo-adrenal axis. The development of new psychotropes remains a challenge to meet the unsatisfied demands in psychiatrics. Drugs with rapid action and minimal side effects are needed.

title: Three-dimensional patterning in biomedicine: Importance and applications in neuropharmacology.
abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) patterning technologies have emerged as transformative tools in neuropharmacology, offering unprecedented control over cellular microenvironments and drug delivery systems. Traditional two-dimensional culture models inadequately recapitulate the complex architecture of neural tissues, limiting the translation of pharmacological findings to clinical applications. This review examines recent advances in 3D biomaterial patterning techniques and their specific applications in neuropharmacological research. We analyzed published literature from 2010-2018 focusing on 3D printing, bioprinting, microfluidics, and scaffold-based approaches for creating physiologically relevant neural models. Key parameters evaluated included spatial resolution, biocompatibility, drug diffusion characteristics, and cellular response reproducibility. Our analysis reveals that 3D-patterned systems demonstrate superior drug penetration profiles compared to conventional models, with up to 40% improved correlation with in vivo pharmacokinetics. Bioprinted neural constructs incorporating multiple cell types showed enhanced synaptic connectivity and more predictable dose-response relationships for neuroactive compounds. Microfluidic devices enabled precise concentration gradients and real-time monitoring of drug effects on neural networks. Scaffold-based systems provided sustained release profiles crucial for chronic neurological conditions. However, standardization challenges and manufacturing costs remain significant barriers to widespread adoption. These findings underscore the critical importance of 3D patterning in advancing neuropharmacological research, particularly for drug screening, toxicity assessment, and personalized medicine applications. Future developments in biocompatible materials and automated fabrication processes will likely accelerate clinical translation of these technologies.
journal: Journal of biomedical materials research. Part B, Applied biomaterials

title: Current state of the use of neuroimaging techniques to understand and alter appetite control in humans.
abstract: Recent advances in neuroimaging technologies have revolutionized our understanding of appetite regulation and opened new avenues for therapeutic interventions in obesity and eating disorders. This review examines current applications of functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), positron emission tomography (PET), and other neuroimaging modalities in appetite research. We systematically analyzed 127 studies published between 2010-2017 investigating neural circuits involved in hunger, satiety, and food reward processing. Key findings demonstrate that hypothalamic, striatal, and prefrontal cortical regions show consistent activation patterns in response to food cues and metabolic signals. fMRI studies reveal that individuals with obesity exhibit altered activity in reward-processing areas, including increased striatal responses to high-calorie food images and reduced prefrontal inhibitory control. PET imaging using dopamine and serotonin tracers has identified neurotransmitter system dysregulation underlying disordered eating behaviors. Emerging interventional studies utilizing real-time fMRI neurofeedback and transcranial magnetic stimulation show promise for modifying appetite-related brain activity. Participants receiving neurofeedback training demonstrated significant reductions in food cravings and body weight over 8-week interventions. However, methodological heterogeneity across studies limits definitive conclusions about optimal targeting strategies. Current evidence supports the feasibility of neuroimaging-guided appetite interventions, though larger randomized controlled trials are needed to establish clinical efficacy. Integration of multimodal imaging approaches with metabolic biomarkers represents the next frontier for precision medicine applications in appetite control and weight management.
journal: Current opinion in clinical nutrition and metabolic care

title: Smell and Stress Response in the Brain: Review of the Connection between Chemistry and Neuropharmacology.
abstract: The stress response in the brain is not fully understood, although stress is one of the risk factors for developing mental disorders. On the other hand, the stimulation of the olfactory system can influence stress levels, and a certain smell has been empirically known to have a stress-suppressing effect, indeed. In this review, we first outline what stress is and previous studies on stress-responsive biomarkers (stress markers) in the brain. Subsequently, we confirm the olfactory system and review previous studies on the relationship between smell and stress response by species, such as humans, rats, and mice. Numerous studies demonstrated the stress-suppressing effects of aroma. There are also investigations showing the effects of odor that induce stress in experimental animals. In addition, we introduce recent studies on the effects of aroma of coffee beans and essential oils, such as lavender, cypress, α-pinene, and thyme linalool on the behavior and the expression of stress marker candidates in the brain. The transfer of volatile components into the brain is also discussed while using the results of thyme linalool as an example. These studies may provide a good opportunity to connect chemical research at the molecular level with neuropharmacological approaches in the future.

title: Advancing equity, diversity, and inclusion in the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP): advances, challenges, and opportunities to accelerate progress.
abstract: It is increasingly accepted that higher levels of excellence and innovation in research can be achieved by organizations that promote equity, diversity, and inclusion across several domains including ethnicity and gender. The purpose of this commentary is to provide an overview of the methods used to increase diversity within ACNP, as well as recommendations for accelerating progress. Annual membership surveys confirm increases in female membership and leadership positions, slower but encouraging signals for “Asian” and “Hispanic” members, and less progress for African American and other ethnic populations. Meetings have become visibly more diverse, due in part to ethnic minority travel awards and apparently increasing diversity among guest attendees. Evidence of increasing inclusion includes well-attended networking events and minority-relevant programming, active communications about diversity-related events and resources, and strong statements by ACNP leadership that embrace diversity as a core value and support collaboration among key committees and task forces to identify and implement pro-inclusion and diversity-enhancing efforts. We believe ACNP can accelerate progress with more scientifically valid approaches to assessing diversity and inclusion. The current membership survey includes five outmoded ethnic options and postmeeting surveys that are not designed to assess inclusion efforts and consequences. Measures should be developed that better characterize diversity and assess efforts to reduce the barriers that exist for potential non-White populations (e.g., annual membership and meeting attendance costs). Increased collaboration with NIH and other organizations that are committed to these same goals may also contribute to acceleration of progress by ACNP and other scientific organizations.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Recent advances in neuropharmacology of cutaneous nociceptors.
abstract: Cutaneous nociceptors are peripheral receptive endings of primary sensory neurons activated by noxious stimuli. Nociceptors detect and signal the presence of tissue-damaging stimuli or the existence of tissue damage. In this short review, we will focus on the molecular mechanism of maintenance, activation, inhibition and sensitization in cutaneous nociceptors. Neurotrophic factors are essential to the development of nociceptors during embryogenesis. Recent evidences have indicated that nociceptors in the adult are maintained by either nerve growth factor (NGF) or glial cell line-derived neurotrophic factor (GDNF). A selective activator of nociceptors is capsaicin, natural product of capsicum peppers. Recently, the receptor for capsaicin (the vanilloid receptor 1: VR1) has been cloned, identified and characterized. VR1 seems to play an important role in the activation and sensitization of nociceptors. In contrast, peripheral endogenous cannabinoids such as anandamide are novel candidates for mediators that inhibit the excitation of nociceptors. Intracellular messengers and the mechanisms of signal transduction in nociceptors have also been studied. Our recent findings provide evidences demonstrate that an activation of both cAMP- and cGMP-second messenger systems is required to induce the sensitization of nociceptors. Such emerging evidences reviewed here would make a significant contribution to further understanding of the molecular mechanism of nociceptors.

title: Behavioral barcoding in the cloud: embracing data-intensive digital phenotyping in neuropharmacology.
abstract: For decades, studying the behavioral effects of individual drugs and genetic mutations has been at the heart of efforts to understand and treat nervous system disorders. High-throughput technologies adapted from other disciplines (e.g., high-throughput chemical screening, genomics) are changing the scale of data acquisition in behavioral neuroscience. Massive behavioral datasets are beginning to emerge, particularly from zebrafish labs, where behavioral assays can be performed rapidly and reproducibly in 96-well, high-throughput format. Mining these datasets and making comparisons across different assays are major challenges for the field. Here, we review behavioral barcoding, a process by which complex behavioral assays are reduced to a string of numeric features, facilitating analysis and comparison within and across datasets.

title: Pharmacotherapy of traumatic brain injury: state of the science and the road forward: report of the Department of Defense Neurotrauma Pharmacology Workgroup.
abstract: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, with particularly high incidence among military personnel. Despite decades of research, no pharmacological intervention has demonstrated consistent clinical efficacy in improving TBI outcomes. The Department of Defense Neurotrauma Pharmacology Workgroup was convened to critically evaluate the current state of TBI pharmacotherapy research and provide recommendations for future investigations. A comprehensive review of preclinical and clinical literature was conducted, encompassing neuroprotective agents, anti-inflammatory compounds, and regenerative therapies tested from 1990-2013. Clinical trial databases were systematically searched, and expert consensus was obtained through structured panel discussions. Analysis of 127 preclinical studies and 43 clinical trials revealed significant translational gaps in TBI drug development. While numerous agents showed promise in animal models, including NMDA receptor antagonists, antioxidants, and progesterone, Phase II and III clinical trials consistently failed to meet primary endpoints. Key barriers identified included heterogeneous patient populations, inadequate outcome measures, suboptimal therapeutic windows, and insufficient consideration of injury mechanisms. The workgroup recommends implementing precision medicine approaches, developing mechanism-based biomarkers, establishing standardized preclinical protocols, and conducting adaptive clinical trial designs. Future pharmacotherapy development should focus on combination therapies targeting multiple injury cascades, personalized treatment strategies based on injury phenotypes, and novel regenerative approaches. Successful TBI drug development requires fundamental changes in research methodology, enhanced military-civilian collaboration, and sustained long-term investment in translational neuroscience infrastructure.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of bifeprunox: merits and limitations in comparison with other third-generation antipsychotics.
abstract: Schizophrenia is characterized by a range of positive and negative symptoms, and cognitive deficits. While positive symptoms respond to current antipsychotic agents, negative symptoms and cognitive deficits are often resistant to pharmacopea. Thus research is now focused on developing third-generation antipsychotics that combine antagonism or partial agonism at dopamine D(2)-like receptors with agonism at serotonin 5-HT(1A) receptors. Such an association is anticipated to provide therapeutic benefits against a broader range of schizophrenia symptoms. Bifeprunox is one such third-generation antipsychotic agent which acts as a partial agonist at D(2)-like receptors and is an efficacious agonist at 5-HT(1A) receptors, with little interaction at 5HT(2A/2C), muscarinic or histaminergic H(1) receptors. This review summarizes the pharmacological profiles of the current antipsychotic agents and describes the rationale behind the development of third-generation antipsychotics. It also evaluates current data concerning bifeprunox in comparison with currently available antipsychotics, as well as those that are still under clinical development.
journal: Current opinion in investigational drugs (London, England : 2000)

title: Neurochemically distinct myenteric neurone populations containing calbindin have specific distribution patterns around the circumference of the gastric corpus.
abstract: We recently described calbindin immunoreactivity in the myenteric plexus of the guinea-pig stomach. To study the neurochemical coding of calbindin D28 k (CALB)-containing myenteric neurones, the presence of calretinin (CALRET), choline acetyltransferase (ChAT), enkephalin (ENK), neuropeptide Y, serotonin (5-HT), somatostatin (SOM) and substance P(SP) was investigated immunohistochemically in colchicine-treated preparations. Nitric oxide synthase-containing neurones were detected by NADPH-diaphorase histochemistry. In addition, we investigated the neurone distribution patterns around the gastric corpus. Most CALB neurones were ChAT positive. ChAT/CALB neurones were either CALRET (ca 75%) or 5-HT positive and most contained in addition SP and/or ENK. All 5-HT neurones contained CALB. CALB labelled on average 2.3, 4.8 and 7.5 neurones per ganglion at the lesser curvature, in the central region and the greater curvature, respectively, which indicated a preferential localisation at the greater curvature. Compared to the total number of myenteric neurones, the proportion of CALB neurones increased significantly from the lesser curvature (6%) towards the greater curvature (18%). This shift, although observed for most ChAT/CALB-positive populations, was most prominent for the ChAT/CALB/CALRET/SP/ENK-encoded neurones. SOM-positive and ChAT-only encoded neurones were preferentially located at the lesser curvature. The remaining ten neurochemically defined populations did not exhibit an uneven distribution. The colocalisation of CALB with CALRET or 5-HT is specific for myenteric neurones in the stomach and represents one significant difference to the neurochemical code of CALB neurones in the guinea-pig intestine. The functional significance of the unevenness of neurone distribution along the circumference of the gastric corpus remains to be studied.

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: I. Cellular, electrophysiological, and neurochemical profile in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: S32504 is a novel naphtoxazine derivative developed as a selective dopamine D3/D2 receptor agonist for potential treatment of Parkinson's disease and other dopaminergic disorders. This study characterized the cellular, electrophysiological, and neurochemical properties of S32504 in comparison with the clinically established D2/D3 agonist ropinirole. In CHO cells expressing human D2 or D3 receptors, S32504 demonstrated high binding affinity (Ki = 0.8 nM for D3, 2.1 nM for D2) with 2.6-fold selectivity for D3 receptors, comparable to ropinirole. Functional studies using cAMP assays revealed S32504 as a full agonist at both receptor subtypes with potency values of EC50 = 1.2 nM (D3) and 3.4 nM (D2). Whole-cell patch-clamp recordings in rat substantia nigra pars compacta neurons showed that S32504 (0.1-10 μM) dose-dependently activated G-protein-coupled inward rectifier potassium currents, producing membrane hyperpolarization blocked by the D2 antagonist sulpiride. In vivo microdialysis in freely moving rats demonstrated that S32504 (0.03-1.0 mg/kg, s.c.) reduced extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens by 35-68%, indicating activation of presynaptic D2/D3 autoreceptors. Behavioral assessment using the 6-OHDA lesioned rat model revealed that S32504 reversed contralateral rotation deficits with ED50 = 0.21 mg/kg, showing 3-fold greater potency than ropinirole. These findings establish S32504 as a potent D3/D2 receptor agonist with favorable neurochemical and behavioral profiles, warranting further investigation as a potential anti-parkinsonian agent.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Cluster headache: diagnosis and treatment.
abstract: Cluster headache is a rare yet exquisitely painful primary headache disorder occurring in either episodic or chronic patterns. The unique feature of cluster headache is the distinctive circadian and circannual periodicity in the episodic forms. The attacks are stereotypic--they are of extreme intensity and short duration, occur unilaterally, and are associated with robust signs and symptoms of autonomic dysfunction. Although the pathophysiology of cluster headache remains to be fully understood, there have been a number of recent seminal observations. To exclude structural mimics, patients presenting with symptoms suggestive of cluster headache warrant at least a brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan in their work-up. The medical treatment of cluster headache includes acute, transitional, and maintenance prophylaxis. Agents used for acute therapy include inhalation of oxygen, triptans, such as sumatriptan, and dihydroergotamine. Transitional prophylaxis refers to the short-term use of fast-acting agents. This typically involves either corticosteroids or an occipital nerve block. The mainstay of prophylactic therapy is verapamil. Yet, other medications, including lithium, divalproex sodium, topiramate, methysergide, gabapentin, and even indomethacin, may be useful when the headache fails to respond to verapamil. For medically refractory patients, surgical interventions, occipital nerve stimulation, and deep brain stimulation remain an option. As the sophistication of functional neuroimaging increases, better insight into the pathophysiological mechanisms that underlie cluster headache is expected.

title: Effects of dopamine D2/D3 receptor antagonism on human planning and spatial working memory.
abstract: Dopamine D2/D3 receptors play a critical role in executive functions, yet their specific contributions to planning and spatial working memory remain poorly understood. Dysregulation of these cognitive processes is implicated in several neuropsychiatric disorders, including schizophrenia and Parkinson's disease, where dopaminergic dysfunction is prominent. This study investigated the effects of selective D2/D3 receptor antagonism on planning ability and spatial working memory performance in healthy human subjects. Twenty-four healthy volunteers (aged 20-35 years) participated in a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study. Participants received either sulpiride (400mg) or placebo and completed computerized versions of the Tower of London task (planning) and the n-back spatial working memory task. Neuropsychological assessments were conducted 2-3 hours post-administration when plasma levels were optimal. Sulpiride significantly impaired planning performance, evidenced by increased solution times (p<0.01) and reduced accuracy on complex problems requiring 4-5 moves (p<0.05). Spatial working memory was also negatively affected, with decreased accuracy in the 2-back (p<0.05) and 3-back conditions (p<0.01) compared to placebo. Reaction times were significantly slower across all working memory conditions under D2/D3 antagonism (p<0.001). These findings demonstrate that D2/D3 receptors are essential for optimal planning and spatial working memory performance in humans. The results provide important insights into dopaminergic mechanisms underlying executive dysfunction in psychiatric disorders and may inform therapeutic strategies targeting cognitive symptoms.

title: Molecular and chemical neuropharmacology of dopamine receptor subtypes.
abstract: The dopamine receptor family plays a crucial role in various neurological and psychiatric disorders, yet the molecular mechanisms underlying subtype-specific pharmacological responses remain poorly understood. Recent molecular cloning studies have identified five distinct dopamine receptor subtypes (D1-D5), which can be classified into D1-like (D1, D5) and D2-like (D2, D3, D4) subfamilies based on their coupling to adenylyl cyclase. We investigated the molecular and chemical neuropharmacology of these receptor subtypes using radioligand binding assays, second messenger studies, and Northern blot analysis in rat brain tissue and transfected cell lines expressing individual receptor subtypes. Saturation binding experiments revealed distinct pharmacological profiles for each subtype, with Ki values ranging from 0.8 nM (D3) to 45 nM (D1) for dopamine binding. D1-like receptors stimulated cAMP accumulation with EC50 values of 12 nM (D1) and 18 nM (D5), while D2-like receptors inhibited forskolin-stimulated cAMP formation. Selective antagonists demonstrated differential affinities: SCH 23390 showed 100-fold selectivity for D1-like receptors, while sulpiride exhibited preferential binding to D2 and D3 subtypes. Regional distribution analysis revealed distinct expression patterns, with D1 and D2 predominating in striatum, D3 in limbic regions, and D4 showing widespread cortical distribution. These findings demonstrate that dopamine receptor subtypes exhibit unique molecular signatures and tissue-specific expression patterns that may underlie their distinct physiological functions and therapeutic targets in neuropsychiatric disorders.

title: The stanley neuropathology consortium integrative database: a novel, web-based tool for exploring neuropathological markers in psychiatric disorders and the biological processes associated with abnormalities of those markers.
