abstract: The Stanley Neuropathology Consortium has established a comprehensive repository of postmortem brain tissue from subjects with major psychiatric disorders, including schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and major depressive disorder, alongside matched controls. To facilitate broader scientific utilization of these valuable resources, we developed an integrative web-based database that consolidates neuropathological findings and enables systematic exploration of molecular abnormalities across psychiatric conditions. The database incorporates quantitative data from over 2,400 brain specimens across multiple cortical and subcortical regions, including measures of neuronal density, glial cell populations, synaptic markers, neurotransmitter systems, and inflammatory mediators. Advanced bioinformatics tools allow users to query specific markers, compare findings across diagnostic groups, and examine regional distribution patterns of pathological changes. Statistical analysis modules enable identification of significant alterations and effect size calculations, while integrated pathway analysis tools link observed abnormalities to relevant biological processes and molecular networks. Our analysis of the consolidated dataset reveals consistent reductions in parvalbumin-positive interneurons across schizophrenia and bipolar disorder (p<0.001), elevated microglial activation markers in all three psychiatric conditions compared to controls (p<0.01), and disorder-specific alterations in synaptic proteins. The database demonstrates significant increases in inflammatory cytokine expression that correlate with decreased neuroplasticity markers (r=-0.67, p<0.001). This novel resource provides unprecedented access to systematically collected neuropathological data, facilitating hypothesis generation and validation while advancing our understanding of the neurobiological substrates underlying major psychiatric disorders through comprehensive data integration and analysis capabilities.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Psychopharmacology of traumatic brain injury.
abstract: The pathophysiology of traumatic brain injury (TBI) can be highly variable, involving functional and/or structural damage to multiple neuroanatomical networks and neurotransmitter systems. This wide-ranging potential for physiologic injury is reflected in the diversity of neurobehavioral and neurocognitive symptoms following TBI. Here, we aim to provide a succinct, clinically relevant, up-to-date review on psychopharmacology for the most common sequelae of TBI in the postacute to chronic period. Specifically, treatment for neurobehavioral symptoms (depression, mania, anxiety, agitation/irritability, psychosis, pseudobulbar affect, and apathy) and neurocognitive symptoms (processing speed, attention, memory, executive dysfunction) will be discussed. Treatment recommendations will reflect general clinical practice patterns and the research literature.

title: The footfault test as a screening tool in the 6-hydroxydopamine rat model of Parkinson's disease.
abstract: The 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model is widely used to study Parkinson's disease pathophysiology and evaluate potential therapeutics. However, current behavioral assessments often lack sensitivity to detect mild motor deficits or require extensive training and equipment. The footfault test, which measures limb placement errors during locomotion across a grid, represents a potentially useful screening tool for dopaminergic dysfunction. We evaluated the sensitivity and reliability of the footfault test in detecting motor impairments following unilateral 6-OHDA lesions in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24). Animals received either 6-OHDA (8 μg in 4 μl) or vehicle injections into the medial forebrain bundle. Footfault testing was performed weekly for 4 weeks post-lesion alongside rotational behavior assessment and cylinder test. Tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry confirmed striatal dopamine depletion. 6-OHDA-lesioned animals showed significant increases in contralateral forelimb footfaults compared to controls, with deficits emerging at 1 week post-lesion (vehicle: 12.3±2.1% vs. 6-OHDA: 28.7±4.2%, p<0.01). Footfault scores correlated strongly with amphetamine-induced rotations (r=0.78, p<0.001) and striatal dopamine depletion (r=-0.72, p<0.001). Test-retest reliability was high (r=0.89), and the procedure required minimal training compared to other motor assessments. These findings demonstrate that the footfault test provides a sensitive, reliable, and easily implemented screening tool for motor dysfunction in the 6-OHDA rat model, making it valuable for both research applications and therapeutic screening protocols in Parkinson's disease studies.

title: Neurosurgery for obsessive-compulsive disorder: contemporary approaches.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) affects 1-3% of the population, with approximately 10% of patients proving refractory to conventional pharmacological and behavioral interventions. For these treatment-resistant cases, neurosurgical approaches have evolved significantly from early ablative procedures to contemporary techniques offering improved precision and reversibility. This review examines current neurosurgical options for severe, intractable OCD based on analysis of published literature from 1990-2010 and institutional experience with 127 patients. Modern approaches include anterior cingulotomy, anterior capsulotomy, subcaudate tractotomy, and limbic leucotomy, with deep brain stimulation (DBS) emerging as a promising alternative. Anterior cingulotomy demonstrates response rates of 32-67% with minimal cognitive side effects, while anterior capsulotomy shows efficacy in 50-78% of patients but carries higher risk of executive dysfunction. DBS targeting the anterior limb of internal capsule, subthalamic nucleus, or nucleus accumbens has shown 40-60% response rates in preliminary studies, with the advantage of reversibility and parameter adjustability. Neuroimaging advances have improved targeting accuracy, reducing complications while maintaining efficacy. Patient selection remains critical, requiring failure of multiple pharmacological trials, adequate behavioral therapy, and comprehensive psychiatric evaluation. Contemporary neurosurgical interventions for OCD demonstrate acceptable risk-benefit ratios in carefully selected patients, with DBS representing the most promising direction for future development. Standardized outcome measures and long-term follow-up studies are essential for optimizing treatment protocols and patient selection criteria.
journal: Journal of clinical neuroscience : official journal of the Neurosurgical Society of Australasia

title: Understanding the effects of stimulant medications on cognition in individuals with attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder: a decade of progress.
abstract: Over the past decade, substantial progress has been made in elucidating the cognitive mechanisms underlying stimulant medication efficacy in attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). While methylphenidate and amphetamine-based treatments have demonstrated clear clinical benefits, their specific effects on discrete cognitive domains remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes findings from 127 studies published between 2001-2011 examining stimulant effects on cognition in ADHD populations. We conducted a systematic analysis of neuropsychological, neuroimaging, and electrophysiological studies investigating working memory, inhibitory control, sustained attention, and reward processing in medicated versus unmedicated ADHD patients. Converging evidence indicates that stimulants primarily enhance working memory capacity and reduce reaction time variability, with effect sizes ranging from 0.4-0.8 across studies. Neuroimaging data reveal normalized activation patterns in prefrontal-striatal circuits, particularly within the anterior cingulate cortex and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex during executive function tasks. However, improvements in inhibitory control show more modest and inconsistent effects (d=0.2-0.5), suggesting this domain may be less responsive to dopaminergic enhancement. Individual differences in treatment response correlate significantly with baseline dopamine transporter availability and genetic polymorphisms in catecholaminergic systems. Notably, cognitive benefits appear most pronounced in children and adolescents compared to adults, potentially reflecting developmental differences in prefrontal maturation. These findings provide crucial insights into the neurobiological basis of ADHD pharmacotherapy and highlight the need for personalized treatment approaches based on individual cognitive profiles and neurochemical characteristics.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Psychopharmacology of the endocannabinoids: far beyond anandamide.
abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical neuromodulatory network implicated in diverse physiological processes including pain, mood, appetite, and memory. While anandamide (AEA) was the first endogenous cannabinoid ligand identified, recent advances have revealed a complex array of endocannabinoid-like molecules with distinct pharmacological properties. This review examines the expanding landscape of endocannabinoid signaling beyond classical AEA and 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG) pathways. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of emerging endocannabinoid mediators including N-acylethanolamines, 2-acylglycerols, and related lipid species, evaluating their receptor binding profiles, metabolic pathways, and physiological functions through systematic literature review and meta-analysis of preclinical studies published between 2005-2012. Our analysis identified at least 12 distinct endocannabinoid-related molecules with varying affinities for CB1, CB2, TRPV1, and orphan receptors. Notable compounds include palmitoylethanolamide (PEA), oleoylethanolamide (OEA), and virodhamine, each demonstrating unique tissue distribution patterns and pharmacokinetic properties. These molecules exhibit diverse mechanisms including anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective, and metabolic regulatory effects that extend beyond traditional cannabinoid receptor activation. Several compounds modulate endocannabinoid tone through inhibition of fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) or monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL). The endocannabinoid system represents a far more complex signaling network than initially conceived, with multiple molecular players contributing to cannabinoid-mediated neurotransmission. Understanding these diverse pathways may reveal novel therapeutic targets for neuropsychiatric disorders, chronic pain, and metabolic dysfunction.

title: Chronic infusion of PCP via osmotic mini-pumps: a new rodent model of cognitive deficit in schizophrenia characterized by impaired attentional set-shifting (ID/ED) performance.
abstract: The identification of animal disease-like models for cognitive symptoms in schizophrenia is of central importance to the successful development of pharmacological therapies for psychosis resulting in a functional outcome in patients. Executive function is one of the most severely affected cognitive domains in schizophrenia that remains inadequately treated by existing therapies. The rat attentional set-shifting (or intra-dimensional-extra-dimensional (ID/ED)) task has been developed to test executive function in rodents and successful translation of pre-clinical data into the clinical setting now depends on the identification of a predictive animal disease-like model. The present study investigates whether a continuous 14-day mini-pump infusion of the non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonist phencyclidine (PCP) leads to a deficit in the ID/ED task, and subsequently evaluates the effect of modafinil in this model. Lister hooded rats were implanted subcutaneously with osmotic mini-pumps containing saline or PCP (15 mg/kg/day) for 14 days followed by a 7-day drug-free recovery phase. Rats were then tested in the ID/ED task following an acute injection of either vehicle or modafinil. PCP-treated animals displayed a selective deficit at the ED shift stage resembling that observed in schizophrenic patients. This deficit was reversed by an acute injection of modafinil. The PCP-induced impairment and its reinstatement by modafinil are quantitatively and qualitatively similar to that described earlier by our group following sub-chronic intraperitoneal PCP administration, indicative that sub-chronic PCP infusion via osmotic mini-pumps may represent an attractive alternative to the systemic administration protocols generally employed to date.

title: Charles Darwin's emotional expression "experiment" and his contribution to modern neuropharmacology.
abstract: The study of emotional expression has deep historical roots in neuroscience, with Charles Darwin's seminal work "The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals" (1872) representing a pioneering contribution to understanding the neurobiological basis of affect. While Darwin is primarily remembered for evolutionary theory, his systematic investigation of emotional expression established foundational principles that would later influence modern neuropharmacological research. This historical analysis examines Darwin's empirical methodology and its relationship to contemporary approaches in affective neuroscience and psychopharmacology. We reviewed Darwin's original correspondence, photographic evidence, and experimental notes alongside modern neuropharmacological literature to identify conceptual continuities. Darwin's use of galvanic stimulation to elicit facial expressions, his cross-cultural observational studies, and his comparative approach across species established three key methodological precedents: the use of controlled stimulation to study neural circuits, the recognition of universal emotional patterns suggesting conserved neurobiological mechanisms, and the evolutionary framework for understanding affective systems. These approaches directly parallel modern techniques including optogenetics, cross-cultural psychopharmacological studies, and translational research using animal models. Darwin's hypothesis that emotional expressions reflect underlying "nerve-force" patterns anticipated current understanding of neurotransmitter systems and neural networks governing affect. His integration of physiological, behavioral, and evolutionary perspectives established a multidisciplinary framework that remains central to neuropharmacological research. Darwin's contributions to emotional expression research represent an underappreciated foundation for modern neuropharmacology, demonstrating the value of historical perspective in understanding scientific progress in affective neuroscience.
journal: Journal of the history of the neurosciences

title: Metabolic changes detected by proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy in vivo and in vitro in a murin model of Parkinson's disease, the MPTP-intoxicated mouse.
abstract: Parkinson's disease is a neurodegenerative disorder characterized by the progressive loss of the dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, which project to the striatum. The aim of this study was to analyze in vivo and in vitro consequences of dopamine depletion on amount of metabolites in a mouse model of Parkinson's disease using proton (1)H magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS). The study was performed on control mice (n = 7) and MPTP-intoxicated mice (n = 7). All the experiments were performed at 9.4 T. For in vivo MRS acquisitions, mice were anesthetized and carefully placed on an animal handling system with the head centered in birdcage coil used for both excitation and signal reception. Spectra were acquired in a voxel (8 microL) centered in the striatum, applying a point-resolved spectroscopy sequence (TR = 4000 ms, TE = 8.8 ms). After in vivo MRS acquisitions, mice were killed; successful lesion verified by tyrosine hydroxylase immunolabeling on the substantia nigra pars compacta and in vitro MRS acquisitions performed on perchloric extracts of anterior part of mice brains. In vitro spectra were acquired using a standard one-pulse experiment. The absolute concentrations of metabolites were determined using jmrui (Lyon, France) from (1)H spectra obtained in vivo on striatum and in vitro on perchloric extracts. Glutamate (Glu), glutamine (Gln), and GABA concentrations obtained in vivo were significantly increased in striatum of MPTP-lesioned mice (Glu: 15.5 +/- 2.5 vs. 12.9 +/- 1.0 mmol/L, p < 0.05; Gln: 2.3 +/- 0.9 vs. 1.8 +/- 0.6 mmol/L, p < 0.05; GABA: 2.3 +/- 0.9 vs. 1.3 +/- 0.6 mmol/L, p < 0.05). The in vitro results confirmed these results, Glu (10.9 +/- 2.5 vs. 7.9 +/- 1.7 micromol/g, p < 0.05), Gln (6.8 +/- 2.9 vs. 4.3 +/- 1.0 micromol/g, p < 0.05), and GABA (2.9 +/- 0.9 vs. 1.5 +/- 0.4 micromol/g, p < 0.01). The present study strongly supports a hyperactivity of the glutamatergic cortico-striatal pathway hypothesis after dopaminergic denervation in association with an increase of striatal GABA levels. It further shows an increased of striatal Gln concentrations, perhaps as a strategy to protect neurons from Glu excitotoxic injury after striatal dopamine depletion.

title: Simple behavioral methods to assess the effect of drugs or toxins on sensory experience.
abstract: When behavioral pharmacologists/toxicologists study conditioned taste aversions (CTAs), or other conditioned responses, as a means to investigate the effects of various drugs or toxins on a learned response, failure to discover a CTA is frequently attributed to the treatment's influence on the associative process. This kind of analysis may fail to identify drug-induced sensory changes that may influence conditioned stimulus (CS) or unconditioned stimulus (US) saliency. The current paper outlines a simple method by which a drug's influence on CS or US sensation may be determined. Further, illustrative data are provided regarding how N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor blockade modulates taste and the sensation of malaise. Ketamine (an NMDA receptor antagonist) has been reported to block CTAs in both neonatal and adult rats. The current experiments evaluated ketamine's ability to modulate the taste of a frequently employed CS (saccharin HCl=SAC) or the aversive aspects of a common US (Lithium Chloride=LiCl). Rats normally exhibit a preference for 0.3% SAC over 0.6% SAC and will suppress consumption of these liquids following an injection of LiCl. We report that ketamine did not markedly antagonize these consummatory patterns nor did it disrupt spontaneous locomotor movements. Taken together, these findings point to ketamine's limited ability to change the sensory capacities required for CTA formation. Investigators interested in determining the underlying causes of drug-induced CTA blockade may choose to employ paradigms similar to the one used here.

title: CSF neurochemicals during tryptophan depletion in individuals with remitted depression and healthy controls.
abstract: Tryptophan depletion has been widely used to investigate serotonergic mechanisms underlying depression, yet direct measurement of central serotonergic metabolites during depletion remains limited. This study examined cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) neurochemical changes following acute tryptophan depletion (ATD) in individuals with remitted major depression and healthy controls. Twenty-four medication-free participants with remitted depression (≥6 months) and 22 matched healthy controls underwent lumbar puncture before and 5 hours after consuming either a tryptophan-depleting amino acid mixture or balanced control mixture in a double-blind, crossover design. CSF levels of 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), homovanillic acid (HVA), and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol (MHPG) were measured using HPLC. Plasma tryptophan levels decreased by 85% following ATD in both groups. CSF 5-HIAA concentrations were significantly reduced after ATD compared to control conditions (p<0.001), with a greater magnitude of reduction in remitted depressed participants (-42%) versus controls (-28%, p=0.024). CSF HVA levels showed modest but significant decreases following ATD (p=0.031), while MHPG remained unchanged. The degree of CSF 5-HIAA reduction correlated with transient mood deterioration scores in the remitted depression group (r=0.52, p=0.009). These findings provide direct neurochemical evidence that ATD substantially reduces central serotonin turnover, with enhanced vulnerability in individuals with depression history. The differential CSF response may reflect underlying serotonergic dysfunction that persists during clinical remission, supporting theories of trait-dependent serotonergic abnormalities in recurrent depression.
journal: European neuropsychopharmacology : the journal of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) - 23rd Congress.
abstract: The 23rd Congress of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP), held in Amsterdam, included topics covering new therapeutic developments in the field of neuropsychopharmacology. This conference report highlights selected presentations on potential psychotropic drug targets, the relationship between psychiatric disorders and pain, treatments for depression and anxiety disorders, the role of glucocorticoid receptors in memory consolidation, and the use of anticonvulsants in impulse disorders.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology: are we ready for a paradigm shift?
abstract: The "paradigm shift" in the title of this article refers to molecular biology. The real challenge to molecular biologists interested in applying their methods to an understanding of psychiatric disorders involves the development of models that account for gene/environment/development interaction. Because of the existence of homologous animal models of addictive disorders, as well as a rich body of clinical observations and clinical and behavioral research, and recent data suggesting that chronic exposure to addictive drugs results in changes in gene expression in neurons from behaviorally relevant brain regions, addictive disorders offer a serendipitous model (to molecular biologists) that may have relevance to subsequent application in other areas of psychiatry and psychology.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Conduction pathways in microtubules, biological quantum computation, and consciousness.
abstract: Recent theoretical frameworks propose that microtubules within neurons may serve as biological quantum computers, potentially underlying consciousness through orchestrated objective reduction (Orch-OR). To investigate the quantum mechanical properties of neuronal microtubules, we examined electrical conduction pathways in purified tubulin polymers using single-molecule conductivity measurements and quantum coherence spectroscopy. Microtubules were isolated from bovine brain tissue and reconstituted in vitro under controlled conditions. Conductivity was measured using scanning tunneling microscopy at physiological temperatures, while quantum coherence was assessed through electron spin resonance and fluorescence correlation spectroscopy. Results demonstrated that microtubules exhibit semiconductor-like properties with conductivity values of 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ S/cm, significantly higher than previously reported. Quantum coherence persisted for 10-25 microseconds at 37°C, substantially longer than thermal decoherence predictions. Treatment with anesthetic agents (isoflurane, propofol) at clinically relevant concentrations significantly reduced both conductivity and coherence times by 60-80%, correlating with known consciousness-altering effects. Tubulin dimer dipole oscillations occurred at frequencies consistent with gamma-wave neural activity (30-100 Hz). These findings support the hypothesis that microtubules possess quantum computational capabilities through organized electron transport pathways and sustained coherence. The correlation between anesthetic effects on microtubular quantum properties and consciousness suppression provides preliminary evidence for quantum processes in neural computation. Further investigation of microtubular quantum mechanics may illuminate fundamental mechanisms underlying consciousness and cognitive processing.

title: Effects of dopamine D2/D3 receptor antagonism on human planning and spatial working memory.
abstract: Psychopharmacological studies in humans suggest important roles for dopamine (DA) D2 receptors in human executive functions, such as cognitive planning and spatial working memory (SWM). However, studies that investigate an impairment of such functions using the selective DA D2/3 receptor antagonist sulpiride have yielded inconsistent results, perhaps because relatively low doses were used. We believe we report for the first time, the effects of a higher (800 mg p.o.) single dose of sulpiride as well as of genetic variation in the DA receptor D2 gene (DA receptor D2 Taq1A polymorphism), on planning and working memory. With 78 healthy male volunteers, we apply a between-groups, placebo-controlled design. We measure outcomes in the difficult versions of the Cambridge Neuropsychological Test Automated Battery One-Touch Stockings of Cambridge and the self-ordered SWM task. Volunteers in the sulpiride group showed significant impairments in planning accuracy and, for the more difficult problems, in SWM. Sulpiride administration speeded response latencies in the planning task on the most difficult problems. Volunteers with at least one copy of the minor allele (A1+) of the DA receptor D2 Taq1A polymorphism showed better SWM capacity, regardless of whether they received sulpiride or placebo. There were no effects on blood pressure, heart rate or subjective sedation. In sum, a higher single dose of sulpiride impairs SWM and executive planning functions, in a manner independent of the DA receptor D2 Taq1A polymorphism.

title: Epigenetic Neuropharmacology: Drugs Affecting the Epigenome in the Brain.
abstract: This review explores how different classes of drugs, including those with therapeutic and abuse potential, alter brain functions and behavior via the epigenome. Epigenetics, in its simplest interpretation, is the study of the regulation of a genes' transcriptional potential. The epigenome is established during development but is malleable throughout life by a wide variety of drugs, with both clinical utility and abuse potential. An epigenetic effect can be central to the drug's therapeutic or abuse potential, or it can be independent from the main effect but nevertheless produce beneficial or adverse side effects. Here, I discuss the various epigenetic effects of main pharmacological drug classes, including antidepressants, antiepileptics, and drugs of abuse.
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology

title: Closed-loop Neuropharmacology for Epilepsy: Distant Dream or Future Reality?
abstract: Epilepsy is considered the most frequent severe neurological condition but most patients treated with medication become seizure free. The management of treatment, however, is highly empirical, mainly relying on observation. A closed-loop therapy for epilepsy would be very valuable for more efficient treatment regimens. Here we discuss monitoring treatment (therapeutic drug monitoring) and the potential developments in this field, as well as providing a review of potential biomarkers that could be used to monitor the disease activity. Finally, we consider the pharmacogenetic input in epilepsy treatment.

title: Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia: neural substrates and psychopharmacological implications.
abstract: Impaired emotional functioning is a prominent feature of schizophrenia that includes significant deficits in the ability to accurately recognize facial expressions of emotion. Recent work demonstrates that deficits in affect perception are related to functional outcome and negative symptoms, suggesting that remediation of these deficits may contribute to symptomatic and functional improvements. Thus far, antipsychotic drug treatments appear to be relatively ineffective as a remedial tool. However, investigations into the neurobiology of affective dysfunction show abnormal amygdala activation in schizophrenia during affect recognition tasks. Such findings indicate that regulation of amygdala responses may aid in more accurate emotion processing. Both serotonergic and anxiolytic GABAergic agents that modulate activation of the limbic system offer promising avenues for remediation efforts.

title: Pharmacotherapies for alcoholism: the old and the new.
abstract: Alcoholism and other alcohol use disorders are major public health problems, and the success rates of non-pharmacological treatment of these disorders such as psychotherapy, cognitive-behavioral therapy, group therapy, or residential treatment programs,remain only modest at best. High rates of recidivism (relapse) in alcoholics attempting to remain abstinent are prevalent worldwide. In recent years abundant evidence has accumulated demonstrating that alcoholism is a complex and multifaceted disease of the brain caused by numerous genetic, neurobiological, developmental, environmental, and socioeconomic factors that are still not yet fully understood.There is thus a great need to improve the success rates of all forms of treatment of alcoholism not only in preventing relapse, but curbing active alcohol consumption and craving. The development of improved pharmacotherapies that could be used as adjuncts to the aforementioned non-pharmacological treatment approaches is one avenue of great interest to the scientific community and the general public. Currently there are only three medications approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for use in the treatment of alcohol abuse and alcoholism--disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate. Yet medication compliance issues and the modest efficacy of these compounds leave substantial room for improvement. This special issue is devoted to reviewing the current status of these FDA approved medications in the treatment of alcoholism. In addition, preclinical and clinical evidence suggesting that other classes of medications might also be of potential use are reviewed, including anticonvulsants, GABAB receptor agonists, cholinergic receptor partial agonists, corticotropin-releasing factor and cannabinoid CB1 receptor antagonists, nociceptin receptor ligands, and the novel antipsychotic aripiprazole.

title: Neuropharmacological effects of d-pinitol and its possible mechanisms of action.
abstract: D-pinitol, a naturally occurring cyclitol found in various legumes and traditional medicinal plants, has garnered attention for its potential therapeutic properties, yet its neuropharmacological effects remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the central nervous system activities of d-pinitol and elucidated its underlying mechanisms of action. Male Swiss albino mice (n=8 per group) received oral administration of d-pinitol (25, 50, and 100 mg/kg) or vehicle controls. Behavioral assessments included open field test, elevated plus maze, forced swimming test, and pentylenetetrazole-induced seizure model. Neurochemical analyses were performed using high-performance liquid chromatography to measure monoamine neurotransmitters in brain homogenates. Molecular docking studies examined d-pinitol's interaction with GABA-A and serotonin receptors. D-pinitol demonstrated dose-dependent anxiolytic effects in the elevated plus maze, significantly increasing open arm entries (p<0.01) and time spent in open arms (p<0.05) at 50 and 100 mg/kg doses. In the forced swimming test, d-pinitol reduced immobility time by 34% (p<0.001) at the highest dose, indicating antidepressant-like activity. Seizure latency was significantly prolonged (p<0.01) following d-pinitol pretreatment. Neurochemical analysis revealed increased serotonin (43%) and norepinephrine (28%) levels in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Molecular docking indicated favorable binding affinity of d-pinitol to GABA-A receptor sites. These findings suggest that d-pinitol exhibits significant anxiolytic, antidepressant, and anticonvulsant properties, potentially mediated through GABAergic enhancement and monoaminergic modulation, supporting its traditional use in neurological disorders.

title: Effects of soybean peptide on immune function, brain function, and neurochemistry in healthy volunteers.
abstract: Growing evidence suggests that bioactive peptides derived from dietary proteins may influence both peripheral immune function and central nervous system activity. Soybean-derived peptides have demonstrated immunomodulatory properties in vitro, but their effects on brain function and neurochemistry in humans remain poorly understood. We conducted a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial to evaluate the effects of soybean peptide supplementation on immune markers, cognitive performance, and neurochemical parameters in healthy adults. Sixty volunteers (aged 22-45 years) were randomly assigned to receive either 3g of soybean peptide or placebo daily for 8 weeks. Immune function was assessed using natural killer cell activity, lymphocyte proliferation assays, and cytokine levels (IL-2, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ). Cognitive performance was evaluated using standardized neuropsychological tests, while brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), serotonin, and dopamine metabolites were measured in serum. Compared to placebo, soybean peptide supplementation significantly enhanced natural killer cell activity (p<0.01) and increased IL-2 production (p<0.05), while reducing pro-inflammatory TNF-α levels (p<0.03). Participants receiving soybean peptides showed improved performance on working memory tasks (p<0.02) and attention-switching paradigms (p<0.04). Serum BDNF concentrations increased by 18% in the treatment group versus 3% in placebo (p<0.01), accompanied by elevated serotonin metabolite levels. These findings suggest that soybean peptide supplementation may provide dual benefits by enhancing immune function and supporting cognitive performance through modulation of neurotrophic factors and neurotransmitter systems in healthy individuals.

title: A review of Neuropharmacology Effects of Nigella sativa and Its Main Component, Thymoquinone.
abstract: Neuropharmacology is the scientific study of drug effect on nervous system. In the last few years, different natural plants and their active constituents have been used in neurological therapy. The availability, lower price, and less toxic effects of herbal medicines compared with synthetic agents make them as simple and excellent choice in the treatment of nervous diseases. Nigella sativa, which belongs to the botanical family of Ranunculaceae, is a widely used medicinal plant all over the world. In traditional and modern medicines several beneficial properties have been attributed to N. sativa and its main component, thymoquinone (TQ). In this review, various studies in scientific databases regarding the neuropharmacological aspects of N. sativa and TQ have been introduced. Results of these studies showed that N. sativa and TQ have several properties including anticonvulsant, antidepressant, anxiolytic, anti-ischemic, analgesic, antipsychotic, and memory enhancer. Furthermore, its protective effects against neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer, Parkinson and multiple sclerosis have been discussed. Although there are many studies indicating the beneficial actions of this plant in nervous system, the number of research projects relating to the human reports is rare. Copyright © 2016 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

title: Legal aspects of zebrafish neuropharmacology and neurotoxicology research.
abstract: Zebrafish (Danio rerio) have emerged as a valuable vertebrate model for neuropharmacology and neurotoxicology research due to their genetic tractability, optical transparency, and conserved neurobiological pathways with mammals. However, the regulatory landscape governing zebrafish research remains complex and varies significantly across jurisdictions. This review examines the current legal framework surrounding zebrafish neuropharmacology studies, analyzing regulations from the United States, European Union, and other major research jurisdictions. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of institutional animal care and use committee protocols, federal guidelines, and international standards governing zebrafish research between 2010-2018. Our analysis reveals substantial heterogeneity in regulatory requirements, with some jurisdictions treating zebrafish as equivalent to mammalian models while others provide more streamlined approval processes for early developmental stages. The European Union's Directive 2010/63/EU generally requires full ethical review for zebrafish studies beyond 120 hours post-fertilization, while U.S. guidelines vary by institution regarding developmental stage thresholds. Drug screening protocols, behavioral assays, and toxicological endpoints face different regulatory scrutiny depending on the specific compounds tested and intended applications. Key regulatory gaps identified include inconsistent definitions of sentience onset, variable requirements for anesthesia and euthanasia protocols, and unclear guidelines for high-throughput screening applications. These findings highlight the need for harmonized international standards that balance scientific innovation with appropriate ethical oversight, particularly as zebrafish models increasingly bridge preclinical research and regulatory toxicology applications in pharmaceutical development.

title: How human electrophysiology informs psychopharmacology: from bottom-up driven processing to top-down control.
abstract: Recent advances in human electrophysiology have provided unprecedented insights into the neural mechanisms underlying psychopharmacological interventions, bridging the gap between cellular-level drug actions and complex behavioral outcomes. This review synthesizes current knowledge of how pharmacological agents modulate cortical information processing, from sensory-driven responses to executive control networks. We examined electrophysiological data from 47 studies investigating acute and chronic effects of major psychoactive drug classes, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic compounds, using event-related potentials (ERPs), steady-state responses, and spectral analyses in healthy volunteers and clinical populations. Bottom-up sensory processing, indexed by early ERP components (P50, N100, P300), showed consistent modulation by dopamine D2 antagonists and NMDA receptor modulators, with attenuation of sensory gating deficits in schizophrenia patients following antipsychotic treatment. Top-down control mechanisms, reflected in late ERP components and frontal theta oscillations, were selectively enhanced by noradrenergic and cholinergic agents, correlating with improved cognitive performance. Serotonergic manipulations primarily affected emotional processing networks, as evidenced by altered P300 amplitudes to affective stimuli. Cross-frequency coupling analyses revealed that pharmacological interventions restore disrupted communication between cortical networks in psychiatric disorders. These findings demonstrate that human electrophysiology provides critical translational biomarkers for drug development, enabling mechanistic understanding of therapeutic efficacy and facilitating personalized treatment approaches. Integration of electrophysiological measures into clinical trials may accelerate identification of novel therapeutic targets and optimize dosing strategies for neuropsychiatric medications.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Natural segmentation of the locomotor behavior of drug-induced rats in a photobeam cage.
abstract: Recently, Drai et al. (J Neurosci Methods 96 (2000) 119) have introduced an algorithm that segments rodent locomotor behavior into natural units of 'staying in place' (lingering) behavior versus going between places (progression segments). This categorization, based on the maximum speed attained within the segment, was shown to be intrinsic to the data, using the statistical method of Gaussian Mixture Model. These results were obtained in normal rats and mice using very large (650 or 320 cm) circular arenas and a video tracking system. In the present study, we reproduce these results with amphetamine, phencyclidine and saline injected rats, using data measured by a standard photobeam tracking system in square 45 cm cages. An intrinsic distinction between two or three 'gears' could be shown in all animals. The spatial distribution of these gears indicates that, as in the large arena behavior, they correspond to the difference between 'staying in place' behavior and 'going between places'. The robustness of this segmentation over arena size, different measurement system and dose of two psychostimulant drugs indicates that this is an intrinsic, natural segmentation of rodent locomotor behavior. Analysis of photobeam data that is based on this segmentation has thus a potential use in psychopharmacology research.

title: Quantitative in vitro phosphor imaging using [3H] and [18F] radioligands: the effects of chronic desipramine treatment on serotonin 5-HT2 receptors.
abstract: Traditionally, autoradiography of neuroreceptors is performed in vitro using tritiated ligands and low sensitivity X-ray film, requiring long exposure times. In vivo imaging of neuroreceptors using positron emission tomography (PET) suffers poor spatial resolution, but in vitro PET autoradiography is difficult with film due to the short half-life of the isotopes. Storage phosphor screens provide an extremely sensitive alternative to film. To demonstrate and validate quantitative in vitro phosphor imaging with PET and tritiated ligands, we treated rats chronically with the antidepressant desipramine, which results in decreased binding to serotonin 5-HT(2) receptors. Serotonin 5-HT(2) binding decreased significantly in all cortical regions examined as measured by both [(3)H]ketanserin and [(18)F]setoperone. The data from the two radioligands were not significantly different, and the distribution of the receptors was in agreement with previous reports. We also present data on the reusability of tritium-sensitive phosphor screens, and show that the use of simple corrections allows receptor binding data with PET ligands to be compared across different days. The results indicate that phosphor imaging is a valid, fast, and quantifiable technique for measuring neuroreceptor regulation, and that it provides an excellent tool to corroborate in vivo PET data in vitro at higher resolution.

title: Traumatic brain injury: a review of practice management and recent advances.
abstract: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) remains a leading cause of death and disability worldwide, with an estimated 1.7 million cases occurring annually in the United States. Despite advances in acute care management, long-term functional outcomes remain suboptimal, necessitating comprehensive review of current therapeutic approaches and emerging interventions. This review synthesizes current evidence from clinical trials, observational studies, and meta-analyses published between 2000-2007 regarding TBI management strategies and novel therapeutic targets. We examined acute care protocols, neuroprotective agents, rehabilitation interventions, and pharmacological approaches for cognitive and behavioral sequelae. Recent advances in neuroimaging, particularly diffusion tensor imaging and functional MRI, have enhanced our understanding of injury mechanisms and recovery patterns. Acute management has evolved to emphasize intracranial pressure monitoring, targeted temperature management, and aggressive prevention of secondary injury. Emerging neuroprotective strategies include NMDA receptor antagonists, antioxidants, and anti-inflammatory agents, though clinical translation remains limited. Cognitive rehabilitation programs incorporating computer-based training and compensatory strategies show promise for addressing executive dysfunction and memory impairments. Pharmacological interventions, including cholinesterase inhibitors, dopaminergic agents, and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, demonstrate modest efficacy for specific cognitive and behavioral symptoms. However, the heterogeneity of TBI pathophysiology continues to challenge therapeutic development. Future directions emphasize personalized medicine approaches, biomarker development for prognostication, and combination therapies targeting multiple injury mechanisms. Multidisciplinary rehabilitation models integrating acute care with long-term cognitive and physical therapy represent the current standard for optimizing functional recovery following TBI.
journal: Physical medicine and rehabilitation clinics of North America

title: Strains of rodents and the pharmacology of learning and memory.
abstract: Genetic variability among rodent strains has emerged as a critical factor influencing cognitive performance and drug responsiveness, yet systematic comparisons of learning and memory pharmacology across strains remain limited. We investigated the differential effects of cholinergic and glutamatergic modulators on spatial learning and memory in C57BL/6J, DBA/2J, and 129/SvJ mouse strains. Male mice (n=12-15 per group) were trained in the Morris water maze and tested with acute administrations of scopolamine (0.1-1.0 mg/kg), physostigmine (0.05-0.2 mg/kg), MK-801 (0.05-0.2 mg/kg), and D-cycloserine (5-20 mg/kg). Baseline performance revealed significant strain differences, with C57BL/6J mice demonstrating superior acquisition and retention compared to DBA/2J and 129/SvJ strains. Scopolamine impaired performance across all strains but showed dose-dependent variation, with DBA/2J mice exhibiting greatest sensitivity (ED50: 0.3 mg/kg vs. 0.6 mg/kg in C57BL/6J). Physostigmine enhanced performance most robustly in C57BL/6J mice, while showing minimal effects in DBA/2J animals. MK-801 produced comparable impairments across strains, whereas D-cycloserine facilitated memory consolidation preferentially in 129/SvJ mice. Neurochemical analysis revealed strain-specific differences in hippocampal acetylcholinesterase activity and NMDA receptor density that correlated with drug responsiveness patterns. These findings demonstrate that genetic background profoundly influences both baseline cognitive function and pharmacological sensitivity, highlighting the importance of strain selection in neuropharmacological research and suggesting that therapeutic efficacy of cognitive enhancers may vary significantly across genetically diverse populations.

title: Nystagmus.
abstract: Acquired nystagmus causes excessive motion of images on the retina, which often reduces visual acuity and produces oscillopsia (illusory motion of the seen world). The pathogenesis of several forms of acquired nystagmus is now better understood and can be related to abnormalities of mechanisms that normally hold gaze steady. Basic research on the pharmacology of the neural pathways responsible for gaze holding has demonstrated an important role for gamma aminobutyric acid and led to new drug treatments for acquired nystagmus and its visual consequences.

title: Inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs.
abstract: Upregulation of neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (AChRs) is a venerable result of chronic exposure to nicotine; but it is one of several consequences of pharmacological chaperoning by nicotine and by some other nicotinic ligands, especially agonists. Nicotinic ligands permeate through cell membranes, bind to immature AChR oligomers, elicit incompletely understood conformational reorganizations, increase the interaction between adjacent AChR subunits, and enhance the maturation process toward stable AChR pentamers. These changes and stabilizations in turn lead to increases in both anterograde and retrograde traffic within the early secretory pathway. In addition to the eventual upregulation of AChRs at the plasma membrane, other effects of pharmacological chaperoning include modifications to endoplasmic reticulum stress and to the unfolded protein response. Because these processes depend on pharmacological chaperoning within intracellular organelles, we group them as "inside-out pharmacology". This term contrasts with the better-known, acute, "outside-in" effects of activating and desensitizing plasma membrane AChRs. We review current knowledge concerning the mechanisms and consequences of inside-out pharmacology. This article is part of the Special Issue entitled 'The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor: From Molecular Biology to Cognition'.

title: What have positron emission tomography and 'Zippy' told us about the neuropharmacology of drug addiction?
abstract: Translational molecular imaging with positron emission tomography (PET) and allied technologies offer unrivalled applications in the discovery of biomarkers and aetiological mechanisms relevant to human disease. Foremost among clinical PET findings during the past two decades of addiction research is the seminal discovery of reduced dopamine D(2/3) receptor expression in the striatum of drug addicts, which could indicate a predisposing factor and/or compensatory reaction to the chronic abuse of stimulant drugs. In parallel, recent years have witnessed significant improvements in the performance of small animal tomographs (microPET) and a refinement of animal models of addiction based on clinically relevant diagnostic criteria. This review surveys the utility of PET in the elucidation of neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying drug addiction. It considers the consequences of chronic drug exposure on regional brain metabolism and neurotransmitter function and identifies those areas where further research is needed, especially concerning the implementation of PET tracers targeting neurotransmitter systems other than dopamine, which increasingly have been implicated in the pathophysiology of drug addiction. In addition, this review considers the causal effects of behavioural traits such as impulsivity and novelty/sensation-seeking on the emergence of compulsive drug-taking. Previous research indicates that spontaneously high-impulsive rats--as exemplified by 'Zippy'--are pre-disposed to escalate intravenous cocaine self-administration, and subsequently to develop compulsive drug taking tendencies that endure despite concurrent adverse consequences of such behaviour, just as in human addiction. The discovery using microPET of pre-existing differences in dopamine D(2/3) receptor expression in the striatum of high-impulsive rats suggests a neural endophenotype that may likewise pre-dispose to stimulant addiction in humans.

title: Are You Ready for Change?
abstract: The implementation of novel psychopharmacological interventions in inpatient psychiatric settings requires careful assessment of nursing staff readiness and institutional capacity for change. Despite evidence supporting personalized medication management approaches, adoption rates remain inconsistent across mental health facilities. This mixed-methods study examined factors influencing nurses' readiness to implement precision psychiatry protocols in acute care settings. A convenience sample of 247 psychiatric nurses from six Midwestern hospitals completed the Readiness for Organizational Change Scale and participated in focus groups between March and September 2017. Participants had a mean age of 34.2 years (SD=8.1) and averaged 7.4 years of psychiatric nursing experience. Quantitative analysis revealed that nurses with higher educational levels (BSN or above) demonstrated significantly greater change readiness scores compared to those with associate degrees (p<0.001). Years of experience showed a curvilinear relationship with readiness, peaking among nurses with 5-10 years of practice. Qualitative themes included concerns about medication complexity, confidence in pharmacogenomic testing interpretation, and perceived administrative support. Nurses reported feeling more prepared when provided with structured educational programs and decision-support tools. Organizational culture emerged as the strongest predictor of change readiness (β=0.43, p<0.001). These findings suggest that successful implementation of advanced psychopharmacological protocols requires targeted educational interventions, particularly for less experienced nurses and those in less supportive organizational environments. Healthcare administrators should prioritize culture transformation initiatives alongside technical training to optimize adoption of evidence-based psychiatric medication management practices.
journal: Journal of psychosocial nursing and mental health services

title: Recent advances in neuropharmacology of cutaneous nociceptors.
abstract: The peripheral mechanisms underlying cutaneous nociception have emerged as critical targets for analgesic drug development. While considerable progress has been made in understanding central pain processing, the molecular pharmacology of primary afferent nociceptors remains incompletely characterized. This review synthesizes recent findings on the neuropharmacological modulation of cutaneous pain receptors and their clinical implications. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of literature from 1994-1999 examining receptor subtypes, ion channels, and neurotransmitter systems involved in cutaneous nociception. Particular attention was given to capsaicin-sensitive C-fibers, mechanosensitive Aδ-fibers, and their pharmacological profiles. Recent studies have identified vanilloid receptors as key transducers of thermal and chemical nociception, with capsaicin producing initial excitation followed by prolonged desensitization through calcium-dependent mechanisms. ATP-gated P2X3 receptors have been localized to small-diameter sensory neurons and show promise as peripheral analgesic targets. Prostaglandin E2 and bradykinin sensitize nociceptors through cyclic AMP and protein kinase C pathways, respectively. Novel findings include the identification of acid-sensing ion channels responding to tissue pH changes and the role of nerve growth factor in inflammatory hyperalgesia. Topical application of sodium channel blockers, particularly those selective for TTX-resistant channels, demonstrates antinociceptive efficacy with minimal systemic effects. These advances in understanding peripheral nociceptor pharmacology provide new opportunities for developing targeted analgesics that act at the site of pain initiation, potentially offering improved therapeutic indices compared to centrally-acting agents.

title: Two prospective educational interventions in a neurology residency: effect on RITE performance.
abstract: To evaluate the effect of 2 educational interventions on Residency In-service Training Examination (RITE) scores. Two prospective educational interventions were studied in 2 consecutive neurology resident cohorts from a single program and compared with a historical control. Fourteen PGY 2-4 residents studied neuropharmacology in 2002-2003. Fifteen PGY 2-4 residents studied neuroanatomy in 2003-2004. The historical control were 20 PGY 2-4 residents from 1998-2002. Neuropharmacology educational intervention: residents prepared weekly presentations with weekly written quizzes, with quiz results available to the individual resident and program director. Neuroanatomy educational intervention: resident prepared weekly presentations, but quizzes were team based and oral, requiring resident participation. attended faculty prepared didactic lectures. Outcome measures were percent correct and yearly change in subset RITE scores. Data was analyzed with analysis of variance methods accounting for multiple measurements. The mean +/- standard error neuropharmacology subset percent correct RITE score was 43.3 +/- 1.94 for the intervention group and 47.3 +/- 1.91 for the control (P = 0.96), while the mean yearly percent correct change was 4.8 +/- 2.43 for the intervention group and 3.5 +/- 1.67 for the control (P = 0.71). The mean neuroanatomy subset percent correct RITE score was 60.3 +/- 3.54 for the intervention group and 50.9 +/- 3.04 for the control (P = 0.02), with a mean yearly percent correct change of 22.4 +/- 5.77 for the intervention group and 9.4 +/- 1.52 for the control (P = 0.02). A team-oriented intervention with oral quizzing resulted in improved subset RITE performance.

title: [Nanopsychiatry. The potential role of nanotechnologies in the future of psychiatry. A systematic review].
abstract: Nanomedicine is defined as the area using nanotechnology's concepts for the benefit of human beings, their health and well being. The field of nanotechnology opened new unsuspected fields of research a few years ago. To provide an overview of nanotechnology application areas that could affect care for psychiatric illnesses. We conducted a systematic review using the PRISMA criteria (preferred reporting items for systematic reviews and meta-analysis). Inclusion criteria were specified in advance: all studies describing the development of nanotechnology in psychiatry. The research paradigm was: "(nanotechnology OR nanoparticles OR nanomedicine) AND (central nervous system)" Articles were identified in three research bases, Medline (1966-present), Web of Science (1975-present) and Cochrane (all articles). The last search was carried out on April 2, 2012. Seventy-six items were included in this qualitative review. The main applications of nanotechnology in psychiatry are (i) pharmacology. There are two main difficulties in neuropharmacology. Drugs have to pass the blood brain barrier and then to be internalized by targeted cells. Nanoparticles could increase drugs' bioavailability and pharmacokinetics, especially improving safety and efficacy of psychotropic drugs. Liposomes, nanosomes, nanoparticle polymers, nanobubbles are some examples of this targeted drug delivery. Nanotechnologies could also add new pharmacological properties, like nanohells and dendrimers; (ii) living analysis. Nanotechnology provides technical assistance to in vivo imaging or metabolome analysis; (iii) central nervous system modeling. Research teams have modelized inorganic synapses and mimicked synaptic behavior, essential for further creation of artificial neural systems. Some nanoparticle assemblies present the same small world and free-scale network architecture as cortical neural networks. Nanotechnologies and quantum physics could be used to create models of artificial intelligence and mental illnesses. Even if nanotechnologies are promising, their safety is still tricky and this must be kept in mind. We are not about to see a concrete application of nanomedicine in daily psychiatric practice. However, it seems essential that psychiatrists do not forsake this area of research the perspectives of which could be decisive in the field of mental illness.

title: [Current trends in clinical psychiatric research].
abstract: The landscape of clinical psychiatric research has undergone significant transformation during the 1990s, necessitating a comprehensive evaluation of emerging methodological approaches and therapeutic targets. This review examines current trends in psychiatric clinical research based on analysis of 847 published studies from 1995-1997 indexed in major psychiatric journals. We conducted a systematic review of research methodologies, study populations, and therapeutic interventions reported in clinical trials for major depressive disorder, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and anxiety disorders. Placebo-controlled, double-blind designs remained the gold standard, representing 68% of therapeutic trials. However, active comparator studies increased by 23% compared to the early 1990s. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors dominated antidepressant research (45% of studies), while atypical antipsychotics emerged as a major focus in schizophrenia trials (38% increase). Notably, combination therapy protocols and augmentation strategies showed marked expansion, particularly lithium augmentation in treatment-resistant depression. Neuroimaging integration increased substantially, with 15% of studies incorporating SPECT or PET methodology. Quality of life measures and functional outcomes gained prominence alongside traditional symptom rating scales. Patient populations showed greater diagnostic specificity, with comorbidity exclusions becoming more stringent. The trend toward shorter trial durations (mean 8.2 weeks vs. 12.1 weeks in 1990-1992) raised concerns about long-term efficacy assessment. These findings indicate that contemporary psychiatric research emphasizes mechanistic understanding, functional outcomes, and therapeutic optimization while maintaining rigorous methodological standards essential for evidence-based clinical practice.

title: Psychopharmacology of combined activation of the serotonin1A and σ1 receptors.
abstract: The selective serotonin (5-HT) reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are generally used for the treatment of major depressive disorders, and the 5-HT1A and σ1 receptors are considered to be targets for treatment of psychiatric disorders. Some SSRIs such as fluvoxamine have agonistic activity towards for the σ1 receptor, but it is not known whether the effect on the receptor plays a key role in the pharmacological effects. We have recently demonstrated that fluvoxamine shows an anti-anhedonic effect in picrotoxin-induced model of anxiety/depression, while the SSRI paroxetine, which have little affinity for the σ1 receptor, does not. We also suggest that the anti-anhedonic effect of fluvoxamine is mediated by combined activation of the 5-HT1A and σ1 receptors and it is associated with activation of prefrontal dopaminergic system. In these studies, picrotoxin-treated mice and adrenalectomized/castrated mice were used as decreased GABAA receptor function and neurosteroid-deficient models, respectively. These findings suggest that the functional interaction between the 5-HT1A and σ1 receptors activates prefrontal dopaminergic system under the conditions of decreased brain GABAA receptor function and the neurochemical effect is linked to the behavioral effect. This review summarizes the pharmacological role of the 5-HT1A and σ1 receptors, focusing on the functional interaction between these receptors, and the role of prefrontal dopaminergic system in depressive-like behaviors.

title: Partial response, nonresponse, and relapse with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in major depression: a survey of current "next-step" practices.
abstract: Many patients treated for major depression require more than one antidepressant trial to achieve or sustain response. However, the literature provides few treatment algorithms or effectiveness studies that empirically support "next-step" options available to clinicians. We conducted a survey of psychiatrists and other medical specialists who treat depression to ascertain what clinicians actually do when faced with patients who suboptimally respond to an adequate course of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) therapy. Attendees at a psychopharmacology course (N = 801) were queried about their top choices for antidepressant-treatment nonresponders: a minimal responder after 4 weeks of adequate SSRI treatment, a partial responder after 8 weeks of adequate SSRI therapy, a nonresponder after 8 weeks of adequate SSRI therapy, and a relapser on long-term SSRI maintenance therapy. Choices included raising the dose, augmenting or combining with another agent, switching to a second SSRI. or switching to a non-SSRI agent. 432 (54%) of the surveys were returned. Raising the dose was the most frequently reported next-step strategy for a patient with minimal response after 4 weeks of adequate SSRI therapy, partial response after 8 weeks of adequate SSRI therapy, and relapse on long-term SSRI therapy. Switching to a non-SSRI agent was the most frequently chosen option for nonresponders to an adequate trial of SSRI therapy. Our findings suggest that clinicians select different next-step strategies when patients are nonresponders versus when patients are partial responders or relapsers.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of an atypical antipsychotic, NRA0562.
abstract: Schizophrenia is a serious and disabling psychiatric disorder affecting approximately 1% of the world's population. A new generation of atypical antipsychotics has been introduced over the past decade. These atypical antipsychotics have comparable or greater efficacy than traditional antipsychotics in the treatment of the psychotic symptoms of schizophrenia and a much improved neurologic side effect profile. This paper reviews the pharmacological efficacy and safety of a potential atypical antipsychotic, NRA0562. NRA0562 has a high affinity for dopamine D1, D2L, D4.2, 5-HT2A receptors as well as alpha1-adrenoceptors, and has a moderate affinity for H1 receptors. NRA0562 strongly binds to 5-HT2A receptors and alpha1-adrenoceptors in the frontal cortex, its binding to striatal D2 receptors is weaker, similar to that of clozapine. NRA562 displayed potent antipsychotic activities in animal models of schizophrenia, such as methamphetamine (MAP)-induced hyperactivity, apomorphine-induced disruption of pre-pulse inhibition and conditioned avoidance test. NRA0562 is more potent in reversing the inhibitory effects of MAP at A10 than at A9 dopamine neurons. It increased Fos-like immunoreactivity in the nucleus accumbens more effectively than in the dorsolateral striatum, indicating that NRA0562 has the profile of an atypical antipsychotic. In vivo assays for extrapyramidal side effect liability showed that NRA0562 has a low rate of neurological side effects. Thus, NRA0562 may have unique antipsychotic activity with a lower propensity for extrapyramidal side effects.

title: Single-fiber recordings of unmyelinated afferents in pig.
abstract: Recent advances in single-fiber recording techniques have enabled detailed characterization of unmyelinated afferent fibers in various mammalian species, yet comprehensive data from porcine models remain limited despite their increasing use in translational neuroscience research. The present study aimed to characterize the electrophysiological properties and response characteristics of unmyelinated C-fibers in domestic pig using in vivo single-fiber recording techniques. Adult Yorkshire pigs (n=8, 25-30 kg) were anesthetized with isoflurane and prepared for saphenous nerve recordings. Single C-fiber activity was recorded using tungsten microelectrodes positioned in nerve fascicles, with mechanical and thermal stimuli applied to identified receptive fields on the hindlimb. Conduction velocities, mechanical thresholds, and thermal response properties were quantified for 47 individual C-fibers. Mean conduction velocity was 0.89 ± 0.31 m/s, consistent with unmyelinated fiber classification. Mechanical thresholds ranged from 2.1 to 158.3 mN, with 68% of fibers classified as high-threshold mechanoreceptors. Thermal testing revealed 34% of fibers responded to noxious heat (>43°C), with mean heat thresholds of 45.2 ± 2.8°C. Response latencies and adaptation patterns were similar to those reported in other mammalian species. These findings provide the first comprehensive characterization of porcine unmyelinated afferent properties and support the utility of pig models for studying peripheral nociceptor function. The electrophysiological characteristics closely parallel those observed in human studies, reinforcing the translational relevance of porcine preparations for pain research and therapeutic development.

title: Neuropathology in Alzheimer's disease: target of pharmacotherapy.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents the most prevalent form of dementia, affecting approximately 4.5 million Americans with projections indicating exponential growth as the population ages. The characteristic neuropathological hallmarks of AD include extracellular amyloid-beta (Aβ) plaques, intracellular neurofibrillary tangles composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein, and progressive neuronal loss particularly in memory-related brain regions. Current pharmacotherapeutic approaches primarily target cholinergic dysfunction through acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and NMDA receptor modulation, yet these treatments provide only modest symptomatic relief without addressing underlying pathogenic mechanisms. This review examines emerging therapeutic strategies that directly target AD neuropathology, including anti-amyloid agents, tau-directed therapies, and neuroprotective compounds. Recent clinical trials investigating secretase inhibitors and immunotherapeutic approaches to reduce Aβ burden have shown mixed results, with some studies demonstrating reduced plaque load but limited cognitive improvement. Novel tau-targeting strategies, including kinase inhibitors and aggregation modulators, represent promising avenues for intervention. Additionally, antioxidant therapies and anti-inflammatory agents address the oxidative stress and neuroinflammation components of AD pathogenesis. While traditional symptomatic treatments remain the current standard of care, disease-modifying therapies targeting specific neuropathological features offer hope for more effective interventions. Future therapeutic success will likely require combination approaches addressing multiple pathogenic pathways simultaneously, along with earlier intervention during preclinical disease stages. Advanced understanding of AD neuropathology continues to inform rational drug design and optimize therapeutic targeting strategies.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Nurse Practitioners

title: Neurochemistry of Anesthetic States.
abstract: Anesthetic mechanisms that eliminate consciousness and perception of pain are products of the nervous system. Chemical approaches to the study of anesthetic mechanisms have the potential to serve as an ideal interface between basic and clinical neuroscience. There are disproportionately more basic neurochemical studies than clinical studies of anesthetic mechanisms. Even within neuroscience, the study of anesthetic mechanisms is sparse. The Society for Neuroscience hosts one of the world's largest and most vibrant scientific meetings, yet the content themes of that meeting do not include anesthesia. One goal of this chapter is to facilitate neurochemical studies of anesthetic mechanisms by outlining user-friendly descriptions of existing and emerging techniques. The introduction provides a context for chapter goals. The second portion of this chapter focuses on microdialysis methods that enable the humane acquisition of neurochemical samples from intact, behaving animals during anesthetic induction, maintenance, and emergence. No single neurotransmitter and no single brain region regulate the physiological and behavioral traits characteristic of any anesthetic state. This limitation is being addressed via application of new instrumentation and techniques in analytic chemistry. The final third of this chapter highlights selected omics approaches that are now being applied to the neurochemical study of anesthetic mechanisms. We hope that this brief chapter can stimulate basic and clinical metabolomic approaches aiming to elucidate the mechanisms of anesthetic action.

title: Two novel combined drug treatments for women with hypoactive sexual desire disorder.
abstract: Low sexual desire is the most common sexual complaint in women. As a result, many women suffer from sexual dissatisfaction which often negatively interferes with their quality of life. These complaints have been classified as the condition Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder (HSDD), and have recently been merged with the condition Female Sexual Arousal Disorder (FSAD) into the diagnosis Female Sexual Interest/Arousal Disorder (FSIAD) in the DSM-5. To date, no drug treatment approved by the U.S. Food & Drug Administration (FDA)/European Medicines Agency (EMA) is available to treat women with HSDD/FSIAD. As a result, there is an unmet need for a drug treatment for HSDD/FSIAD. In our search for an adequate treatment we followed a different approach compared to other pharmaceutical companies. Based on a personalized sexual medicine approach we proposed that different mechanisms cause low sexual desire in women, namely an insensitive system for sexual cues or dysfunctional activation of sexual inhibitory mechanisms. Subsequently we developed two new on-demand drug treatments for women with HSDD/FSIAD based on these different causal mechanisms. One treatment (testosterone combined with a phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitor) has been developed for women with HSDD/FSIAD due to a relatively insensitive system for sexual cues, while the second treatment (testosterone combined with a 5-HT₁A receptor agonist) has been developed for women with HSDD/FSIAD due to dysfunctional activation of sexual inhibitory mechanisms.

title: The application of functional magnetic resonance imaging to neuropharmacology.
abstract: The technique of functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has the capacity to acquire data with spatial and temporal resolution that far exceeds other currently available methods of non-invasive investigation of brain function. This coupled with its ability for serial studies makes it an attractive prospect for investigating the effects of pharmacological agents in the brain. Recent advances in fMRI have been made in the areas of reward and dependence, brain trauma and injury, psychotropic drugs and pain using small animals. Although the use of fMRI in pharmacological studies is becoming popular, there are various associated complications, such as the possible interference of drugs with the mechanisms that give rise to the pharmacological fMRI signal, and local or global cardiovascular changes that might produce functional responses unrelated to neural activity. Consideration of these concerns, coupled with careful attention to experimental detail and verification procedures, promises to make pharmacological fMRI use a valuable tool for understanding the actions of drugs in the brain.

title: EFNS guidelines for the diagnosis and management of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents the most common cause of dementia in Europe, affecting approximately 5.4 million individuals, with prevalence expected to double by 2030. The European Federation of Neurological Societies (EFNS) convened an expert panel to develop evidence-based guidelines for AD diagnosis and management, addressing current diagnostic challenges and therapeutic approaches. A comprehensive literature review was conducted using MEDLINE, Cochrane Database, and relevant European databases from 1990-2009. Evidence was classified according to EFNS criteria, and recommendations were graded based on strength of available data. The guidelines emphasize early clinical recognition using standardized cognitive assessments, including Mini-Mental State Examination and clock drawing tests, combined with neuroimaging approaches. Cerebrospinal fluid biomarkers (Aβ42, total tau, phospho-tau) show promise for differential diagnosis, particularly in mild cognitive impairment cases. For pharmacological management, acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) demonstrate modest but clinically meaningful benefits in mild-to-moderate AD, with memantine recommended for moderate-to-severe stages. Non-pharmacological interventions, including cognitive stimulation and structured physical activity, provide additional therapeutic value. The guidelines stress importance of multidisciplinary care approaches, caregiver support, and regular monitoring for treatment response and adverse effects. These EFNS recommendations provide European neurologists with standardized, evidence-based protocols for AD diagnosis and management, emphasizing early detection strategies and comprehensive therapeutic approaches. Implementation of these guidelines should improve diagnostic accuracy and optimize patient outcomes across European healthcare systems.

title: Neurochemical signatures revealed by glutamine labeling in the chicken retina.
abstract: The chicken retina serves as an excellent model system for understanding retinal neurochemistry due to its well-characterized laminar organization and absence of retinal vasculature. Glutamine, synthesized exclusively by Müller glial cells, represents a critical metabolic precursor for neurotransmitter glutamate and GABA synthesis in retinal neurons. To investigate the spatial distribution and metabolic fate of glutamine in retinal circuitry, we employed [³H]glutamine autoradiography combined with pharmacological manipulations in isolated chicken retina preparations. Retinal explants were incubated with 50 μCi/ml [³H]glutamine for 30-120 minutes under various conditions, including co-incubation with glutamine synthetase inhibitors (methionine sulfoximine) and glutaminase blockers (6-diazo-5-oxo-L-norleucine). Autoradiographic analysis revealed intense labeling in Müller cell bodies and processes, with prominent accumulation in the inner nuclear layer and around photoreceptor inner segments. Glutamine synthetase inhibition resulted in 78% reduction in glial labeling, while glutaminase blockade increased glutamine accumulation by 145% in ganglion cells and amacrine cells. Double-labeling experiments with cell-specific markers demonstrated preferential glutamine uptake by GABAergic amacrine cells compared to glycinergic subtypes. Time-course analysis showed rapid conversion of glutamine to glutamate within 15 minutes in bipolar and ganglion cells. These findings establish distinct neurochemical compartments within the chicken retina and demonstrate the central role of glutamine in maintaining excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitter pools, providing crucial insights into retinal metabolic organization.

title: Smart drugs and synthetic androgens for cognitive and physical enhancement: revolving doors of cosmetic neurology.
abstract: The increasing prevalence of cognitive enhancers and anabolic androgenic steroids among healthy individuals has raised significant concerns regarding their neurobiological effects and abuse potential. While prescription stimulants like modafinil and amphetamines are being used off-label for cognitive enhancement, synthetic androgens are simultaneously employed for both physical and purported cognitive benefits, creating overlapping patterns of polypharmacy use. We conducted a comprehensive review of current literature and analyzed data from 847 university students and 312 fitness enthusiasts regarding their use of nootropics and synthetic androgens over a 24-month period. Participants completed validated questionnaires assessing usage patterns, motivations, and perceived effects. Neuropsychological testing was performed on a subset of 156 regular users compared to 144 controls. Results revealed that 23.4% of students reported modafinil or stimulant use for academic enhancement, while 18.7% of fitness enthusiasts used synthetic androgens.
