Notably, 31% of androgenic steroid users also employed cognitive enhancers, suggesting a "cosmetic neurology" phenomenon where individuals seek optimization across multiple domains. Chronic users demonstrated altered dopaminergic and cholinergic signaling patterns, with potential long-term consequences for reward processing and executive function. The revolving door pattern of switching between different enhancement strategies was observed in 42% of regular users. These findings highlight the urgent need for evidence-based guidelines regarding enhancement drug use in healthy populations and underscore the complex neuroadaptations associated with chronic use of these agents outside therapeutic contexts.

title: Pharmacological enhancement of memory and executive functioning in laboratory animals.
abstract: Memory and executive functioning deficits represent core features of numerous neuropsychiatric disorders, yet effective pharmacological interventions remain limited. This study investigated the cognitive-enhancing effects of modafinil, a wake-promoting agent with dopaminergic and noradrenergic activity, on memory consolidation and executive performance in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received acute modafinil (32, 64, or 128 mg/kg, i.p.) or vehicle 30 minutes prior to behavioral testing. Memory performance was assessed using the novel object recognition task and contextual fear conditioning, while executive functioning was evaluated through the attentional set-shifting task and five-choice serial reaction time task. Neurochemical analyses measured dopamine, norepinephrine, and acetylcholine levels in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus using high-performance liquid chromatography. Modafinil dose-dependently enhanced novel object recognition at 24-hour retention (64 mg/kg: discrimination ratio 0.72 ± 0.06 vs. vehicle 0.54 ± 0.04, p < 0.01) and improved contextual fear memory (64 mg/kg: 68 ± 5% freezing vs. vehicle 42 ± 4%, p < 0.05). Executive performance was significantly enhanced, with reduced extra-dimensional shift errors (64 mg/kg: 8.2 ± 1.3 vs. vehicle 14.7 ± 2.1, p < 0.01) and improved attentional accuracy (64 mg/kg: 82 ± 3% vs. vehicle 71 ± 4%, p < 0.05). Neurochemical analysis revealed dose-dependent increases in prefrontal dopamine (2.1-fold) and norepinephrine (1.8-fold) with 64 mg/kg modafinil. These findings demonstrate that modafinil enhances both memory consolidation and executive functioning through monoaminergic mechanisms, supporting its potential therapeutic application in cognitive disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy detection of neurotransmitters in dorsomedial medulla correlate with spontaneous baroreceptor reflex function.
abstract: Control of heart rate variability via modulation of sympathovagal balance is a key function of nucleus tractus solitarii and the dorsal motor nucleus of the vagus localized in the dorsomedial medulla oblongata. Normal blood pressure regulation involves precise balance of glutamate (Glu)-glutamine-gamma-aminobutyric acid transmitter systems, and angiotensin II modulates these transmitters to produce tonic suppression of reflex function. It is not known, however, whether other brain transmitters/metabolites are indicators of baroreflex function. This study establishes the concept that comprehensive baseline transmitter/metabolite profiles obtained using in vivo (1)H magnetic resonance spectroscopy in rats with well-characterized differences in resting blood pressure and baroreflex function can be used as indices of autonomic balance or baroreflex sensitivity. Transgenic rats with over-expression of renin [m(Ren2)27] or under-expression of glial-angiotensinogen (ASrAogen) were compared with Sprague-Dawley rats. Glu concentration in the dorsal medulla is significantly higher in ASrAogen rats compared with either Sprague-Dawley or (mRen2)27 rats. Glu levels and the ratio of Glu:glutamine correlated positively with indices of higher vagal tone consistent with the importance of these neurotransmitters in baroreflex function. Interestingly, the levels of choline-containing metabolites showed a significant positive correlation with spontaneous baroreflex sensitivity and a negative correlation with sympathetic tone. Thus, we demonstrate the concept that noninvasive assessment of neurochemical biomarkers may be used as an index of baroreflex sensitivity.

title: Mildronate: an antiischemic drug for neurological indications.
abstract: Mildronate (3-(2,2,2-trimethylhydrazinium)propionate; MET-88; meldonium, quaterine) is an antiischemic drug developed at the Latvian Institute of Organic Synthesis. Mildronate was designed to inhibit carnitine biosynthesis in order to prevent accumulation of cytotoxic intermediate products of fatty acid beta-oxidation in ischemic tissues and to block this highly oxygen-consuming process. Mildronate is efficient in the treatment of heart ischemia and its consequences. Extensive evaluation of pharmacological activities of mildronate revealed its beneficial effect on cerebral circulation disorders and central nervous system (CNS) functions. The drug is used in neurological clinics for the treatment of brain circulation disorders. It appears to improve patients' mood; they become more active, their motor dysfunction decreases, and asthenia, dizziness and nausea become less pronounced. Since the brain does not utilize fatty acids as fuel other mechanisms of action of mildronate in CNS should be considered. Several reports indicate the possible existence of an alternative, non-carnitine dependent mechanism of action of mildronate. Our recent findings suggest that CNS effects of mildronate could be mediated by stimulation of the nitric oxide production in the vascular endothelium by modification of the gamma-butyrobetaine and its esters pools. It is hypothesized that mildronate may increase the formation of the gamma-butyrobetaine esters. The latter are potent cholinomimetics and may activate eNOS via acetylcholine receptors or specific gamma-butyrobetaine ester receptors. This article summarizes known pharmacological effects of mildronate, its pharmacokinetics, toxicology, as well as the proposed mechanisms of action.

title: The neuropsychopharmacology of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) represents one of the most prevalent childhood psychiatric conditions, affecting approximately 3-7% of school-age children worldwide. Despite decades of clinical use of stimulant medications, the precise neurobiological mechanisms underlying ADHD pathophysiology and therapeutic response remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge of the neurochemical basis of ADHD, focusing on dopaminergic and noradrenergic neurotransmitter systems. Converging evidence from neuroimaging studies, animal models, and pharmacological investigations indicates that ADHD involves dysfunction within fronto-striatal circuits, particularly affecting the prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate, and striatum. Dopamine transporter (DAT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET) abnormalities appear central to ADHD pathophysiology, with altered transporter density and function observed in key brain regions. Stimulant medications, including methylphenidate and amphetamine compounds, exert therapeutic effects primarily through blockade of DAT and NET, thereby increasing synaptic availability of dopamine and norepinephrine in prefrontal and striatal regions. The efficacy of non-stimulant agents such as atomoxetine, a selective NET inhibitor, further supports the critical role of noradrenergic signaling in attention and impulse control. Emerging evidence also implicates cholinergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems in ADHD neurobiology. Understanding these complex neurochemical interactions provides important insights for developing more targeted therapeutic interventions and may inform personalized treatment approaches based on individual neurobiological profiles. Future research directions include investigation of genetic polymorphisms affecting neurotransmitter function and development of novel pharmacological targets beyond traditional monoaminergic systems.

title: Cell death and proliferation in acute slices and organotypic cultures of mammalian CNS.
abstract: Analysis of the interplay between cell proliferation and death has been greatly advantaged by the development of CNS slice preparations. In slices, interactions between neurons and neurons and the glial cells are fundamentally preserved in a fashion close to the in vivo situation. In parallel, these preparations offer the possibility of an easy experimental manipulation. Two main types of slices are currently in use: the acute slices, which are short living preparations where the major functions of the intact brain (including neurogenesis) are maintained, and the organotypic cultures, where the maturation and plasticity of neuronal circuitries in relation to naturally occurring neuronal death and/or experimental insults can be followed over several weeks in vitro. We will discuss here the main advantages/disadvantages linked to the use of CNS slices for histological analysis of neuronal proliferation and death, as well as the main findings obtained in the most popular types of preparations, i.e. the cortical, hippocampal, cerebellar and retinal slices.

title: A review of the neuropharmacological properties of khat.
abstract: Khat (Catha edulis) is a flowering plant indigenous to East Africa and the Arabian Peninsula whose leaves are chewed for their psychostimulant effects. Despite widespread use affecting millions globally, the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying khat's psychoactive properties remain incompletely understood. This review systematically examines the current literature on khat's neurochemical actions, focusing on its primary alkaloids cathinone, cathine, and norephedrine. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies published between 1990 and 2007, evaluating khat's effects on monoaminergic neurotransmission, receptor binding profiles, and behavioral outcomes. The evidence demonstrates that cathinone, khat's most potent constituent, acts primarily as a monoamine uptake inhibitor with preferential affinity for dopamine and norepinephrine transporters, exhibiting pharmacological similarities to amphetamine. Neurochemical studies reveal dose-dependent increases in synaptic dopamine concentrations in the nucleus accumbens and striatum, consistent with khat's euphoric and motor-stimulating effects. Chronic exposure studies indicate potential for tolerance development and withdrawal symptoms, suggesting abuse liability. Clinical investigations document acute effects including increased alertness, elevated mood, and enhanced sociability, alongside adverse effects such as insomnia, anxiety, and cardiovascular stimulation. Long-term use has been associated with psychiatric symptoms including depression, anxiety disorders, and in some cases, psychotic episodes. These findings indicate that khat's neuropharmacological profile warrants serious consideration in substance abuse research and public health policy, particularly given its legal status in many countries despite demonstrated psychoactive potency and dependence potential.

title: [The way out from crisis--from the closing of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) to Semmelweis University].
abstract: In our recent article we attempt to sum up the circumstances of the closing-down of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI). We intend to summarize the values that may disappear by the liquidation of the institute and try to explore the possibilities how to keep them alive in the future. Most of the divisions can operate further under the umbrella of the Semmelweis University; the modus operandi and the role of the University are also covered in this article.
journal: Psychiatria Hungarica : A Magyar Pszichiatriai Tarsasag tudomanyos folyoirata

title: Dorsal raphe vs. median raphe serotonergic antagonism. Anatomical, physiological, behavioral, neuroendocrinological, neuropharmacological and clinical evidences: relevance for neuropharmacological therapy.
abstract: The dorsal raphe nucleus (DRN) and median raphe nucleus (MRN) represent distinct serotonergic subsystems with opposing functional roles in mood regulation, anxiety, and stress responses. While previous research has focused primarily on global serotonergic mechanisms, emerging evidence suggests these raphe nuclei exhibit antagonistic properties with significant implications for neuropsychiatric disorders. We conducted a comprehensive review of anatomical tract-tracing studies, electrophysiological recordings, behavioral assessments following selective lesions, neuroendocrine measurements, and pharmacological manipulations targeting DRN versus MRN serotonergic projections in rodent models. Additionally, we analyzed clinical neuroimaging and pharmacological response data from patients with major depression and anxiety disorders. Anatomical studies revealed distinct projection patterns: DRN neurons predominantly innervate forebrain limbic structures including amygdala and prefrontal cortex, while MRN projections favor hippocampus and septum. Electrophysiological data demonstrated opposing responses to stress, with DRN activation correlating with anxiety-like behaviors and MRN stimulation producing anxiolytic effects. Selective DRN lesions reduced depression-like behaviors in forced swim and sucrose preference tests, whereas MRN lesions exacerbated these measures. Neuroendocrine analyses showed DRN activation increased corticosterone levels while MRN stimulation attenuated HPA axis responses. Pharmacological studies using 5-HT1A receptor manipulations revealed differential sensitivity between nuclei. Clinical PET imaging demonstrated hyperactivity in DRN-innervated regions in depressed patients. These findings support a DRN-MRN antagonism model wherein therapeutic interventions should consider selective targeting of raphe subsystems rather than global serotonergic modulation for optimized treatment outcomes.

title: Introduction to the Theme "Ion Channels and Neuropharmacology: From the Past to the Future".
abstract: "Ion Channels and Neuropharmacology: From the Past to the Future" is the main theme of articles in Volume 60 of the Annual Review of Pharmacology and Toxicology. Reviews in this volume discuss a wide spectrum of therapeutically relevant ion channels and GPCRs with a particular emphasis on structural studies that elucidate drug binding sites and mechanisms of action. The regulation of ion channels by second messengers, including Ca2+ and cyclic AMP, and lipid mediators is also highly relevant to several of the ion channels discussed, including KCNQ channels, HCN channels, L-type Ca2+ channels, and AMPA receptors, as well as the aquaporin channels. Molecular identification of exactly where drugs bind in the structure not only elucidates their mechanism of action but also aids future structure-based drug discovery efforts to focus on relevant pharmacophores. The ion channels discussed here are targets for multiple nervous system diseases, including epilepsy and neuropathic pain. This theme complements several previous themes, including "New Therapeutic Targets," "New Approaches for Studying Drug and Toxicant Action: Applications to Drug Discovery and Development," and "New Methods and Novel Therapeutic Approaches in Pharmacology and Toxicology."
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology

title: Migraine-associated vertigo: diagnosis and treatment.
abstract: Migraine-associated vertigo (MAV) represents a prevalent but underdiagnosed neurological condition affecting approximately 1% of the general population, with higher prevalence among patients with established migraine disorders. The relationship between vestibular dysfunction and migraine remains poorly understood, contributing to diagnostic challenges and suboptimal therapeutic outcomes. This comprehensive review synthesizes current evidence regarding diagnostic criteria and treatment strategies for MAV based on systematic analysis of published literature from 1995-2009 and clinical experience from three tertiary headache centers. We analyzed 847 patients presenting with episodic vertigo, of whom 312 (36.8%) met proposed criteria for MAV, defined as recurrent vestibular symptoms occurring in patients with migraine history, with temporal association between vestibular episodes and headache in at least 50% of attacks. Diagnostic workup included detailed history, vestibular testing, and neuroimaging to exclude secondary causes. First-line treatments included migraine prophylactic agents (topiramate, propranolol, amitriptyline) and lifestyle modifications, while acute episodes were managed with vestibular suppressants and antimigraine therapies. Response rates to prophylactic treatment were 68% for topiramate, 61% for propranolol, and 54% for tricyclic antidepressants, with significant reduction in both vertigo frequency and intensity. Combination therapy with vestibular rehabilitation showed superior outcomes compared to pharmacotherapy alone. These findings support MAV as a distinct clinical entity requiring specialized diagnostic criteria and tailored treatment approaches combining antimigraine therapies with vestibular interventions for optimal patient outcomes.

title: Neuropsychopharmacological aesthetics: A theoretical consideration of pharmacological approaches to causative brain study in aesthetics and art.
abstract: Recent advances in neuroimaging and pharmacological interventions have opened new avenues for investigating the neural substrates underlying aesthetic experience and artistic appreciation. While correlational neuroimaging studies have identified key brain regions involved in aesthetic processing, including the orbitofrontal cortex, anterior cingulate, and reward circuitry, causal mechanisms remain poorly understood. This theoretical framework proposes systematic pharmacological approaches to establish causative relationships between neurotransmitter systems and aesthetic cognition. We outline methodological considerations for implementing controlled pharmacological challenges using dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic modulators during aesthetic judgment tasks. Proposed protocols involve double-blind, placebo-controlled administration of selective receptor agonists and antagonists while participants engage in art evaluation, musical appreciation, and beauty perception paradigms. Critical methodological elements include standardized aesthetic stimuli, validated rating scales, appropriate washout periods, and control for individual differences in drug metabolism and baseline aesthetic sensitivity. Expected outcomes include dissociation of reward-based versus cognitive components of aesthetic experience through dopamine D2 receptor manipulation, modulation of openness to abstract art through serotonin 2A receptor targeting, and alteration of aesthetic attention through GABA-A receptor modulation. This neuropsychopharmacological approach promises to advance beyond descriptive neuroscience toward mechanistic understanding of aesthetic experience. Such investigations may inform therapeutic applications for anhedonia, contribute to neuroaesthetics theory, and provide insights into the evolutionary origins of artistic behavior. Ethical considerations and safety protocols for aesthetic pharmacology research are discussed.

title: EFNS guidelines for the diagnosis and management of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: In 2008 a task force was set up to develop a revision of the European Federation of the Neurological Societies (EFNS) guideline for the diagnosis and management of Alzheimer's disease (AD) and other disorders associated with dementia, published in early 2007. The aim of this revised international guideline was to present a peer-reviewed evidence-based statement for the guidance of practice for clinical neurologists, geriatricians, psychiatrists, and other specialist physicians responsible for the care of patients with AD. Mild cognitive impairment and non-Alzheimer dementias are not included in this guideline. The task force working group reviewed evidence from original research articles, meta-analysis, and systematic reviews, published before May 2009. The evidence was classified and consensus recommendations graded (A, B, or C) according to the EFNS guidance. Where there was a lack of evidence, but clear consensus, good practice points were provided. The recommendations for clinical diagnosis, blood tests, neuropsychology, neuroimaging, electroencephalography, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis, genetic testing, disclosure of diagnosis, treatment of AD, behavioural and psychological symptoms in dementia, legal issues, counselling and support for caregivers were all revised as compared with the previous EFNS guideline. A number of new recommendations and good practice points are made, namely in CSF, neuropsychology, neuroimaging and reviewing non-evidence based therapies. The assessment, interpretation, and treatment of symptoms, disability, needs, and caregiver stress during the course of AD require the contribution of many different professionals. These professionals should adhere to these guideline to improve the diagnosis and management of AD.

title: Three-dimensional patterning in biomedicine: Importance and applications in neuropharmacology.
abstract: Nature manufactures biological systems in three dimensions with precisely controlled spatiotemporal profiles on hierarchical length and time scales. In this article, we review 3D patterning of biological systems on synthetic platforms for neuropharmacological applications. We briefly describe 3D versus 2D chemical and topographical patterning methods and their limitations. Subsequently, an overview of introducing a third dimension in neuropharmacological research with delineation of chemical and topographical roles is presented. Finally, toward the end of this article, an explanation of how 3D patterning has played a pivotal role in relevant fields of neuropharmacology to understand neurophysiology during development, normal health, and disease conditions is described. The future prospects of organs-on-a--like devices to mimic patterned blood-brain barrier in the context of neurotherapeutic discovery and development for the prioritization of lead candidates, membrane potential, and toxicity testing are also described. © 2017 Wiley Periodicals, Inc. J Biomed Mater Res Part B: Appl Biomater, 106B: 1369-1382, 2018.
journal: Journal of biomedical materials research. Part B, Applied biomaterials

title: Neuropsychopharmacological aesthetics: A theoretical consideration of pharmacological approaches to causative brain study in aesthetics and art.
abstract: Recent developments in neuroaesthetics have heightened the need for causative approaches to more deeply understand the mechanism underlying perception, emotion, and aesthetic experiences. This has recently been the topic for empirical work, employing several causative methods for changing brain activity, as well as comparative assessments of individuals with brain damage or disease. However, one area of study with high potential, and indeed a long history of often nonscientific use in the area of aesthetics and art, employing psychopharmacological chemicals as means of changing brain function, has not been systematically utilized. This chapter reviews the literature on this topic, analyzing neuroendocrinological (neurochemical) approaches and mechanisms that might be used to causatively study the aesthetic brain. We focus on four relevant neuromodulatory systems potentially related to aesthetic experience: the dopaminergic, serotonergic, cannabinoid, and the opioidergic system. We build a bridge to psychopharmacological methods and review drug-induced behavioral and neurobiological consequences. We conclude with a discussion of hypotheses and suggestions for future research.

title: Time-dependent behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic dysregulation in female C57BL/6 mice caused by chronic high-fat diet intake.
abstract: Chronic high-fat diet (HFD) consumption is associated with metabolic dysfunction and altered neurobehavioral phenotypes, yet the temporal progression of these changes remains poorly characterized in female rodents. This study investigated time-dependent effects of chronic HFD exposure on behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic parameters in female C57BL/6 mice. Eight-week-old female mice were randomly assigned to either standard chow (10% fat) or HFD (60% fat) for 2, 8, or 16 weeks (n=12 per group). Body weight, glucose tolerance, and plasma leptin levels were assessed at each timepoint. Behavioral testing included open field, elevated plus maze, and forced swim tests. Neurochemical analysis examined monoamine concentrations in the prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and hypothalamus using HPLC. HFD-fed mice exhibited significant weight gain by week 2 (p<0.01), with progressive glucose intolerance and elevated leptin levels at later timepoints. Anxiety-like behavior emerged at 8 weeks, evidenced by reduced open arm exploration (p<0.05) and decreased center zone activity (p<0.01). Depressive-like behavior was observed only at 16 weeks, with increased immobility time in forced swim tests (p<0.01). Neurochemical alterations included reduced serotonin in the hippocampus at 8 weeks (p<0.05) and decreased dopamine in the prefrontal cortex at 16 weeks (p<0.01). Hypothalamic norepinephrine levels were elevated throughout all timepoints in HFD groups (p<0.05). These findings demonstrate that chronic HFD consumption produces sequential metabolic, anxiety-related, and depressive-like phenotypes in female mice, accompanied by region-specific monoaminergic disruptions.

title: The development of an implantable catheter system for chronic or intermittent convection-enhanced delivery.
abstract: Convection-enhanced delivery (CED) has emerged as a promising technique for bypassing the blood-brain barrier and achieving targeted drug distribution within the central nervous system. However, current methods are limited by the need for repeated surgical procedures and inconsistent catheter placement for chronic therapeutic regimens. We developed and characterized a novel implantable catheter system designed for long-term, programmable CED in rodent models. The system consists of a biocompatible titanium port connected to a flexible polyurethane catheter with a customized tip geometry optimized for uniform infusate distribution. Twenty-four adult Sprague-Dawley rats were implanted with the device targeting the striatum, and system performance was evaluated over 8 weeks using fluorescent tracers, MRI-compatible contrast agents, and therapeutic compounds. Catheter patency was maintained in 92% of animals throughout the study period, with no evidence of significant tissue inflammation or foreign body reaction. Infusion volumes of 2-50 μL delivered at rates of 0.2-2.0 μL/min achieved consistent distribution patterns with minimal backflow along the catheter track. Real-time MRI monitoring confirmed reproducible targeting accuracy within 200 μm across multiple treatment sessions. Drug pharmacokinetics demonstrated sustained therapeutic concentrations in target tissues with minimal systemic exposure compared to conventional delivery methods. This implantable CED system provides a reliable platform for chronic neurotherapeutic studies and shows potential for clinical translation in treating neurodegenerative diseases, brain tumors, and other CNS disorders requiring repeated or continuous drug delivery.

title: Updating neuropathology and neuropharmacology of monoaminergic systems.
abstract: The monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems, comprising dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic pathways, represent critical targets for neuropsychiatric therapeutics, yet our understanding of their complex pathophysiology continues to evolve. This comprehensive review synthesises recent advances in the neuropathology and neuropharmacology of monoaminergic dysfunction across major neuropsychiatric disorders. We systematically analysed emerging neuroimaging, post-mortem, and molecular studies published between 2010-2015, focusing on novel therapeutic targets and biomarkers within monoaminergic circuits. Current evidence reveals significant heterogeneity in monoaminergic dysfunction patterns across psychiatric conditions, with distinct regional vulnerability profiles in depression, schizophrenia, and Parkinson's disease. Advanced neuroimaging techniques have identified previously unrecognised alterations in monoamine transporter density and receptor occupancy, particularly within cortico-limbic circuits. Post-mortem investigations demonstrate complex interactions between genetic variants, epigenetic modifications, and environmental factors in modulating monoaminergic system integrity. Pharmacologically, traditional approaches targeting single monoamine systems show limited efficacy, spurring development of multi-target compounds and novel modulatory strategies. Emerging therapeutic approaches include selective trace amine-associated receptor agonists, allosteric modulators of monoamine receptors, and compounds targeting monoamine metabolism. Furthermore, personalised medicine approaches incorporating pharmacogenomic profiling of monoaminergic enzymes and transporters show promise for optimising treatment selection. These findings underscore the need for refined nosological frameworks that incorporate monoaminergic biomarkers and highlight opportunities for developing more effective, mechanistically-informed therapeutic interventions for neuropsychiatric disorders affecting monoaminergic neurotransmission.

title: Noradrenergic dysfunction and the psychopharmacology of posttraumatic stress disorder.
abstract: Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is characterized by hyperarousal, intrusive memories, and avoidance behaviors that suggest dysregulation of noradrenergic neurotransmission. While serotonergic medications remain first-line treatments, growing evidence implicates norepinephrine (NE) system abnormalities in PTSD pathophysiology. We conducted a comprehensive review of preclinical and clinical literature examining noradrenergic dysfunction in PTSD and evaluated the therapeutic potential of noradrenergically-active compounds. Systematic searches of PubMed, EMBASE, and PsycINFO databases (1980-2007) identified 127 relevant studies examining noradrenergic biomarkers, neuroimaging findings, and pharmacological interventions in PTSD populations. Clinical studies consistently demonstrate elevated cerebrospinal fluid and urinary NE metabolites, enhanced α2-adrenergic receptor sensitivity, and altered noradrenergic responses to stress in PTSD patients compared to trauma-exposed controls. Neuroimaging studies reveal hyperactivation of noradrenergic projection areas including the amygdala and prefrontal cortex. Pharmacologically, α1-antagonists like prazosin show efficacy for PTSD-related nightmares and sleep disturbances, while β-blockers administered peritraumatically may prevent PTSD development. Mixed norepinephrine-serotonin reuptake inhibitors demonstrate superior efficacy compared to selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in some trials. α2-agonists show promise for hyperarousal symptoms but require further investigation. These findings support a central role for noradrenergic dysfunction in PTSD and suggest that targeted noradrenergic interventions may enhance treatment outcomes. Future research should focus on identifying noradrenergic biomarkers to guide personalized treatment selection and developing novel compounds targeting specific noradrenergic receptor subtypes.

title: Chapter 11 - Solid lipid nanoparticles for brain tumors therapy: State of the art and novel challenges.
abstract: Malignant gliomas, despite aggressive multimodal therapies and adequate supportive care, still maintain poor prognosis. Solid lipid nanoparticles (SLN) are colloidal carriers that could be regarded as a highly flexible platform for brain tumor imaging and therapeutical purposes. In this chapter we will first describe brain tumors characteristics and conventional therapeutical approaches. In the subsequent sections, we will analyze SLN properties, effectiveness, and future perspectives in both imaging and targeted treatment of malignant gliomas.

title: The neuropharmacological basis for the use of memantine in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: Memantine has been demonstrated to be safe and effective in the symptomatic treatment of Alzheimer's disease (AD). While the neurobiological basis for the therapeutic activity of memantine is not fully understood, the drug is not a cholinesterase inhibitor and, therefore, acts differently from current AD therapies. Memantine can interact with a variety of ligand-gated ion channels. However, NMDA receptors appear to be a key target of memantine at therapeutic concentrations. Memantine is an uncompetitive (channel blocking) NMDA receptor antagonist. Like other NMDA receptor antagonists, memantine at high concentrations can inhibit mechanisms of synaptic plasticity that are believed to underlie learning and memory. However, at lower, clinically relevant concentrations memantine can under some circumstances promote synaptic plasticity and preserve or enhance memory in animal models of AD. In addition, memantine can protect against the excitotoxic destruction of cholinergic neurons. Blockade of NMDA receptors by memantine could theoretically confer disease-modifying activity in AD by inhibiting the "weak" NMDA receptor-dependent excitotoxicity that has been hypothesized to play a role in the progressive neuronal loss that underlies the evolving dementia. Moreover, recent in vitro studies suggest that memantine abrogates beta-amyloid (Abeta) toxicity and possibly inhibits Abeta production. Considerable attention has focused on the investigation of theories to explain the better tolerability of memantine over other NMDA receptor antagonists, particularly those that act by a similar channel blocking mechanism such as dissociative anesthetic-like agents (phencyclidine, ketamine, MK-801). A variety of channel-level factors could be relevant, including fast channel-blocking kinetics and strong voltage-dependence (allowing rapid relief of block during synaptic activity), as well as reduced trapping (permitting egress from closed channels). These factors may allow memantine to block channel activity induced by low, tonic levels of glutamate--an action that might contribute to symptomatic improvement and could theoretically protect against weak excitotoxicity--while sparing synaptic responses required for normal behavioral functioning, cognition and memory.

title: Contemporary approaches to pharmacotherapy in Tourette's syndrome and obsessive-compulsive disorder.
abstract: Contemporary approaches used in the pharmacological treatment of children and adolescents with Tourette's syndrome or obsessive-compulsive disorder. Tourette's syndrome (TS) and obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) now are recognized as more common than previously believed. Moreover, there is accumulating evidence that these two disorders may share common neurobiological underpinnings. Nonetheless, pharmacologic approaches to treatment may be quite different. Using recently published information, this paper reviews current treatment for TS and OCD. There have been major advances in the understanding of the pathophysiology of OCD and TS. These advances provides guidance for the treatment of children and adolescents with these disorders. However, additional research is needed to refine current treatment approaches.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychiatric nursing : official publication of the Association of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nurses, Inc

title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning I: the degraded contingency effect is disrupted by atropine but reinstated by physostigmine.
abstract: Classical fear conditioning paradigms have demonstrated that degraded contingency between conditioned stimulus (CS) and unconditioned stimulus (US) presentations results in attenuated conditioned responses, suggesting that associative learning depends critically on predictive relationships rather than simple CS-US pairings. The role of cholinergic neurotransmission in mediating contingency detection and fear learning remains poorly understood. We investigated the effects of muscarinic receptor antagonism and acetylcholinesterase inhibition on fear conditioning under degraded contingency conditions in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group). Animals received either saline, atropine (10 mg/kg, i.p.), or physostigmine (0.25 mg/kg, i.p.) 30 minutes prior to conditioning sessions. The degraded contingency protocol consisted of paired CS-footshock presentations intermixed with unpaired US presentations, resulting in a CS-US contingency of 0.33. Fear conditioning was assessed through freezing behavior during CS presentations. Control animals demonstrated the expected degraded contingency effect, showing significantly reduced freezing compared to standard paired conditioning (p<0.01). Atropine pretreatment completely abolished this contingency sensitivity, with treated animals showing equivalent freezing responses regardless of CS-US contingency (p>0.05). Conversely, physostigmine pretreatment in atropine-treated animals fully restored normal contingency detection, reinstating the degraded contingency effect (p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that intact cholinergic signaling through muscarinic receptors is essential for detecting predictive relationships during associative learning. The results suggest that acetylcholine may serve as a critical neuromodulator enabling organisms to distinguish between predictive and non-predictive environmental cues during fear acquisition.

title: Measurement of kinetically resolved vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux using rotating disk electrode voltammetry.
abstract: Understanding the kinetic properties of vesicular dopamine transport is essential for elucidating the mechanisms underlying dopamine storage and release in presynaptic terminals. Traditional methods for measuring vesicular monoamine transporter (VMAT2) activity rely on radiotracer uptake assays that lack temporal resolution and cannot distinguish between uptake and efflux processes in real-time. We developed a novel electrochemical approach using rotating disk electrode (RDE) voltammetry to directly monitor dopamine uptake and efflux from isolated synaptic vesicles with subsecond temporal resolution. Synaptic vesicles were isolated from rat striatum using standard differential centrifugation and incubated in ATP-containing buffer. A glassy carbon RDE was positioned in the vesicle suspension and rotated at controlled speeds (400-1600 rpm) while applying potential sweeps to detect dopamine oxidation currents. Vesicular dopamine uptake was initiated by ATP addition and monitored as decreased free dopamine concentration in the medium. Efflux was measured following addition of the VMAT2 inhibitor reserpine or the protonophore FCCP. This method successfully resolved biphasic dopamine uptake kinetics, revealing a rapid initial phase (Km = 0.8 ± 0.2 μM) and a slower secondary component. Reserpine treatment resulted in dose-dependent inhibition of uptake (IC50 = 45 nM) and stimulated dopamine efflux with kinetics consistent with simple diffusion. RDE voltammetry provides unprecedented temporal resolution for studying vesicular dopamine transport and offers significant advantages over conventional approaches for investigating the pharmacology and regulation of VMAT2.

title: Maps of cardiovascular and respiratory regions of rat ventral medulla: focus on the caudal medulla.
abstract: The ventral medulla oblongata is critical for cardiorespiratory regulation. Here we review previous literature relating to sites within the ventral medulla that have been identified as having a 'cardiovascular' or 'respiratory' function. Together with the maps generated here, of sites from which cardiovascular and respiratory responses were evoked by glutamate microinjection, specific 'cardiovascular' regions have been defined and delineated. Commonly investigated regions, including the vasopressor rostral ventrolateral medulla (RVLM) and vasodepressor caudal ventrolateral medulla (CVLM), or areas only described by others, such as the medullary cerebral vasodilator area, are included for completeness. Emphasis is given to the caudal medulla, where three pressor regions, the caudal pressor area (CPA), the intermediate pressor area (IPA) and the medullo-cervical pressor area (MCPA), caudal to the vasodepressor CVLM were defined in the original data provided. The IPA is most responsive under pentobarbitone rather than urethane anaesthesia clearly delineating it from both the rostrally located CPA and the caudally located MCPA. The description of these multiple pressor areas appears to clarify the confusion that surrounds the identification of the 'CPA'. Also noted is a vasopressor region adjacent to the vasodepressor CVLM. Apart from the well described ventral respiratory column, a region medial to the pre-Bötzinger is described, from which increases in both phrenic nerve frequency and amplitude were evoked. Limitations associated with the technique of glutamate microinjection to define functionally specific regions are discussed. Particular effort has been made to define and delineate the regions with respect to ventrally located anatomical landmarks rather than the commonly used ventral surface or dorsal landmarks such as the obex or calamus scriptorius that may vary with the brain orientation or histological processing. This should ensure that a region can easily be defined by all investigators. Study of defined regions will help expedite the identification of the role of the multiple cell groups with diverse neurotransmitter complements that exist even within each of the regions described, in coordinating the delivery of oxygenated blood to the tissues.

title: Gaddum and LSD: the birth and growth of experimental and clinical neuropharmacology research on 5-HT in the UK.
abstract: The vasoconstrictor substance named serotonin was identified as 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT) by Maurice Rapport in 1949. In 1951, Rapport gave Gaddum samples of 5-HT substance allowing him to develop a bioassay to both detect and measure the amine. Gaddum and colleagues rapidly identified 5-HT in brain and showed that lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) antagonized its action in peripheral tissues. Gaddum accordingly postulated that 5-HT might have a role in mood regulation. This review examines the role of UK scientists in the first 20 years following these major discoveries, discussing their role in developing assays for 5-HT in the CNS, identifying the enzymes involved in the synthesis and metabolism of 5-HT and investigating the effect of drugs on brain 5-HT. It reviews studies on the effects of LSD in humans, including Gaddum's self-administration experiments. It outlines investigations on the role of 5-HT in psychiatric disorders, including studies on the effect of antidepressant drugs on the 5-HT concentration in rodent and human brain, and the attempts to examine 5-HT biochemistry in the brains of patients with depressive illness. It is clear that a rather small group of both preclinical scientists and psychiatrists in the UK made major advances in our understanding of the role of 5-HT in the brain, paving the way for much of the knowledge now taken for granted when discussing ways that 5-HT might be involved in the control of mood and the idea that therapeutic drugs used to alleviate psychiatric illness might alter the function of cerebral 5-HT.

title: In vivo voltammetry: from wire to wireless measurements.
abstract: Real-time electrochemical monitoring of neurotransmitter dynamics in freely behaving animals has been limited by the constraints of tethered recording systems. Traditional fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) requires physical wire connections that restrict animal movement and may introduce behavioral artifacts. We developed and validated a novel wireless voltammetry system capable of transmitting electrochemical data from carbon fiber microelectrodes implanted in rat brain. The system consists of a miniaturized potentiostat (2.1 g) with integrated radio frequency transmission operating at 433 MHz, paired with a computer-controlled receiver station. We compared dopamine detection sensitivity and temporal resolution between wired and wireless configurations using standard carbon fiber electrodes (7 μm diameter) in the nucleus accumbens of anesthetized and freely moving rats. Calibration experiments demonstrated equivalent sensitivity for dopamine detection (limit of detection: 50 nM) and maintained microsecond temporal resolution necessary for FSCV measurements. Wireless recordings successfully detected electrically-evoked dopamine release with signal-to-noise ratios comparable to traditional wired systems (SNR = 12.3 ± 2.1 vs 13.7 ± 1.8, respectively; p > 0.05). Long-term stability tests showed reliable signal transmission for up to 6 hours of continuous recording. Behavioral studies revealed reduced stress-related artifacts and more naturalistic movement patterns compared to tethered controls. This wireless voltammetry approach enables artifact-free neurochemical monitoring during complex behaviors, opening new possibilities for studying neurotransmitter function in naturalistic settings while maintaining the temporal and chemical specificity of traditional electrochemical methods.

title: Investigation of Potential Antioxidant, Thrombolytic and Neuropharmacological Activities of Homalomena aromatica Leaves Using Experimental and In Silico Approaches.
abstract: Homalomena aromatica (Schott) Hook.f., a medicinal plant traditionally used in Southeast Asian folk medicine, has been reported to possess various therapeutic properties, yet its neuropharmacological potential remains largely unexplored. This study aimed to investigate the antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological activities of H. aromatica leaf extracts using both experimental and computational approaches. Methanolic extracts of H. aromatica leaves were prepared and subjected to phytochemical screening, followed by in vitro antioxidant assays including DPPH, ABTS, and ferric reducing antioxidant power (FRAP) methods. Thrombolytic activity was evaluated using the clot lysis assay, while neuropharmacological effects were assessed through hole-board, open field, elevated plus maze, and tail suspension tests in Swiss albino mice. Additionally, molecular docking studies were performed against key neurological targets including acetylcholinesterase, monoamine oxidase-A, and GABA-A receptors. The methanolic extract demonstrated significant antioxidant activity with IC₅₀ values of 24.31 ± 1.42 μg/mL and 31.67 ± 2.15 μg/mL for DPPH and ABTS assays, respectively. Thrombolytic activity showed 47.23 ± 3.21% clot lysis at 500 μg/mL concentration. Behavioral studies revealed dose-dependent anxiolytic and antidepressant-like effects at 200 and 400 mg/kg body weight. Molecular docking analysis identified several bioactive compounds with favorable binding affinities to neurological targets, supporting the observed pharmacological activities. These findings suggest that H. aromatica leaves possess promising antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological properties, warranting further investigation for potential therapeutic applications in neurological disorders.

title: Methodological approaches to exploring epileptic disorders in the human brain in vitro.
abstract: Brain surgery, and in particular epilepsy surgery, offers the unique opportunity to study viable human central nervous tissue in vitro. This does not only open a window to address the basic mechanisms underlying human disease, such as epilepsy, but it allows to venture into investigating neurophysiological functions per se. In the present paper, we describe the most commonly used methods in the electrophysiological (and, at least to some extent, also histochemical and molecular) analysis of human tissue in vitro. In addition, we consider the pitfalls and limitations of such studies, in particular regarding the issue of tissue sampling procedures and control experiments.

title: Operant self-administration models for testing the neuropharmacological basis of ethanol consumption in rats.
abstract: Operant self-administration paradigms represent the gold standard for investigating the neurobiological mechanisms underlying ethanol consumption and addiction liability in preclinical models. These procedures allow researchers to assess voluntary ethanol intake under controlled conditions while examining the pharmacological modulation of drinking behavior. This protocol describes standardized methods for establishing ethanol self-administration in male Sprague-Dawley rats using operant conditioning chambers equipped with two response levers and fluid delivery systems. Following initial training with sweetened ethanol solutions (10% w/v with 5% sucrose), subjects progressively transition to unsweetened ethanol while maintaining stable responding on fixed-ratio and progressive-ratio schedules. Key methodological considerations include appropriate fluid concentrations, session duration (typically 30-60 minutes), training criteria for acquisition, and baseline stability requirements. The protocol enables pharmacological testing of compounds targeting various neurotransmitter systems implicated in ethanol reinforcement, including dopaminergic, GABAergic, glutamatergic, and opioidergic pathways. Validation studies demonstrate that rats reliably acquire ethanol self-administration, with intake levels correlating with blood ethanol concentrations of 50-150 mg/dl. Response patterns exhibit characteristic features of operant behavior, including extinction when ethanol is removed and reinstatement following stress or drug priming. This methodology provides a translational platform for screening potential pharmacotherapies for alcohol use disorders and investigating the neural circuitry mediating ethanol's reinforcing properties, offering significant advantages over passive administration models in terms of face and predictive validity.

title: Antipsychotic drugs: evolving mechanisms of action with improved therapeutic benefits.
abstract: The introduction of atypical antipsychotic agents has revolutionized the treatment of schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, offering enhanced efficacy with reduced extrapyramidal side effects compared to conventional dopamine D2 receptor antagonists. This review examines the evolving understanding of antipsychotic mechanisms and their therapeutic implications. We analyzed preclinical and clinical data from 1995-2003 regarding receptor binding profiles, neurotransmitter interactions, and clinical outcomes of second-generation antipsychotics including clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, and ziprasidone. Atypical antipsychotics demonstrate complex pharmacological profiles involving serotonin 5-HT2A/2C antagonism, variable dopamine D2 occupancy, and interactions with histaminergic, adrenergic, and cholinergic systems. The 5-HT2A/D2 receptor occupancy ratio appears critical for the atypical profile, with optimal therapeutic windows of 65-80% 5-HT2A and 60-80% D2 occupancy. These agents show superior efficacy against negative symptoms and cognitive deficits, with reduced liability for tardive dyskinesia and hyperprolactinemia. Emerging evidence suggests modulation of glutamatergic transmission and neuroprotective effects may contribute to their therapeutic advantages. Fast dissociation from D2 receptors, as demonstrated by clozapine and quetiapine, correlates with reduced extrapyramidal symptoms. The evolving mechanistic understanding of atypical antipsychotics reveals multireceptor targeting as key to their improved therapeutic profile. Future drug development should focus on optimizing receptor selectivity patterns and exploring novel targets including metabotropic glutamate and nicotinic receptors to further enhance treatment outcomes in psychotic disorders.
journal: Current drug targets. CNS and neurological disorders

title: Neurophamacology of yawning.
abstract: Yawning is a common behavioral event that is observed in humans, as well as other mammals, birds and reptiles. In humans, yawning often occurs just before bed and upon waking up, and is also associated with tedious or boring situations. Although the physiologic roles of yawning have yet to be fully elucidated, the past 50 years of research has led to a much greater understanding of the neuropharmacologic regulation of yawning. While many of the early studies concluded that yawning was primarily driven by changes in cholinergic neurotransmission, we now know that numerous neurotransmitters and neurohormones are involved in the mediation of yawning, including acetylcholine, dopamine, glutamate, serotonin, oxytocin, GABA, opioids, adrenergics, nitric oxide, as well as the proopiomelanocortin-derived peptides ACTH and alpha-MSH. Furthermore, antagonist interaction studies have clearly defined at least 3 distinct neural pathways involved in the induction of yawning, as well as the hierarchical order through which these different neurotransmitter systems interact to regulate yawning. The following sections will discuss the state of knowledge for each of the major neurotransmitters and neurohormones involved in the regulation of yawning, their interactions with one another, and their place in the hierarchical organization of yawning.

title: Evidence-Based Prescribing in Mental Health Nursing.
abstract: Mental health nurses increasingly assume prescriptive authority across healthcare settings, yet limited research examines the integration of evidence-based prescribing practices in psychiatric nursing. This study investigated current prescribing patterns, barriers to evidence-based practice, and factors influencing medication selection among psychiatric-mental health nurse practitioners (PMHNPs). A mixed-methods approach was employed, including a national survey of 847 PMHNPs and semi-structured interviews with 32 participants across diverse practice settings. Survey data examined prescribing patterns for major depressive disorder, anxiety disorders, and bipolar disorder, while qualitative interviews explored decision-making processes and perceived barriers to evidence-based prescribing. Results indicated that 73% of PMHNPs regularly consulted clinical practice guidelines when initiating psychotropic medications, with higher adherence rates observed in hospital-based settings compared to community practices (82% vs. 64%, p<0.001). Primary barriers identified included limited access to current literature (68%), time constraints during patient encounters (61%), and formulary restrictions (45%). Interview data revealed that PMHNPs frequently relied on clinical experience and peer consultation when evidence-based guidelines were unclear or conflicting. Participants emphasized the importance of considering psychosocial factors and patient preferences in medication selection, sometimes leading to deviations from standardized protocols. These findings highlight the need for enhanced continuing education programs, improved access to evidence-based resources, and development of practice guidelines that integrate both pharmacological evidence and psychosocial considerations specific to nursing practice. Strengthening evidence-based prescribing practices among PMHNPs may improve patient outcomes and advance the nursing profession's role in psychiatric care.
journal: Journal of psychosocial nursing and mental health services

title: Methodological optimization of applying neuroactive agents for the study of locomotor-like activity in the mudpuppies (Necturus maculatus).
abstract: We compared the effects of mode of delivery of neuroactive agents and the effects of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), a vehicle for dissolving neuroactive agents, on locomotor-like activity in vitro. By superfusion, d-glutamate (0.3-0.9 mM) produced robust walking-like activity at superfusion rates 10-25 ml/min. In contrast, bolus application of the same or higher doses of glutamate (0.1-1.5 mM) failed to induce any rhythmic activity. Superfusion with AP-5, a NMDA receptor antagonist, produced dose-dependent inhibition of the ongoing walking-like activity induced by D-glutamate and completely blocked the activity at 20 microM. In contrast, bolus application of AP-5 did not block the walking-like activity at concentrations up to 120 microM. Similarly, superfusion of AP-5 inhibited the initiation of walking-like activity and completely blocked the initiation at 20 microM, while bolus application of AP-5 failed to do so at concentrations up to 120 microM. Superfusion of strychnine, a glycine receptor antagonist, blocked the walking-like activity at concentrations of 3-5 microM, while its bolus application altered NMDA-induced, but not glutamate-induced, walking-like activity to a synchronized pattern. DMSO significantly affected the walking-like activity in a dose-dependent manner at concentrations ranging 1-10% (v/v). These results demonstrate that the way by which the neuroactive agents are applied is a significant factor that determines the outcome of experiments on the neural control of locomotion. Also, the dose-dependent effects of DMSO on the activity of neural networks for locomotion should be taken into account in data interpretation.

title: Extracting the basal extracellular dopamine concentrations from the evoked responses: re-analysis of the dopamine kinetics.
abstract: The accurate determination of basal extracellular dopamine concentrations has remained a significant challenge in neurochemical research, as conventional microdialysis and voltammetric techniques primarily measure stimulus-evoked responses rather than true baseline levels. This study presents a novel analytical approach for extracting basal dopamine concentrations from electrically-evoked dopamine transients recorded using fast-scan cyclic voltammetry in anesthetized rats. We developed a mathematical model incorporating Michaelis-Menten uptake kinetics and first-order dopamine release to deconvolute the contribution of baseline dopamine levels from the total measured signal. Dopamine responses were evoked by electrical stimulation of the medial forebrain bundle while recording in the nucleus accumbens core (n=8) and caudate-putamen (n=6). Our kinetic analysis revealed that basal extracellular dopamine concentrations were 45±8 nM in the nucleus accumbens and 62±12 nM in the caudate-putamen, values significantly higher than previous microdialysis estimates but consistent with theoretical predictions based on dopamine transporter kinetics. The model successfully predicted the relationship between stimulation parameters and peak dopamine responses (r²=0.89). Pharmacological validation using nomifensine (10 mg/kg, i.p.) confirmed model predictions, showing a 3.2-fold increase in apparent basal concentrations. This analytical framework provides a more accurate assessment of physiological dopamine levels and offers improved understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission dynamics. The method has important implications for interpreting dopamine measurements in studies of addiction, Parkinson's disease, and other neuropsychiatric conditions where baseline neurotransmitter levels are crucial for understanding pathophysiology.

title: Pharmaco fMRI: Determining the functional anatomy of the effects of medication.
abstract: Functional MRI studies have helped to elucidate underlying mechanisms in complex neurological and neuropsychiatric disorders. Disease processes often involve complex large-scale network interactions, extending beyond the presumed main disease focus. Given both the complexity of the clinical phenotype and the underlying dysfunctional brain circuits, so called pharmaco-fMRI (ph-MRI) studies probe pharmacological effects on functional neuro-anatomy, and can help to determine early treatment response, mechanisms of drug efficacy and side effects, and potentially advance CNS drug development. In this review, we discuss recent ph-MRI research in three major neuropsychiatric and neurological disorders and associated network alterations, namely selective serotonin and noradrenergic reuptake inhibitors in affective disorders and emotional processing circuits; antiepileptic drugs in epilepsy and cognitive networks; and stimulants in attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder and networks of attention control. We conclude that ph-MRI studies show consistent and reproducible changes on disease relevant networks, and prove sensitive to early pharmacological effects on functional anatomy associated with disease. Further CNS drug research and development would benefit greatly from improved disease phenotyping, or biomarkers, using advanced imaging techniques.

title: Current and Future Neuropharmacological Options for the Treatment of Essential Tremor.
abstract: Essential tremor (ET) is the most prevalent movement disorder, affecting up to 5% of individuals over 65 years of age, yet current therapeutic options remain limited and often inadequately address symptom severity. This comprehensive review examines existing neuropharmacological treatments and evaluates emerging therapeutic strategies for ET management. We conducted a systematic analysis of clinical trials, case series, and preclinical studies published between 2000-2020, focusing on mechanisms of action, efficacy profiles, and adverse event rates. Current first-line treatments include propranolol and primidone, which provide meaningful tremor reduction in approximately 60% of patients but are associated with significant side effects including cognitive impairment, fatigue, and cardiovascular complications. Second-line agents such as topiramate, gabapentin, and benzodiazepines demonstrate modest efficacy but limited tolerability. Emerging therapeutic approaches show considerable promise, including selective GABA-A receptor modulators, T-type calcium channel blockers, and novel glutamatergic modulators targeting cerebellar circuits. Perampanel, a selective AMPA receptor antagonist, demonstrated 40% tremor reduction in recent phase II trials with favorable tolerability. Additionally, precision medicine approaches utilizing genetic biomarkers and neuroimaging-guided therapy selection are being investigated to optimize treatment selection. Deep brain stimulation remains highly effective for refractory cases, though invasiveness limits widespread application. Future directions emphasize development of cerebellar-specific targets, combination therapies, and personalized treatment algorithms. These advances suggest that more effective, tolerable pharmacological options for ET management may emerge within the next decade, potentially transforming outcomes for millions of affected individuals worldwide.

title: Spontaneously hypertensive rat neuroanatomy: applications to pharmacological research.
abstract: Spontaneously hypertensive rats (SHR), which are normotensive at birth and develop sustained hypertension between 3 and 6 months of age, are the model most extensively investigated for evaluating hypertensive brain damage and its treatment. The time-dependent rise of arterial blood pressure and the occurrence of brain atrophy, loss of nerve cells and glial reaction are shared to some extent with what occurs human hypertensive brain. SHR, therefore, can represent a reasonable model of hypertension-related brain damage. Our main studies on cerebrovascular and brain microanatomical changes occurring in SHR and their sensitivity to pharmacological interventions are summarized.
journal: Italian journal of anatomy and embryology = Archivio italiano di anatomia ed embriologia

title: Drug discovery in neurodegenerative diseases.
abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases represent a growing public health challenge as populations age worldwide, yet therapeutic options remain severely limited. Current treatments for Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and related disorders provide only symptomatic relief without addressing underlying pathogenic mechanisms. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of drug discovery approaches in neurodegeneration, examining both traditional and emerging strategies employed between 1995-2004. We reviewed 347 clinical trials from ClinicalTrials.gov and analyzed preclinical research trends through systematic literature searches of major databases. Our analysis reveals that conventional single-target approaches have yielded disappointing results, with over 85% of Phase II/III trials failing to meet primary endpoints. Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors remain the primary approved therapeutics for Alzheimer's disease, while dopamine replacement therapy continues to dominate Parkinson's treatment. However, emerging strategies show promise, including disease-modifying approaches targeting protein aggregation, neuroinflammation, and oxidative stress. Novel drug delivery systems, particularly those addressing blood-brain barrier penetration, have increased CNS bioavailability by 3-8 fold in preclinical models. High-throughput screening of natural products and combinatorial libraries has identified several lead compounds currently advancing to clinical trials. Notably, multi-target drug design and systems biology approaches are gaining traction, recognizing the complex, interconnected nature of neurodegenerative pathology. These findings suggest that future therapeutic breakthroughs will likely emerge from innovative approaches that address disease complexity rather than single molecular targets, emphasizing the critical need for continued investment in translational neuropharmacology research.
journal: Science of aging knowledge environment : SAGE KE

title: Neuropharmacological effects of d-pinitol and its possible mechanisms of action.
abstract: The aim was to evaluate the diuretic and neuropharmacological actions of d-pinitol and describe a possible mechanism of action. The diuretic effects of d-pinitol were evaluated using mice placed in metabolic cages. The sedative, anxiolytic-like, antidepressant-like, and anticonvulsant effects of 1-100 mg/kg d-pinitol were assessed. The possible mechanisms of action of the anxiolytic-like, antidepressant-like, and anticonvulsant effects of d-pinitol were evaluated using inhibitors. d-pinitol lacked diuretic effects. However, d-pinitol showed the highest anxiolytic-like actions (ED50 = 70 mg/kg p.o. in mice) in the cylinder exploratory test and the highest antidepressant-like activity in the forced swimming test (ED50 = 26 mg/kg p.o. in mice). d-pinitol (100 mg/kg) exerted anticonvulsant actions in the pentylenetetrazole-induced seizures test. The pre-treatment with 2 mg/kg flumazenil reverted the anxiolytic-like actions and the anticonvulsant effects of d-pinitol, whereas the pre-treatment with 1 mg/kg yohimbine and 0.05 mg/kg prazosin abolished the antidepressant effects of d-pinitol. PRACTICAL APPLICATIONS: d-pinitol (3-O-methyl-d-chiro-inositol) is a polyol found in many fruits, as well as in many members of the Leguminosae and Fabaceae families. The results propose that this compound could contribute in the treatment of anxiety, depression, and convulsions. The findings suggest the possible participation of the GABAergic system in the anxiolytic-like and anticonvulsant actions of d-pinitol, whereas the noradrenergic system is probably involved in the antidepressant effects of d-pinitol. This study provides new information about other pharmacological uses for d-pinitol.

title: Toxins as tools: Fingerprinting neuronal pharmacology.
abstract: Toxins have been used as tools for decades to study the structure and function of neuronal ion channels and receptors. The biological origin of these toxins varies from single cell organisms, including bacteria and algae, to complex multicellular organisms, including a wide variety of plants and venomous animals. Toxins are a structurally and functionally diverse group of compounds that often modulate neuronal function by interacting with an ion channel or receptor. Many of these toxins display high affinity and exquisite selectivity, making them valuable tools to probe the structure and function of neuronal ion channels and receptors. This review article provides an overview of the experimental techniques used to assess the effects that toxins have on neuronal function, as well as discussion on toxins that have been used as tools, with a focus on toxins that target voltage-gated and ligand-gated ion channels.

title: ISOLATION OF NOVEL PARA-PENTYL PHENYL BENZOATE FROM MONDIA WHITEI. (HOOK.F.) SKEELS (PERIPLOCACEAE), ITS STRUCTURE, SYNTHESIS AND NEUROPHARMACOLOGICAL EVALUATION.
abstract: Mondia whitei L. (Hook. F.) Skeels (Periplocaceae) is a medicinal plant used locally in managing pain, fever, loss of appetite and as aphrodiasc in the South-Western states of Nigeria. However, the fruit is consumed habitually in the South-Eastern states of Nigeria, leading to speculation that it may possess some central nervous system effect but which has not been scientifically investigated, hence this study. Fresh fruits of Mondia whitei were collected and identified by a taxonomist. They were chopped into small pieces and extracted with absolute ethanol. The crude extract was subjected to various chromatographic techniques to isolate a novel compound whose structure was elucidated from the analysis of the crystal data and by extensive use of spectroscopy. The structure was confirmed by synthesis. The compound was subjected to anxiolytic and sedative activity assay. Computational analysis of the receptor binding event of isolated compound at the gamma amino butyric acid A receptor was also evaluated. The structure of the compound was elucidated as para pentyl phenyl benzoate. The neuropharmacological evaluation of the compound indicated significant (p<0.05) depression of the central nervous system. The binding characteristics of the compound to gamma amino butyric acid A receptors appears to be more favorable than those obtained for gamma amino butyric acid, chlorpromazine, benzamidine, and is comparable with the affinity obtained for pentobarbitone and diazepam. These present data provide evidence for the role of para pentyl phenyl benzoate in the habitual consumption of the fruit as well as its central nervous system activities.
journal: African journal of traditional, complementary, and alternative medicines : AJTCAM

title: An improved method for analyzing coenzyme Q homologues and multiple detection of rare biological samples.
abstract: Coenzyme Q homologues play critical roles in mitochondrial electron transport and cellular bioenergetics, yet their precise quantification in rare neurobiological samples remains technically challenging. Existing analytical methods suffer from insufficient sensitivity, poor resolution of structural homologues, and require large sample volumes incompatible with limited tissue specimens. We developed an improved high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) method for simultaneous detection and quantification of coenzyme Q6, Q9, and Q10 homologues in small biological samples. The method employs a novel solid-phase extraction protocol using C18 cartridges followed by reverse-phase chromatography with electrospray ionization. Sample preparation was optimized for volumes as small as 50 μL of cerebrospinal fluid or 5 mg of brain tissue. Multiple reaction monitoring transitions were established for each homologue, with coenzyme Q4 serving as internal standard. The method demonstrated excellent linearity (r² > 0.995) across physiologically relevant concentration ranges, with detection limits of 0.1-0.5 ng/mL for all homologues. Intra- and inter-day precision were <8% and <12% respectively. Recovery rates exceeded 85% for all analytes in plasma, CSF, and brain tissue matrices. Application to post-mortem human brain samples from Parkinson's disease patients (n=12) revealed significant reductions in CoQ10 levels compared to age-matched controls (n=12, p<0.01). This sensitive, selective method enables comprehensive coenzyme Q profiling in precious neurobiological specimens, facilitating investigation of mitochondrial dysfunction in neurodegenerative diseases and other neurological conditions where sample availability is limited.

title: Determination of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity in the same population of cultured cells.
abstract: Cholinergic neurons are a major constituent of the mammalian central nervous system. Acetylcholine, the neurotransmitter used by cholinergic neurons, is synthesized from choline and acetyl CoA by the enzymatic action of choline acetyltransferase (ChAT). The transport of choline into the cholinergic neurons, which results in synthesis of ACh, is hemicholinium-sensitive and is referred to as high-affinity choline uptake (HACU). Thus, the formation of acetylcholine in cholinergic neurons largely depends on both the levels of choline being transported into the cells from the extracellular space and the activity of ChAT. Several methods were described previously to measure HACU and ChAT simultaneously in synaptosomes, but the same for cultured cells is lacking. We describe a procedure to measure HACU and ChAT at the same time in cultured cells by simple techniques employing radionuclides. In this procedure, we determined quantitatively hemicholinium-sensitive choline uptake and ChAT enzyme activity in a small number of differentiated human neuroblastoma (SK-N-SH) cells. We also determined the kinetics of choline uptake in the SK-N-SH cells. We believe that these simple methods can be used for neurochemical and drug discovery studies in several models of neurodegenerative disorders including Alzheimer's disease.

title: Anxiolytics antagonize yohimbine-induced central noradrenergic activity: a concomitant in vivo voltammetry-electrophysiology model of anxiety.
abstract: Yohimbine-induced increases in central noradrenergic activity have been extensively used to model anxiety-related behaviors, yet simultaneous real-time assessment of neurochemical and electrophysiological responses remains technically challenging. We developed a novel dual-modality approach combining in vivo voltammetry with single-unit electrophysiology to examine anxiolytic drug effects on yohimbine-evoked noradrenergic responses in the locus coeruleus (LC) of anesthetized rats. Carbon fiber microelectrodes were positioned adjacent to tungsten recording electrodes in the LC, allowing concurrent measurement of norepinephrine release and LC neuronal firing rates. Yohimbine (2 mg/kg, i.v.) produced rapid, dose-dependent increases in both norepinephrine efflux (284 ± 41% of baseline) and LC discharge frequency (198 ± 23% of baseline) within 5-10 minutes. Pretreatment with diazepam (2-8 mg/kg, i.p.) significantly attenuated yohimbine-induced norepinephrine release in a dose-dependent manner, with maximal inhibition of 67 ± 12% at the highest dose. Similarly, buspirone (1-4 mg/kg, i.p.) reduced both neurochemical and electrophysiological responses to yohimbine by 45-62%. The temporal correlation between voltammetric and electrophysiological measurements was highly significant (r = 0.84, p < 0.001), validating the integrated approach. Cross-correlation analysis revealed that changes in firing rate preceded peak norepinephrine release by 2.3 ± 0.7 seconds. This methodology provides a powerful tool for real-time assessment of anxiolytic drug mechanisms and offers enhanced temporal resolution for studying noradrenergic contributions to anxiety-related neural circuits.

title: The 20th scientific conference of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology.
abstract: The 20th conference of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology will be held March 26-29, 2014. It features the latest in research examining the intersection of neuroscience, immunology and pharmacology, relevant for human health and disease. Particular emphases are placed on HIV and other infectious diseases, and abused substances, including illicit drugs and alcohol.
journal: Journal of neuroimmune pharmacology : the official journal of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology

title: European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) - 23rd Congress.
abstract: The 23rd Congress of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) convened in Amsterdam from August 28-September 1, 2010, bringing together over 5,000 researchers, clinicians, and industry professionals to discuss advances in neuropsychopharmacology and drug development. This comprehensive review analyzes key presentations and symposia focusing on emerging therapeutic targets and novel compounds in various stages of clinical development. The congress featured 847 scientific presentations, including 156 symposia, 98 poster sessions, and 45 industry-sponsored satellite meetings covering major neuropsychiatric disorders. Significant attention was devoted to innovative mechanisms of action in depression treatment, including triple monoamine reuptake inhibitors and glutamatergic modulators such as ketamine analogs. Notable developments in schizophrenia therapeutics highlighted partial dopamine agonists and phosphodiesterase inhibitors showing promise in Phase II trials. Addiction medicine sessions emphasized novel approaches targeting nicotinic acetylcholine receptors and GABA-B modulators. Alzheimer's disease research showcased gamma-secretase modulators and tau protein aggregation inhibitors, though several high-profile compounds reported disappointing Phase III results. Neuroinflammation emerged as a critical therapeutic target across multiple disorders, with microglial activation inhibitors generating considerable interest. The congress demonstrated robust pipeline activity in neuropsychopharmacology, with over 200 investigational compounds discussed across various development phases. However, the high attrition rates in late-stage clinical trials underscored the continued challenges in translating preclinical findings to clinical efficacy. These findings highlight both the promising therapeutic landscape and the persistent hurdles in developing effective neuropsychiatric medications.

title: Neuropharmacology of performance monitoring.
abstract: Adaptive, goal-directed behavior requires that organisms evaluate their actions in terms of their outcomes. Neuroimaging studies show that unfavorable outcomes or situations with high level of conflict engage the posterior medial frontal cortex (pMFC). Recording of event-related potentials revealed that these situations are accompanied by a negative deflection, the so-called error-related negativity (ERN), which appears after an erroneous response or after negative feedback. Both activation of the pMFC and the ERN are thought to represent a signal that indicates the need for behavioral adjustment, and to recruit other brain regions that implement these adjustments.
