While many fMRI and EEG studies have shed light on the anatomical structures and the cognitive processes involved in performance monitoring, only very recently have researchers begun to investigate the underlying neurochemical mechanisms. Drawing on the putative involvement of dopamine (DA) neurons in coding a reward prediction error, an influential theory has ascribed a pivotal role to DA in performance monitoring. However, although important, DA is certainly not the only neuromodulator involved. Recent studies point to a role for serotonin, norepinephrine and GABA, but also for adenosine in performance monitoring. Here, we review the evidence for neurotransmitter effects on this function in humans. In this light, we critically discuss currently debated models of performance monitoring and potential alternatives.

title: Validation of a robust and sensitive method for detecting hydroxyl radical formation together with evoked neurotransmitter release in brain microdialysis.
abstract: The simultaneous detection of hydroxyl radical formation and neurotransmitter release in vivo remains technically challenging due to the short half-life of hydroxyl radicals and the requirement for sensitive analytical methods. We developed and validated a microdialysis approach combining salicylate trapping with high-performance liquid chromatography-electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) to concurrently measure hydroxyl radical production and dopamine release in the rat striatum. Male Spritz-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with microdialysis probes perfused with artificial cerebrospinal fluid containing 2.0 mM salicylate. Hydroxyl radical formation was quantified by measuring 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2,3-DHBA), the hydroxylated product of salicylate, while dopamine was simultaneously analyzed using HPLC-ECD. Method validation demonstrated linear responses for both 2,3-DHBA (r²=0.998, range 0.5-50 nM) and dopamine (r²=0.995, range 1-100 nM) with detection limits of 0.2 nM and 0.5 nM, respectively. Intraday and interday coefficients of variation were <8% for both analytes. Potassium-evoked depolarization (100 mM KCl) increased dopamine release by 450±65% above baseline while simultaneously elevating 2,3-DHBA formation by 180±25%. Iron-induced oxidative stress (10 μM FeCl₂) produced a 300±40% increase in hydroxyl radical formation with minimal effect on basal dopamine levels. The method demonstrated excellent temporal resolution (5-minute sampling) and reproducibility across multiple experimental sessions. This validated approach provides a robust tool for investigating the relationship between oxidative stress and neurotransmission in neurodegeneration models and therapeutic interventions.

title: The FGIN period: electrophysiological studies.
abstract: Recent investigations into FGIN-1-27 (N,N-dihexyl-2-(4-fluorophenyl)indole-3-acetamide), a synthetic ligand with high affinity for the peripheral benzodiazepine receptor, have revealed intriguing neurosteroidogenic properties that may influence neuronal excitability. However, the acute electrophysiological effects of FGIN-1-27 on central nervous system neurons remain poorly characterized. We investigated the direct electrophysiological actions of FGIN-1-27 on hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings in acute rat brain slices. FGIN-1-27 (1-100 μM) produced concentration-dependent effects on neuronal membrane properties, including a significant hyperpolarization of resting membrane potential (-2.8 ± 0.4 mV at 10 μM, p<0.01) and reduction in input resistance (18.3 ± 3.2% decrease, p<0.05). Action potential threshold was shifted to more depolarized potentials (+3.6 ± 0.8 mV at 50 μM), while action potential amplitude and half-width remained unchanged. FGIN-1-27 also enhanced the medium afterhyperpolarization following action potentials, suggesting modulation of calcium-activated potassium channels. These effects were partially reversed by the neurosteroid synthesis inhibitor finasteride (50 μM) but were insensitive to the GABAA receptor antagonist bicuculline (10 μM), indicating both neurosteroid-dependent and independent mechanisms. Field potential recordings demonstrated that FGIN-1-27 reduced population spike amplitude and delayed peak latency in a concentration-dependent manner. These findings suggest that FGIN-1-27 exerts complex modulatory effects on hippocampal neuronal excitability through multiple mechanisms, providing new insights into peripheral benzodiazepine receptor ligand actions in the central nervous system.

title: Addiction: decreased reward sensitivity and increased expectation sensitivity conspire to overwhelm the brain's control circuit.
abstract: The neurobiological basis of addiction involves complex alterations in reward processing and cognitive control systems that perpetuate compulsive drug-seeking behavior despite negative consequences. While previous research has focused on individual components of addiction pathology, the integrative mechanisms by which reward dysfunction and enhanced drug expectancy jointly compromise prefrontal control remain poorly understood. We propose a novel framework wherein addiction emerges from the convergence of two key neuroadaptations: diminished sensitivity to natural rewards in the ventral striatum and heightened expectation-driven responses in the anterior cingulate and orbitofrontal cortex. Using functional magnetic resonance imaging and pharmacological challenge studies in cocaine-dependent individuals (n=45) and matched controls (n=38), we demonstrate that blunted dopaminergic signaling in reward circuits correlates with enhanced anticipatory responses to drug-related cues. Critically, these opposing neuroadaptations create an imbalanced state where weakened reward processing reduces the motivational salience of adaptive behaviors while amplified expectancy responses increasingly monopolize limited cognitive control resources in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex. Computational modeling reveals that this dual dysfunction creates a positive feedback loop, progressively eroding the capacity for executive override of drug-seeking impulses. Our findings suggest that effective addiction treatments must simultaneously address reward deficits and expectation sensitization rather than targeting either component in isolation. This mechanistic insight provides a neurobiological foundation for combination therapies that restore reward sensitivity while dampening cue reactivity to rebalance the brain's control architecture.
journal: BioEssays : news and reviews in molecular, cellular and developmental biology

title: A history of the Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (1957-2004).
abstract: The Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) was established in 1957 as the premier international scientific organization dedicated to advancing research in neuropsychopharmacology and biological psychiatry. This historical analysis examines the evolution, scientific contributions, and global impact of CINP during its first 47 years (1957-2004). Comprehensive archival research was conducted using CINP congress proceedings, institutional records, published abstracts, and interviews with founding members and past presidents. Meeting attendance records, publication databases, and scientific program analyses were systematically reviewed to assess the organization's growth and scientific output. From its founding congress in Rome with 85 participants, CINP expanded to over 2,400 members representing 65 countries by 2004. The organization convened 14 biennial world congresses during this period, with total attendance increasing from 85 to over 3,000 participants. Analysis of congress programs revealed a progressive shift from early psychoactive drug discovery (1960s) to molecular mechanisms and neurotransmitter systems (1970s-1980s), followed by integration of neuroimaging, genetics, and evidence-based therapeutics (1990s-2004). CINP fostered international collaboration through standardized research methodologies, consensus statements on psychotropic drug classification, and establishment of regional divisions in Europe, Asia-Pacific, and the Americas. The organization played a crucial role in legitimizing neuropsychopharmacology as a distinct scientific discipline, facilitating knowledge transfer between basic and clinical research, and promoting evidence-based approaches to psychiatric treatment during the foundational decades of modern biological psychiatry.

title: Role of GIRK channels on the noradrenergic transmission in vivo: an electrophysiological and neurochemical study on GIRK2 mutant mice.
abstract: G-protein-activated inwardly rectifying potassium (GIRK) channels mediate the inhibitory effects of various neurotransmitters and are abundantly expressed in noradrenergic neurons of the locus coeruleus. The GIRK2 subunit is essential for functional GIRK channel assembly, yet its specific role in noradrenergic transmission remains poorly understood. Here we investigated the contribution of GIRK2 channels to norepinephrine (NE) signaling using GIRK2 knockout mice (Girk2−/−) through combined electrophysiological and neurochemical approaches. In vivo single-unit recordings from locus coeruleus neurons revealed significantly increased spontaneous firing rates in Girk2−/− mice compared to wild-type controls (4.2 ± 0.3 Hz vs 2.8 ± 0.2 Hz, p<0.01). The α2-adrenergic agonist clonidine produced markedly reduced inhibition of firing in knockout animals (23% vs 71% reduction in wild-type, p<0.001). Microdialysis measurements in the prefrontal cortex showed elevated baseline NE levels in Girk2−/− mice (245 ± 18% of wild-type values) and enhanced NE release following acute stress exposure. High-performance liquid chromatography analysis revealed increased NE content in cortical and hippocampal tissues from knockout animals. Behaviorally, Girk2−/− mice exhibited hyperactivity and reduced anxiety-like behaviors consistent with enhanced noradrenergic tone. Western blot analysis confirmed the absence of GIRK2 protein in knockout tissue while showing compensatory increases in GIRK1 and GIRK3 subunit expression. These findings demonstrate that GIRK2-containing channels are critical regulators of noradrenergic neuron excitability and NE release, providing important insights into the molecular mechanisms controlling central noradrenergic transmission and its behavioral consequences.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of an atypical antipsychotic, NRA0562.
abstract: The development of atypical antipsychotics with improved efficacy and reduced extrapyramidal side effects remains a critical goal in schizophrenia treatment. NRA0562, a novel benzisoxazole derivative, was characterized for its neuropharmacological properties and compared to risperidone and haloperidol. Radioligand binding assays were performed using rat brain membranes to determine receptor affinities. In vivo studies employed conditioned avoidance response (CAR), catalepsy, and apomorphine-induced stereotypy paradigms in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Microdialysis was used to measure dopamine and serotonin levels in the prefrontal cortex and striatum following acute administration. NRA0562 demonstrated high affinity for dopamine D2 (Ki = 2.1 nM) and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors (Ki = 0.8 nM) with a 5-HT2A/D2 ratio of 0.38. The compound showed moderate affinity for α1-adrenergic (Ki = 15 nM) and histamine H1 receptors (Ki = 45 nM), with minimal binding to muscarinic receptors. In behavioral studies, NRA0562 effectively blocked CAR (ED50 = 0.25 mg/kg) and apomorphine-induced stereotypy (ED50 = 0.18 mg/kg) while producing minimal catalepsy at therapeutic doses, yielding a favorable separation index of 12.8 compared to haloperidol (2.1). Microdialysis revealed preferential enhancement of cortical dopamine release over striatal dopamine, consistent with atypical antipsychotic properties. These findings suggest NRA0562 possesses a promising neuropharmacological profile with potential for treating positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia while minimizing motor side effects, warranting further clinical investigation.

title: Stimulating the development of drug treatments to improve cognition in schizophrenia.
abstract: Cognitive impairment in schizophrenia is a core feature of the illness (i.e., not a result of clinical symptoms or drug treatments) that is related to the daily functioning of patients. Because schizophrenia is associated with poor community functioning, there is considerable interest in finding treatments to improve cognition in schizophrenia in the hopes that such improvement will yield functional benefits. Before the U.S. Food and Drug Administration could consider granting approval to any new drug for improving cognition in schizophrenia, it was first necessary to achieve consensus on the measurements and methods that would be used in clinical trials, as well as neuropharmacological targets. The U.S. National Institute of Mental Health launched an initiative to help address these obstacles to drug approval (MATRICS). This initiative has generated several additional follow-up initiatives including a clinical trial network and consensus projects for other clinical targets, such as negative symptoms. This review describes how an area that was primarily of academic interest (cognition in schizophrenia) became a focus of public health concerns and drug-development policy.

title: Consensus Guidelines for Therapeutic Drug Monitoring in Neuropsychopharmacology: Update 2017.
abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is the quantification and interpretation of drug concentrations in blood to optimize pharmacotherapy. It considers the interindividual variability of pharmacokinetics and thus enables personalized pharmacotherapy. In psychiatry and neurology, patient populations that may particularly benefit from TDM are children and adolescents, pregnant women, elderly patients, individuals with intellectual disabilities, patients with substance abuse disorders, forensic psychiatric patients or patients with known or suspected pharmacokinetic abnormalities. Non-response at therapeutic doses, uncertain drug adherence, suboptimal tolerability, or pharmacokinetic drug-drug interactions are typical indications for TDM. However, the potential benefits of TDM to optimize pharmacotherapy can only be obtained if the method is adequately integrated in the clinical treatment process. To supply treating physicians and laboratories with valid information on TDM, the TDM task force of the Arbeitsgemeinschaft für Neuropsychopharmakologie und Pharmakopsychiatrie (AGNP) issued their first guidelines for TDM in psychiatry in 2004. After an update in 2011, it was time for the next update. Following the new guidelines holds the potential to improve neuropsychopharmacotherapy, accelerate the recovery of many patients, and reduce health care costs.

title: Mass spectrometry imaging, an emerging technology in neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: Mass spectrometry imaging is a powerful tool for directly determining the distribution of proteins, peptides, lipids, neurotransmitters, metabolites and drugs in neural tissue sections in situ. Molecule-specific imaging can be achieved using various ionization techniques that are suited to different applications but which all yield data with high mass accuracies and spatial resolutions. The ability to simultaneously obtain images showing the distributions of chemical species ranging from metal ions to macromolecules makes it possible to explore the chemical organization of a sample and to correlate the results obtained with specific anatomical features. The imaging of biomolecules has provided new insights into multiple neurological diseases, including Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease. Mass spectrometry imaging can also be used in conjunction with other imaging techniques in order to identify correlations between changes in the distribution of important chemical species and other changes in the properties of the tissue. Here we review the applications of mass spectrometry imaging in neuroscience research and discuss its potential. The results presented demonstrate that mass spectrometry imaging is a useful experimental method with diverse applications in neuroscience.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Neuropharmacology of drugs and alcohol in mother and fetus.
abstract: Maternal substance use during pregnancy poses significant risks to fetal neurodevelopment through direct pharmacological effects and altered intrauterine environment. This review examines the neuropharmacological mechanisms by which commonly abused substances cross the placental barrier and affect the developing central nervous system. We analyzed current literature on placental transfer kinetics, fetal drug metabolism, and neurodevelopmental consequences of prenatal exposure to alcohol, cocaine, opioids, nicotine, and cannabis. Systematic review of clinical studies and animal models published between 1990-2006 was conducted using MEDLINE and EMBASE databases. Alcohol readily crosses the placenta and disrupts neuronal migration, synaptogenesis, and myelination, particularly affecting the developing hippocampus and cortex through NMDA receptor antagonism and GABA receptor enhancement. Cocaine blocks dopamine reuptake in fetal brain regions, leading to altered neurotransmitter development and increased risk of cerebral infarction. Opioids cross the placenta efficiently, causing fetal physical dependence and affecting endogenous opioid systems crucial for stress response and behavioral regulation. Nicotine exposure alters acetylcholine receptor development, affecting attention and learning pathways. Cannabis compounds accumulate in fetal brain tissue, disrupting endocannabinoid signaling essential for neural connectivity. Fetal pharmacokinetics differ significantly from maternal due to immature hepatic metabolism and blood-brain barrier properties. These findings emphasize that maternal substance use during pregnancy results in direct fetal neuropharmacological effects that can lead to long-term cognitive, behavioral, and developmental impairments, highlighting the critical need for early intervention and prevention strategies.

title: Psychopharmacology of tic disorders in children and adolescents.
abstract: Tic disorders, including Tourette syndrome, affect 1-3% of school-age children and can significantly impair academic performance, social functioning, and quality of life. While behavioral interventions remain first-line treatment for mild cases, pharmacological intervention is often necessary for moderate to severe tics or when comorbid conditions are present. This comprehensive review examines current evidence for psychopharmacological treatments of tic disorders in pediatric populations. We conducted a systematic literature review of randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series published between 2000-2011, focusing on efficacy, tolerability, and safety profiles of anti-tic medications in children and adolescents. Traditional antipsychotics, particularly haloperidol and pimozide, demonstrate robust efficacy with 70-80% of patients showing significant tic reduction, but are limited by extrapyramidal side effects and potential cardiac complications. Atypical antipsychotics, including risperidone, aripiprazole, and ziprasidone, show comparable efficacy with improved tolerability profiles, though weight gain and metabolic effects remain concerning. Alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, guanfacine) provide moderate tic suppression with favorable safety profiles, making them particularly suitable for children with comorbid ADHD. Emerging treatments including topiramate, baclofen, and botulinum toxin injections show promise in preliminary studies. Treatment selection should be individualized based on tic severity, comorbid conditions, family preferences, and side effect tolerability. Future research should focus on head-to-head comparisons, long-term outcomes, and identification of predictive biomarkers to optimize treatment selection in pediatric tic disorders.

title: Neuroimmune Pharmacology, 2nd Edition - A Perspective.
abstract: The field of neuroimmune pharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation since its inception, evolving from a niche area of investigation to a cornerstone of modern neuroscience and drug discovery. This perspective examines the major advances in neuroimmune pharmacology over the past decade, highlighting key therapeutic targets and emerging concepts that have reshaped our understanding of brain-immune interactions. We conducted a comprehensive review of literature from 2007-2017, analyzing breakthrough discoveries in microglial pharmacology, neuroinflammation modulators, and immune-to-brain signaling pathways. Our analysis reveals that the identification of microglial phenotypic switching, the characterization of resolution-promoting lipid mediators, and the discovery of glymphatic system dysfunction have fundamentally altered therapeutic approaches to neurological disorders. Notable advances include the development of CSF1R antagonists for microglial depletion, specialized pro-resolving mediators for neuroinflammation resolution, and blood-brain barrier modulators for enhanced drug delivery. Clinical translation has accelerated, with over 15 neuroimmune-targeted therapeutics entering Phase II/III trials for conditions ranging from Alzheimer's disease to major depression. The integration of systems pharmacology approaches and biomarker-guided patient stratification has enhanced drug development efficiency. These developments underscore the maturation of neuroimmune pharmacology as a distinct discipline with immense therapeutic potential. Future directions point toward precision medicine approaches, combination therapies targeting multiple neuroimmune pathways, and the exploration of peripheral immune modulation for central nervous system disorders. The field stands poised to deliver transformative treatments for previously intractable neurological and psychiatric conditions.
journal: Journal of neuroimmune pharmacology : the official journal of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology

title: Drugs used to treat Parkinson's disease, present status and future directions.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) affects approximately 1% of individuals over 60 years of age and represents one of the most prevalent neurodegenerative disorders worldwide. Current pharmacological interventions primarily target the dopaminergic system to compensate for progressive loss of substantia nigra neurons, yet significant limitations persist regarding long-term efficacy and motor complications. This comprehensive review examined existing therapeutic approaches and emerging treatment strategies through systematic analysis of clinical trials, preclinical studies, and epidemiological data published between 1990-2008. We evaluated the efficacy, safety profiles, and mechanisms of action for established medications including levodopa, dopamine agonists, monoamine oxidase-B inhibitors, and catechol-O-methyltransferase inhibitors. Additionally, we assessed novel therapeutic targets including adenosine A2A receptor antagonists, glutamate modulators, and neuroprotective agents currently in clinical development. Our analysis reveals that while levodopa remains the gold standard for symptomatic relief, its long-term use is associated with motor fluctuations and dyskinesias in up to 50% of patients within 5-10 years of treatment. Dopamine agonists demonstrate comparable efficacy with reduced dyskinesia risk but increased psychiatric side effects. Emerging non-dopaminergic approaches show promise for addressing motor symptoms while potentially offering neuroprotective benefits. Future therapeutic directions should focus on combination strategies targeting multiple pathways, personalized medicine approaches based on genetic profiling, and disease-modifying treatments that address underlying neurodegeneration rather than solely providing symptomatic relief. Integration of these advances may significantly improve long-term outcomes for PD patients.

title: In-vivo brain dialysis of neurotransmitters.
abstract: In-vivo brain microdialysis has emerged as a powerful technique for monitoring extracellular neurotransmitter concentrations in the brains of freely-moving animals. This method represents a significant advancement over traditional approaches such as brain homogenization and push-pull perfusion, offering improved spatial resolution and reduced tissue damage. We implanted concentric dialysis probes (2 mm membrane length, 20,000 Da cutoff) into the striatum and nucleus accumbens of male Sprague-Dawley rats and perfused artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 2 μL/min. Dialysate samples were collected at 20-minute intervals and analyzed for dopamine, serotonin, and their metabolites using high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection. Basal extracellular dopamine concentrations averaged 8.2 ± 1.4 nM in striatum and 4.6 ± 0.8 nM in nucleus accumbens. Administration of amphetamine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) produced a 15-fold increase in striatal dopamine within 40 minutes, while haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg, i.p.) elevated dopamine metabolite levels by 300% over 2 hours. Local perfusion of high-potassium solution (100 mM KCl) through the probe increased dopamine release 8-fold, confirming the neuronal origin of measured transmitters. Recovery studies using standard solutions indicated 12-15% probe efficiency for monoamines. These findings demonstrate that microdialysis provides reliable, real-time measurements of neurotransmitter dynamics with minimal behavioral disruption, establishing this technique as an invaluable tool for neuropharmacological research and drug development.

title: Astra Award Lecture. The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
abstract: The 5-HT3 receptor represents a unique member of the serotonin receptor family, functioning as a ligand-gated ion channel rather than a G-protein coupled receptor. Unlike other serotonin receptors, the 5-HT3 receptor mediates fast synaptic transmission through direct activation of cation-selective channels. This review synthesizes current knowledge of 5-HT3 receptor psychopharmacology based on radioligand binding studies, electrophysiological recordings, and behavioral pharmacology in rodent models. Selective 5-HT3 receptor antagonists, including ondansetron (GR38032F) and granisetron (BRL43694), have been instrumental in characterizing receptor function. Autoradiographic studies using [3H]GR65630 reveal high 5-HT3 receptor density in limbic structures, particularly the entorhinal cortex, amygdala, and hippocampus, with moderate expression in brainstem areas including the area postrema and nucleus tractus solitarius. Electrophysiological investigations demonstrate that 5-HT3 receptor activation produces rapid membrane depolarization through increased sodium and calcium conductance. Behavioral studies indicate that 5-HT3 receptor antagonists possess anxiolytic-like properties in conflict paradigms and attenuate ethanol consumption in animal models. Furthermore, these compounds effectively block chemotherapy-induced emesis and radiation-induced conditioned taste aversion, suggesting involvement in nausea and vomiting mechanisms. The 5-HT3 receptor appears to modulate anxiety, drug reinforcement, and emetic responses, positioning selective antagonists as promising therapeutic agents for anxiety disorders, substance abuse, and chemotherapy-induced nausea. These findings establish the 5-HT3 receptor as a crucial target for neuropsychiatric drug development.

title: Techniques: applications of the nerve-bouton preparation in neuropharmacology.
abstract: The nerve-bouton preparation has emerged as a powerful experimental model for investigating presynaptic mechanisms and neurotransmitter release dynamics in neuropharmacological research. This preparation preserves the functional integrity of nerve terminals while providing exceptional accessibility for direct pharmacological manipulation and electrophysiological recording. We present a comprehensive evaluation of methodological approaches utilizing the nerve-bouton preparation across multiple experimental paradigms. Freshly isolated nerve terminals from rat hippocampus and cortex were maintained in physiological saline and subjected to patch-clamp recording, calcium imaging, and neurotransmitter release assays. Pharmacological agents including calcium channel blockers (ω-conotoxin GVIA, nimodipine), vesicle fusion inhibitors (botulinum toxin A), and monoamine uptake blockers were applied via microperfusion systems. The preparation demonstrated robust calcium-dependent exocytosis with pharmacological profiles consistent with native synapses. Voltage-clamp recordings revealed distinct calcium channel subtypes mediating transmitter release, with N-type channels contributing 65% and P/Q-type channels 30% of total calcium influx. Real-time monitoring of vesicle dynamics using FM dyes showed differential effects of various pharmacological modulators on vesicle pool mobilization. The nerve-bouton preparation proved particularly valuable for studying presynaptic plasticity mechanisms and drug actions at concentrations difficult to achieve in intact tissue preparations. This methodology provides unprecedented resolution for dissecting molecular mechanisms of synaptic transmission and represents a significant advancement in neuropharmacological research techniques, bridging the gap between biochemical and physiological approaches to understanding synaptic function.

title: A chronically implantable, hybrid cannula-electrode device for assessing the effects of molecules on electrophysiological signals in freely behaving animals.
abstract: The study of neuropharmacological effects on neural activity in awake, behaving animals requires simultaneous drug delivery and electrophysiological recording at precise brain locations. Current methodologies often rely on separate implantation procedures or acute preparations that limit long-term studies and behavioral assessments. We developed a novel hybrid cannula-electrode device that enables chronic simultaneous microinjection and extracellular recording in freely moving rats. The device consists of a 26-gauge stainless steel guide cannula surrounding a moveable tungsten microelectrode (1-5 MΩ impedance) with a 33-gauge injection cannula positioned 0.5 mm ventral to the recording tip. Twenty-four male Sprague-Dawley rats were implanted with devices targeting the ventral tegmental area. Animals were allowed 7-10 days recovery before testing. We validated the system by examining dopaminergic responses to local apomorphine (10 μg/0.5 μL) and glutamatergic responses to NMDA (1 μg/0.5 μL) while recording single-unit and local field potential activity. Histological analysis confirmed accurate placement in 92% of implants. The device maintained stable recording properties for 4-6 weeks post-implantation, with signal-to-noise ratios comparable to standard electrodes. Drug injections produced dose-dependent changes in firing rates and spectral power without mechanical artifacts. Apomorphine decreased firing rates by 68±12% within 5 minutes, while NMDA increased activity by 156±28%. This hybrid system provides a reliable method for chronic neuropharmacological studies, offering advantages over existing techniques by combining spatial precision with long-term stability in behaving animals.

title: Neuropharmacological activity of the pericarp of Passiflora edulis flavicarpa degener: putative involvement of C-glycosylflavonoids.
abstract: Passiflora edulis has been used in traditional medicine as a sedative and to treat or prevent central disorders such as anxiety and insomnia. In this study, the central effects of the aqueous extract (AE), the butanolic fraction (BF), and the aqueous residual fraction (ARF) obtained from the pericarp of P. edulis flavicarpa were investigated in mice and the possible compounds involved in these putative neuropharmacologic effects were determined. AE, BF, and ARF increased the total time spent in the light compartment of the light:dark box, an anxiolytic-like effect, and AE also potentiated the hypnotic effects of ethyl ether, a sedative effect. The thin layer chromatography and high-performance liquid chromatography analysis indicated the predominance of C-glycosylflavonoids in these extracts and fractions, which were identified as isoorientin, vicenin-2, spinosin, and 6,8-di-C-glycosylchrysin.

title: Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia: neural substrates and psychopharmacological implications.
abstract: Affect recognition deficits represent a core feature of schizophrenia, contributing significantly to functional disability and social impairment. These deficits appear to emerge early in the illness course and persist despite symptomatic improvement with antipsychotic treatment. We conducted a comprehensive review of neuroimaging and pharmacological studies examining the neural substrates underlying facial emotion recognition deficits in schizophrenia and evaluated current therapeutic approaches. Functional neuroimaging studies consistently demonstrate hypoactivation in key limbic and paralimbic regions, including the amygdala, fusiform gyrus, and superior temporal sulcus, during emotion processing tasks in patients compared to healthy controls. Structural MRI investigations reveal gray matter reductions in these same regions, suggesting both functional and anatomical abnormalities contribute to impaired affect recognition. Post-mortem studies implicate disrupted GABAergic interneuron function and altered dopaminergic modulation within emotion-processing circuits. Current antipsychotic medications show limited efficacy in ameliorating these deficits, with typical antipsychotics potentially exacerbating emotion recognition through extrapyramidal side effects that impair facial expression processing. Atypical antipsychotics demonstrate modest improvements, possibly through enhanced serotonergic modulation and reduced motor side effects. Emerging pharmacological targets include NMDA receptor modulators, cholinesterase inhibitors, and oxytocin, which show preliminary promise in small clinical trials. Social cognitive training programs combined with pharmacological interventions may offer the most comprehensive approach. Understanding the neurobiological basis of affect recognition deficits is crucial for developing targeted interventions that address this fundamental aspect of schizophrenia pathophysiology and improve long-term functional outcomes.

title: The neuropharmacology of L-theanine(N-ethyl-L-glutamine): a possible neuroprotective and cognitive enhancing agent.
abstract: L-theanine, a unique amino acid found predominantly in tea leaves (Camellia sinensis), has garnered increasing attention for its potential neuroprotective and cognitive-enhancing properties. This study investigated the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying L-theanine's effects on neuronal function and cognitive performance. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were administered L-theanine (50, 100, or 200 mg/kg, p.o.) daily for 14 days. Cognitive function was assessed using Morris water maze and novel object recognition tasks. Neurochemical analyses included measurement of neurotransmitter levels via HPLC, oxidative stress markers, and calcium homeostasis in hippocampal tissue. Additionally, primary cortical neurons were exposed to glutamate-induced excitotoxicity with or without L-theanine pretreatment (10-100 μM). L-theanine administration significantly improved spatial memory retention and object recognition performance in a dose-dependent manner. Neurochemical analysis revealed increased GABA levels (34% increase, p<0.01) and enhanced dopamine and serotonin concentrations in treated animals. L-theanine demonstrated potent antioxidant activity, reducing malondialdehyde levels by 28% and increasing superoxide dismutase activity. In vitro studies showed that L-theanine protected neurons against glutamate toxicity by modulating intracellular calcium influx and reducing apoptotic cell death by 45%. These findings suggest that L-theanine exerts neuroprotective effects through multiple mechanisms including GABAergic modulation, antioxidant activity, and calcium homeostasis regulation. The compound's ability to cross the blood-brain barrier and enhance cognitive function without sedative effects positions L-theanine as a promising natural nootropic agent warranting further clinical investigation.

title: [New directions in GABAergic pharmacology of anxiety and depression].
abstract: New prospects in the use of drugs affecting central GABAergic system for the treatment of anxiety and depressive states are critically assessed. Both clinical (human) and experimental (animal) data on the mutual influence of various GABA-receptor modulators as well as the interaction of GABAergic system with neurohumoral mechanisms of stress are considered. Based on this analysis, new strategies in the GABAergic pharmacotherapy of anxiety and depression spectrum disorders are outlined.

title: Amino acid neurochemistry of the vertebrate retina.
abstract: The dominant neurochemicals involved in encoding sensory information are the amino acid neurotransmitters, glutamate, gamma-aminobutyrate (GABA) and glycine, which mediate fast point-to-point synaptic transmission in the retina and other parts of the central nervous system. The relative abundance of these neurochemicals and the existence of neuronal and glial uptake mechanisms as well as a plethora of receptors support the key role these neurochemicals play in shaping neural information. However, in addition to subserving neurotransmitter roles, amino acids subserve normal metabolic,cellular functions, may be precursors for other amino acids, and may also be associated with protein synthesis. Post-embedding immunocytochemistry of small molecules has allowed the characterization of multiple amino acid profiles within subpopulations of neurons in the vertebrate retina. The general theme emerging from these studies is that the retinal through pathway uses glutamate as its neurotransmitter, and the lateral elements, GABA and/or glycine. Co-localization studies using quantitative immunocytochemistry have shown that virtually all neuronal space can be accounted for by the three dominant amino acids. In addition, co-localization studies have demonstrated that there are no purely aspartate, glutamine, alanine. leucine or ornithine immunoreactive neurons and thus these amino acids are likely to act as metabolites and may sustain glutamate production through a multitude of enzymatic pathways. The mapping of multiple cellular metabolic profiles during development or in degenerating retinas has shown that amino acid neurochemistry is a sensitive marker for metabolic activity. In the degenerating retina, (RCS retina), neurochemical anomalies were evident early in development (from birth), even before photoreceptors mature at PND6-8 implying a generalized metabolic dysfunction. Identification of metabolic anomalies within subpopulation of neurons is now possible and can be used to investigate a multitude of retinal functions including amino acid metabolic and neurochemical changes secondary to external insult as well as to expand our understanding of the intricate interrelationship between neurons and glia.
journal: Progress in retinal and eye research

title: Microdialysis applications in neuroscience.
abstract: Microdialysis is a technique to monitor extracellular changes in living tissue. Substances present in the extracellular space, such as neurotransmitters and metabolites transported between cells and capillaries in the extracellular fluid (ECF), are major object. Since its introduction to the research of the nervous system, microdialysis has become a popular method for the measurements of brain chemistry and greatly affected in the fields of neuropharmacology, neuroanatomy and neurophysiology. Most of published papers using microdialysis have focused on the area of neuroscience, recently more biomedical application. In this review, we focused on cerebral microdialysis as a monitoring tool for physiologic and pathophysiologic changes in chemical processes in the brain. Then we presented the principle and various applications of cerebral microdialysis.

title: Effects of soman (pinacolyl methylphosphonofluoridate) on coronary blood flow and cardiac function in swine.
abstract: Soman, an organophosphate nerve agent, produces life-threatening toxicity through acetylcholinesterase inhibition, resulting in excessive cholinergic stimulation. While the neurological effects of soman have been extensively studied, its direct cardiovascular effects remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the acute effects of soman on coronary hemodynamics and cardiac function in anesthetized swine. Twenty-four Yorkshire swine (25-30 kg) were instrumented with electromagnetic flow probes around the left anterior descending coronary artery and left ventricular pressure catheters. Animals received either saline (n=8), low-dose soman (0.5 × LD50, n=8), or high-dose soman (1.0 × LD50, n=8) intravenously. Hemodynamic parameters were monitored continuously for 120 minutes post-exposure. Low-dose soman produced biphasic cardiovascular responses: initial increases in heart rate (142 ± 12 vs 98 ± 8 bpm, p<0.05) and coronary blood flow (185 ± 23 vs 135 ± 18 ml/min, p<0.01) at 5 minutes, followed by progressive decreases by 60 minutes. High-dose soman caused immediate severe bradycardia (45 ± 8 bpm), marked reduction in coronary flow (68 ± 15 ml/min), and decreased left ventricular contractility (dP/dt decreased 58%, p<0.001) within 15 minutes. Plasma cholinesterase activity was inhibited >85% in both soman groups. These findings demonstrate that soman produces dose-dependent direct cardiovascular toxicity characterized by impaired coronary perfusion and myocardial function, independent of respiratory compromise. These cardiac effects may contribute significantly to soman-induced mortality and should be considered in developing therapeutic countermeasures.
journal: Fundamental and applied toxicology : official journal of the Society of Toxicology

title: The neuropharmacology of sleep paralysis hallucinations: serotonin 2A activation and a novel therapeutic drug.
abstract: Sleep paralysis is a state of involuntary immobility occurring at sleep onset or offset, often accompanied by uncanny "ghost-like" hallucinations and extreme fear reactions. I provide here a neuropharmacological account for these hallucinatory experiences by evoking the role of the serotonin 2A receptor (5-HT2AR). Research has shown that 5-HT2AR activation can induce visual hallucinations, "mystical" subjective states, and out-of-body experiences (OBEs), and modulate fear circuits. Hallucinatory experiences triggered by serotonin-serotonergic ("pseudo") hallucinations, induced by hallucinogenic drugs-tend to be "dream-like" with the experiencer having insight ("meta-awareness") that he is hallucinating, unlike dopaminergic ("psychotic" and "life-like") hallucinations where such insight is lost. Indeed, hallucinatory experiences during sleep paralysis have the classic features of serotonergic hallucinations, and are strikingly similar to perceptual and subjective states induced by hallucinogenic drugs (e.g., lysergic acid diethylamide [LSD] and psilocybin), i.e., they entail visual hallucinations, mystical experiences, OBEs, and extreme fear reactions. I propose a possible mechanism whereby serotonin could be functionally implicated in generating sleep paralysis hallucinations and fear reactions through 5-HT2AR activity. Moreover, I speculate on the role of 5-HT2C receptors vis-à-vis anxiety and panic during sleep paralysis, and the orbitofrontal cortex-rich with 5-HT2A receptors-in influencing visual pathways during sleep paralysis, and, in effect, hallucinations. Finally, I propose, for the first time, a drug to target sleep paralysis hallucinations and fear reactions, namely the selective 5-HT2AR inverse agonist, pimavanserin. This account implicates gene HTR2A on chromosome 13q as the underlying cause of sleep paralysis hallucinations and could be explored using positron emission tomography.

title: Neuropharmacological basis of combining antidepressants.
abstract: The combination of antidepressants has become increasingly common in clinical practice, yet the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying potential synergistic effects remain poorly understood. We investigated the pharmacodynamic interactions between selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) and noradrenergic agents in both preclinical models and human subjects. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=72) received acute and chronic treatment with fluoxetine (10 mg/kg), desipramine (15 mg/kg), or combination therapy, followed by microdialysis measurement of extracellular monoamine concentrations in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Additionally, 45 treatment-resistant patients with major depressive disorder underwent combination therapy with escitalopram (10-20 mg/day) plus bupropion (150-300 mg/day) for 12 weeks, with serial assessments of plasma drug concentrations and Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale scores. In rats, combination treatment produced supraadditive increases in cortical norepinephrine (278% above baseline vs. 156% for desipramine alone, p<0.01) and serotonin levels (245% vs. 189% for fluoxetine alone, p<0.05) after 14 days. Human subjects showed significantly greater clinical response rates with combination therapy compared to historical SSRI monotherapy controls (67% vs. 43%, p<0.05), without evidence of pharmacokinetic interactions. These findings suggest that antidepressant combinations may enhance monoaminergic neurotransmission through complementary mechanisms involving both presynaptic reuptake inhibition and postsynaptic receptor modulation. The neurochemical synergy observed supports the clinical utility of rationally designed combination strategies in treatment-resistant depression.

title: Determination of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity in the same population of cultured cells.
abstract: Cholinergic neurons rely on high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) for acetylcholine synthesis, with choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) catalyzing the final synthetic step. While both processes are critical for cholinergic function, technical limitations have prevented simultaneous measurement in identical cell populations, potentially obscuring important regulatory relationships. We developed a novel dual-assay protocol to sequentially measure HACU and ChAT activity in the same cultured cholinergic neurons, enabling direct correlation of these functionally linked processes. Primary septal cholinergic neurons were cultured from embryonic day 18 rat brains and maintained for 7-14 days in vitro. HACU was first assessed using [³H]-choline uptake assays in the presence or absence of hemicholinium-3 to determine high-affinity transport. Following washout, cells were harvested and ChAT activity measured using standard radioenzymatic assays with [¹⁴C]-acetyl-CoA and choline substrates. Protein content was determined for normalization. The sequential protocol yielded reproducible measurements with minimal cross-contamination between assays. HACU rates correlated positively with ChAT activity (r=0.78, p<0.001) across different culture conditions and developmental time points. Chronic exposure to nerve growth factor increased both HACU (2.3-fold) and ChAT activity (2.8-fold) proportionally, while muscarinic receptor stimulation selectively enhanced HACU without affecting ChAT levels. This methodology provides a valuable tool for investigating cholinergic regulation and demonstrates coordinate but distinct control mechanisms governing choline transport and acetylcholine synthetic capacity in individual neuronal populations.

title: The pediatric psychopharmacology of autism spectrum disorder: A systematic review - Part I: The past and the present.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1 in 54 children, with increasing prevalence driving demand for evidence-based pharmacological interventions. Despite decades of research, psychopharmacological treatment remains challenging due to heterogeneous symptom presentations and limited pediatric-specific data. This systematic review synthesized the historical development and current state of pediatric psychopharmacology for ASD core symptoms and associated comorbidities. We searched PubMed, Embase, PsycINFO, and Cochrane databases from inception through December 2020 for randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series examining psychotropic medications in children and adolescents with ASD (ages 2-18 years). Studies were evaluated using PRISMA guidelines, with quality assessment performed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool. Of 2,847 identified records, 156 studies met inclusion criteria, encompassing 8,234 pediatric participants. Antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, demonstrated the strongest evidence for managing irritability and aggression, with FDA approval for pediatric ASD populations. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors showed mixed efficacy for repetitive behaviors, with significant safety concerns in younger children. Stimulants effectively addressed comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms but required careful monitoring for adverse effects. Alpha-2 agonists and anticonvulsants showed promise for specific symptom clusters but lacked robust pediatric data. Emerging treatments including oxytocin, N-acetylcysteine, and cannabinoids require further investigation. Current evidence supports limited pharmacological options for pediatric ASD, primarily targeting associated symptoms rather than core deficits. Future research must prioritize age-stratified studies, biomarker development, and personalized treatment approaches to optimize outcomes in this vulnerable population.

title: The development of an implantable catheter system for chronic or intermittent convection-enhanced delivery.
abstract: Convection-enhanced delivery (CED) is a promising technique for the administration of therapeutic agents such as cytotoxics, neurotrophins and enzymes to the brain. In this study we describe the development of an implantable catheter system that is compatible with long-term intermittent CED. Catheters made from fused silica, PEEK or carbothane, and of various internal and external diameters were implanted in the striatum of rats and assessed for patency at 21 or 28 days. A high-rate of catheter blockage was observed with all fused silica and PEEK catheters. Carbothane catheters with an outer diameter of 0.6mm and an inner diameter of 0.35 mm had significantly lower rates of blockage (P≤0.01). Carbothane catheters were then implanted into 4 Large White/Landrace pigs and 4 NIH miniature pigs and infusions undertaken at monthly intervals to evaluate catheter patency and infusate distribution. Catheter patency was demonstrated for a maximum period of 163 days in one animal. Widespread and reproducible intraputamenal CED could be achieved with intermittent drug delivery at flow-rates as high as 5 μl/min. Problems were encountered using the pig model due to catheter distortion from rapid animal growth. In conclusion, it is possible to achieve intermittent high-flow CED with a chronic implanted carbothane catheter with a low rate of catheter blockage.

title: I Believe I Can Fly!: Use of Drosophila as a Model Organism in Neuropsychopharmacology Research.
abstract: Neuropsychiatric disorders are of complex etiology, often including a large genetic component. In order to help identify and study the molecular and physiological mechanisms that such genes participate in, numerous animal models have been established in a variety of species. Over the past decade, this has increasingly included the vinegar fly, Drosophila melanogaster. Here, we outline why we study an invertebrate organism in the context of neuropsychiatric disorders, and we discuss how we can gain insight from studies in Drosophila. We focus on a few disorders and findings to make the larger point that modeling these diseases in flies can have both mechanistic and predictive validity. Highlighting some translational examples, we underline the fact that their brains works more like ours than one would have anticipated.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Applying small quantities of multiple compounds to defined locations of in vitro brain slices.
abstract: Precise application of pharmacological agents to specific brain regions in slice preparations remains a significant methodological challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional bath application techniques lack spatial resolution and consume large quantities of expensive compounds, while pressure ejection systems often produce variable results. We developed a novel microfluidic delivery system enabling simultaneous application of multiple compounds to defined locations within acute brain slices with nanoliter precision. The system utilizes computer-controlled piezoelectric micropipettes (tip diameter 2-5 μm) connected to independent reservoirs containing test solutions. Custom software allows preprogrammed delivery sequences with temporal resolution of 10 ms and spatial accuracy within 25 μm. We validated this approach using 400 μm coronal hippocampal slices from adult Wistar rats, applying GABA (100 μM), glutamate (50 μM), and bicuculline (10 μM) to CA1 pyramidal cell dendrites while recording extracellular field potentials. Drug applications produced consistent, reproducible responses with onset latencies of 45 ± 8 ms and minimal cross-contamination between application sites (n=24 slices). Compound consumption was reduced by >95% compared to bath application. The system successfully delivered up to six different solutions simultaneously to distinct slice regions without mechanical disruption of tissue architecture. This microfluidic approach provides enhanced spatial and temporal control over drug delivery in brain slice preparations, offering significant advantages for investigating local circuit pharmacology, receptor distribution, and synaptic transmission mechanisms while minimizing reagent costs and experimental variability.

title: Simple behavioral methods to assess the effect of drugs or toxins on sensory experience.
abstract: The assessment of drug and toxin effects on sensory processing remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research, yet current methodologies often require complex instrumentation or invasive procedures that may confound results. We developed and validated a battery of simple behavioral assays to evaluate sensory function across multiple modalities in laboratory rats following pharmacological manipulation. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=96) were trained on four behavioral tasks: tactile discrimination using textured surfaces, auditory startle response to pure tones, visual cliff avoidance, and olfactory preference testing with familiar scents. Following baseline establishment, animals received either vehicle control, scopolamine (0.5 mg/kg), ketamine (10 mg/kg), or lead acetate (50 mg/kg daily for 14 days). Performance was assessed at 30 minutes, 2 hours, and 24 hours post-treatment for acute compounds, and weekly for chronic lead exposure. Scopolamine significantly impaired tactile discrimination (p<0.01) and olfactory preference (p<0.05) without affecting auditory or visual responses. Ketamine selectively disrupted visual cliff performance (p<0.001) while preserving other sensory modalities. Chronic lead exposure produced dose-dependent deficits across all sensory domains by day 10. Inter-rater reliability exceeded 0.85 for all measures, and test-retest correlation was 0.79±0.12. These simple, cost-effective behavioral assays provide sensitive and specific detection of sensory dysfunction induced by diverse pharmacological agents. The methodology offers significant advantages over existing techniques through reduced stress, minimal equipment requirements, and rapid throughput, making it suitable for routine screening of potential neurotoxic compounds.

title: Microdialysis in freely moving mice: determination of acetylcholine, serotonin and noradrenaline release in galanin transgenic mice.
abstract: In the present study, we describe micro-surgical methods for simultaneous implantation of a microdialysis probe and an intraventricular injection cannula via their respective guide cannulas into the mouse brain. Basal and stimulated release of acetylcholine (ACh), serotonin (5-HT) and noradrenaline (NA) was determined in the ventral hippocampus of freely moving mice. NA and 5-HT were determined in one run by a newly developed HPLC method based on precolumn derivatization with benzylamine and fluorescence detection. The mice with a loss-of-function mutation of the galanin gene (KO) and the mice that over-expressed galanin (OE) were studied. No significant differences in basal, potassium-stimulated or scopolamine-induced extracellular ACh levels were observed in 4-month-old wild-type (WT) and KO mice. In the aged, 10-month-old animals, the basal extracellular ACh levels were significantly reduced in both WT and KO groups. Galanin (1 nmol i.c.v.) caused a significant reduction of basal extracellular NA by about 40% in both WT and galanin OE mice, however, in the latter group the effect was delayed by almost 2 h. A 10-min forced swimming stress caused a higher increase in release of NA and 5-HT in the OE group than in the corresponding WT mice. Finally, venlafaxin (10 mg/kg i.p.) increased extracellular NA to 400% of the control values in the CBA mice, but only to 250% in the C57BL mice. It is concluded that galanin may play an important role in the cholinergic mechanisms underlying cognitive disorders. Furthermore, modulation by galanin and by behavioral activation, of NA and 5-HT neurotransmission in galanin over-expressing mice indicates its possible role in the aetiology of mood disorders.

title: Glutamate-based antidepressants: preclinical psychopharmacology.
abstract: Over the past 20 years, converging lines of evidence have both linked glutamatergic dysfunction to the pathophysiology of depression and demonstrated that the glutamatergic synapse presents multiple targets for developing novel antidepressants. The robust antidepressant effects of the N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor antagonists ketamine and traxoprodil provide target validation for this family of ionotropic glutamate receptors. This article reviews the preclinical evidence that it may be possible to develop glutamate-based antidepressants by not only modulating ionotropic (N-methyl-D-aspartate and alpha-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazole-propionic acid) and metabotropic glutamate (mGlu) receptors, including mGlu2/3, mGLu5 and mGlu7 receptors, but also by altering synaptic concentrations of glutamate via specialized transporters such as glial glutamate transporter 1 (excitatory amino-acid transporter 2).

title: Past and future of neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation since its inception in the 1950s with the discovery of chlorpromazine's antipsychotic properties. This review examines the historical development of psychotropic medications and projects future directions based on emerging neuroscientific understanding. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of pharmaceutical developments from 1952-1989, reviewing clinical trials, mechanistic studies, and therapeutic outcomes across major drug classes including antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, and mood stabilizers. The evolution from serendipitous discoveries to rational drug design is traced through landmark medications: chlorpromazine and haloperidol for psychosis, imipramine and subsequent tricyclics for depression, benzodiazepines for anxiety, and lithium for bipolar disorder. Each class revealed fundamental neurotransmitter systems - dopaminergic, serotonergic, noradrenergic, and GABAergic - establishing the neurochemical basis of mental illness. Recent advances in receptor subtype characterization, second messenger systems, and brain imaging techniques now enable more targeted therapeutic approaches. Emerging areas include selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors showing improved tolerability profiles, atypical antipsychotics with reduced extrapyramidal effects, and novel anxiolytics acting beyond the benzodiazepine receptor. Future developments will likely focus on neuropeptide systems, glutamate modulation, and personalized medicine approaches based on genetic polymorphisms affecting drug metabolism. The integration of molecular biology, neuroimaging, and pharmacokinetic modeling promises more efficacious treatments with fewer adverse effects, representing a paradigm shift toward precision neuropsychopharmacology.

title: Neuropharmacologic correlates of deglutition: lessons from fictive swallowing.
abstract: Pharmacologic investigations into the transmission processes underlying fictive swallowing in the rat have disclosed the potential diversity of chemical signals used in central deglutitive pathways. Monoaminergic mechanisms appear to serve as links between subcortical structures and the medullary pattern generator of swallowing (PGS), and may play a critical role in maintaining internal facilitatory drive, required by the PGS for optimal responsivity to peripheral sensory input. Cholinergic bulbar interneurons form an integral component of the PGS subnetwork controlling esophageal peristalsis. Local GABA neurons exert a tonic inhibition of the buccopharyngeal stage, may regulate buccopharyngeal-esophageal coupling, and may contribute to peristaltic rhythmic generation at both the premotoneuronal and motoneuronal level. Receptor subtypes for excitatory amino acids (glutamate, aspartate) are differentially associated with deglutitive premotoneurons for both the buccopharyngeal and esophageal stage, as well as with ambiguus motoneurons. Preliminary evidence suggests the existence of excitatory peptidergic mechanisms involving thyrotropin-releasing hormone, vasopressin, oxytocin, and somatostatin, a probable candidate for excitatory transmitter in the solitarioambigual internuncial projection to motoneurons innervating esophageal striated musculature. Further validation of this experimental model may ultimately help to establish a framework for the clinical recognition, management, and exploitation of drug actions on central deglutitive neuroeffectors.

title: Neuropharmacology of the mesolimbic system and associated circuits on social hierarchies.
abstract: Most socially living species are organized hierarchically, primarily based on individual differences in social dominance. Dominant individuals typically gain privileged access to important resources, such as food, mating partners and territories, whereas submissive conspecifics are often devoid of such benefits. The benefits associated with a high social status provide a strong incentive to become dominant. Importantly, motivational- and reward-related processes are regulated, to a large extent, by the mesolimbic system. Consequently, several studies point to a key role for the mesolimbic system in social hierarchy formation. This review summarizes the growing body of literature that implicates the mesolimbic system, and associated neural circuits, on social hierarchies. In particular, we discuss the neurochemical and pharmacological studies that have highlighted the contributions of the mesolimbic system and associated circuits including dopamine signaling through the D1 or D2 receptors, GABAergic neurotransmission, the androgen receptor system, and mitochondria and bioenergetics. Given that low social status has been linked to the emergence of anxiety- and depressive-like disorders, a greater understanding of the neurochemistry underlying social dominance could be of tremendous benefit for the development of pharmacological treatments to dysfunctions in social behaviors. This article is part of the Special Issue entitled 'The neuropharmacology of social behavior: from bench to bedside'.

title: The stanley neuropathology consortium integrative database: a novel, web-based tool for exploring neuropathological markers in psychiatric disorders and the biological processes associated with abnormalities of those markers.
abstract: An integrative database, Stanley Neuropathology Consortium Integrative Database (SNCID) (http://sncid.stanleyresearch.org), has been developed to facilitate psychiatric research. The SNCID includes 1749 neuropathological markers measured in 12 different brain regions in 60 human subjects (15 each schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, depression, and unaffected controls). Genome-wide expression microarray datasets from three independent studies are also included. Statistical analysis tools such as variance analysis, correlation analysis, and functional annotation tools have been integrated into the database. In this report, we first replicate an earlier correlation analysis between genome-wide expression profiles and an abnormal cytoarchitectural marker using the SNCID. We then show the potential for identifying neuropathological markers that are abnormal in subjects with psychiatric disorders. We also identify biological pathways associated with several abnormal neuropathological markers, including those in the dopamine, glutamate, Reelin, and gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA)ergic systems. Data exploration using the SNCID may provide insights into the biological pathways associated with the neurotransmitter abnormalities identified in subjects with major psychiatric disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: PET Neurochemical Imaging Modes.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as the premier neuroimaging modality for investigating brain neurochemistry in vivo, offering unprecedented insights into neurotransmitter systems and their alterations in neuropsychiatric disorders. This review examines the current state and future directions of PET neurochemical imaging modes, with emphasis on methodological advances and clinical applications. We analyzed data from 127 published studies between 2010-2016 utilizing various PET radioligands targeting dopaminergic, serotonergic, GABAergic, glutamatergic, cholinergic, and noradrenergic systems. Novel imaging approaches including dual-tracer paradigms, displacement studies with pharmacological challenges, and kinetic modeling techniques were systematically evaluated for their ability to quantify neurotransmitter synthesis, release, and receptor occupancy. Results demonstrate significant progress in radioligand development, particularly for previously challenging targets such as metabotropic glutamate receptors (mGluR5) and norepinephrine transporters. Advanced kinetic modeling approaches, including the simplified reference tissue model and Logan graphical analysis, have improved quantification accuracy by 15-30% compared to traditional methods. Pharmacological challenge studies using amphetamine, fenfluramine, and other agents have enabled measurement of endogenous neurotransmitter release with enhanced sensitivity. Clinical applications in Parkinson's disease, depression, schizophrenia, and addiction have yielded new biomarkers for disease progression and treatment response. Future developments in radiochemistry, scanner technology, and computational modeling promise to further expand PET's capacity to elucidate brain neurochemistry, supporting both basic neuroscience research and precision medicine approaches in psychiatry and neurology.

title: The roles of dopamine and serotonin in decision making: evidence from pharmacological experiments in humans.
abstract: Neurophysiological experiments in primates, alongside neuropsychological and functional magnetic resonance investigations in humans, have significantly enhanced our understanding of the neural architecture of decision making. In this review, I consider the more limited database of experiments that have investigated how dopamine and serotonin activity influences the choices of human adults. These include those experiments that have involved the administration of drugs to healthy controls, experiments that have tested genotypic influences upon dopamine and serotonin function, and, finally, some of those experiments that have examined the effects of drugs on the decision making of clinical samples. Pharmacological experiments in humans are few in number and face considerable methodological challenges in terms of drug specificity, uncertainties about pre- vs post-synaptic modes of action, and interactions with baseline cognitive performance. However, the available data are broadly consistent with current computational models of dopamine function in decision making and highlight the dissociable roles of dopamine receptor systems in the learning about outcomes that underpins value-based decision making. Moreover, genotypic influences on (interacting) prefrontal and striatal dopamine activity are associated with changes in choice behavior that might be relevant to understanding exploratory behaviors and vulnerability to addictive disorders. Manipulations of serotonin in laboratory tests of decision making in human participants have provided less consistent results, but the information gathered to date indicates a role for serotonin in learning about bad decision outcomes, non-normative aspects of risk-seeking behavior, and social choices involving affiliation and notions of fairness. Finally, I suggest that the role played by serotonin in the regulation of cognitive biases, and representation of context in learning, point toward a role in the cortically mediated cognitive appraisal of reinforcers when selecting between actions, potentially accounting for its influence upon the processing salient aversive outcomes and social choice.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Annual Research Review: New frontiers in developmental neuropharmacology: can long-term therapeutic effects of drugs be optimized through carefully timed early intervention?
abstract: Our aim is to present a working model that may serve as a valuable heuristic to predict enduring effects of drugs when administered during development. Our primary tenet is that a greater understanding of neurodevelopment can lead to improved treatment that intervenes early in the progression of a given disorder and prevents symptoms from manifesting. The immature brain undergoes significant changes during the transitions between childhood, adolescence, and adulthood. Such changes in innervation, neurotransmitter levels, and their respective signaling mechanisms have profound and observable changes on typical behavior, but also increase vulnerability to psychiatric disorders when the maturational process goes awry. Given the remarkable plasticity of the immature brain to adapt to its external milieu, preventive interventions may be possible. We intend for this review to initiate a discussion of how currently used psychotropic agents can influence brain development. Drug exposure during sensitive periods may have beneficial long-term effects, but harmful delayed consequences may be possible as well. Regardless of the outcome, this information needs to be used to improve or develop alternative approaches for the treatment of childhood disorders. With this framework in mind, we present what is known about the effects of stimulants, antidepressants, and antipsychotics on brain maturation (including animal studies that use more clinically-relevant dosing paradigms or relevant animal models). We endeavor to provocatively set the stage for altering treatment approaches for improving mental health in non-adult populations.
journal: Journal of child psychology and psychiatry, and allied disciplines

title: Interaction Between the Trace Amine-Associated Receptor 1 and the Dopamine D2 Receptor Controls Cocaine's Neurochemical Actions.
abstract: Cocaine's reinforcing effects are primarily mediated through blockade of the dopamine transporter, yet the molecular mechanisms underlying individual vulnerability to cocaine addiction remain poorly understood. The trace amine-associated receptor 1 (TAAR1) has emerged as a potential modulator of dopaminergic signaling, but its interaction with dopamine D2 receptors in cocaine's neurochemical actions has not been thoroughly characterized. We investigated the functional interaction between TAAR1 and D2 receptors using heterologous expression systems, primary striatal cultures, and in vivo microdialysis in rats. Co-immunoprecipitation and proximity ligation assays demonstrated direct physical interaction between TAAR1 and D2 receptors in striatal neurons. Functional studies revealed that TAAR1 activation attenuated D2 receptor-mediated inhibition of adenylyl cyclase activity and reduced D2 receptor surface expression through enhanced internalization. In primary striatal cultures, the selective TAAR1 agonist RO5256390 significantly reduced cocaine-induced dopamine overflow, while TAAR1 knockdown using siRNA enhanced this response. In vivo microdialysis experiments showed that intra-striatal administration of RO5256390 reduced cocaine-evoked dopamine release by 40% compared to vehicle controls. Conversely, TAAR1 knockout mice exhibited enhanced cocaine-induced dopamine efflux and increased locomotor sensitization following repeated cocaine administration. These findings demonstrate that TAAR1 forms functional heteromeric complexes with D2 receptors that negatively modulate dopaminergic signaling and cocaine's neurochemical effects. The TAAR1-D2 receptor interaction represents a novel regulatory mechanism that may contribute to individual differences in cocaine sensitivity and could serve as a therapeutic target for substance use disorders.

title: Neuropharmacology of 3,4-Methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV), Its Metabolites, and Related Analogs.
abstract: 3,4-Methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV) is a psychoactive component of so-called bath salts products that has caused serious medical consequences in humans. In this chapter, we review the neuropharmacology of MDPV and related analogs, and supplement the discussion with new results from our preclinical experiments. MDPV acts as a potent uptake inhibitor at plasma membrane transporters for dopamine (DAT) and norepinephrine (NET) in nervous tissue. The MDPV formulation in bath salts is a racemic mixture, and the S isomer is much more potent than the R isomer at blocking DAT and producing abuse-related effects. Elevations in brain extracellular dopamine produced by MDPV are likely to underlie its locomotor stimulant and addictive properties. MDPV displays rapid pharmacokinetics when injected into rats (0.5-2.0 mg/kg), with peak plasma concentrations achieved by 10-20 min and declining quickly thereafter. MDPV is metabolized to 3,4-dihydroxypyrovalerone (3,4-catechol-PV) and 4-hydroxy-3-methoxypyrovalerone (4-OH-3-MeO-PV) in vivo, but motor activation produced by the drug is positively correlated with plasma concentrations of parent drug and not its metabolites. 3,4-Catechol-PV is a potent uptake blocker at DAT in vitro but has little activity after administration in vivo. 4-OH-3-MeO-PV is the main MDPV metabolite but is weak at DAT and NET. MDPV analogs, such as α-pyrrolidinovalerophenone (α-PVP), display similar ability to inhibit DAT and increase extracellular dopamine concentrations. Taken together, these findings demonstrate that MDPV and its analogs represent a unique class of transporter inhibitors with a high propensity for abuse and addiction.

title: Obsessive-compulsive disorder and tic syndromes.
abstract: The phenomenology of OCD and TS seem to match perfectly with the existing conceptualization of the functional relationship between frontal cortical and subcortical circuits. Failed editing of thoughts and impulses, perseverative patterns, and inhibitory deficits are the most convenient descriptors of the symptoms, and some operationalized measures can capture evidence for such deficits in TS and OCD patients. Beyond these expectations borne from conceptual models and some broad patterns of distributed metabolic disturbances in neuroimaging studies, a specific causal pathology within CSPT circuitry needs to be identified in these disorders. This is not a criticism of the existing studies of TS and OCD; to the contrary, the scarcity of pathologic material, the limits of resolution of existing technologies, and the heterogeneity of the phenotypes make the accomplishments of these studies more impressive. As clinicians strive to integrate clinical and scientific findings into coherent models for the pathophysiology of OCD and TS, it is useful to identify practical and effective strategies for therapeutic interventions.
journal: The Medical clinics of North America

title: Succi nervorum: a brief history of neurochemistry.
abstract: The nature of intracellular communication and integration in the central nervous system remained a source of controversy long after it had been accepted that the brain is intrinsically involved in the reception of external and internal sensory impressions, in the control of both voluntary and involuntary physiological functions, and in the processes associated with consciousness and psychic function in humans. The role of the specific chemistry of the brain in these functions was specifically addressed only in the 20th century, although chemical examination of brain tissue can be traced at least as far back as 1719 to Hensing's Cerebri examen chemicum. Throughout the 1940s and 1950s evidence accumulated from a variety of laboratories that certain chemical substances, such as acetylcholine, noradrenaline and histamine, might be involved in central nervous system neurotransmission, but conclusive evidence for such communication was difficult to obtain. Commencing with Carlsson's 1957 paper on the anti-reserpine effects of DOPA and culminating in the successful amelioration of parkinsonian akinesia by Birkmayer and Hornykiewicz via administration of L-DOPA in 1961, followed by the identification of specific nervous tracts which utilized dopamine as a transmitter, chemical neurotransmission in the brain was ultimately demonstrated through a combination of pharmacological, physiological and clinical research. Neurochemistry had thereby graduated from a branch of general physiology to being centrally involved in models of central nervous system function.

title: Imaging drugs with and without clinical analgesic efficacy.
abstract:
**Background:** Despite decades of research, the translation of preclinical analgesic compounds to successful clinical therapeutics remains challenging, with high failure rates in Phase II and III trials. Neuroimaging approaches may provide translational biomarkers to predict clinical efficacy earlier in drug development.
