We investigated whether functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) can differentiate between compounds with established clinical analgesic efficacy and those that failed in clinical trials despite showing preclinical promise.

**Methods:** Sixteen healthy volunteers underwent pharmacological fMRI in a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study. Participants received single doses of morphine (0.1 mg/kg IV), pregabalin (150 mg oral), substance P NK1 receptor antagonist aprepitant (125 mg oral), and placebo across four sessions. During scanning, subjects underwent experimental pain stimulation using calibrated thermal stimuli applied to the forearm. Blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) signal changes were analyzed in pain-processing regions including anterior cingulate cortex, insula, thalamus, and periaqueductal gray.

**Results:** Morphine and pregabalin, both clinically effective analgesics, significantly reduced pain-evoked BOLD responses in the anterior cingulate cortex (-23% and -18% respectively, p<0.01) and anterior insula (-19% and -15%, p<0.05). In contrast, aprepitant, which failed Phase III clinical trials, showed no significant modulation of pain-related brain activity despite adequate plasma concentrations. Psychophysical pain ratings correlated with neuroimaging findings only for clinically effective compounds (r=0.67, p<0.001).

**Conclusions:** Pharmacological fMRI can distinguish between analgesic compounds with and without clinical efficacy, suggesting this approach may serve as a valuable translational tool for early-stage drug development and reduce late-stage clinical trial failures.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Listening to the brain: microelectrode biosensors for neurochemicals.
abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in the living brain remains a fundamental challenge in neuroscience research. Traditional microdialysis techniques, while valuable, suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage, limiting their utility for studying rapid neurotransmitter fluctuations during behavior and pathological states. Here we review recent advances in microelectrode biosensor technology that enable direct, real-time detection of neurochemicals with unprecedented spatial and temporal resolution. We examine three primary biosensor approaches: enzyme-linked amperometric sensors for glutamate, glucose, and acetylcholine detection; aptamer-based electrochemical sensors for dopamine and serotonin monitoring; and novel carbon fiber electrodes modified with selective recognition elements. Validation studies in anesthetized rats demonstrate detection limits in the low micromolar range with sub-second temporal resolution and minimal tissue disruption. In behaving animals, these biosensors successfully recorded phasic dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens during reward-seeking behavior and monitored glutamate dynamics in the hippocampus during spatial learning tasks. Comparative analyses with established techniques, including fast-scan cyclic voltammetry and microdialysis, confirm the reliability and selectivity of these biosensor platforms. The technology shows particular promise for investigating neurochemical dysfunction in disease models, with preliminary studies in Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease paradigms revealing distinct neurotransmitter signatures. These miniaturized biosensors represent a significant technological advancement, offering researchers powerful tools to decode the chemical language of neural communication with unprecedented precision and enabling new insights into brain function and dysfunction.

title: The ethics of elective psychopharmacology.
abstract: The increasing availability of psychoactive pharmaceuticals for non-therapeutic enhancement has raised significant ethical concerns within neuropharmacology practice and research. While cognitive enhancers, mood stabilizers, and anxiolytics demonstrate clear therapeutic benefits for diagnosed psychiatric conditions, their off-label use by healthy individuals seeking performance enhancement or lifestyle modification presents novel bioethical challenges. This review examines current literature on elective psychopharmacology use, analyzing empirical data from surveys of university students, healthcare professionals, and general population cohorts regarding attitudes toward and prevalence of non-medical psychoactive drug use. We systematically reviewed 127 peer-reviewed articles published between 2008-2012, incorporating data from cross-sectional surveys (n=23,847 participants), longitudinal cohort studies, and expert consensus statements from major psychiatric organizations. Results indicate that 15-30% of university students report lifetime use of prescription stimulants without medical indication, while 8-12% of healthcare professionals acknowledge using cognitive enhancers for performance improvement. Key ethical concerns identified include: informed consent challenges given limited long-term safety data, questions of coercion in competitive environments, distributive justice issues regarding access and affordability, and potential medicalization of normal human variation. Professional guidelines remain inconsistent across jurisdictions, with 67% of surveyed physicians expressing uncertainty about appropriate prescribing boundaries. These findings highlight the urgent need for evidence-based ethical frameworks governing elective psychopharmacology use, incorporating considerations of individual autonomy, societal benefit, and professional responsibility. Further research is warranted to establish safety profiles and develop comprehensive regulatory approaches for enhancement-oriented psychopharmaceutical use.

title: The presence of comorbidity in Tourette syndrome increases the need for pharmacological treatment.
abstract: Tourette syndrome (TS) is a complex neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by motor and vocal tics, frequently accompanied by comorbid conditions including attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), and anxiety disorders. While tics may not always require pharmacological intervention, the impact of comorbidities on treatment decisions remains poorly understood. This retrospective study examined the relationship between comorbid conditions and pharmacological treatment patterns in pediatric TS patients. We analyzed medical records of 247 children and adolescents (ages 6-17 years) diagnosed with TS at two tertiary care centers between 2004-2008. Patients were categorized as TS-only (n=89) or TS with comorbidities (n=158). Comorbid conditions included ADHD (64%), OCD (31%), anxiety disorders (23%), and mood disorders (18%). Clinical Global Impression-Severity scores, tic severity ratings, and medication history were documented. Children with TS plus comorbidities were significantly more likely to receive pharmacological treatment compared to those with TS alone (78% vs 34%, p<0.001). The most commonly prescribed medications in the comorbid group were stimulants (42%), selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (28%), and alpha-2 agonists (35%), often in combination. Antipsychotic medications were used more frequently in patients with severe tics and comorbid behavioral symptoms (31% vs 12%, p<0.01). Functional impairment scores were significantly higher in the comorbid group (p<0.001). These findings suggest that the presence of psychiatric comorbidities, rather than tic severity alone, drives the need for pharmacological intervention in pediatric TS, highlighting the importance of comprehensive psychiatric evaluation in treatment planning.

title: Carbon nanofiber electrode for neurochemical monitoring.
abstract: The development of sensitive, biocompatible electrodes for real-time neurochemical monitoring remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional carbon fiber microelectrodes, while widely used for fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) measurements of neurotransmitters, suffer from limited sensitivity and fouling over extended recording periods. Here, we investigate the electrochemical properties and in vivo performance of carbon nanofiber (CNF) electrodes for detecting dopamine and serotonin in the rodent brain. CNF electrodes were fabricated by pyrolysis of electrospun polyacrylonitrile fibers, resulting in structures with diameters of 200-500 nm and lengths of 50-100 μm. Surface characterization revealed increased electroactive surface area compared to conventional carbon fibers, with enhanced edge plane defects promoting electron transfer kinetics. In vitro calibrations demonstrated 3-fold greater sensitivity for dopamine detection (412 ± 38 nA/μM) and improved selectivity against ascorbic acid interference. Chronic implantation studies in anesthetized rats showed stable dopamine responses in the nucleus accumbens for up to 6 hours, compared to 2-3 hours for standard carbon fiber electrodes. CNF electrodes successfully resolved pharmacologically-evoked dopamine transients following cocaine administration (1 mg/kg, i.p.) with superior signal-to-noise ratios. Histological analysis revealed minimal tissue inflammatory responses around CNF implant sites. These findings demonstrate that carbon nanofiber electrodes offer significant advantages for neurochemical monitoring applications, providing enhanced sensitivity, stability, and biocompatibility for investigating neurotransmitter dynamics in behaving animals and advancing our understanding of neuropsychiatric disorders.

title: Neuropharmacology of memory consolidation and reconsolidation: Insights on central cholinergic mechanisms.
abstract: Memory consolidation and reconsolidation represent critical phases in the formation and maintenance of long-term memories, yet the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying these processes remain incompletely understood. The cholinergic system, particularly through muscarinic and nicotinic receptor subtypes, has emerged as a key modulator of memory processing. Here, we investigated the differential roles of central cholinergic mechanisms in memory consolidation versus reconsolidation using a contextual fear conditioning paradigm in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received bilateral hippocampal or medial prefrontal cortex infusions of the muscarinic antagonist scopolamine (10 μg/side), the nicotinic antagonist mecamylamine (5 μg/side), or vehicle at specific time points relative to training or memory reactivation. Post-training scopolamine administration significantly impaired consolidation when delivered within 6 hours but not 24 hours after acquisition, demonstrating a time-dependent consolidation window. Conversely, scopolamine infusion immediately following memory reactivation disrupted subsequent recall, indicating cholinergic involvement in reconsolidation. Mecamylamine showed similar but less pronounced effects, with regional specificity favoring hippocampal over prefrontal involvement. Interestingly, co-administration of the acetylcholinesterase inhibitor physostigmine (0.5 μg/side) rescued scopolamine-induced impairments in both consolidation and reconsolidation phases. These findings demonstrate that central cholinergic signaling, primarily through muscarinic receptors, is essential for both the initial stabilization and subsequent restabilization of contextual memories. Our results provide novel insights into the temporal dynamics and receptor specificity of cholinergic modulation in memory processing, with potential implications for therapeutic interventions targeting memory-related disorders.

title: A Pressure Injection System for Investigating the Neuropharmacology of Information Processing in Awake Behaving Macaque Monkey Cortex.
abstract: The top-down modulation of feed-forward cortical information processing is functionally important for many cognitive processes, including the modulation of sensory information processing by attention. However, little is known about which neurotransmitter systems are involved in such modulations. A practical way to address this question is to combine single-cell recording with local and temporary neuropharmacological manipulation in a suitable animal model. Here we demonstrate a technique combining acute single-cell recordings with the injection of neuropharmacological agents in the direct vicinity of the recording electrode. The video shows the preparation of the pressure injection/recording system, including preparation of the substance to be injected. We show a rhesus monkey performing a visual attention task and the procedure of single-unit recording with block-wise pharmacological manipulations.

title: Psychopharmacology and ethnicity: A comparative study on Senegalese and Italian men.
abstract: Ethnic variations in drug metabolism and response represent a critical yet understudied aspect of psychopharmacology, with potential implications for personalized medicine approaches in psychiatry. This study investigated pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic differences in psychotropic drug response between Senegalese and Italian men to inform evidence-based prescribing practices across diverse populations. We conducted a randomized, double-blind, crossover study involving 84 healthy male volunteers (42 Senegalese, 42 Italian, aged 21-35 years). Participants received single doses of risperidone (2mg), sertraline (50mg), and lorazepam (1mg) in separate sessions with 14-day washout periods. Primary outcomes included plasma drug concentrations, metabolite ratios, and standardized cognitive and psychomotor assessments. Genotyping for CYP2D6, CYP2C19, and CYP3A4 polymorphisms was performed using TaqMan assays. Senegalese participants demonstrated significantly higher plasma concentrations of risperidone (mean AUC: 28.4±8.2 vs 19.7±6.1 ng·h/mL, p<0.001) and sertraline (mean AUC: 124.3±31.8 vs 89.2±24.6 ng·h/mL, p<0.01) compared to Italian participants. CYP2D6 poor metabolizer phenotypes were more prevalent among Senegalese participants (23.8% vs 7.1%, p<0.05). Lorazepam pharmacokinetics showed no significant ethnic differences. Cognitive impairment scores were correspondingly higher in Senegalese participants for risperidone and sertraline conditions. These findings demonstrate clinically relevant ethnic differences in psychotropic drug metabolism, primarily mediated by CYP2D6 polymorphisms. Our results support the implementation of pharmacogenomic testing and ethnicity-informed dosing strategies to optimize therapeutic outcomes and minimize adverse effects in diverse psychiatric populations.
journal: The world journal of biological psychiatry : the official journal of the World Federation of Societies of Biological Psychiatry

title: Methodological approaches to exploring epileptic disorders in the human brain in vitro.
abstract: The investigation of human epileptic tissue remains challenging due to limited availability and methodological constraints. We developed and validated a comprehensive in vitro approach for studying epileptogenic mechanisms using surgically resected human brain tissue from patients with medically refractory epilepsy. Fresh tissue specimens from temporal lobe (n=24) and neocortical (n=18) resections were obtained within 30 minutes of surgical removal and maintained in oxygenated artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 32°C. Slice preparations (400 μm thickness) were prepared using a vibrating microtome and subjected to electrophysiological recordings using multi-electrode arrays and patch-clamp techniques. Neuronal viability was assessed through propidium iodide staining and measurement of ATP levels over 8-hour periods. Spontaneous epileptiform activity was recorded in 67% of hippocampal slices and 43% of neocortical preparations. Application of 4-aminopyridine (100 μM) reliably induced seizure-like events in previously quiescent tissue, with characteristic high-frequency oscillations (80-200 Hz) preceding ictal discharges. Pharmacological testing with standard antiepileptic drugs (phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate) demonstrated concentration-dependent suppression of epileptiform activity, validating the model's utility for drug screening. Neuronal viability remained above 85% for up to 6 hours post-resection when optimal storage conditions were maintained. This methodology provides a robust platform for investigating human epileptogenic mechanisms and evaluating novel therapeutic interventions using authentic pathological tissue, offering significant advantages over animal models for translational epilepsy research.

title: 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline antagonizes a rise in brain dopamine metabolism, glutamate release in frontal cortex and locomotor hyperactivity produced by MK-801 but not the disruptions of prepulse inhibition, and impairment of working memory in rat.
abstract: 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (1MeTIQ) is an endogenous compound that is constantly present in the brain, and that exhibits neuroprotective activity. Our earlier study has suggested that 1MeTIQ may play a crucial physiological role in the mammalian brain as an endogenous regulator of dopaminergic activity. It is well known that central nervous system stimulants such as: amphetamine, cocaine, phencyclidine, and selective NMDA receptor antagonists, e.g., MK-801 produce neuropsychotoxicity (psychosis, addiction) that is indistinguishable from paranoid type schizophrenia. In rodents, phencyclidine and MK-801 are often used to evoke schizophrenia-like behavioral abnormalities which are inhibited by neuroleptics. The present study was designed to further investigate potential antipsychotic properties of 1MeTIQ by using both behavioral and neurochemical studies in the rat. We investigated the influence of 1MeTIQ (25 and 50 mg/kg ip) on locomotor hyperactivity, disruptions of prepulse inhibition (PPI), and working memory impairment induced by the NMDA receptor antagonist, MK-801 (0.2-0.3 mg/kg ip). In addition in the biochemical study, we analyzed the effect of 1MeTIQ on the changes in dopamine metabolism in different brain structures and in extraneuronal release of dopamine and glutamate in the rat frontal cortex, produced by MK-801. The concentration of dopamine (DA) and its metabolites: 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC), 3-methoxytyramine (3-MT), and homovanillic acid (HVA), as well as the extraneuronal concentration of dopamine and glutamate were established by HPLC. MK-801 (0.25 mg/kg ip) evoked significant disruptions of PPI and working memory impairment, and co-administration of 1MeTIQ at two investigated doses of 25 and 50 mg/kg ip did not antagonize these effects. On the other hand hyperactivity evoked by MK-801 as well as a rise in dopamine metabolism in specific brain structures and glutamate release in the frontal cortex was completely antagonized by pretreatment with 1MeTIQ. If the hyperlocomotion elicited by acutely administered MK-801 is a valid model of at least some aspects of schizophrenia, these results indicate that 1MeTIQ will show efficacy in treating this condition. In conclusions, the present study suggests that 1MeTIQ, an endogenous neuroprotective compound, exhibits also antipsychotic-like efficacy in some animal tests, and may be useful in clinical practice as a psychosis-attenuating drug in schizophrenic patients. However, 1MeTIQ did not attenuate sensorimotor gating deficit or working memory impairment evoked by MK-801 which may be served as a model of negative symptoms of schizophrenia.

title: Inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs.
abstract: Traditional approaches to nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) pharmacology have focused on drug binding and functional modulation at the extracellular surface, yet emerging evidence suggests that intracellular mechanisms play crucial roles in therapeutic efficacy. Here we investigated the "inside-out" neuropharmacological properties of nicotinic drugs using a combination of patch-clamp electrophysiology, fluorescence imaging, and behavioral assays in rodent models. We examined the effects of varenicline, cytisine, and novel synthetic compounds on α4β2 and α7 nAChRs when applied to the intracellular membrane surface of excised patches from rat hippocampal neurons. Intracellular application of varenicline (10-100 μM) produced sustained receptor activation distinct from its extracellular partial agonist profile, with efficacy reaching 85% of maximum ACh responses. Cytisine showed similar inside-out enhancement, while traditional antagonists like mecamylamine exhibited unexpected positive modulatory effects when applied intracellularly. Calcium imaging revealed that inside-out nicotinic activation triggered sustained Ca²⁺ elevations lasting 3-fold longer than extracellular stimulation. In behavioral studies, intracerebroventricular administration of varenicline produced enhanced cognitive performance in novel object recognition tasks compared to systemic delivery, despite lower total brain exposure. These findings demonstrate that nicotinic drugs exhibit fundamentally different pharmacological profiles when accessing receptors from the intracellular compartment, suggesting that drug trafficking across membranes and accumulation in cytoplasmic domains may be critical determinants of therapeutic outcomes. This inside-out perspective opens new avenues for nicotinic drug development and may explain variable clinical responses to current smoking cessation therapies.

title: Addiction: decreased reward sensitivity and increased expectation sensitivity conspire to overwhelm the brain's control circuit.
abstract: Based on brain imaging findings, we present a model according to which addiction emerges as an imbalance in the information processing and integration among various brain circuits and functions. The dysfunctions reflect (a) decreased sensitivity of reward circuits, (b) enhanced sensitivity of memory circuits to conditioned expectations to drugs and drug cues, stress reactivity, and (c) negative mood, and a weakened control circuit. Although initial experimentation with a drug of abuse is largely a voluntary behavior, continued drug use can eventually impair neuronal circuits in the brain that are involved in free will, turning drug use into an automatic compulsive behavior. The ability of addictive drugs to co-opt neurotransmitter signals between neurons (including dopamine, glutamate, and GABA) modifies the function of different neuronal circuits, which begin to falter at different stages of an addiction trajectory. Upon exposure to the drug, drug cues or stress this results in unrestrained hyperactivation of the motivation/drive circuit that results in the compulsive drug intake that characterizes addiction.
journal: BioEssays : news and reviews in molecular, cellular and developmental biology

title: Evidence-Based Prescribing in Mental Health Nursing.
abstract: Since Sackett et al.'s epic throw down of the gauntlet in 1996, the hallmark of quality health care practice has been evidence-based clinical decision making. But what does it mean to base one's practice on evidence, and what are the barriers to evidence-based prescribing? The current article addresses these questions and offers suggestions to improve one's evidence-based practice. [Journal of Psychosocial Nursing and Mental Health Services, 57(6), 9-13.].
journal: Journal of psychosocial nursing and mental health services

title: In vivo voltammetry: from wire to wireless measurements.
abstract: A novel telemetric system based on either differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) or direct current amperometry (DCA) by using a diffused infrared transmission channel is presented. Unlike similar pre-existing instruments based on infrared transmission, the present system works on a single-way communication, thus avoiding problems related to cross-talking between two-way channels. The infrared channel is also immune from electromagnetic interferences from the surrounding environment. Further advancement is the development of an original miniaturised system (dimension 1cm x 1.2 cm x 0.5 cm) with reduced weight (5-6 g), suitable for affixing to the rat head and allowing real time telemetric monitoring using DCA sampling of neurotransmitters such as dopamine or serotonin every 100 ms. The set-up is based on a transmitter (TX) circuit mounted on the animal's head and connected to the electrodes inserted into its brain. The TX circuit generates the proper electrical signals for DPV or DCA, collects the electrical response of the brain and transmits it, via an infrared channel, to a receiving station (RX) interfaced with a personal computer. The PC performs the sampling and elaboration of polarographic traces in a flexible and programmable way.

title: Future treatments for Parkinson's disease: surfing the PD pipeline.
abstract: Our current wish list for the treatment of Parkinson's disease (PD) includes therapies that will provide robust and sustained antiparkinsonian benefit through the day, ameliorate or prevent dyskinesia, and slow or prevent the progression of the disease. In this article, I review selected new therapies in clinical development for motor features or treatment complications of PD, and some that may slow disease progression. These include adenosine 2a (A2a) antagonists (istradefylline, preladenant, and SYN115), levodopa/carbidopa intestinal gel (LCIG), IPX066--an extended-release formulation of carbidopa/levodopa, XP21279--a sustained-release levodopa prodrug, ND0611--a carbidopa subcutaneous patch, safinamide--a mixed mechanism of action medication that may provide both MAO-B and glutamate inhibition, PMY50028--an oral neurotrophic factor inducer, antidyskinesia medications (AFQ056 and fipamezole), and gene therapies (AAV2-neurturin and glutamic acid decarboxylase gene transfer). Some of these therapies will never be proven efficacious and will not come to market while others may play a key role in the future treatment of PD.

title: Phencyclidine/schizophrenia: one view toward the past, the other to the future.
abstract: The N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction hypothesis of schizophrenia has gained substantial support over the past two decades, largely driven by observations that phencyclidine (PCP) and other NMDA receptor antagonists produce both positive and negative symptoms resembling schizophrenia in healthy individuals. This comprehensive review examines the evolution of PCP research from its initial discovery as a dissociative anesthetic to its current role as a leading translational model for schizophrenia pathophysiology. We systematically analyzed preclinical and clinical literature spanning 1959-2012, focusing on PCP's mechanisms of action, behavioral effects, and neurobiological consequences relevant to schizophrenia research. Historical studies demonstrated that acute PCP administration in humans produced hallucinations, delusions, and thought disorder, while chronic use led to persistent cognitive deficits and negative symptoms. Preclinical models revealed that PCP treatment induces gamma oscillation abnormalities, parvalbumin-positive interneuron dysfunction, and altered dopaminergic signaling—findings consistently replicated in schizophrenia patients. Furthermore, PCP-treated animals exhibit deficits in prepulse inhibition, working memory, and social behavior that respond to both typical and atypical antipsychotics. Recent molecular studies have elucidated downstream signaling cascades involving mTOR, DISC1, and neuregulin pathways, providing mechanistic links between NMDA hypofunction and schizophrenia risk genes. While acknowledging limitations in directly extrapolating from PCP effects to schizophrenia etiology, this model has proven invaluable for understanding circuit-level dysfunction and continues to guide novel therapeutic development targeting glutamatergic, GABAergic, and cholinergic systems in schizophrenia treatment.

title: Therapeutic potential of vanilloid receptor TRPV1 agonists and antagonists as analgesics: Recent advances and setbacks.
abstract: The vanilloid receptor TRPV1 is a homotetrameric, non-selective cation channel abundantly expressed in the nociceptors (c-fibers). TRPV1 is considered as a highly validated pain target because, i) its agonists such as capsaicin cause desensitization of TRPV1 channels that relieves pain behaviors in preclinical species, and ii) its antagonists relieve pain behaviors in rodent models of inflammation, osteoarthritis, and cancer. Hence, both agonists and antagonists of TRPV1 are being evaluated as potential analgesics in clinical trials. Clinical trial results of TRPV1 agonists such as resiniferatoxin in interstitial cystitis, NGX 4010 in post-herpetic neuralgia, and 4975 (Adlea) in osteoarthritis, bunionectomy, and Morton's neuroma have been reported. Similarly, clinical trial results of TRPV1 antagonists such as SB-705498 and AMG 517 have also been published recently. Overall, some molecules (e.g., capsaicin) demonstrated potential analgesia in certain conditions (postsurgical pain, postherpetic neuralgia, pain in diabetic neuropathy, osteoarthritis, bunionectomy, and Morton's neuroma), whereas others fell out of the clinic due to on-target liabilities or failed to demonstrate efficacy. This review summarizes recent advances and setbacks of TRPV1 agonists and antagonists in the clinic and predicts future directions.
